{"core": "Per RBR-4 section 6, international third-party communications during emergencies or disaster relief are expressly permitted unless specifically prohibited by the foreign administration involved.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The permission has nothing to do with whether satellite links can originate in the disaster area; the only limiting condition is a prohibition by the foreign administration.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a plausible practical caveat about communication capability during a disaster."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A declared state of war is not the condition cited; the rule turns solely on whether the foreign administration prohibits such traffic.", "why_tempting": "War seems like a logical reason a country might restrict communications, so it feels reasonable."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Internet availability is irrelevant to amateur third-party emergency traffic rules; the only exception is a prohibition by the foreign administration.", "why_tempting": "It superficially connects to whether emergency communications are 'needed,' but the regulation makes no such qualification."}], "concept": "Third-party traffic means messages passed on behalf of someone who is not a licensed amateur. RBR-4 states Canada does not prohibit international third-party communications, and in emergencies or disaster relief such traffic is expressly permitted — the single exception being where the foreign administration specifically forbids it. The governing factor is always the rules of the other country's regulator, not technical conditions.", "misconception": "Believing the exception relates to technical circumstances (satellite, internet) or geopolitical state (war) rather than the regulatory stance of the foreign administration.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "The foreign administration has the final say — only their prohibition stops emergency third-party traffic.", "edge_cases": "Note that some countries do restrict third-party traffic in normal (non-emergency) operation; the emergency/disaster-relief provision is the relaxed default unless that country opts out."}

{"core": "A \"third party\" is any non-licensed person whose message is passed through the amateur stations. Both the local friend at the Canadian end and the non-certified person at the foreign end are third parties, since neither holds amateur certification.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The licensed foreign amateur is an operator, not a third party; only the non-certified person at the foreign station qualifies.", "why_tempting": "It correctly identifies the foreign non-certified person but wrongly lumps in the licensed foreign operator."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The foreign amateur operator holds a licence and operates the station, so they are never the third party. Only the two non-certified individuals are.", "why_tempting": "It captures both non-certified persons but incorrectly adds the licensed operator."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This counts only the Canadian-side friend and ignores the equally non-certified person at the foreign station.", "why_tempting": "From a Canadian operator's viewpoint, the local friend feels like 'the' third party, but third-party status applies at both ends."}], "concept": "Third-party traffic is communication on behalf of, or involving, persons who are not licensed amateur operators. The licensed operators at each end are the station controllers; anyone they relay messages for who lacks certification is a third party. Here there are two such people — one at the Canadian station and one at the foreign station — so both are third parties. RBR-4 section 6 notes Canada does not prohibit international third-party communications, which presupposes that non-certified persons on either end are the third parties whose traffic is being handled.", "misconception": "Believing 'third party' is defined relative to your own station only (the foreign person counts), or that a licensed foreign operator can somehow be a third party.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Licence = operator; no licence = third party — count heads on BOTH ends.", "edge_cases": "Operators can speak to each other freely; the third-party rules only matter when an unlicensed person's message is being relayed. In emergencies/disaster relief, international third-party traffic is expressly permitted unless a foreign administration prohibits it."}

{"core": "A U.S. licensee operating in Canada must add the Canadian call sign prefix appropriate to their geographic location, which means the contact must convey where in Canada (the province/region) they are operating — i.e. identify their location by city and province.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A grid square (Maidenhead locator) is not a required identification element under the station-identification rules; it's a convenience used in VHF/contesting, not a regulatory requirement.", "why_tempting": "Grid squares are commonly exchanged in contacts, so it feels like 'location' information."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Output power is never part of required station identification; identification is about call sign and location, not transmitter parameters.", "why_tempting": "Power limits exist in RBR-4, so a learner may conflate power restrictions with identification duties."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The rule requires the Canadian prefix matching the geographic location *in Canada* — city and province — not the operator's U.S. city/state of residence.", "why_tempting": "It mirrors the correct answer's structure (city + jurisdiction) but uses U.S. residence instead of the Canadian operating location."}], "concept": "Per RBR-4 section 9, a U.S.-licensed operator in Canada identifies by: (a) transmitting the FCC-assigned call sign; (b) adding 'mobile'/'portable' (phone) or '/' (CW); and (c) adding the Canadian call sign prefix from Schedule V Column I corresponding to the geographic location (Column II) of the station. Because the appended prefix is geographic, the operator is effectively indicating where they are — city and province — at least once during the exchange. Identification must occur at the start and end of each contact and at intervals not exceeding 30 minutes.", "misconception": "Confusing the operator's home residence (U.S. city/state) with the required indication of the present Canadian operating location, or assuming identification includes technical data like power or grid square.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "FCC call + 'portable/mobile' + Canadian prefix = where you ARE in Canada (city & province).", "edge_cases": "This applies to authorized foreign (U.S.) amateurs operating physically in Canada; a Canadian operator simply transmits their own assigned call sign."}

{"core": "Per RBR-4 s.9, a US-licensed operator in Canada must transmit their FCC call sign, append \"mobile\"/\"portable\" (voice), AND add the Canadian call sign prefix from Schedule V corresponding to the geographic location of the station.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The rule requires a standardized Canadian prefix code, not the name of a town or community.", "why_tempting": "Naming a location seems like a reasonable way to convey where you are operating."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "You add the prefix code (e.g. VE3, VE7), not the spoken name of the province or territory.", "why_tempting": "The prefix does map to a geographic region, so the province seems equivalent — but the regulation specifies the prefix itself."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Transmit frequency is set by your rig and band plan; it has nothing to do with identification requirements.", "why_tempting": "Frequency is operationally important and a tempting 'extra info' answer, but it is not part of the ID rule."}], "concept": "RBR-4 s.9 sets identification rules for US-licensed amateurs operating in Canada: (1) transmit the FCC-assigned call sign; (2) add \"mobile\" or \"portable\" for radiotelephony (or \"/\" for radiotelegraphy); and (3) append the Canadian prefix from Column I of Schedule V matching the station's geographic location (Column II). This tells Canadian stations both who you are and where, using the recognized Canadian prefix system rather than free-form location text.", "misconception": "Believing the location must be stated as a plain-language place name (community or province), when the regulation actually requires the formal Canadian call sign prefix code.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Call + portable/mobile + Canadian PREFIX (not the place name).", "edge_cases": "For radiotelegraphy (CW) the qualifier is the oblique \"/\" instead of the spoken words \"mobile\"/\"portable\"."}

{"core": "RBR-4 states Canada does not prohibit international third-party communications, and any foreign administration may permit its amateur stations to handle such traffic. With no Canadian restriction, a Canadian station may pass third-party traffic with any amateur station (subject to the foreign administration's own rules).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Emergency/disaster relief is the one case where third-party traffic is *expressly* permitted even if normally prohibited — but it is not the *only* permitted case. Canada permits it generally.", "why_tempting": "Many countries (notably the US) heavily restrict third-party traffic except in emergencies, so this matches the rule learners may recall from elsewhere."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Canada does not require a third-party traffic agreement; it simply does not prohibit such communications. The 'agreement' model is a US/ITU convention, not the Canadian rule.", "why_tempting": "The phrase 'third-party traffic agreement' is a real concept in international amateur radio and sounds authoritative."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "CFARS is a separate military-affiliate service with its own frequencies and authorizations; it has nothing to do with amateur third-party communications under RBR-4.", "why_tempting": "CFARS is associated with passing messages/traffic, so it loosely connects to the idea of relaying communications for others."}], "concept": "Per RBR-4, third-party communications are messages an amateur passes on behalf of someone who is not a licensed operator. The Canadian standard explicitly says Canada does not prohibit international third-party communications, and that a foreign administration may permit its own stations to engage in such traffic without a special arrangement with Canada. In emergencies or disaster relief, international third-party traffic is expressly permitted unless a foreign administration specifically prohibits it. The practical limit therefore comes from the *other* country's rules, not Canada's.", "misconception": "That third-party traffic always requires a formal bilateral agreement, or is restricted to emergencies only — a rule that applies to some administrations (e.g. the US) but not to Canada's permissive stance.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Canada doesn't say no — third party with any station, the limit is the *other* country's rules.", "edge_cases": "The receiving station's home administration may still forbid third-party traffic; the openness is on the Canadian side."}

{"core": "When your station transmits under your call sign, the privileges in effect are tied to YOUR operator certificate, not the operator's. The presence of a more-qualified person at the controls does not extend the station's authorization beyond what your own qualifications permit.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Privileges follow the call sign being used and the certificate behind it. A guest operator's higher qualifications do not transfer to your station identity.", "why_tempting": "It feels natural that a more-qualified hand could 'unlock' more bands or modes, the way a licensed driver can supervise a learner."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "There is no split where emission privileges come from the guest and frequency privileges from you — all privileges are capped by your certificate when your call sign is in use.", "why_tempting": "The artificial separation of emission vs. frequency sounds like a plausible regulatory nuance."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Same flaw as C but with the categories reversed; no such partial transfer of privileges exists.", "why_tempting": "It mirrors C and the symmetry makes the pair look like one must be the 'real' rule."}], "concept": "Operating authority is granted to the certificate holder and is exercised under that holder's call sign. The controlling concept: the call sign identifies the responsible licensee, and the operating privileges available are exactly those of that licensee's qualification level (Basic, Basic with Honours, Advanced, Morse). A guest with Advanced qualification operating your Basic station under YOUR call sign is bound to Basic privileges, because the transmission is identified as and attributable to you. Conversely, if a more-qualified operator wanted to use their own privileges, they would identify with their own call sign. There is no mechanism to mix-and-match emission and frequency privileges between two operators.", "misconception": "Believing that a more-qualified person physically at the controls raises the station's privilege ceiling. The qualification that matters is the one tied to the call sign being transmitted, not who is turning the dial.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Privileges follow the call sign, not the hand on the knob.", "edge_cases": "If the guest instead operates under their own call sign at your station, their qualifications then govern — the distinction hinges on whose call sign identifies the transmission."}

{"core": "Operating privileges follow the certificate of the person at the controls (the control operator), not the owner of the station. Your privileges are capped by your own qualifications regardless of whose equipment you use.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "You cannot borrow the station owner's frequency privileges at all; both frequency and emission privileges are bound to your own certificate.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a reasonable compromise where the equipment grants band access but you keep your emission limits."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The station's own equipment or owner does not elevate your authority; privileges are personal to the control operator's qualifications.", "why_tempting": "Learners assume that using a higher-qualified operator's station inherits that station's full privileges."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "There is no split where you gain emission privileges but keep your frequency limits; everything is governed by your certificate.", "why_tempting": "It mirrors option A as a plausible-sounding partial inheritance and may confuse those who can't recall which way the split goes."}], "concept": "The 'control operator' is the licensed amateur responsible for the transmissions at any given moment. Privileges (which bands, which emission types, what power) are an attribute of the individual's Amateur Radio Operator Certificate qualifications, not of the radio station or its owner. So if you hold only a Basic qualification and operate a friend's station who holds Basic with Honours or Advanced, you are still restricted to Basic-qualification privileges. Conversely, a higher-qualified operator at your station could use their fuller privileges. The hardware never expands what your certificate authorizes.", "misconception": "That borrowing a better-equipped station, or operating gear owned by a more-qualified amateur, somehow upgrades your operating authority. Authority is tied to the operator, not the equipment.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "The privileges sit in the operator's pocket, not in the radio's chassis.", "edge_cases": "This is distinct from supervised operation arrangements; here you are the responsible control operator, so your own limits apply throughout."}

{"core": "HF privileges (bands below 30 MHz) require more than a bare Basic pass: you need Basic with Honours (80% or above), OR additionally hold the Morse Code (5 w.p.m.) qualification, OR the Advanced qualification.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "No mandatory class exists; qualifications are earned by examination, not attendance.", "why_tempting": "HF operation seems complex enough that formal instruction sounds plausible."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "There is no notification requirement to ISED to unlock HF — privileges flow from the qualifications you hold, not from a notice you file.", "why_tempting": "ISED does require notifying address changes within 30 days, so a notification rule sounds familiar."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Amateur certification is federal (ISED), not provincial; no provincial exam exists.", "why_tempting": "Many trades and licences are provincially administered, so this feels reasonable."}], "concept": "Per Schedule I of RBR-4, every band below 30 MHz lists the operator qualifications as \"B and 5, B/H, B and A\". A plain Basic pass (50–79%) only authorizes the VHF/UHF bands (50 MHz and up). To access HF you must hold one of: Basic with Honours (B/H, a Basic score of 80% or above), Basic plus the 5 w.p.m. Morse Code qualification, or Basic plus Advanced.", "misconception": "Believing the Basic written pass alone grants all-band privileges. The Basic pass mark (50%) gives only VHF/UHF; the 80% Honours threshold (or Morse/Advanced) is the gate to HF.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "\"Honours, Code, or Advanced\" = the three keys below 30 MHz.", "edge_cases": "The three routes are equivalent for HF access — none gives more HF spectrum than the others at the Basic level; only Advanced raises your power limits."}

{"core": "RBR-4 limits radio control of models to any frequency within the amateur bands above 30 MHz, as set out in Schedule I. Any amateur certificate holder may use these bands for model control.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "No modulation type is mandated for model control; the rule restricts the frequency range, not the emission format.", "why_tempting": "Pulse-position/pulse-width modulation is the dominant scheme in hobby RC gear, so it sounds like a legal requirement."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "There is no special 15 kHz bandwidth rule for model control; the normal Schedule I bandwidth limits for the band apply (e.g. 30 kHz at 50–148 MHz).", "why_tempting": "It looks like a plausible technical spec and narrow-channel RC controllers reinforce the idea of a tight bandwidth cap."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Model control is explicitly confined to bands above 30 MHz; HF bands below 30 MHz are not permitted for this purpose.", "why_tempting": "It is the exact mirror of the correct answer, easy to pick if you misremember the direction of the limit."}], "concept": "Section 5 of RBR-4 states that the frequency for radio control of a model is limited to any frequency within the frequency bands above 30 MHz, as set out in Schedule I. The reason is practical: above 30 MHz includes the VHF/UHF amateur allocations where line-of-sight, short-range control fits the local-coverage propagation, and it keeps RC traffic away from the long-haul HF spectrum used for distant communication.", "misconception": "Believing the regulation specifies a modulation type or a unique bandwidth for model control, when it only specifies the >30 MHz frequency constraint.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Models fly HIGH — keep RC control above 30 MHz.", "edge_cases": "The usual per-band bandwidth limits in Schedule I still apply; the >30 MHz rule is in addition to, not a replacement for, those bandwidth values."}

{"core": "The 80-metre band in Canada spans 3.500–4.000 MHz, as listed in Schedule I of RBR-4 (item 4). A 3.5 MHz signal has a wavelength near 80 m, matching the band name.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "4.5–5.0 MHz is not an allocated amateur HF band; it falls between 80 m and the 60 m channels.", "why_tempting": "Plausible-looking HF numbers that bracket the 60-metre channelized region (around 5.3 MHz), but they don't correspond to any band."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "3.0–3.5 MHz is below the 80 m allocation; the band starts at 3.500 MHz, not 3.000.", "why_tempting": "3.5 MHz sits at the upper edge, so a learner who anchors on '3.5' may shift the window the wrong way."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "4.0–4.5 MHz lies above the 80 m band's 4.000 MHz upper edge — no amateur allocation there.", "why_tempting": "4.0 MHz is the band's top edge, so this range looks like it could be a continuation."}], "concept": "Wavelength λ = c/f. For f = 3.75 MHz (mid-band), λ ≈ 3×10⁸ / 3.75×10⁶ ≈ 80 m, which is how the band gets its name. Canadian Basic+ privileges cover the full 3.500–4.000 MHz with a 6 kHz maximum bandwidth (Schedule I, item 4). The convention is to name HF bands by the approximate wavelength at the band edge/center, not by the exact frequency.", "misconception": "Confusing the band's nominal name (80 m) with off-by-edge frequency windows, or assuming bands are exactly 0.5 MHz blocks starting at round numbers.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "80 m → about 3.5 MHz: λ(m) ≈ 300 / f(MHz), so 300/3.75 ≈ 80.", "edge_cases": "Note 3.5 MHz also corresponds to the 80 m band start, but the band extends to 4.0 MHz — sometimes called the '75/80 m' band, where the upper phone segment is loosely the '75 m' portion."}

{"core": "The 160-metre band in Canada spans 1.800–2.000 MHz, per Schedule I of RBR-4, item 3.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "2.25–2.5 MHz is not an amateur allocation and lies above the 160 m band.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible MF range and the numbers are near the right magnitude."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "1.5 MHz is below the band edge; the band starts at 1.8 MHz, not 1.5 MHz.", "why_tempting": "The upper edge (2.0 MHz) is correct, so it's half-right and easy to pick."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "2.0–2.25 MHz starts exactly where 160 m ends and is not allocated to amateurs.", "why_tempting": "Begins at the real upper edge (2.0 MHz), so it feels adjacent and plausible."}], "concept": "Band names refer to the approximate wavelength: λ = c/f. At 1.8 MHz, λ = 3×10⁸ / 1.8×10⁶ ≈ 167 m, and at 2.0 MHz, λ ≈ 150 m — both round to roughly 160 metres, hence the band's nickname. The actual regulatory edges are fixed at 1.800–2.000 MHz (a 200 kHz wide MF allocation), open to Basic-qualified operators with a maximum bandwidth of 6 kHz.", "misconception": "Confusing the nominal wavelength label with the exact frequency edges, or sliding the band up/down by 200 kHz. The center-wavelength name does not pin down the precise MHz limits.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "160 m = 'eighteen-hundred to two-thousand' kHz (1.8–2.0 MHz).", "edge_cases": "This is an MF band; despite the wavelength label it behaves like the top of the broadcast AM region, with strong daytime D-layer absorption and good night-time skip."}

{"core": "The 40-metre band in Canada spans 7.000–7.300 MHz, as listed in Schedule I, item 10 of RBR-4. A wavelength of ~40 m corresponds to roughly 7 MHz (λ = c/f → 300/7.15 ≈ 42 m).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "7.7–8.0 MHz lies above the allocated band; there is no amateur allocation there.", "why_tempting": "Numerically close to the upper edge of the real band, so it looks plausible if you misremember the limits."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "6.5–6.8 MHz is below the allocation and not an amateur band.", "why_tempting": "6.5 MHz computes to a wavelength near 46 m, which a careless estimate might round toward '40 m'."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "6.0–6.3 MHz is outside any amateur allocation (it falls in broadcast/shortwave territory).", "why_tempting": "Looks like a tidy round-number band and can be confused if you anchor on '6' for a 'low-HF' band."}], "concept": "Amateur band names are nominal wavelengths derived from the band's approximate centre frequency via λ = c/f. For 40 m, take f ≈ 7.15 MHz: λ = 3×10⁸ / 7.15×10⁶ ≈ 42 m, which rounds to the '40-metre' designation. The actual Canadian allocation (RBR-4 Schedule I, item 10) is 7.000–7.300 MHz with a 6 kHz maximum bandwidth, available to Basic-with-extra qualifications (B and 5, B/H, B and A).", "misconception": "Assuming the band edges equal the nominal wavelength directly, or sliding the frequency range up/down by a few hundred kHz. The band name is an approximation, not an exact frequency.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "40 m ↔ '7-point-oh to 7-point-three' — the 7 MHz band.", "edge_cases": "Band edges differ by country/region; 7.000–7.300 MHz is the Canadian/Region-2 allocation. Note footnote 5.142 constrains use of 7.200–7.300 MHz so as not to interfere with Region 1/3 broadcasting."}

{"core": "The 20-metre band corresponds to a wavelength of about 20 m, which maps to roughly 14 MHz. Per Schedule I of RBR-4, the Canadian allocation is 14.000–14.350 MHz.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "13.500–14.000 MHz is below the allocated band and includes broadcast/utility spectrum, not amateur.", "why_tempting": "It ends at 14.000 MHz so it looks like it brackets the band, but it sits just below the real allocation."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "15.000–15.750 MHz is outside any amateur allocation; it overlaps the 19-metre shortwave broadcast region.", "why_tempting": "It's near 14 MHz and the round 15 MHz figure feels close to '20 m.'"}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "16.350–16.830 MHz is not an amateur band at all; nothing in Schedule I lists it.", "why_tempting": "Plausible-looking HF numbers between the 20 m and 17 m bands can fool someone guessing."}], "concept": "Verify with λ = c/f: f = 300/λ(m) = 300/20 = 15 MHz nominal, and the actual amateur band sits just below at 14.000–14.350 MHz (300/14.0 ≈ 21.4 m, 300/14.35 ≈ 20.9 m). Amateur band names are nominal wavelengths, not exact, so always tie the name back to the Schedule I allocation rather than computing an exact wavelength.", "misconception": "Assuming the metre-band name converts to an exact frequency (20 m → exactly 15 MHz) instead of recognizing it as a nominal label for the 14 MHz allocation.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "20 m → 14 MHz: the popular HF DX band sits at 14.0–14.35 MHz.", "edge_cases": "The maximum bandwidth for this band is 6 kHz per Schedule I, and all certificate classes (B and 5, B/H, B and A) may use it."}

{"core": "The 15-metre band spans 21.000–21.450 MHz, matching item 14 of Schedule I in RBR-4.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "14.000–14.350 MHz is the 20-metre band, not 15 metres.", "why_tempting": "It is an adjacent HF band also available to Basic-with-code/honours operators, and the numbers are easy to confuse."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "28.000–29.700 MHz is the 10-metre band.", "why_tempting": "Learners who reverse the wavelength/frequency relationship may pick a higher frequency for a 'shorter' number."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "18.068–18.168 MHz is the 17-metre band.", "why_tempting": "It is another HF band near 21 MHz and easy to mismatch when memorizing band edges."}], "concept": "Use λ = 300 / f(MHz) to sanity-check: for 21 MHz, λ ≈ 300/21 ≈ 14.3 m, which rounds to the conventional 15-metre designation. Conversely 14 MHz → ~21 m (20 m band) and 28 MHz → ~10.7 m (10 m band). The 'metre' labels are nominal/historical, so the wavelength won't be exactly the band name, but the calculation reliably maps each band to its harmonic family (the classic 80/40/20/15/10 m HF set).", "misconception": "Confusing the metre-band nickname with the numeric MHz value, or inverting the inverse relationship between wavelength and frequency.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "15 m sits near the third harmonic of 7 MHz: 3 × 7 = 21 MHz.", "edge_cases": "The 15 m allocation is exactly 21.000–21.450 MHz (450 kHz wide); don't extend it to 21.450+ MHz."}

{"core": "The 10-metre band corresponds to 28.000–29.700 MHz, which is what a wavelength of ~10 m gives (λ = 300/f → 300/28 ≈ 10.7 m, 300/29.7 ≈ 10.1 m). This is per Schedule I, item 16, of RBR-4.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "24.890–24.990 MHz is the 12-metre band (λ ≈ 300/24.9 ≈ 12 m), not 10 m.", "why_tempting": "It's an adjacent HF band with a similar 'MHz' value and is also Basic-accessible, so it's easy to confuse the band labels."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "21.000–21.450 MHz is the 15-metre band (λ ≈ 300/21 ≈ 14.3 m).", "why_tempting": "Another HF amateur band that looks plausible if you don't compute the wavelength."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "50.000–54.000 MHz is the 6-metre band (λ ≈ 300/50 = 6 m), in the VHF range.", "why_tempting": "It's the next amateur band up from 10 m and is also Basic-only, so learners who skip the wavelength check may grab it."}], "concept": "Band names are wavelengths, not frequencies. Convert with λ(m) = 300 / f(MHz). For 28 MHz: 300/28 ≈ 10.7 m, rounded to the '10-metre band.' Quickly screening the four options by this formula instantly rules out the 12 m, 15 m, and 6 m bands.", "misconception": "Treating the numeric band name as if it were a frequency, or memorizing band edges without anchoring them to the wavelength-frequency relationship.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "λ = 300/f: 28 MHz → ~10 m. The classic HF set is 80/40/20/15/10 m at roughly 3.5/7/14/21/28 MHz.", "edge_cases": "In Canada the 10 m band (28.000–29.700 MHz) is available with Basic qualification alone; the 20 kHz max bandwidth (Schedule I) allows wider emissions like FM and the FM repeater segment near 29.6 MHz."}

{"core": "RBR-4 limits radio control of models to any frequency within the amateur bands above 30 MHz, as set out in Schedule I. Option D matches that exactly.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The 6 m band is permitted, but it is not the only band above 30 MHz allowed for model control — the privilege covers all amateur bands above 30 MHz.", "why_tempting": "6 m is a common band and learners may recall a single VHF band being mentioned."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Bands below 30 MHz (HF) are excluded; the cutoff is specifically 30 MHz.", "why_tempting": "It seems plausible that any amateur band could be used, ignoring the explicit frequency floor."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This lists only three specific VHF bands, but the rule is not restricted to an enumerated set — it includes every amateur allocation above 30 MHz (UHF and microwave too).", "why_tempting": "It looks specific and 'official,' tempting learners who think the rule names particular bands."}], "concept": "RBR-4 section 5 states the frequency for radio control of a model is limited to any frequency within the frequency bands above 30 MHz, as set out in Schedule I. The reason is practical: model control should not occupy congested HF spectrum, and VHF/UHF/microwave provides ample short-range channels with manageable interference characteristics. The 30 MHz line is the conventional HF/VHF boundary used throughout the regulations.", "misconception": "Believing model control is confined to one band or a short fixed list, rather than understanding it is a blanket privilege across all amateur bands above 30 MHz.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Models fly high — above 30 MHz, no HF.", "edge_cases": "The privilege still requires holding the qualification for the band used and obeying that band's bandwidth and interference provisions in Schedule I."}

{"core": "Per RBR-4 Schedule I, both the 6-metre (50–54 MHz) and 2-metre (144–148 MHz) bands carry a maximum permissible bandwidth of 30 kHz in Column II.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "20 kHz is the limit on the 10-metre band (28.000–29.700 MHz), not on 6 m or 2 m.", "why_tempting": "It's a real bandwidth limit in the table, just for an adjacent HF band, so it looks plausible if you misremember which band it applies to."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "6 kHz is the limit assigned to the HF phone bands (e.g. 80/40/20/15/10 m at 1.8–28 MHz), where SSB voice fits comfortably.", "why_tempting": "6 kHz is the most common HF allocation, so a learner who anchors on the typical HF voice figure may pick it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "5 kHz is not a bandwidth value used anywhere in Schedule I; it's a distractor with no basis.", "why_tempting": "It's close to the 6 kHz HF figure and sounds like a 'narrow' voice channel, so it can feel right by association."}], "concept": "Bandwidth limits scale with the band: as you move up in frequency the percentage of spectrum each band occupies grows, and wider emission modes (FM, wide-deviation) become practical. RBR-4 reflects this — HF bands cap at 6 kHz (or 1 kHz/100 Hz/2.8 kHz on the narrow allocations), 10 m allows 20 kHz, and the VHF bands 6 m and 2 m allow 30 kHz to accommodate FM phone (which needs roughly 2×(deviation + max audio) ≈ 16 kHz for ±5 kHz deviation, plus margin). Bandwidth is officially measured at the points 26 dB below the peak amplitude.", "misconception": "Assuming a single 'voice channel' bandwidth applies everywhere, or that VHF uses the same 6 kHz as HF SSB. FM on VHF intentionally occupies far more spectrum, hence the wider 30 kHz allowance.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "VHF FM is wide: 6 m and 2 m both get 30 kHz — 'thirty for the two VHFs.'", "edge_cases": "30 kHz applies to 50–54 MHz and 144–148 MHz; 222 MHz and the 219/220 MHz allocations jump to 100 kHz, and bandwidth is always defined at the −26 dB level, not −3 dB."}

{"core": "Per RBR-4 Schedule I, the 28.000–29.700 MHz band (10 metres) has a maximum permissible bandwidth of 20 kHz, which is option D.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "6 kHz is the limit assigned to the HF voice bands from 1.8 MHz up through 24 MHz (e.g. 80m, 40m, 20m), not the 10m band.", "why_tempting": "Most HF phone bands a Basic operator uses do carry the 6 kHz limit, so it's the reflexive 'HF answer'."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "30 kHz is the bandwidth limit for the VHF bands 50–54 MHz and 144–148 MHz, not for 28–29.7 MHz.", "why_tempting": "It's the next value up in the schedule and feels like a wider band should allow more bandwidth, so it's easy to over-shoot."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "15 kHz simply does not appear as a bandwidth value anywhere in Schedule I for amateur bands.", "why_tempting": "It sits plausibly between the 6 kHz and 20/30 kHz figures, making it a believable middle-ground guess."}], "concept": "RBR-4 defines maximum bandwidth per band in Column II of Schedule I, measured at the 26 dB-below-peak points of the emission. The 10m band (28–29.700 MHz) is allocated 20 kHz to accommodate wider emission modes (FM, packet) common on the upper portion of 10m, sitting between the 6 kHz HF phone allotment and the 30 kHz VHF allotment.", "misconception": "Assuming all HF bands share the 6 kHz limit, or that bandwidth scales monotonically with frequency. The 10m band is the HF exception at 20 kHz.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "Climbing the schedule: HF = 6 kHz, 10m steps up to 20 kHz, VHF jumps to 30 kHz.", "edge_cases": "The 60m channels (5 MHz) are capped at 2.8 kHz and 28 MHz at 20 kHz — these are exceptions to the generic 6 kHz HF figure."}

{"core": "Per Schedule I of RBR-4, the HF phone bands (40 m through 12 m: 7.0, 14.0, 18.068, 21.0, 24.89 MHz) all carry a 6 kHz maximum bandwidth. The single exception in that 7–25 MHz span is the 30 m band (10.1 MHz), limited to 1 kHz.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "15 kHz is not the bandwidth listed for any amateur band in Schedule I.", "why_tempting": "It looks like a plausible 'voice channel' figure, halfway between 6 and 30 kHz, so it reads like a sensible compromise."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "20 kHz is the maximum bandwidth assigned to the 10 m band (28.0–29.7 MHz), which lies above 25 MHz, not within the 7–25 MHz range.", "why_tempting": "It is a real Schedule I value, just for a band just outside the stated range, so it tempts anyone who confuses 10 m with the lower HF bands."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "30 kHz applies to the VHF bands (6 m and 2 m, 50–148 MHz), far above the HF range in question.", "why_tempting": "It is also a genuine Schedule I number, and the largest one given, so it appeals if you assume wider bands are allowed lower in frequency."}], "concept": "RBR-4 Schedule I, Column II sets a maximum occupied bandwidth per band, measured 26 dB below peak amplitude. For the contiguous HF allocations spanning 7–25 MHz, every band is 6 kHz except 30 m (10.100–10.150 MHz), which is restricted to 1 kHz because that narrow secondary allocation is intended for CW and data only — no phone. The 6 kHz figure comfortably accommodates an SSB voice signal, while 30 m's 1 kHz forbids it.", "misconception": "Assuming bandwidth scales with frequency, or mixing up the HF 6 kHz limit with the wider 20 kHz (10 m) and 30 kHz (VHF) limits which apply only above 25 MHz.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "HF phone bands = 6 kHz; 30 m is the odd one out (1 kHz, no voice).", "edge_cases": "The 60 m channels (5 MHz) use 2.8 kHz, but they sit below 7 MHz so they are outside this question's range; the 'one band' exception being referenced is specifically 30 m at 1 kHz."}

{"core": "Per Schedule I of RBR-4, the 144.000–148.000 MHz band (item 18) has a maximum permissible bandwidth of 30 kHz, so D is correct.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "6 kHz is the limit for the HF voice bands (e.g. 80, 40, 20, 15, 10 m down to 28 MHz region), not 2 m.", "why_tempting": "6 kHz is the most commonly cited bandwidth on HF, so it's an easy default if you don't recall the VHF figure."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "20 kHz is the maximum bandwidth assigned to the 28.000–29.700 MHz (10 m) band, not 2 m.", "why_tempting": "20 kHz is a real value in the table just above the VHF entries, so it looks plausible if you slip one row."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "15 kHz does not appear as a bandwidth limit for any amateur band in Schedule I; it's a fabricated value.", "why_tempting": "It sits numerically between 6 and 30 kHz and 'feels' like a reasonable middle value for FM voice."}], "concept": "RBR-4 Schedule I, Column II sets a maximum occupied bandwidth per band, measured at the −26 dB points relative to peak amplitude. The bandwidth ceilings widen as you go up in frequency because higher bands accommodate wider modes (e.g. FM, which needs roughly Carson's-rule bandwidth of ~16 kHz for typical deviation). Both 50–54 MHz and 144–148 MHz are set at 30 kHz, comfortably allowing standard NBFM voice with margin.", "misconception": "Assuming the common HF 6 kHz figure applies everywhere, or confusing the 10 m band's 20 kHz with the VHF bands' 30 kHz.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "VHF voice = FM = wider channel → 30 kHz for both 6 m and 2 m.", "edge_cases": "The 30 kHz applies to 50–54 MHz and 144–148 MHz; 219–225 MHz jumps to 100 kHz, and microwave bands are often 'Not specified.'"}

{"core": "Per RBR-4 Schedule I, the 50.000–54.000 MHz band (6 m) carries a maximum permitted bandwidth of 30 kHz, so D is correct.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "20 kHz is the limit for the 28.000–29.700 MHz (10 m) band, not 6 m.", "why_tempting": "It is the next allocation below 6 m and a plausible-sounding wider HF limit, so it's easy to confuse with the VHF figure."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "6 kHz is the bandwidth for the HF voice bands (e.g. 80/40/20/15 m), far narrower than the VHF allocations.", "why_tempting": "6 kHz is the most-cited bandwidth on the exam, so candidates default to it."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "15 kHz is not a value used in Schedule I for any amateur band — it's a fabricated figure.", "why_tempting": "It sits numerically between 6 and 30 kHz and resembles broadcast FM channel spacing, making it look reasonable."}], "concept": "RBR-4 Schedule I, Column II specifies the maximum permissible bandwidth per band, measured at the points 26 dB below peak amplitude. Bandwidth limits widen as you go up in frequency because higher bands have more spectrum room and support wider modes: HF voice bands are 6 kHz, 10 m is 20 kHz, and the VHF bands 50–54 MHz and 144–148 MHz are both 30 kHz. The 30 kHz figure on 6 m accommodates wideband modes like FM.", "misconception": "That the common 6 kHz HF voice bandwidth applies universally, or that bandwidth limits stay constant across bands rather than increasing on VHF.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "6 m and 2 m share the same 30 kHz roof — both VHF, both 30.", "edge_cases": "144–148 MHz also uses 30 kHz; 28 MHz is the 20 kHz exception that often gets mixed up with 6 m."}

{"core": "The 30 m band (10.100–10.150 MHz) is restricted to a maximum bandwidth of 1 kHz per Schedule I of RBR-4, well below 6 kHz. This narrow limit reflects its status as a narrow, shared band intended primarily for CW and narrow digital modes.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The 160 m band (1.800–2.000 MHz) has a maximum bandwidth of 6 kHz, not less.", "why_tempting": "As a low-frequency HF band it might seem to warrant a narrow allocation, but it permits full 6 kHz."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The 17 m band (18.068–18.168 MHz) allows a maximum bandwidth of 6 kHz.", "why_tempting": "It is a relatively narrow band in total spectrum width, which can be confused with a narrow per-signal bandwidth limit."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The 12 m band (24.890–24.990 MHz) allows a maximum bandwidth of 6 kHz.", "why_tempting": "Another physically narrow HF allocation, easily mistaken for having a reduced signal bandwidth limit."}], "concept": "RBR-4 Schedule I, Column II lists a maximum permissible transmitted bandwidth for each band. Most HF phone bands allow 6 kHz; some special allocations are tighter. The 10.100–10.150 MHz (30 m) band is capped at 1 kHz because it is a small, internationally shared band where Canadian amateurs operate on a non-protected basis (footnote C6) and must avoid interference to fixed services — narrow CW/data modes only. Bandwidth itself is defined as the band occupied measured 26 dB below peak amplitude.", "misconception": "Confusing the total spectral width of a band (MHz of allocation) with the per-signal maximum bandwidth limit (the Column II value). The two are unrelated; a physically narrow band can still allow a 6 kHz signal.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "30 m = narrow band, narrow signal: 1 kHz, no phone.", "edge_cases": "The 135.7 kHz band is even tighter at 100 Hz, and 472 kHz is 1 kHz — but those aren't offered here. The 60 m channels are 2.8 kHz."}

{"core": "The 30 m band (10.100–10.150 MHz) has a maximum permitted bandwidth of only 1 kHz (per Schedule I, item 11). A standard SSB voice signal occupies roughly 2.4–2.8 kHz, far exceeding that limit, so SSB is effectively prohibited; the band is reserved for narrow modes like CW and data.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The 17 m band (18.068–18.168 MHz) allows a 6 kHz maximum bandwidth, easily accommodating SSB voice.", "why_tempting": "It is a WARC band like 30 m, so a learner may assume it carries the same narrow-mode restriction."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The 12 m band (24.890–24.990 MHz) permits 6 kHz bandwidth, so SSB is allowed.", "why_tempting": "Another WARC band lumped together with 30 m and 17 m, inviting a guess that all WARC bands ban phone."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The 40 m band (7.000–7.300 MHz) allows 6 kHz bandwidth, fully supporting SSB voice operation.", "why_tempting": "It carries footnote 5.142 (broadcasting constraint), which may look like a phone restriction but actually concerns Region 1/3 broadcasting, not SSB."}], "concept": "Canada controls modes indirectly through a maximum occupied bandwidth limit (measured at the −26 dB points) rather than by naming modes. Schedule I assigns 10.100–10.150 MHz a 1 kHz cap. Since SSB telephony occupies about 2.4–2.8 kHz, it cannot fit within 1 kHz, leaving only narrowband emissions (CW, narrow data, RTTY). Bands with a 6 kHz cap comfortably allow a single voice sideband.", "misconception": "Believing certain modes are banned by name. In reality the regulation specifies a bandwidth ceiling, and any mode exceeding it on that band is the thing that's prohibited.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "30 m = 1 kHz = narrow only: 'three-zero, no audio.'", "edge_cases": "30 m is also under Canadian footnote C6 (secondary, no interference protection vs. fixed service) — a separate restriction from the bandwidth limit, but it reinforces 30 m's narrow, non-voice character."}

{"core": "Your transmitted signal occupies a band of frequencies (the occupied bandwidth), not a single point. When operating near a band edge you must offset your carrier far enough inside the edge that the entire occupied bandwidth — including sidebands — stays within the authorized amateur band.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "There is no requirement to change emission mode near a band edge; any authorized mode is fine provided its bandwidth fits inside the band.", "why_tempting": "CW and digital signals are narrow, so they let you operate closer to the edge — but that's a convenience, not a regulatory precaution."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Band plans are voluntary gentleman's agreements about mode segments; they're courtesy, not the legal precaution against radiating outside the allocated band.", "why_tempting": "Band-plan compatibility is good practice and sounds 'official,' so it can be confused with the actual edge requirement."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Lowering power reduces signal strength but does nothing to keep the occupied bandwidth inside the band — sidebands still extend the same frequency span regardless of power.", "why_tempting": "Reducing power feels like a safe, conservative move, but it addresses interference level, not frequency occupancy."}], "concept": "For SSB phone the carrier is suppressed and the displayed VFO frequency typically marks the carrier point, while audio (≈300–2800 Hz) extends the occupied bandwidth ~2.8–3 kHz to one side (USB above, LSB below). For an emission of bandwidth BW you must keep the dial offset from the edge by at least the spread on the side that pushes toward the edge. Schedules in RBR-4 list a maximum occupied bandwidth per band (measured 26 dB below peak); the whole of that occupied bandwidth must remain inside the band limits.", "misconception": "Believing the dial/carrier frequency is what must stay inside the band, forgetting that sidebands occupy real spectrum and can spill across the edge even when the displayed frequency is legal.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Tune for the SIGNAL, not the dial — keep the whole skirt inside the fence.", "edge_cases": "Direction matters: on USB place yourself well below the upper edge; on LSB stay well above the lower edge, since the sideband extends away from the carrier into the band."}

{"core": "Fast-scan (analog) television occupies several MHz of bandwidth. On 145 MHz the maximum permitted bandwidth is 30 kHz, so an ATV signal there is far too wide and is prohibited.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "440 MHz falls in the 430-450 MHz band where the maximum bandwidth is effectively the full 12 MHz allocation, easily wide enough for ATV.", "why_tempting": "ATV is unusual on most bands, so a learner unsure of the bandwidth rule might guess any ATV entry is illegal."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "14.074 MHz is the standard FT8 calling frequency within the 14.000-14.350 MHz band; FT8 is narrowband (well under the 6 kHz limit) and entirely legal.", "why_tempting": "Learners may not recognize the specific FT8 sub-band frequency and second-guess it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "10.145 MHz lies in the 30 m band (10.100-10.150 MHz), max bandwidth 1 kHz; CW occupies only ~150 Hz, so it is well within limits.", "why_tempting": "The 1 kHz limit on 30 m is tight, so a learner might wrongly think CW is excluded; in fact CW is the natural mode there."}], "concept": "Each amateur band has a maximum occupied bandwidth set in Column II of Schedule I. Below 30 MHz the HF bands are 6 kHz (or 1 kHz on 30 m), 28 MHz is 20 kHz, and 50/144 MHz are 30 kHz. Analog fast-scan TV (NTSC) needs on the order of 4-6 MHz of bandwidth, so it only fits on bands whose limit is in the MHz range — i.e. 430 MHz and up. Compare the emission's occupied bandwidth (measured 26 dB below peak) against the band's ceiling: 6 MHz ATV vs. a 30 kHz limit on 2 m is a clear violation.", "misconception": "Assuming any mode is allowed on any band the operator has privileges for; in reality the limiting factor here is permitted bandwidth, not just frequency access.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "Wide video needs wide bands: ATV only above 430 MHz.", "edge_cases": "Above 30 MHz radio control of models is allowed, but that's a separate provision; the bandwidth ceiling is what disqualifies ATV on 2 m and 6 m."}

{"core": "Fast-scan television (ATV) requires roughly 6 MHz of bandwidth, but the 14.000–14.350 MHz band permits a maximum bandwidth of only 6 kHz (Schedule I, item 12). ATV cannot fit, so it is prohibited there.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "SSB on 80 m occupies ~2.8 kHz, well within the 6 kHz maximum bandwidth allowed in the 3.500–4.000 MHz band; 3.76 MHz is inside the band.", "why_tempting": "Learners may worry SSB is too wide, but it is far narrower than the 6 kHz cap."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "SSTV is a narrowband mode sent within an audio-channel bandwidth (~2.8 kHz), fitting easily inside the 6 kHz limit of the 20 m band.", "why_tempting": "The word 'television' suggests wide bandwidth, but slow-scan transmits a still image slowly over a voice-width channel, unlike fast-scan."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "29.60 MHz lies in 28.000–29.700 MHz, where the maximum bandwidth is 20 kHz — enough to accommodate narrowband FM voice.", "why_tempting": "FM seems wide, but 10 m narrowband FM stays within the generous 20 kHz allowance on that band."}], "concept": "Each band in Schedule I has a Column II maximum occupied bandwidth, measured at 26 dB below peak amplitude (RBR-4 s.4). HF voice/data bands cap at 6 kHz (or 20 kHz on 10 m); fast-scan/NTSC-style ATV needs roughly 6 MHz of RF bandwidth, far exceeding any HF allowance. Compare the mode's required bandwidth against the band's permitted bandwidth: SSB ~2.8 kHz, SSTV ~2.8 kHz, FM voice ~16 kHz, ATV ~6 MHz. Only ATV violates the limit on 20 m.", "misconception": "Assuming both 'television' modes (SSTV and ATV) are equally wide. Slow-scan is narrowband (audio-width) while fast-scan is video-bandwidth — the bandwidth difference is what makes ATV illegal on HF.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "Fast = fat: fast-scan TV needs MHz of room, so it only lives on UHF.", "edge_cases": "ATV is permissible on bands like 430–450 MHz where the maximum bandwidth is 12 MHz — the prohibition is band-specific, not a blanket ban on the mode."}

{"core": "The 30 m band (10.100–10.150 MHz) has a maximum permissible bandwidth of only 1 kHz per Schedule I. An SSB voice signal occupies roughly 2.4–2.8 kHz, which exceeds this limit, so SSB is prohibited there.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "29.6 MHz lies in the 28.000–29.700 MHz band, where the maximum bandwidth is 20 kHz — comfortably accommodating an FM signal.", "why_tempting": "FM is wide (typically 10–16 kHz), so a learner might assume it's always disallowed; but the 10 m band's 20 kHz allowance permits it."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "CW occupies only ~100–150 Hz, well within the 1 kHz limit of the 30 m band, so it is permitted on 10.11 MHz.", "why_tempting": "Both C and D are on 30 m, so a learner who knows the band is restrictive might wrongly flag the narrow modes too."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "FT8 is a narrow digital mode (~50 Hz bandwidth) and easily fits within the 1 kHz limit at 10.136 MHz.", "why_tempting": "Digital modes can sound exotic and 'wide,' tempting a guess, but FT8 is among the narrowest modes in use."}], "concept": "Schedule I of RBR-4 assigns each amateur band a maximum permissible bandwidth (Column II), measured at the −26 dB points. The 30 m band (10.100–10.150 MHz) is capped at 1 kHz. Compare typical occupied bandwidths: CW ≈ 0.1–0.15 kHz, FT8 ≈ 0.05 kHz, SSB ≈ 2.4–2.8 kHz, FM ≈ 10–16 kHz. The test is simply: does the mode's bandwidth exceed the band's limit? SSB's ~2.8 kHz > 1 kHz, so it is prohibited on 30 m. (Note C6: the 30 m band is also non-protected, but the disqualifier here is bandwidth.)", "misconception": "Believing a mode is allowed or banned by its nature alone, rather than checking it against the specific band's maximum bandwidth. Legality depends on the band, not the mode in isolation.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "30 m = 1 kHz: 'thirty's thin' — only narrow modes (CW, digital) fit; no voice.", "edge_cases": "30 m is a WARC band traditionally reserved for narrow CW/digital work; SSB phone is conventionally avoided even where bandwidth might marginally fit, but here it's the explicit 1 kHz limit that makes SSB prohibited."}

{"core": "Good operating practice—and the underlying regulatory philosophy—requires using the minimum power necessary to maintain reliable communication, not the maximum the certificate allows. The power limits in RBR-4 are ceilings, not targets.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "560 W is not a DC-input figure; the Basic DC-input ceiling is 250 W to the anode/collector. More importantly, the question asks what you *should* use at all times, not the legal maximum.", "why_tempting": "560 W is a real Basic limit figure, so it looks authoritative, and pairing it with 'DC input' mimics the regulatory wording."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "560 W PEP is the maximum SSB RF output a Basic holder may use, but a maximum is not what you should use 'at all times'—you run the least power that does the job.", "why_tempting": "It is the correct Basic SSB PEP ceiling from RBR-4 section 10.2, so a learner who memorized the number grabs it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "400 W PEP is not a defined Canadian Basic or Advanced limit, and like the others it states a fixed power level rather than the minimum-necessary principle.", "why_tempting": "It's a plausible-sounding round number that resembles power limits in other jurisdictions."}], "concept": "RBR-4 section 10 specifies maximum transmitter power (Basic: 250 W DC input, 560 W PEP SSB, 190 W carrier other modes). These are absolute ceilings. Operating best practice—and the spirit of interference avoidance in section 8—is to use only the power needed for the contact: more power means more potential interference, higher RF-exposure levels, and no communication benefit once the link is solid. The exam distinguishes the *legal maximum* from the *operationally correct* choice.", "misconception": "Confusing the maximum permitted power (a hard limit) with the power you should habitually run. Numeric distractors bait recall of limit figures instead of the minimum-necessary operating principle.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Limits are ceilings, not goals—run the QRP-est power that works.", "edge_cases": "The exact power ceilings (250 W DC input / 560 W PEP / 190 W carrier for Basic) are still worth knowing for other questions; just don't confuse them with the 'how much should I use' answer."}

{"core": "RBR-4 sets the Advanced Qualification SSB limit at 2,250 W peak envelope power measured across an impedance-matched load.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "560 W PEP is the Basic-qualification SSB limit, not the Advanced limit.", "why_tempting": "It is the correct SSB PEP figure—but for the wrong certificate class."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "250 W is the Basic-qualification DC input power limit (anode/collector circuit), not an Advanced PEP figure.", "why_tempting": "It's a real RBR-4 number and looks like a plausible power ceiling."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "190 W is the Basic carrier-power limit for non-SSB emissions, unrelated to Advanced SSB.", "why_tempting": "Another genuine RBR-4 value, easy to confuse among the cluster of limits."}], "concept": "RBR-4 expresses power limits three ways: DC input power, SSB peak envelope power (PEP), and carrier power for other emissions. For Advanced Qualification: 1,000 W DC input, 2,250 W PEP for SSB, or 750 W carrier for other modes. For Basic: 250 W DC input, 560 W PEP SSB, or 190 W carrier. PEP limits are higher because SSB has a low duty cycle/average-to-peak ratio, so a higher instantaneous peak is permitted while average power stays moderate. Note also the amplifier rule: an installed amplifier may not be capable of exceeding the applicable limit by more than 3 dB.", "misconception": "Mixing up the six numerically distinct power figures—Basic vs Advanced, and DC-input vs PEP vs carrier—and grabbing a real RBR-4 number that applies to the wrong class or measurement basis.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Advanced SSB = 2,250 PEP; Basic SSB = 560 PEP. The bigger licence gets the bigger number.", "edge_cases": "Watch that some bands (e.g. 60 m channels, 135.7 kHz, 472 kHz) impose their own much lower ERP/EIRP caps that override the general certificate limit."}

{"core": "Power limits in RBR-4 are expressed as RF output power measured across an impedance-matched load — i.e. at the output of the transmitter (or the external amplifier feeding the antenna). That is the regulated point where you verify the limit is not exceeded.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The antenna feed point is past the feedline; line loss means the power there is lower than the regulated transmitter/amplifier output, so it isn't where the legal limit is verified.", "why_tempting": "RF exposure thinking pushes people toward the radiating element, but the power restriction is defined at the transmitter output stage, not the radiator."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "DC input at the power-supply terminals isn't the RF output power; the regulation's DC-input limit applies to the anode/collector circuit of the final RF stage, not the supply terminals, and supply power includes losses unrelated to RF output.", "why_tempting": "RBR-4 does list a DC-input option, so a supply-terminal answer sounds plausible — but that DC measurement is taken at the final amplifier stage, not at the PSU."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The tuner output is even further along the chain and adds its own loss; the limit is referenced to the transmitter/amplifier output, not after the matching network.", "why_tempting": "Since limits are measured 'across an impedance-matched load,' a tuner output seems like the matched point, but the matched load for measurement is at the transmitter/amp output itself."}], "concept": "Per RBR-4 section 10, Basic operators are limited to 560 W PEP (SSB) or 190 W carrier when expressed as RF output power measured across an impedance-matched load, or 250 W DC input to the anode/collector of the final stage. The regulated reference point is therefore the output of the transmitter — and if used, the external amplifier — into a matched (typically 50 Ω) load. Section 10.1 adds that an amplifier must not be capable of exceeding the limit by more than 3 dB. Anything downstream (feedline, tuner, antenna) sees loss and is not the defined measurement plane.", "misconception": "Conflating RF-exposure compliance (about radiated/feed-point fields) with the transmitter power limit, which is defined at the transmitter/amplifier output, not at the antenna.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Measure power where it's MADE, not where it's radiated.", "edge_cases": "If you run an external amplifier, the relevant output is the amplifier's, and the 3 dB capability rule of section 10.1 applies; the DC-input alternative is measured at the final stage's anode/collector, not the wall supply."}

{"core": "A Basic-qualified operator (with or without Morse) is limited to 560 W PEP for single-sideband emissions when power is expressed as RF output across an impedance-matched load. The Morse endorsement adds HF band access but does not raise the Basic power ceiling.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "2250 W PEP is the SSB output limit for the Advanced qualification, not Basic.", "why_tempting": "It pairs with SSB and looks like the 'high-power' answer; a learner who confuses Basic and Advanced tiers grabs it."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "1000 W is the Advanced DC-input limit (anode/collector circuit), not an SSB PEP figure for Basic.", "why_tempting": "It's a round, familiar 'kilowatt' number that often gets misremembered as the legal-limit reference."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "1500 W is the US FCC PEP limit, not a Canadian figure at all.", "why_tempting": "Many study from US-influenced material where 1500 W PEP is the headline number."}], "concept": "RBR-4 section 10 specifies Basic-qualification limits two ways: DC input power of 250 W to the final stage, OR RF output across a matched load of 560 W PEP for any single-sideband emission and 190 W carrier power for other emissions. 3750 kHz sits in the 80 m band (3.500–4.000 MHz, Schedule I item 4), available to B and 5 holders, so the SSB PEP cap of 560 W applies. The Morse (5 wpm) qualification only affects band/mode access historically — it does not alter the power tier set by the Basic certificate.", "misconception": "Believing the Morse endorsement upgrades you toward Advanced power levels, or importing the US 1500 W PEP limit into Canadian rules.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Basic SSB = 560 PEP; Advanced SSB = 2250 PEP (roughly 4×).", "edge_cases": "An amplifier may not be capable of exceeding these limits by more than 3 dB (RBR-4 10.1). The 560 W figure is specifically for SSB; other emission types are held to 190 W carrier power under Basic."}

{"core": "A Basic-with-Honours holder is limited to Basic-qualification power. For any single-sideband emission expressed as RF output PEP, that limit is 560 W (per RBR-4 s.10.2).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "190 W is the carrier-power limit for non-SSB emissions under Basic qualification, and 200 W is just a rounded distractor near it — neither is the SSB PEP figure.", "why_tempting": "It looks like a plausible 'basic-level' power and resembles the 190 W carrier figure that also appears in the Basic limits."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "1000 W is the Advanced DC-input limit, not an SSB PEP figure, and applies to Advanced qualification only.", "why_tempting": "It's the well-known 'maximum amateur power' number, so learners grab it when not tracking qualification level or measurement basis."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "2250 W PEP is the Advanced-qualification SSB limit; a Basic/Honours holder does not get Advanced privileges.", "why_tempting": "It is the correct SSB PEP value — but for the wrong (Advanced) certificate level."}], "concept": "RBR-4 s.10 ties power limits to certificate level, and Basic-with-Honours conveys only Basic operating privileges (the Honours score does not unlock Advanced power). Power can be stated three equivalent ways: DC input to the final stage, RF output PEP for SSB, or RF output carrier power for other emissions. For Basic: 250 W DC input, 560 W PEP (SSB), or 190 W carrier (other). 7055 kHz is within the 40 m band (7.000–7.300 MHz, Schedule I item 10) where Basic holders may operate, so the SSB PEP limit of 560 W applies.", "misconception": "Believing 'with Honours' grants higher power or Advanced privileges. It only reflects an exam score ≥80% and gives the same operating limits as a plain Basic certificate.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Basic SSB = 560 PEP; Advanced SSB = 2250 PEP. Honours ≠ Advanced.", "edge_cases": "The three power figures (250 W DC input / 560 W PEP / 190 W carrier) describe the same Basic limit under different measurement conventions — match the figure to how the question states the measurement (here PEP for SSB)."}

{"core": "On the 60 m channels, RBR-4 imposes a special per-channel ERP cap of 100 W PEP that applies to ALL amateurs regardless of qualification, so even an Advanced holder is limited to 100 W ERP there.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "250 W is the Basic-qualification DC input power limit, not a 60 m ERP figure, and ERP on 60 m is capped at 100 W for everyone.", "why_tempting": "250 W is a real, memorable Basic power number, so it looks like a plausible authoritative limit."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "560 W PEP is the Basic SSB RF-output limit under section 10, not an allowed 60 m ERP; the band-specific 100 W ERP rule overrides it.", "why_tempting": "It's a genuine SSB output limit, and 60 m is SSB-capable, so it seems relevant."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "2250 W PEP is the Advanced SSB RF-output ceiling for most HF bands, but the 60 m allocation explicitly restricts ERP to 100 W regardless of certificate.", "why_tempting": "Since the question highlights the Advanced certificate, the maximum Advanced PEP figure feels like the intended answer."}], "concept": "The 60 m allocation (footnote C21) is a narrow secondary/non-interference channelized allocation. Unlike the general HF bands where power follows the operator's certificate (Basic vs Advanced per section 10), 60 m carries its own band-specific cap: 100 W PEP effective radiated power per channel, with prescribed emission designators and ≤2.8 kHz bandwidth. ERP is referenced to a dipole, so the cap accounts for antenna gain — it's a radiated limit, not a transmitter-output limit. Because it is set by the allocation footnote, it supersedes the higher generic certificate limits.", "misconception": "That the Advanced certificate's high generic power limit (2250 W PEP) applies on every band. Band-specific allocation footnotes can impose stricter caps that apply to all operators.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "60 m = 100 — one hundred watts, one allocation rule for all.", "edge_cases": "The 100 W limit on 60 m is ERP (referenced to a dipole), so with a gain antenna you must reduce transmitter output to stay within the radiated cap."}

{"core": "An Advanced-qualified operator is limited to 1000 W DC input power to the anode or collector circuit of the final RF stage that supplies energy to the antenna, per RBR-4 section 10.3(a).", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "250 W DC input is the Basic Qualification limit, not Advanced.", "why_tempting": "It's a real number from the same power table, so it's easy to confuse the two qualification tiers."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "1500 W is the US (FCC) PEP output limit and does not appear in the Canadian DC-input limits.", "why_tempting": "1500 W is widely cited in amateur literature and gear specs, making it a familiar but jurisdictionally wrong figure."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "560 W is the Basic single-sideband PEP *output* limit, not a DC-input figure, and not the Advanced value.", "why_tempting": "It's another genuine RBR-4 number, but it belongs to the Basic SSB output column, not Advanced DC input."}], "concept": "RBR-4 expresses power limits two ways: DC input to the final stage, or RF output across a matched load. For Advanced: 1000 W DC input, or as RF output 2250 W PEP (SSB) / 750 W carrier (other modes). For Basic: 250 W DC input, or 560 W PEP / 190 W carrier. The DC-input metric (anode/collector circuit) is the legacy way of rating capability since output power depends on stage efficiency. Don't cross-mix the input and output columns or the Basic and Advanced rows.", "misconception": "Conflating the DC-input limit with RF-output limits, or applying the Basic tier's numbers to an Advanced certificate.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Advanced DC input = 1000 W (a clean 1 kW); Basic DC input = 250 W (a quarter of that).", "edge_cases": "An installed amplifier may not be capable of exceeding these limits by more than 3 dB (RBR-4 s.10.1)."}

{"core": "EIRP = transmitter power × antenna gain (relative to an isotropic radiator), so the antenna's gain is the factor that converts your conducted power into the radiated figure the regulation caps. To stay within the 1 W EIRP limit on 630 m and 2200 m you must account for antenna gain.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Resonance affects how efficiently the antenna accepts power and how it presents a feed-point match, but it does not appear in the EIRP relationship.", "why_tempting": "On these very long wavelengths antennas are electrically tiny and far from resonant, so resonance feels like the dominant issue."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Bandwidth describes the frequency span over which the antenna works acceptably; it has nothing to do with computing radiated power for an EIRP limit.", "why_tempting": "These bands are extremely narrow (100 Hz / 1 kHz max), so bandwidth is on the learner's mind."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Impedance governs the match between transmitter and feedline, influencing power transfer, but EIRP is defined purely through power times gain.", "why_tempting": "Short LF/MF antennas have wildly reactive, hard-to-match impedances, so impedance seems like the key concern."}], "concept": "EIRP is the power that would have to be fed to a lossless isotropic radiator to produce the same field strength as your actual antenna in its peak direction: EIRP = P_in × G_i, where G_i is gain referenced to isotropic. (EIRP = ERP × 1.64, since a dipole has 2.15 dBi gain.) Per RBR-4, the 135.7–137.8 kHz (2200 m) and 472–479 kHz (630 m) bands are capped at 1 W EIRP (notes 5.67A and 5.80A). Because the regulated quantity is radiated power, you must know antenna gain to back-calculate the maximum transmitter power you may apply. Ironically, electrically short LF antennas usually have gain well below isotropic, so the conducted power needed to hit 1 W EIRP can be far higher than 1 W.", "misconception": "Assuming EIRP is just transmitter output power. EIRP folds in antenna gain (and effectively system losses), so the antenna's gain — not its match, resonance, or bandwidth — is the parameter that ties conducted power to the regulatory limit.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "EIRP = Power × Gain — Effective Isotropic Radiated Power literally builds gain into its name.", "edge_cases": "On 630 m/2200 m, real antennas have large negative dBi gain, so achieving the full 1 W EIRP may require far more than 1 W of transmitter power — the limit is on what leaves the antenna, not what enters it."}

{"core": "A Basic Qualification holder is limited to 250 W DC input power to the anode or collector circuit of the final amplifier stage that supplies RF energy to the antenna.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "1000 W DC input is the Advanced Qualification limit, not Basic.", "why_tempting": "It's the headline 'kilowatt' figure that exam takers associate with full amateur privileges, and it's the matching DC-input number one tier up."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "750 W is the Advanced limit for carrier power (RF output, non-SSB emissions), not a DC-input figure and not a Basic limit.", "why_tempting": "It's a real RBR-4 power figure, so it looks plausible if you confuse the measurement method (DC input vs RF carrier output) or the qualification level."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "560 W is the Basic limit for PEP RF output of SSB transmitters, not the DC input power limit.", "why_tempting": "It is the correct Basic-level figure but for a different measurement basis — the question specifically asks DC input, not RF output PEP."}], "concept": "RBR-4 specifies power limits two ways: DC input power to the final stage, or RF output across a matched load. For Basic Qualification the three limits are: 250 W DC input, 560 W PEP (SSB RF output), and 190 W carrier power (any other emission). The DC-input figure (250 W) is the relevant one here. Note these are different physical quantities — efficiency and emission type mean you can't directly equate 250 W DC input with 560 W PEP output.", "misconception": "Mixing up the measurement basis (DC input vs RF output PEP vs carrier power) and the qualification tier (Basic vs Advanced). The exam deliberately offers a valid figure from each category so only the right pairing of method and tier is correct.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Basic = 250 in, 560 SSB out; Advanced = 1000 in, 2250 SSB out (×~4 jump).", "edge_cases": "An installed amplifier may not be capable of exceeding these limits by more than 3 dB (RBR-4 s.10.1)."}

{"core": "For a Basic Qualification holder, RF output power for emissions other than single sideband is capped at 190 W carrier power measured across an impedance-matched load (per RBR-4 s.10.2).", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "250 W is the Basic limit expressed as DC input power to the anode/collector circuit, not RF carrier output power.", "why_tempting": "It is a genuine Basic limit, just measured under a different convention (DC input vs RF output), so it looks correct if you ignore the units."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "560 W PEP is the Basic limit for single sideband emissions specifically — the question asks about emissions OTHER than SSB.", "why_tempting": "It's the correct Basic figure but for the wrong emission type; the question deliberately excludes SSB."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "750 W carrier power is the Advanced Qualification limit for non-SSB emissions, not the Basic limit.", "why_tempting": "It's the right metric (carrier power, non-SSB) but for the Advanced certificate, which the question explicitly excludes."}], "concept": "RBR-4 s.10 specifies power three ways, and you must match the metric to the question. Basic limits: 250 W DC input; or RF output across a matched load — 560 W PEP for SSB, 190 W carrier for any other emission. Advanced limits: 1000 W DC input; or 2250 W PEP SSB, 750 W carrier other. Note also the s.10.1 rule that an installed amplifier may not be capable of exceeding these limits by more than 3 dB.", "misconception": "Treating all the power figures as interchangeable. They are distinct measurement conventions (DC input vs RF output) and distinct emission categories (SSB vs other), and the exam tests whether you pick the one matching both the certificate level and the emission type.", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Basic non-SSB carrier = 190; SSB doubles-ish to 560. Advanced non-SSB carrier = 750; SSB jumps to 2250.", "edge_cases": "560 (SSB) and 190 (carrier) both belong to Basic — the only difference is emission type. Watch the wording 'carrier power' vs 'PEP' as the discriminator."}

{"core": "A repeater is an amateur station that automatically receives signals on one frequency and simultaneously retransmits them on another, extending the range of low-power and mobile stations.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A remote-control station controls another station or device over a radio link; it does not receive and rebroadcast other amateurs' transmissions.", "why_tempting": "The word 'remote' suggests automatic, unattended operation, which sounds similar to a repeater's mode."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A beacon transmits its own continuous or repeating identification/test signal for propagation studies; it originates a signal rather than relaying others'.", "why_tempting": "Beacons run automatically and unattended, so they can be confused with automatic retransmission."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A satellite control station commands an amateur satellite (uplink for telemetry/control); the retransmitting function is the satellite's transponder, not the control station.", "why_tempting": "Satellites do retransmit signals, blurring the line between the orbiting transponder and the ground control station."}], "concept": "A repeater consists of a receiver and transmitter on different (offset) frequencies sharing a duplexer/antenna, with a controller that keys the transmitter whenever a valid input signal is present. The frequency offset and duplexer isolation prevent the transmitter from desensitizing its own receiver. This automatic relaying is what distinguishes a repeater from stations that originate (beacon) or merely command (control/remote) signals. Note RBR-4's rule that signals may not be automatically retransmitted in bands below 29.5 MHz unless received from a station qualified to operate below 30 MHz — relevant context for repeater operation.", "misconception": "Assuming any unattended/automatic station qualifies; the defining trait of a repeater specifically is retransmitting *other stations'* received signals, not generating or controlling them.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "REpeater = REceives then REtransmits.", "edge_cases": "Below 29.5 MHz, automatic retransmission is restricted to signals received from operators qualified for sub-30 MHz operation (RBR-4)."}

{"core": "RBR-4 section 11 states that an unmodulated carrier in a frequency band below 30 MHz may be transmitted only for brief tests. A continuous unmodulated carrier carries no information and wastes spectrum, so it's restricted to short testing intervals.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Power level is irrelevant to the rule; the restriction is about purpose (brief tests) and band (below 30 MHz), not a wattage threshold.", "why_tempting": "5 W appears in other amateur rules (e.g. EIRP limits on 472-479 kHz), so it looks like a plausible regulatory number."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "CW (Morse) is not an unmodulated carrier in operation — the carrier is keyed on and off to form characters, which is modulation in the practical sense. A steady, never-keyed carrier conveys nothing.", "why_tempting": "CW is often loosely described as 'just a carrier,' so a learner may equate the two."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Holding a frequency by transmitting a dead carrier is exactly the kind of unnecessary occupancy the rule prohibits; it is not a permitted test purpose.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a practical operating need, so it seems reasonable."}], "concept": "An unmodulated carrier is a pure RF sine wave with no superimposed information. Since it conveys no message it serves no communication purpose, and a continuous one would needlessly occupy a channel. The regulation therefore permits it only for brief tests, and only in bands below 30 MHz where this allowance is specified. The qualifying conditions are both 'brief' (duration) and 'tests' (purpose) — neither power nor mode changes that.", "misconception": "Believing CW equals an unmodulated carrier, or that the limiting factor is power. The actual constraint is purpose (brief testing) and band (below 30 MHz).", "link": "section 1", "mnemonic": "Carrier alone = brief tests below 30.", "edge_cases": "This rule (RBR-4 s.11) specifically references bands below 30 MHz; it pairs with the rule that signals may not be automatically retransmitted below 29.5 MHz unless from a qualified operator."}

{"core": "Per RBR-4 §11, signals may not be automatically retransmitted (the function of a repeater) in any frequency band below 29.5 MHz.
