{"core": "A standard voice CQ uses a balanced \"three-by-three\" pattern: \"CQ CQ CQ, this is [call sign] [call sign] [call sign].\" This gives a listening station enough repetitions of both the call and your identification to copy under marginal conditions without wasting airtime.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Five \"CQ\"s with the call sign given only once is unbalanced—if a station misses the single call sign, they cannot reply, and the extra CQs add nothing useful.", "why_tempting": "\"At least\" wording sounds flexible and conservative, and repeating CQ many times feels like it improves the chance of being heard."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Ten \"CQ\"s is excessively long, ties up the frequency, and again pairs many CQs with only one call sign reading—poor operating practice.", "why_tempting": "More repetitions seem safer for weak-signal or noisy conditions, so a bigger number looks like the cautious choice."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A single \"CQ\" is too brief for a listener tuning across the band to catch the call before deciding to respond; it inverts the recommended emphasis.", "why_tempting": "It correctly gives the call sign three times, so it looks like the mirror image of the right answer and is easy to confuse with 3×3."}], "concept": "The convention is the \"3×3\" (three-by-three) call: the attention signal \"CQ\" three times, the bridge \"this is,\" then your call sign three times—ideally using the phonetic alphabet for the call sign. The logic is symmetry and efficiency: three CQs are enough to alert a tuning operator, and three call-sign readings give them enough chances to copy your identification accurately, all while keeping the transmission short. Endless repetition (5×, 10×) is discouraged as it monopolizes the frequency.", "misconception": "That \"more is better\"—that piling on extra CQs improves the odds of a contact. In practice an unbalanced or overly long call is worse operating; the call sign repetition matters as much as the CQ repetition.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "\"Three-by-three\": CQ ×3, this is, call sign ×3.", "edge_cases": "In contests or busy nets a shorter \"CQ contest [call]\" is common; the 3×3 is the general-purpose textbook form, not a legal requirement."}

{"core": "To answer a CQ, you give the calling station's call sign once (they already announced it, so excessive repetition wastes airtime), then \"this is\" to clearly separate identities, then your own call sign so they know who is responding.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This is the format for sending CQ (announcing yourself to all), not for answering one; saying your own call first and \"calling\" is backwards for a reply.", "why_tempting": "It contains the right elements (call signs, a connecting word) and sounds procedurally plausible if you confuse calling with answering."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Repeating the other station's call five times and yours twice is grossly inefficient; once each (with yours possibly repeated for clarity) is the standard on a clear channel.", "why_tempting": "More repetition feels safer, and heavy repetition is used in weak-signal or CW CQ calls, blurring the line."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Three-plus and five-plus repetitions is the kind of pattern used for an initial CQ in poor conditions, not for a reply where the contact is already partly established.", "why_tempting": "The 'three times... five times' rhythm echoes traditional CQ phrasing, so it feels authentically procedural."}], "concept": "Voice answering protocol is built for efficiency and unambiguous identification. The replying station's job is simply: address the station you heard (their call once), insert \"this is\" as the universal handover phrase, then state your own call sign. Contrast with the CQ itself, where you may repeat \"CQ\" and your call several times because no one yet knows who is calling. Once a station has announced its presence, one repetition of its call confirms you are answering them specifically.", "misconception": "Confusing the format for ANSWERING a CQ with the format for SENDING one — and assuming more repetition is always more correct.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "\"Them once, this is, me\" — point at the other op first, then announce yourself.", "edge_cases": "Under marginal or noisy conditions you may repeat your own call sign two or three times for copy, but you still only need the other station's call once."}

{"core": "Simplex means both stations transmit and receive on a single, common frequency, taking turns (half-duplex in practice) without any intermediate repeater.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "One-way transmission describes a broadcast or beacon mode, not simplex; simplex is a two-way exchange where stations alternate on the same channel.", "why_tempting": "The word 'simplex' superficially suggests 'simple/single direction,' which maps onto one-way communication in some non-radio contexts."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Coverage area is unrelated to the definition; simplex is defined by frequency usage, not how far the signal reaches.", "why_tempting": "Learners may confuse simplex with a network or coverage characteristic rather than a frequency relationship."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Transmitting on one frequency and receiving on another is duplex (split) operation, exactly how repeaters and many HF/VHF links work — the opposite of simplex.", "why_tempting": "It's the natural counterpart to the correct answer, and learners who mix up simplex and duplex pick it."}], "concept": "Radio links are classified by their frequency relationship. Simplex: TX and RX share one frequency, so only one station talks at a time on that channel. Duplex (or semi-duplex/split): TX and RX use two different frequencies — a repeater listens on the input frequency and simultaneously retransmits on the output frequency, and users program a fixed offset (e.g. ±600 kHz on 2 m). Note 'simplex' here refers to the frequency arrangement, not to directionality of information flow (the data-communications meaning of simplex as strictly one-way is a different convention).", "misconception": "Conflating the radio-operating meaning of simplex (single shared frequency) with the data-comms meaning (one-way only), or swapping simplex with duplex.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "Simplex = Single frequency; Duplex = Different (two) frequencies.", "edge_cases": "On a simplex channel only one station can transmit at a time; trying to talk on the repeater's output frequency directly is a common newcomer error."}

{"core": "Simplex (direct station-to-station) is the appropriate choice when the two stations can hear each other reliably without the help of a repeater, since it frees the repeater for stations that genuinely need its added range.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "An emergency phone call would be handled by an autopatch or other facility, and emergencies actually argue for using the repeater's coverage, not simplex.", "why_tempting": "Emergency scenarios feel urgent and 'direct,' so the word simplex sounds like a quick, no-frills option."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Seeking local info while travelling is exactly when a repeater's wide coverage helps you reach local operators you can't hear directly.", "why_tempting": "Travelling sounds like a casual/local activity that simplex could serve, but coverage is the limiting factor here."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "When maximum reliability/range is needed you use the repeater, which extends coverage; that is the opposite of the simplex use case.", "why_tempting": "'Most reliable' is a generically attractive phrase, but reliability over distance is precisely what a repeater provides."}], "concept": "A repeater receives on one frequency and simultaneously retransmits on another, sitting at a high site to bridge stations that cannot reach each other directly. This extra hop is valuable only when path loss or terrain blocks a direct link. When both parties already have solid signals between them, simplex (transmit and receive on a single frequency) is courteous operating practice: it keeps the shared repeater available for those who truly need it and avoids unnecessary loading of repeater infrastructure.", "misconception": "Believing the repeater is always the 'better' choice. The deciding factor is whether a direct path is reliable, not how important or urgent the contact is.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "If you can hear each other direct, keep the repeater free.", "edge_cases": "Operators often have a designated simplex calling/working frequency for each band; after establishing contact on a repeater it's good practice to move a reliable pair to simplex."}

{"core": "VHF/UHF propagation is largely line-of-sight, making it ideal for local contacts, while HF bands support ionospheric long-distance work. Using VHF/UHF for local traffic keeps the limited HF spectrum free for the long-haul communication only HF can reliably provide.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Signal strength at the receiver depends on path loss, power, and antennas — not inherently on whether the band is VHF/UHF versus HF. The choice is about spectrum etiquette, not raw signal strength.", "why_tempting": "Local VHF/UHF contacts often sound strong and full-quieting, so it feels like the signals are intrinsically 'stronger.'"}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Power limits in the amateur service are tied to qualification level, not to the band; VHF/UHF is not granted higher permitted output than HF.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume higher frequencies allow more power, conflating power rules with band choice."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "HF absolutely propagates locally — ground wave and near-vertical incidence skywave provide local coverage. The issue is efficient use of spectrum, not an inability to be heard nearby.", "why_tempting": "Since HF is famous for DX, it's easy to wrongly conclude it can't do local work."}], "concept": "The real reasoning is spectrum management. HF (below 30 MHz) refracts off the ionosphere, enabling reliable long-distance/DX contacts — a capability VHF/UHF normally lacks (those bands are mostly line-of-sight, augmented occasionally by sporadic-E, ducting, or aurora). Because HF spectrum is comparatively narrow and uniquely valuable for long-haul links, conducting routine local QSOs there crowds out distant stations. VHF/UHF offers ample bandwidth, repeaters, and good local coverage, so steering local traffic there is the courteous, efficient practice.", "misconception": "That the recommendation is driven by a physical advantage (stronger signals, more power, or HF failing locally), rather than by good operating practice and conservation of scarce HF spectrum.", "link": "section 7", "mnemonic": "HF = Hops Far; keep it for DX, keep local chatter on VHF/UHF.", "edge_cases": "VHF/UHF can occasionally go long via sporadic-E, tropospheric ducting, or aurora, but these are intermittent — they don't change the everyday line-of-sight assumption behind this guidance."}

{"core": "Repeater input (uplink) frequencies are offset from the output you hear. If you transmit simplex on a repeater's input frequency, you key up the repeater and interfere with users without realizing it, since nothing comes back on that frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Any clear frequency within the band's simplex segment can support simplex; there's no inherent inability of a frequency to handle direct station-to-station contact.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you confuse band-plan segment designations (repeater vs simplex sub-bands) with a physical limitation."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Implanted medical devices operate in entirely different, dedicated spectrum allocations, not the amateur VHF/UHF simplex segments.", "why_tempting": "RF safety concerns are emphasized elsewhere, so it feels like a responsible-sounding answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Amateur VHF/UHF FM voice uses one standard narrowband deviation; there isn't a wideband/narrowband channel split that drives simplex frequency choice.", "why_tempting": "Broadcast FM is wideband while two-way FM is narrowband, so the wide/narrow distinction sounds technically real—but it's not what governs simplex selection here."}], "concept": "Repeaters use a duplex pair: they listen on an input frequency and retransmit on an output frequency separated by a standard offset (e.g. ±600 kHz on 2 m, ±5 MHz on 70 cm). A simplex station transmits and receives on the same frequency. The hazard is that a repeater's input lies on an ordinary-looking frequency; if you pick it for simplex, your transmissions are relayed by (or jam) the repeater while you hear nothing unusual. Consulting the band plan to use designated simplex calling/working frequencies avoids landing on a repeater input.", "misconception": "Believing any seemingly idle frequency is free for simplex—failing to recognize that a repeater's input frequency carries no signal you can hear, so it sounds vacant even while in active use.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "Input is invisible: a quiet frequency may be a repeater's ear.", "edge_cases": "Repeater offsets and designated simplex frequencies vary by band; always check the local/regional band plan rather than assuming a fixed split."}

{"core": "A repeater retransmits whatever it hears on its input frequency. If you can hear the other station directly on that input frequency, then you and they are within direct (simplex) range and don't need the repeater.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A third station confirming reception doesn't tell you whether you two can hear each other directly — it only proves a triangular path, not the direct point-to-point link.", "why_tempting": "Adding a relay station feels like a thorough test, and reception confirmation sounds rigorous."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Receiving a different, more distant repeater is irrelevant to whether the path between you and the other station supports simplex.", "why_tempting": "It involves listening to a frequency, so it superficially resembles the correct technique."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Asking the station to shift 10 kHz off-frequency just moves them off the repeater channel and tests nothing about a direct path; you'd lose the repeater link without gaining a direct one.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a deliberate 'test the path' manoeuvre, which is the right idea but the wrong method."}], "concept": "A repeater operates on a duplex pair: it listens on the input frequency and transmits on the output frequency, offset by a fixed amount. When you key up, your signal reaches the repeater on its input frequency. If your direct signal is strong enough to reach the other station on that same input frequency, the two of you are within line-of-sight range and could work simplex, freeing the repeater for others. The practical check is to monitor the repeater's input frequency (your radio's reverse function does this): if you hear the other station clearly there, simplex will work.", "misconception": "Believing you need a roundabout test (third party, frequency shift) when the answer is simply to listen on the repeater's input where the other station is already transmitting.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "Listen on the INPUT — that's where the other station already is.", "edge_cases": "Most VHF/UHF radios have a 'reverse' button that temporarily swaps RX to the input frequency for exactly this check; clear copy on reverse means try simplex."}

{"core": "A repeater's input and output frequencies are reserved for its operation; using one of those frequencies for simplex contacts can desensitize or block the repeater for everyone relying on it, so you should move to a clear simplex channel.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Receiver overload from a repeater's transmitter is a separate front-end issue and is not the operating-courtesy reason for vacating a repeater frequency.", "why_tempting": "It sounds technically plausible to a learner who knows about strong nearby signals causing overload."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The relative number of repeater versus simplex users is irrelevant; the issue is occupying a frequency dedicated to repeater traffic.", "why_tempting": "It offers a vaguely social/statistical rationale that seems to favor giving way to the larger group."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "You are not changing the repeater's frequency at all—you are moving your own simplex operation. No ISED authorization is involved here.", "why_tempting": "It mixes in a regulatory-sounding requirement that may seem authoritative on an exam."}], "concept": "A repeater listens on an input frequency and retransmits on an output frequency (split by a standard offset). If you transmit simplex on either of those channels, your signal competes directly with repeater users—you may key the repeater unintentionally, or your transmissions collide with stations using the machine, degrading the shared resource. Good operating practice (a courtesy/etiquette principle, not a hard regulation) is to relocate to a recognized simplex calling or working frequency so the repeater channel stays available.", "misconception": "Confusing the act of moving your own operation off a busy/dedicated channel with somehow altering or controlling the repeater itself, or attributing the courtesy to a technical or regulatory cause.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "Repeater frequencies belong to the repeater—take simplex chats elsewhere.", "edge_cases": "This is operating etiquette to avoid interference, not a legal prohibition on transmitting on that frequency; the underlying obligation is simply to avoid causing harmful interference."}

{"core": "By long-standing amateur convention, USB (upper sideband) is used on all HF bands at and above 14 MHz, which includes the 20-metre band.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Lower sideband is the convention only on the lower HF bands (160, 80, and 40 metres), not on 20 metres.", "why_tempting": "LSB is genuinely correct on adjacent lower bands, so it seems plausible if you forget the 9–10 MHz crossover point."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Independent sideband (ISB) transmits two different signals on the upper and lower sidebands simultaneously; it is a specialized commercial/military mode, not standard amateur voice practice.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a legitimate single-sideband variant and may be confused with the SSB family."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Double sideband (DSB) is full AM-style transmission with both sidebands and (usually) carrier — inefficient and not the SSB voice convention asked about.", "why_tempting": "DSB is a real emission type that precedes SSB historically, so it can look like a valid 'sideband' answer."}], "concept": "SSB suppresses the carrier and one sideband to save power and bandwidth. Which sideband is kept is purely convention, not a technical requirement: amateurs use LSB below ~10 MHz (160/80/40 m) and USB at and above 14 MHz (20/17/15/12/10 m), plus USB on VHF/UHF SSB and most digital modes. 20 m = 14 MHz, so USB.", "misconception": "Believing the choice of sideband is dictated by physics rather than by a band-dependent operating agreement, or assuming all HF voice uses one fixed sideband.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "Below 10 MHz, sink to the Lower; 14 MHz and up, rise to the Upper.", "edge_cases": "60 m (5 MHz) channels use USB by exception despite being below 10 MHz, and digital/data modes generally use USB regardless of band."}

{"core": "By long-standing amateur convention, lower sideband (LSB) is used for voice on the HF bands below 10 MHz — including 80 m (3755 kHz). Upper sideband is used above 10 MHz.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Double-sideband (full AM) is bandwidth-inefficient and is not the standard SSB voice mode on 80 m.", "why_tempting": "'Double' sounds like a legitimate sideband option and AM does exist on HF, but it isn't the convention for this frequency."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "USB is the convention above 10 MHz (and on VHF/UHF SSB); 3755 kHz is below that boundary, so LSB applies.", "why_tempting": "Learners who remember 'SSB' but forget the band-dependent rule, or who recall USB for VHF, may default to it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Independent sideband transmits two separate signals on each sideband — a specialized mode never used for ordinary 80 m voice.", "why_tempting": "It's a real sideband term, so it can seem plausible to someone unfamiliar with normal SSB practice."}], "concept": "SSB suppresses the carrier and one sideband. Which sideband is transmitted is arbitrary technically, but amateurs adopted a convention split at roughly 10 MHz: LSB below (160, 80, 40 m), USB above (20 m and up, plus VHF/UHF SSB). 3755 kHz lies in the 80 m band, hence LSB. This is purely operating practice, not a regulatory requirement.", "misconception": "Assuming all SSB voice uses USB, or that the choice is per-operator. It's a band-based community convention so stations can demodulate each other correctly.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "Low frequency → Lower sideband; the dividing line is 10 MHz.", "edge_cases": "60 m (5 MHz) channels conventionally use USB despite being below 10 MHz, an exception to the simple rule."}

{"core": "The preferred directed-call format names the station you want first, then identifies yourself: the called station's call sign, \"this is,\" then your own call sign. This addresses the intended station unambiguously and immediately tells it who is calling.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"CQ\" is a general call to ANY station, not a directed call to one specific station, so it's the wrong procedure entirely when you already know who you want.", "why_tempting": "CQ is the most familiar calling phrase a new operator learns, so it gets reached for reflexively."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Leading with your own call sign is backwards — the addressed station should hear who's being called first. \"Calling\" is not the standard procedural word; \"this is\" is.", "why_tempting": "It contains the right ingredients (both calls plus a word) and \"calling\" sounds intuitively correct."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "\"For\" is not a recognized procedural word, and your own call is placed first, addressing the wrong end of the link.", "why_tempting": "Doubling your call sign and using a connecting word mimics real-sounding radio chatter."}], "concept": "In voice operating procedure a directed (point-to-point) call follows the convention [called station] \"this is\" [calling station]. The logic is addressing-then-identifying: the station you want hears its own ID at the front and knows the transmission is for it, then learns who is sending. \"This is\" is the standard plain-language separator (analogous to DE in CW). Contrast with a CQ call, which is omnidirectional and used when you'll accept a reply from anyone.", "misconception": "Confusing a general CQ call (any station may answer) with a directed call (one named station), and getting the order of call signs reversed.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "\"Them, this is, me\" — say the other station's call first, then \"this is,\" then yours.", "edge_cases": "Once contact is established and the band is uncluttered, full call-sign exchanges can be reduced, but station identification at required intervals still applies."}

{"core": "Before transmitting you must listen first to confirm the frequency is clear, so you don't interfere with a QSO already in progress. This is basic courteous, interference-avoiding operating practice.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Antenna resonance is a station-setup concern checked during tune-up into a dummy load, not an action required immediately before every transmission.", "why_tempting": "It sounds technically responsible and a strong-electronics learner may over-weight equipment checks over operating etiquette."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "You want SWR as LOW as possible (close to 1:1); high SWR means a poor match and reflected power. The statement is backwards.", "why_tempting": "It mentions a real parameter you do verify, and the wording 'high enough' mimics legitimate pre-transmit checks."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Always use the minimum power necessary for reliable communication; blindly increasing power risks interference and violates good practice.", "why_tempting": "More power intuitively seems to guarantee being heard, especially under marginal conditions."}], "concept": "The first rule of spectrum sharing is to listen before transmitting. Amateur frequencies are shared, not assigned, so monitoring the channel and asking 'is this frequency in use?' prevents doubling on or jamming an existing contact. This is an operating-procedure/etiquette item, distinct from the technical readiness checks (resonance, SWR, power) that belong to station tune-up done into a dummy load beforehand.", "misconception": "That a technically correct transmitter setup is the precondition for transmitting. The exam tests operating courtesy: a perfectly tuned rig is still in the wrong if it transmits over someone already on frequency.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "LBT — Listen Before Transmit.", "edge_cases": "Listening alone isn't foolproof: a hidden/weak station may be present, which is why you also briefly ask whether the frequency is in use before calling."}

{"core": "Good amateur practice is to use the minimum power necessary for reliable communication. If your signal is already extremely strong and perfectly readable, you have excess margin, so reduce power to the minimum that still maintains the contact.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Continuing at full power when much less would suffice wastes power and needlessly increases interference potential to other users sharing the band.", "why_tempting": "The contact is working perfectly, so it seems there's no problem to fix — but the question is about courteous operating practice, not whether the link works."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A speech processor increases average talk power/intelligibility; you'd only want it when your signal is weak, not when it's already perfectly readable.", "why_tempting": "It's a real transmitter adjustment, and learners may grab any plausible 'improvement' control without asking whether more output is warranted."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Bypassing the antenna tuner affects impedance matching/SWR, not link margin, and would more likely degrade your signal — it has nothing to do with an over-strong signal.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a legitimate transmitter/feedline adjustment, so it can fool someone matching 'adjustment' to any RF setting."}], "concept": "The operating principle is 'use the minimum power necessary to carry out the desired communications.' Excess transmit power doesn't improve a contact that's already loud and 100% copy; it only raises your interference footprint, shortens battery life, and increases RF exposure. The correct response to a report of an extremely strong, fully readable signal is therefore to back off power until you reach the threshold of reliable copy.", "misconception": "Believing that a strong, clear signal means everything is optimal — confusing 'the contact works' with 'I'm operating correctly.' The exam targets the courtesy/minimum-power principle, not link performance.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "MPN — Minimum Power Necessary.", "edge_cases": "Minimum-necessary power still allows full legal power when conditions genuinely require it (weak signals, poor propagation); the rule is about matching power to need, not always running low."}

{"core": "Tuning a transmitter (antenna tuner adjustment) injects an unmodulated or test carrier that will splatter across nearby frequencies. To avoid disrupting the net in progress, you move 3–5 kHz away — clear of an SSB voice channel's ~2.7–3 kHz bandwidth — find an unoccupied spot, and tune there.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "1 kHz is well inside an SSB signal's occupied bandwidth (~2.7–3 kHz), so your test carrier lands squarely on top of the net traffic.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a safe small offset, and 1 kHz seems 'away' from the net frequency."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Transmitting on the exact net frequency dumps your tuning carrier directly onto ongoing communications — the worst choice for courtesy and interference.", "why_tempting": "You ultimately operate on the net frequency, so a learner might think you tune there too."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The band centre frequency is arbitrary relative to the net and could land on someone else's QSO; there's no reason it would be clear, and it ignores the actual goal of finding empty spectrum near where you'll operate.", "why_tempting": "'Centre frequency' sounds like a designated neutral/calibration point."}], "concept": "An SSB voice signal occupies roughly 2.7–3 kHz of spectrum. Any tune-up emission must clear that bandwidth on a frequency confirmed to be unoccupied. Best practice: listen first, then move 3–5 kHz off the net, verify the frequency is clear, and use minimum power (ideally into a dummy load when no antenna match is required). Tuning is brief and at low duty to minimize spectrum pollution.", "misconception": "That a 'small' offset like 1 kHz is enough separation — it isn't, because it falls inside the occupied bandwidth of the adjacent signal.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "Tune three-to-five, keep the net alive.", "edge_cases": "Where the tuner can be adjusted into a dummy load, do that instead of radiating; on-air tuning is only needed when matching the actual antenna system, and even then use minimum power for the shortest time."}

{"core": "A dummy load is a resistive, non-radiating load (typically 50 Ω) that absorbs the transmitter's RF output as heat. Feeding the transmitter into a dummy load during testing means essentially no signal is radiated, so on-air interference is eliminated regardless of how long the testing lasts.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An unoccupied frequency still radiates a real signal that can interfere with distant stations, propagate via skip, or land on a frequency you cannot hear but others use. It reduces local QRM but doesn't eliminate radiated interference.", "why_tempting": "Picking a clear frequency is good practice for brief on-air tests, so it feels like the responsible answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A non-resonant antenna presents a poor match (high SWR) but still radiates whatever power it accepts, so it does not minimize interference and adds reflected-power problems.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a 'bad' antenna would radiate less, but mismatch reduces transmitted power without eliminating radiation."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A resonant antenna is an efficient radiator — it maximizes radiated signal, which is the opposite of what you want during testing.", "why_tempting": "Resonant antennas are 'correct' antennas, so a learner may pick it as the proper engineering choice."}], "concept": "A dummy load is a non-inductive power resistor (commonly 50 Ω to match coax) rated to dissipate the transmitter's output as heat. Because its radiation resistance is negligible and it is shielded, the antenna current produces essentially no far-field signal. This lets you key up for extended periods to tune, measure power, or check modulation without putting energy into the air. It also gives a stable, purely resistive load (SWR ≈ 1:1), which protects the final amplifier and gives clean, repeatable measurements.", "misconception": "That avoiding interference during testing is about WHERE you transmit (frequency) rather than WHETHER you radiate at all. For lengthy testing the robust solution is to not radiate — use a dummy load.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "Testing? Load it, don't broadcast it.", "edge_cases": "Dummy loads have a power/duty-cycle rating; high-power continuous testing can overheat a small load. Some radiation can leak from a poorly shielded load or feedline, but it's far below an antenna's."}

{"core": "A dummy load presents a matched, non-radiating 50-ohm resistive impedance so you can transmit while testing or tuning your transceiver without putting a signal on the air to interfere with other stations.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A dummy load does not radiate, so no distant station can hear you — it cannot produce any signal report at all.", "why_tempting": "Signal reports involve transmitting, and tuning sometimes precedes contacts, so the words feel related to on-air activity."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A dummy load does not make tuning faster; it simply provides a clean, fixed, matched load for safe testing.", "why_tempting": "Operators often tune into a dummy load before going on the air, so it can seem like a speed/efficiency tool."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The transmitter still produces full output power into the dummy load; that power is dissipated as heat in the resistor, not reduced.", "why_tempting": "Since the energy goes into a resistor instead of an antenna, it can feel like the output is being 'lowered' or wasted."}], "concept": "A dummy load is a non-inductive power resistor (typically 50 ohms to match standard coax/transmitter output impedance) with a heat-dissipating capacity rated for the transmitter's power. Because it absorbs essentially all forward power and reflects negligibly (SWR ~1:1), it lets you key the transmitter to check output, set bias, adjust drive, or tune an amplifier under realistic loading conditions while radiating only a tiny stray field. This keeps the spectrum clean and protects the final amplifier from the high SWR an unmatched or open antenna would present.", "misconception": "Confusing 'no radiated signal' with 'reduced power' — the transmitter still develops full power; the load just converts it to heat rather than radio waves.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Dummy load = a 'silent antenna': full power in, near-zero RF out.", "edge_cases": "A dummy load is rated for a specific power and duty cycle — prolonged high-power transmission can overheat and damage it; oil-cooled (\"cantenna\") types extend that limit."}

{"core": "No amateur has a priority claim to any frequency. The courteous and correct action is to politely ask the existing occupants if they would mind moving; if not, you relocate your net to a clear frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Cancelling is unnecessary and ignores the simple solution of just moving the net elsewhere.", "why_tempting": "Seems like the polite, non-confrontational choice if you assume the assigned frequency is fixed."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Reducing power doesn't clear the channel — both you and the existing users would interfere with each other, degrading both communications.", "why_tempting": "Lowering power sounds courteous, but it doesn't actually resolve a shared-frequency conflict."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Deliberately overpowering existing activity is willful interference and the opposite of good operating practice.", "why_tempting": "It appears to 'solve' the problem of being heard, and net control may feel entitled to its regular frequency."}], "concept": "Amateur frequencies are a shared resource — no station or net 'owns' a frequency by tradition or regular use. Good operating procedure (and interference avoidance) requires listening first, then politely negotiating. If occupants decline to move, you accommodate them by finding clear spectrum, since they had it first.", "misconception": "That a regularly scheduled net has a priority right to 'its' frequency and may displace casual users. It does not.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "Listen, ask, then move — never bully the band.", "edge_cases": "This is operating etiquette, not a hard regulation; the underlying rule is simply that no one has exclusive rights to a frequency and interference must be avoided."}

{"core": "A scheduled net has no inherent priority, but courteous operating practice means yielding the frequency to a group that needs it rather than forcing a conflict. Moving to a clear frequency avoids mutual interference.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Turning off your radio ends your QSO entirely instead of simply relocating it — there's no need to stop operating, just to change frequency.", "why_tempting": "It does technically clear the frequency, so it superficially solves the conflict."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Increasing power deliberately to dominate the channel is the opposite of courteous operation and causes harmful interference; amateurs should use the minimum power needed.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a way to keep your communication working, but it weaponizes power against another group."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Intentionally holding a frequency to deny it to others is willful interference and unacceptable operating behaviour.", "why_tempting": "It frames 'using the frequency first' as a right, but no one owns a frequency."}], "concept": "No amateur or net holds an exclusive right to any frequency. Spectrum is shared, and operating etiquette resolves overlaps through mutual courtesy: minimum necessary power, listening before transmitting, and yielding to avoid interference. A net coordinates many stations, so moving one or two stations is usually the lower-disruption choice.", "misconception": "Believing that whoever is on a frequency 'first' owns it, or that a net automatically outranks an existing QSO. Neither is true — cooperation, not priority, governs frequency sharing.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "Courtesy clears the channel.", "edge_cases": "This is general good practice; it differs from declared emergency traffic, which does take precedence over routine communications."}

{"core": "When propagation shifts and another station's signal rises on your frequency, the cooperative and practical fix is to relocate your contact to a clear frequency—nobody owns a frequency in amateur radio.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Raising power to override others escalates mutual interference and is contrary to courteous operating practice; it doesn't resolve the conflict, it worsens it for everyone.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like 'winning' the frequency, and Basic privileges do allow up to 250 W input, so it feels like a legitimate tool."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Frequencies are not owned; there is no 'I was here first' priority that obligates other stations to leave. Demanding they move is neither enforceable nor courteous.", "why_tempting": "Seems fair on a human level and mirrors how some operators actually behave on crowded bands."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Routine band congestion from changing propagation is normal operating—not harmful interference requiring a report. A Section Manager is not a regulatory enforcement contact for ordinary QRM.", "why_tempting": "Sounds responsible and procedural, and RAC roles do exist, so it reads as the 'proper channel.'"}], "concept": "Amateur operating etiquette treats spectrum as a shared resource. When propagation opens (e.g., an ionospheric path lengthens or sporadic-E appears), distant stations on the same frequency suddenly become audible and you experience co-channel QRM. No operator has a claim to a given frequency, so the correct response to ordinary congestion is to find a clear spot and continue, after listening before transmitting. This is distinct from deliberate harmful interference, which is a separate matter.", "misconception": "That being on a frequency first grants ownership or priority, or that more power or escalating to an authority is the right way to handle normal shared-band congestion.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "No one owns a frequency—when it gets crowded, move, don't muscle.", "edge_cases": "This applies to incidental QRM from propagation changes. Deliberate, malicious interference is a regulatory issue handled differently than simply finding a new frequency."}

{"core": "An SSB voice signal occupies roughly 2.4–3 kHz of bandwidth, so you should offset your operating frequency by about 3 kHz from an ongoing QSO to keep your sideband from overlapping theirs.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "150–500 Hz is far less than one SSB channel width; signals offset this little would heavily overlap and cause severe mutual interference.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a 'safe small margin,' and these values resemble CW spacing or tuning resolution figures."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "6 kHz is about double an SSB signal's width — it works but wastes spectrum, so it isn't the minimum needed to avoid interference.", "why_tempting": "Doubling the bandwidth feels conservative and 'safe,' so it seems like a sensible choice."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "10 kHz is roughly the channel spacing for AM broadcast, not SSB amateur voice; it grossly over-spaces and crowds out other users unnecessarily.", "why_tempting": "Learners may confuse it with AM broadcast band channelization or assume more separation is always better."}], "concept": "A standard SSB voice channel carries audio of roughly 300 Hz–2.7 kHz, giving an occupied bandwidth near 2.4–3 kHz. To place your signal adjacent to an existing QSO without spectral overlap, your suppressed-carrier (dial) frequency must differ by at least one signal bandwidth — about 3 kHz. Less spacing causes overlap; much more spacing simply wastes a scarce band.", "misconception": "Thinking the spacing should match a narrow tone offset (Hz) or a wide AM-style channel (10 kHz), rather than the actual ~3 kHz occupied bandwidth of an SSB voice signal.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "SSB voice ≈ 3 kHz wide → leave ~3 kHz between stations.", "edge_cases": "This is a courtesy/practical minimum, not a regulatory limit; actual maximum allowed bandwidth per band is set in RBR-4. In crowded conditions a touch more separation reduces splatter from over-driven transmitters."}

{"core": "A band plan is a voluntary, community-agreed convention that subdivides an amateur band into segments by mode (CW, SSB, FM, digital, beacons, etc.) so that incompatible modes don't interfere with one another. It is custom, not law.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Mandatory operating restrictions within a band are set by regulation (RBR-4) and ISED, not by a band plan. A band plan has no force of law.", "why_tempting": "Both involve dividing up a band, and learners conflate ISED's legal allocations with the voluntary gentlemen's-agreement band plan."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A contest operating strategy is a temporary club activity, not the standing mode-segmentation convention the term 'band plan' refers to.", "why_tempting": "The word 'plan' plus 'club' and 'frequency band' sounds plausible if you don't know the formal meaning."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Allocating frequency bands and recommending allocations to the ITU is an international/national regulatory function, not what a band plan is.", "why_tempting": "It uses official-sounding terms (allocations, ITU) that suggest authority, and band plans do roughly follow ITU region conventions."}], "concept": "Distinguish three levels of band organization: (1) ITU/national allocations — which services may use which frequencies, set by treaty and regulation; (2) regulatory privileges/restrictions — what your qualification permits (RBR-4); (3) band plans — voluntary agreements among amateurs assigning sub-segments to specific modes to reduce mutual interference. Only level 3 is the 'band plan,' and it is purely advisory — violating it is poor etiquette, not an offence.", "misconception": "Believing a band plan is legally binding or government-prescribed. The key word is 'voluntary' — it organizes coexistence of modes by consensus, not by force of law.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "Band plan = 'Be a Good neighbour' — voluntary courtesy, not a ticket.", "edge_cases": "While voluntary, ignoring the band plan (e.g. running wide FM in a narrow CW/data segment) can still cause harmful interference, which IS a regulatory matter — so the plan and the law overlap in effect."}

{"core": "Good operating practice requires you to listen first to confirm a frequency is clear, so you don't transmit over an existing QSO. Listening is passive and causes no interference, so it must come before any active step.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Adjusting receiver volume is a personal comfort/operating detail, not the prerequisite for avoiding interference. It does nothing to determine whether the frequency is in use.", "why_tempting": "You do want to be able to hear before transmitting, so it sounds like a sensible 'first' step, but it's a setup detail, not the courteous-operating rule being tested."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Asking 'is the frequency in use?' is itself a transmission—it can interrupt a weak or distant station you haven't yet heard. You must listen before you key up at all.", "why_tempting": "It's a real and proper step in correct procedure, but it comes *after* a period of listening, not first."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Announcing your intent to call is also a transmission and could splatter onto an in-progress contact you haven't detected. Transmitting blind is exactly what listening prevents.", "why_tempting": "Announcing yourself feels polite and proactive, but doing so without first listening risks the very interference the rule guards against."}], "concept": "Courteous-operation procedure (RIC-3 syllabus topic 2-4, tune-ups/testing/courteous operation) establishes a sequence: listen → ask if occupied → identify and call. The ordering follows from interference avoidance: any RF you emit can disrupt a station already on frequency, including one too weak or fading to hear instantly. Listening is the only zero-emission diagnostic, so it is always step one. Note hidden-transmitter and propagation effects (a distant station may be inaudible to you but active for others), which is precisely why a passive listen, then a polite query, precedes a call.", "misconception": "Confusing 'first courteous step' with 'first active step.' Options C and D are valid parts of correct procedure, but transmitting—in any form—cannot be first because it can interfere before you've confirmed the channel is clear.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "LAC: Listen, Ask, Call—in that order.", "edge_cases": "Because of one-way propagation and hidden stations, even a clear-sounding frequency may be in use elsewhere; that's why you both listen and then ask before calling."}

{"core": "The standard CW CQ call uses the \"3x3\" pattern: \"CQ CQ CQ DE [your call] [your call] [your call]\" — three CQs, then DE (\"from\"), then your call sign three times so a responding station can copy it reliably.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Sending the call sign only once gives the answering station too little chance to copy it correctly under fading or QRM.", "why_tempting": "The triple \"CQ\" is right, so it looks correct until you notice the call sign should also be repeated three times."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Ten CQs is excessive and is poor operating practice; the convention is three.", "why_tempting": "More repetition seems like it would improve copy, but it just wastes airtime and ignores the standard format."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Sending CQ endlessly with no DE or call sign is non-standard and fails to identify the calling station.", "why_tempting": "It sounds practical (\"keep calling until answered\"), but it omits identification and the recognized structure."}], "concept": "\"CQ\" is the general call to any station; \"DE\" is the procedural sign meaning \"from\" (French \"de\"), separating the CQ from the sender's identity. The balanced 3x3 structure (CQ ×3, DE, call ×3) lets the listener confirm both that a call is being made and exactly who is making it — important on CW where a single missed character changes a call sign. The pattern can be repeated as a whole if no answer comes, rather than padding either element.", "misconception": "Believing that repeating CQ more times, or sending the call sign fewer times, is acceptable. The format is symmetric and standardized: three of each.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "\"Three and three with a DE in between.\"", "edge_cases": "Under good conditions experienced operators sometimes shorten to a 2x2 call, but the exam expects the standard 3x3 format."}

{"core": "To answer a CQ, you direct the call to the station that called you: send their call sign twice, the prosign \"DE\" (meaning \"from\"), then your own call sign twice. This identifies both who you are calling and who you are.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Sending only your own call sign four times never names the station you are answering, so the calling operator can't tell the reply is for them.", "why_tempting": "It looks efficient and uses repetition for clarity, but it omits the addressee entirely."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The format is inverted: convention is the other station's call twice and your call twice, not their call once and yours four times. The lopsided repetition is non-standard.", "why_tempting": "It correctly includes both call signs and the \"DE\" prosign, so it mimics proper procedure closely—only the repetition counts are wrong."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Name, location, and signal report belong in the QSO after contact is established, not in the initial answer to a CQ, and this option omits the other station's call sign and \"DE\" entirely.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like polite, informative operating, but it skips proper call-up identification."}], "concept": "A Morse call-up follows a fixed grammar: [station being called] DE [station calling]. \"DE\" is French/Latin for \"from\" and separates the two call signs. When answering a CQ, the station that called CQ is being called, so it goes first; you are the caller, so your call follows DE. Doubling each call sign (twice) is standard for a routine, decent-signal contact—you'd send more repetitions only under poor conditions.", "misconception": "Believing that answering a call is just about announcing yourself; in fact you must address the specific station first, then identify yourself, in the prescribed order.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "\"Them DE Me\"—the call you're answering goes before DE, your call goes after.", "edge_cases": "Under weak or noisy conditions you may repeat call signs more than twice; \"twice\" is the routine, good-conditions norm."}

{"core": "A CQ is an invitation for anyone to reply, so you must call no faster than the speed at which you can reliably copy the answering station — otherwise you cannot read the reply you solicited.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Your keying ability is irrelevant; the limiting factor is your receiving (copying) ability, not how fast you can send.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like good operating to demonstrate maximum skill, and sending speed is the obvious thing you control."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "There is no fixed 'customary speed' for a band that overrides your own copying limit; sending faster than you can receive defeats the purpose of calling CQ.", "why_tempting": "Bands do have informal CW speed cultures, so matching the crowd seems courteous and conventional."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Keyer capability sets no useful limit — modern keyers send far faster than any human can copy, so this leads to calling at an uncopyable speed.", "why_tempting": "Phrased almost identically to A, it implies 'show off your equipment's full capability,' which feels like efficient operating."}], "concept": "Good CW operating practice ties calling speed to comprehension, not transmission capability. A CQ invites an unknown station to reply at roughly the speed you called; if you key faster than you can decode, you'll be unable to copy the QSO you initiated. The governing principle is symmetry between send and receive rates, with receive being the binding constraint.", "misconception": "That faster sending is always better, or that 'speed' refers to your keyer/sending capability rather than your reliable copying ability.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "Call no faster than you can copy.", "edge_cases": ""}

{"core": "\"CQ\" is the universal general call meaning \"calling any station\" — an open invitation for any operator hearing it to respond.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Indicating a frequency is in use is done with \"QRL?\" (asking) or \"QRL\" (stating), not CQ.", "why_tempting": "Both are common pre-call procedures, so a learner may conflate the question you ask before calling with the call itself."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "CQ has no timing meaning; it is not tied to any clock interval. This is a pure invented distractor playing on the letter 'Q'.", "why_tempting": "The 'Q' might suggest 'quarter', and scheduled-net habits make a time-based answer seem plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Antenna or equipment testing uses a dummy load and an identifying \"test\" call, not CQ; CQ solicits contacts.", "why_tempting": "Both involve transmitting without a specific recipient, so the 'no particular station' aspect can be confused."}], "concept": "CQ is a procedural call derived from the French \"sécurité\"/general-call tradition, standardized in radio as \"seek you\" — i.e., I am seeking any station to make contact. Operationally an operator first verifies the frequency is clear (\"QRL?\"), then transmits \"CQ CQ CQ de [callsign]\" to invite responses. It is distinct from Q-signals like QRL (busy), QRZ (who is calling me), and QSO (a contact).", "misconception": "Treating CQ as a Q-signal or assigning it a literal/time-based meaning, when it is simply a general call to anyone listening.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "CQ = \"seek you\" → seeking any station.", "edge_cases": "A directed CQ narrows the audience, e.g. \"CQ DX\" (calling distant stations) or \"CQ contest,\" but the base meaning remains a general call."}

{"core": "\"DE\" is a procedural sign borrowed from French (\"de\" = \"from\") used in CW to separate the called station's call sign from the calling station's, i.e. \"VE3ABC DE VA7XYZ\" means \"VE3ABC, this is (from) VA7XYZ.\"", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Received all correctly\" is conveyed by the Q-signal QSL or the prosign R, not DE.", "why_tempting": "Both are short CW abbreviations, so a learner unsure of the abbreviation set may guess this acknowledgement meaning."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A general call to any station is CQ, not DE; DE always introduces the sender's own identity.", "why_tempting": "CQ and DE often appear together (\"CQ CQ DE ...\"), so the two get conflated."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "\"Directional Emissions\" is a fabricated expansion; DE is not an acronym for English words at all.", "why_tempting": "Treating DE as an initialism (like SSB or FM) makes an invented technical phrase seem plausible."}], "concept": "CW operating uses a compact set of prosigns, Q-signals and abbreviations to keep transmissions terse. DE comes directly from the French word \"de\" (from/of) and functions purely as a separator/identifier: [called call sign] DE [calling call sign]. It carries no acknowledgement or directional meaning—it simply marks whose call follows as the originator.", "misconception": "Assuming every two-letter CW token is an English acronym; DE is a foreign-language abbreviation, not an initialism.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "DE = \"this is\" / \"from\" — think of the email-like \"DE: sender\" used in French correspondence.", "edge_cases": "DE is also commonly typed in digital/keyboard modes and even voice nets in CW-style identification, but its meaning—\"from\"—never changes."}

{"core": "\"K\" is the prosign inviting any station to transmit — it means \"go ahead, anyone may reply.\" It is appended after a general call (CQ) or at the end of an over when you're open to whoever wants to respond.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "End of message/end of work is the prosign \"AR\" (didahdidahdit), not \"K.\"", "why_tempting": "\"K\" does come at the end of a transmission, so it feels like an end-of-message marker — but it specifically invites a reply rather than closing communication."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "\"Reply from the called station only\" is the prosign \"KN\" — the added N restricts the invitation to one specific station.", "why_tempting": "\"KN\" is built from \"K,\" so the bare \"K\" looks like the restricted version; the distinction (open vs. directed) is the whole point of the question."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "\"All received correctly\" is conveyed by \"R\" (received) or RST reports, not \"K.\"", "why_tempting": "In casual operating people loosely use letters as acknowledgements, so \"received\" can be confused with the go-ahead signal."}], "concept": "Morse prosigns are operating shorthand. Three closely related invitations to transmit: K (didahdit) = go ahead, ANY station; KN (-·-·-) = go ahead, NAMED/called station only (the N closes the door to others); and AR = end of message (no reply implied yet). R = received/copied correctly. Knowing the K vs KN contrast is the core skill — K opens the floor, KN restricts it.", "misconception": "Treating \"K\" as a generic end-of-transmission or acknowledgement marker, rather than as a specific invitation for anyone to reply.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "K = \"Komm in, anybody.\" Add N (KN) and you Narrow it to the Named station only."}

{"core": "\"DX\" is amateur shorthand for a distant station or distant contact, typically rare or far-away DXing being the pursuit of long-distance communication.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Go ahead\" is conveyed by the prosign K (or KN for a specific station), not DX.", "why_tempting": "Both are common on-air abbreviations, so a learner unfamiliar with the term may guess any operating shorthand."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "\"Best regards\" is the meaning of the number 73, a traditional sign-off, not DX.", "why_tempting": "73 and DX are both heard at the end of contacts, blurring them in memory."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "\"Calling any station\" is the general call CQ, not DX.", "why_tempting": "DX and CQ often appear together (\"CQ DX\"), so the meanings can be conflated."}], "concept": "Amateur operating jargon: DX derives from telegraphy shorthand where D = distance and X = a suffix marker, meaning \"distance\" or distant station. \"Working DX\" means making long-haul contacts. Distinguish from the related abbreviations: CQ = general call to any station, K = invitation to transmit, 73 = best regards. These belong to the broader set of on-air courtesy and procedure terms (Q-signals, prosigns, ham number codes).", "misconception": "Treating all common on-air abbreviations as interchangeable rather than learning each one's distinct function.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "DX = Distance (the X just tags the D)."}

{"core": "\"73\" is a traditional amateur/telegraphy code meaning \"best regards,\" used as a friendly sign-off at the end of a contact.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Go ahead\" is signalled by \"K\" (or \"KN\" for go ahead, named station only), not 73.", "why_tempting": "Both are common operating shorthand, so a learner who hasn't memorized the specific numeric codes may confuse procedural prosigns with closing pleasantries."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "\"Long distance\" is conveyed by \"DX,\" an entirely separate abbreviation.", "why_tempting": "DX and 73 are both heard constantly in QSOs, so the two pieces of jargon can blur together."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "\"Love and kisses\" is the meaning of \"88,\" the companion code to 73 — closely related but distinct.", "why_tempting": "73 and 88 sit side-by-side in the same family of numeric sign-offs, making 88 the most plausible wrong answer."}], "concept": "These numeric codes descend from the 19th-century landline-telegraph \"Phillips Code\" / Western Union numeral set, later adopted by radio amateurs. The two most enduring survivors are 73 = best regards and 88 = love and kisses. They are pure rote-memory items with no technical derivation — just convention. Note that \"73\" is already plural; saying \"73s\" is technically redundant though common.", "misconception": "Treating 73 as interchangeable with other operating shorthand (Q-signals, prosigns, DX) rather than recognizing it as a specific closing courtesy meaning best regards.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "73 = beSt regardS (sign-off); 88 = hugs & kisses. The bigger, friendlier number (88) gets the more affectionate meaning."}

{"core": "Full break-in (QSK) lets the transmitter switch to receive in the gaps between the dots and dashes of your own keying, so you can hear other stations the instant you stop sending a character — even mid-word.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Sending \"BK\" is a manual procedural signal to invite a break, not the automatic hardware feature QSK refers to.", "why_tempting": "\"BK\" literally stands for \"break\" and the question mentions break-in, so it looks like a direct match."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "QSK is about fast transmit/receive switching, independent of whether the operator uses a paddle/keyer or a straight hand key.", "why_tempting": "Keyers and QSK are both modern CW conveniences often found together, blurring the distinction."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "That describes the opposite of QSK — manual semi-break-in (or simple PTT) where you cannot hear between elements; QSK is automatic and continuous.", "why_tempting": "It correctly describes a real, common operating method, so it sounds plausible if you don't know QSK is the automatic case."}], "concept": "In CW the transmitter is only producing RF during the marks (dots/dashes). QSK circuitry sequences the T/R switching fast enough (RF relay or PIN-diode switching) that the receiver is unmuted during the spaces between elements. This requires keying-speed switching with no relay chatter latency, so the operator effectively monitors the band continuously while sending and can be interrupted at any moment.", "misconception": "Confusing QSK (an automatic, element-by-element T/R feature) with the procedural act of one station 'breaking in' by sending BK, or with semi-break-in that only switches between transmissions.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "QSK = 'hear in the Quiet between Sent Keys.'", "edge_cases": "Semi-break-in returns to receive only after a short delay following the last element, so you hear between words/letters but not between individual dots — that is NOT full break-in."}

{"core": "CW signals occupy a narrow bandwidth (roughly 100–200 Hz for typical keying speeds), so allowing about 150–500 Hz of separation places your signal clear of an ongoing QSO while still keeping stations conveniently grouped on the band.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "5–50 Hz is narrower than the occupied bandwidth of a single CW signal, so you would land essentially on top of the contact in progress and cause direct interference.", "why_tempting": "CW is very narrowband, so a learner may assume only a few Hz of guard space is needed."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "1–3 kHz is far more separation than a CW signal needs; that spacing is appropriate for SSB phone (≈2.4–3 kHz bandwidth), not Morse.", "why_tempting": "It looks like a safe 'big gap,' and 3 kHz is the familiar SSB channel figure, so it is easy to misapply it to CW."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "3–6 kHz is wildly excessive for CW and would waste band space, fitting many CW signals between you and the other station.", "why_tempting": "Bigger separation seems safer, and these numbers echo phone-channel or wide-bandwidth thinking."}], "concept": "A CW emission is keyed on/off, and its occupied bandwidth depends on keying speed and rise/fall time — roughly B ≈ K × WPM, giving on the order of 100–200 Hz at normal speeds. To avoid overlapping sidebands you need a guard interval comparable to or a bit larger than that bandwidth. 150–500 Hz comfortably clears the other signal's keying sidebands without packing stations dangerously close or wastefully far apart. Receiver CW filters are also typically 250–500 Hz wide, so this spacing matches what an operator can selectively copy.", "misconception": "Confusing the very narrow bandwidth of CW with the much wider channel spacing used for SSB phone, leading to either far too little (A) or far too much (B, C) separation.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "CW guard ≈ filter width: a few hundred Hz, not a few kHz.", "edge_cases": "At very high keying speeds or with hard keying (key clicks), occupied bandwidth grows, so erring toward the upper end (closer to 500 Hz) is prudent."}

{"core": "In Morse/CW procedure, \"R\" (di-dah-dit) is the prosign for \"Roger\" — it means everything you sent was received correctly and in full.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "There is no single-letter prosign meaning \"only the called station may reply\"; that intent is conveyed differently (e.g. directed calls or \"K\" to a specific station).", "why_tempting": "Learners conflate the many CW prosigns and may guess that R restricts who responds."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A request to repeat everything is \"RPT\" (or the Q-signal QRS for slow down / QSM for repeat); \"R\" alone never asks for a repeat.", "why_tempting": "The letter R suggests \"Repeat\" by first-letter association, which is the trap."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "End of message is the prosign \"AR\" (di-dah-di-dah-dit), and end of contact is \"SK\". A lone R is not an ending signal.", "why_tempting": "R appears inside the AR prosign, so it's easy to misattribute the end-of-message meaning to R by itself."}], "concept": "CW operating uses short procedural signals (prosigns) to keep exchanges efficient. \"R\" = di-dah-dit = \"received / Roger\", an acknowledgement that the transmission was copied 100%. Distinguish it from related prosigns: AR (end of message), SK (end of work/contact), K (invitation to transmit — anyone), KN (invitation to a specific station only), BK (break/back to you), RPT (request repeat). Memorizing the small set of prosigns prevents the first-letter guessing trap.", "misconception": "That \"R\" means \"Repeat\" because both start with R — in fact R is an affirmative acknowledgement, the opposite of asking for a repeat.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "R = Roger = Received (all three start with R, and all mean \"got it\")."}

{"core": "RST is a signal-quality reporting code: R = Readability, S = Strength, T = Tone. It characterizes how the received signal is perceived at the receiving station — i.e. signal reception.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Transmitter power is set at the sending station and isn't conveyed by RST; the report describes the received signal, not the source's output.", "why_tempting": "S (Strength) feels related to power, but strength is a received-signal-strength judgment affected by path loss, antennas, and propagation — not raw TX wattage."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Sunspot activity is a propagation/space-weather metric (solar flux index), entirely unrelated to a per-contact signal report.", "why_tempting": "Both involve HF operating jargon, and propagation does influence how strong a signal arrives — but RST reports the result, not the cause."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Ionospheric conditions describe the propagation medium, not the assessment of an individual received signal.", "why_tempting": "Ionospheric fading directly affects readability and strength, so a learner may conflate the cause (ionosphere) with the report itself."}], "concept": "RST is a three-part subjective code. Readability is scored 1–5 (1 = unreadable, 5 = perfectly readable). Strength is scored 1–9 (often read directly off the S-meter, 9 = extremely strong). Tone is scored 1–9 and applies to CW/keyed signals, describing the purity/stability of the note (9 = pure, stable tone; lower values indicate hum, ripple, chirp or AC modulation). On phone, the T digit is dropped, giving a two-digit RS report. So a CW report of '599' means perfectly readable, extremely strong, pure tone; a phone report of '59' is the equivalent without tone.", "misconception": "Assuming a signal report tells you something about the transmitter's intrinsic settings (power) or about external propagation conditions, rather than recognizing it as the receiving operator's subjective assessment of the received signal.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "RST = Readability, Strength, Tone (in that order).", "edge_cases": "The Tone digit is only meaningful for CW/keyed emissions; for voice you give just R and S. Many operators report '59'/'599' reflexively regardless of actual conditions, so reports are subjective and not measurements."}

{"core": "The RST signal report system rates three qualities of a received signal: Readability (1–5), Strength (1–9), and Tone (1–9), giving answer C.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Recovery\" and \"signal speed\" are not parts of RST; only the final \"tone\" is correct.", "why_tempting": "It includes the correct word \"tone\" at the end, which can make the whole option look partly right."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "\"Readability\" is the correct first term, but \"signal speed\" and \"tempo\" are inventions—RST measures strength and tone, not speed or tempo.", "why_tempting": "It correctly starts with \"Readability,\" baiting you into accepting the rest."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "\"Recovery\" and \"tempo\" are wrong; only \"signal strength\" is a genuine RST component.", "why_tempting": "\"Signal strength\" is a real RST element, lending false credibility to the option."}], "concept": "RST is the standard CW/voice signal-reporting code. R = Readability on a 1–5 scale (1 unreadable, 5 perfectly readable). S = Strength on a 1–9 scale, often tied to the receiver's S-meter. T = Tone on a 1–9 scale, describing the purity/stability of a CW note (hum, ripple, chirp). For phone (voice) reports only R and S are used (e.g. \"59\"), since tone applies to a keyed carrier. A report like \"599\" means perfectly readable, very strong, pure tone.", "misconception": "Confusing the three RST dimensions with unrelated metrics like sending speed or tempo, or mixing in plausible-sounding but invented words.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "RST = Read-Strength-Tone, in that order.", "edge_cases": "On voice the T (tone) is dropped, so phone reports are two digits (e.g. 59); tone only matters for CW/keyed carriers."}

{"core": "In the RST system the first digit is Readability (1–5) and the second is Signal Strength (1–9). \"5 7\" means readability 5 (perfectly readable) and strength 7 (moderately strong), so the signal is perfectly readable and moderately strong.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "\"Readable with considerable difficulty\" corresponds to a low readability digit (around R2–R3), not R5.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a plausible degraded-signal description if you forget that 5 is the top of the readability scale."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Tone (T) is the THIRD digit and only applies to CW/Morse reports (RST). A two-digit phone report (RS) has no tone component, so \"near pure tone\" is irrelevant here.", "why_tempting": "Mentioning tone makes it look knowledgeable, and learners may confuse the two-number RS report with the three-number RST report."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "R5 is correct (perfectly readable), but S7 is moderately strong, not weak. A weak signal would be S1–S3.", "why_tempting": "Gets the readability right and only errs on interpreting the strength digit, so it's a near-miss."}], "concept": "RST = Readability, Strength, Tone. Readability runs 1 (unreadable) to 5 (perfectly readable). Strength runs 1 (faint, barely perceptible) to 9 (extremely strong). Tone (CW only) runs 1 (rough) to 9 (pure). For voice (phone) you give only the first two numbers: R then S. Here 5 = perfect copy, 7 = a fairly strong signal (roughly upper-mid scale).", "misconception": "Treating a phone RS report as if it had a tone meaning, or assuming S7 is weak. The strength scale tops at 9, so 7 is well above the midpoint and indicates a moderately strong, not weak, signal.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "R-S-T = Readability, Strength, Tone — and Tone only matters for CW.", "edge_cases": "On SSB/FM voice you report two digits (RS); the third Tone digit is added only for CW/Morse signals."}

{"core": "In the RST system, the first digit is Readability (1–5) and the second is Strength (1–9). A report of \"3 3\" means readability 3 (readable with considerable difficulty) and strength 3 (weak signals).", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "\"Unreadable, very weak\" would correspond to R1 S1, not 3 3. Both digits here are mid-low, not the bottom of each scale.", "why_tempting": "Both '3's sound poor, so a learner who knows the signal is mediocre may overshoot to the worst description."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "RST is a signal-quality report, not a geographic coordinate. Latitude has nothing to do with the format.", "why_tempting": "The two numbers superficially resemble coordinate notation (33 degrees)."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Contest serial/exchange numbers are a separate convention; a signal report is explicitly a readability/strength assessment, not a contact count.", "why_tempting": "Contesters do exchange serial numbers, so someone familiar with contests might conflate the two."}], "concept": "The RST code: R (Readability) 1–5, S (Strength) 1–9, T (Tone) 1–9, used only for CW. For voice (phone) only RS is given, so two-digit reports like \"3 3\" or \"5 9\" are normal. Readability scale: 1=unreadable, 2=barely readable, 3=readable with considerable difficulty, 4=readable with practically no difficulty, 5=perfectly readable. Strength scale runs 1 (faint, barely perceptible) to 9 (extremely strong). So R3 = considerable difficulty, S3 = weak.", "misconception": "Assuming both equal digits mean the same severity, or that a low number automatically means \"worst.\" Each digit maps to a different scale with a different range and meaning.", "link": "section 2", "mnemonic": "R-S-T = Readability, Strength, Tone — and R only goes to 5 while S and T go to 9.", "edge_cases": "The Tone (T) digit is used only for CW/Morse signals; on phone you give just two numbers (RS)."}

{"core": "In the RST system, \"5 9\" means readability 5 (perfectly readable) and strength 9 (S9). \"Plus 20 dB\" indicates the received signal is 20 dB stronger than the S9 reference on the receiver's S-meter.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "While 20 dB does correspond to a 100× power ratio, this option ignores the readability digit and the S9 reference entirely — it describes a relative change, not the actual report meaning.", "why_tempting": "A learner strong in electronics correctly recognizes 20 dB = 10^(20/10) = 100× power and latches onto the math instead of the operating-procedure meaning."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "RST reports describe readability, signal strength, and (for CW) tone — never bandwidth or linearity. \"Linearity\" is an amplifier characteristic, not part of a signal report.", "why_tempting": "The phrase 'dB above linearity' sounds technical and plausible to someone thinking about transmitter performance and two-tone tests."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "RST is a signal-quality report, not a frequency-change instruction. Nothing in '5 9 plus 20 dB' refers to QSY or kHz offsets.", "why_tempting": "It mimics the format of operating shorthand and could be confused with a procedural instruction by someone unfamiliar with RST."}], "concept": "The RST system encodes three (or two for phone) parameters: Readability 1–5, Strength 1–9, and Tone 1–9 (CW only). The S-meter is calibrated so S9 is a defined reference (often ~50 µV at the antenna for HF). Above S9, meters read in decibels above S9: 'plus 20 dB' means the signal is 20 dB stronger than the S9 mark. Since 20 dB = 10·log10(P2/P1) → P2/P1 = 100 in power (or 10× in voltage), the signal is indeed 100× the power at S9 — but the report's meaning is 'fully readable, S9 +20 dB', not merely 'increased by 100×'.", "misconception": "Treating the report as a pure power-ratio statement (the dB math) rather than as the standard RST signal report, where the '5 9' digits carry the readability/strength meaning and '+20 dB' is referenced specifically to S9.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "R-S-T: Readability, Strength, Tone. '5 9 +20' = perfectly readable, 20 dB over S9.", "edge_cases": "On phone only R and S are given; the T (tone) digit applies to CW. S-meter calibration above S9 varies between receivers, so '+20 dB' is a relative indication, not a precise field-strength measurement."}

{"core": "A signal report describes how well you can copy the distant station's transmission as it reaches you — through the repeater you report on the readability and audio quality of that station, not the repeater itself.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The repeater's own signal is constant and strong by design; reporting it tells the distant station nothing about how their transmission is being received.", "why_tempting": "You are listening on the repeater output, so it feels natural to describe the strength of what your radio actually hears."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Your S-meter on the output frequency measures the repeater's transmitter strength, which is fixed and unrelated to the distant station's signal getting into the repeater.", "why_tempting": "S-meter readings are the basis of RST signal strength on simplex, so applying it here seems consistent."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "You normally cannot hear the repeater input frequency, and even if you could, your S-meter at your location says nothing about the signal level arriving at the repeater's receiver.", "why_tempting": "The input frequency is where the distant station's signal actually exists, so it sounds like the 'real' measurement point."}], "concept": "On a repeater, the link between the distant station and the repeater receiver is the variable you cannot directly measure with your S-meter — the repeater fully regenerates the signal before retransmitting. Strength readings (the 'S' in RST) are therefore meaningless; the useful information is readability and audio quality: clarity, noise, distortion, hum, over/under-deviation, and whether the signal is full-quieting. So through a repeater you give a qualitative audio/readability report rather than a numeric strength.", "misconception": "That an S-meter or strength report on the repeater output reflects how well the distant station is being heard. It only reflects the repeater's own constant output.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Through a repeater: report what you HEAR (audio/readability), not how STRONG the repeater hits your meter."}

{"core": "One S-unit = 6 dB, and 6 dB of power corresponds to a factor of 4 (since 10·log₁₀(4) ≈ 6 dB).
