'Runs above' fixes the sign: add the IF.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "LO above ⇒ add the IF; LO below ⇒ subtract.", "edge_cases": "High-side vs low-side injection determines the sign and also flips where the image frequency lands (image = f_sig + 2·f_IF for high-side)."}

{"core": "A modulated signal occupies a finite bandwidth that carries its sidebands (the actual information). If the receiver passband is narrower than that bandwidth, the outer sidebands are filtered out and that information is irretrievably lost — producing muffled audio or distorted/garbled data.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Narrowing bandwidth actually reduces the noise power admitted (noise scales with bandwidth), so SNR tends to improve, not degrade.", "why_tempting": "Learners associate any 'wrong' bandwidth with worse SNR, and SNR is the headline metric they remember."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Dynamic range is set by the receiver's front-end and converter (noise floor to overload point), not by the IF/audio passband width.", "why_tempting": "Dynamic range is another performance buzzword that sounds plausibly degraded by limiting the signal."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The carrier and inner sidebands still pass, so detected signal strength is largely unaffected; you lose content, not amplitude.", "why_tempting": "It feels intuitive that cutting bandwidth would 'cut down' the signal level."}], "concept": "A modulated carrier spreads energy across a bandwidth determined by the modulation: roughly B \\u2248 2(\\u0394f + f_m) for FM (Carson's rule) or B \\u2248 2\\u00d7(highest baseband freq) for AM/DSB. The sidebands are where the modulating information resides; the carrier alone carries none. Reproducing the signal faithfully requires a passband at least as wide as the occupied spectrum. Make it too wide and you let in excess noise (noise power \\u221d bandwidth, lowering SNR); make it too narrow and you truncate the sidebands, removing information you can never reconstruct. The art of receiver filtering is matching bandwidth to the signal's occupied spectrum.", "misconception": "Assuming narrower bandwidth is always beneficial because it lowers noise. It improves SNR only down to the point where it starts clipping the signal's own sidebands — beyond that you trade noise reduction for permanent loss of content.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Too wide = noisy; too narrow = no news (information lost).", "edge_cases": "For CW or very narrowband digital modes a narrow filter is desirable because the signal occupies little spectrum — 'too narrow' is relative to the signal's own bandwidth, not an absolute value."}

{"core": "The three classic indicators of communications receiver performance are sensitivity, selectivity, and dynamic range — the latter being the ratio between the weakest detectable signal and the strongest signal the receiver can handle without distortion or overload.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Fidelity (how accurately the audio reproduces the original) matters for broadcast/hi-fi receivers but is not a primary spec for communications receivers, where intelligibility, not audio quality, dominates.", "why_tempting": "Fidelity is a legitimate receiver characteristic and sounds technical, so it can pass as the 'third' metric if you don't recall the standard trio."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Volume range is just an audio amplifier/AF gain attribute, not a defining receiver performance metric.", "why_tempting": "It sounds plausibly like a measurable 'range' and pairs superficially with dynamic range."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Frequency range (tuning coverage) describes what bands a receiver can tune, not how well it performs on any given signal.", "why_tempting": "Coverage is a real and important spec, and 'range' echoes the correct answer's wording."}], "concept": "Sensitivity sets the noise floor (smallest signal recoverable, often a function of internal noise / noise figure). Selectivity is the ability to reject adjacent off-frequency signals (set by IF/filter bandwidth and shape factor). Dynamic range is the span — usually expressed in dB — between the minimum discernible signal and the maximum signal the front end can process before nonlinearities (gain compression, intermodulation, blocking) degrade reception. A receiver with poor dynamic range overloads from a strong nearby station even when the front end is otherwise sensitive. Together these three describe weak-signal performance, frequency discrimination, and strong-signal handling.", "misconception": "Assuming any plausible-sounding 'range' (volume or frequency) completes the set, when the established triad specifically pairs dynamic range with sensitivity and selectivity.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "The three S-D's of receivers: Sensitivity, Selectivity, Dynamic range.", "edge_cases": "Dynamic range is often quoted as a specific figure like 'two-tone third-order IMD dynamic range' or 'blocking dynamic range,' which are distinct measurements but all describe strong-signal handling."}

{"core": "Single sideband voice occupies roughly 2.1–3 kHz of audio bandwidth, so a 2.4 kHz filter passes the entire intelligible speech band while rejecting adjacent-channel noise and signals.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "500 Hz is far too narrow for voice; it would strip most of the speech spectrum, leaving muffled, unintelligible audio.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a reasonable 'narrow but not extreme' choice if you forget that voice needs roughly 2–3 kHz."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "250 Hz is a CW (Morse) filter — it passes only a single tone's worth of bandwidth, useless for voice.", "why_tempting": "Learners may pick the narrowest filter thinking 'narrower = cleaner,' confusing selectivity with passing enough audio."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "6 kHz is wider than needed for SSB; it admits extra adjacent noise and interference without improving voice quality.", "why_tempting": "It's wide enough to pass all the voice, so it seems 'safe' — and 6 kHz is the right ballpark for AM, which is easy to confuse with SSB."}], "concept": "Filter bandwidth should match the signal's occupied bandwidth. SSB suppresses the carrier and one sideband, transmitting only ~2.4–3 kHz of audio in a single sideband. Matching the IF filter to that width maximizes signal-to-noise ratio: too narrow (250/500 Hz, the CW filters) clips the voice; too wide (6 kHz, an AM filter) passes unnecessary noise and adjacent QRM. The 2.4 kHz filter is the standard SSB voice choice. As a rule of thumb: 250 Hz/500 Hz → CW, 2.4 kHz → SSB, 6 kHz → AM/FM-narrow.", "misconception": "That a narrower filter is always better. Filter choice must match the mode's bandwidth — narrower than the signal destroys intelligibility.", "link": "Tools — Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "CW is cramped (250/500 Hz), SSB is speech (2.4 kHz), AM is ample (6 kHz).", "edge_cases": "Some operators deliberately use a slightly narrower SSB filter (e.g. ~1.8 kHz) in heavy interference, trading some highs for selectivity — but 2.4 kHz remains the standard match."}

{"core": "For copying CW amid heavy interference, the narrowest filter—250 Hz—passes only the bandwidth needed for the Morse signal while rejecting adjacent-frequency interference, maximizing the signal-to-noise/interference ratio.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "6 kHz is wide enough for AM/SSB voice and lets in enormous amounts of adjacent interference; completely wrong for narrow-bandwidth CW in a crowded band.", "why_tempting": "Wider sounds 'better' if you mistakenly think you need to capture more of the signal."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "500 Hz is a usable CW filter but, given a 250 Hz option exists, it passes twice the bandwidth and thus more interference; not the optimum choice when interference is severe.", "why_tempting": "500 Hz is a classic CW filter width, so it looks like the 'standard' correct answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "2.4 kHz is the typical SSB voice bandwidth—far too wide for CW and admits substantial interference.", "why_tempting": "It's a common, familiar filter value and might seem like a reasonable middle ground."}], "concept": "CW (on-off keyed Morse) occupies only a narrow bandwidth—roughly a few times the keying speed in baud (B ≈ K·WPM, on the order of tens to ~150 Hz for normal speeds). Receiver bandwidth should match the signal's occupied bandwidth: narrowing the IF/audio filter cuts noise power (which scales with bandwidth) and rejects off-frequency signals. Choosing the narrowest filter that still passes the keying sidebands gives the best SNR and selectivity. Of the choices, 250 Hz is closest to optimal CW bandwidth while still passing the keying transitions cleanly.", "misconception": "Believing a wider filter is always 'safer' or that you need lots of bandwidth to hear a signal—when in fact for CW the narrowest practical filter rejects interference best.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Narrow signal → narrow filter. CW is the thinnest signal, so pick the thinnest filter.", "edge_cases": "Extremely narrow filters can cause ringing and make tuning/fast CW harder; in practice you choose the narrowest that still cleanly passes the keying speed, but for exam purposes with heavy QRM, narrowest wins."}

{"core": "CW reception uses a single audio beat note, so the optimum band-pass filter is narrow and centred on the typical CW pitch (~800 Hz). A 750–850 Hz window (~100 Hz wide) passes the tone while rejecting adjacent signals and noise.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A 1000 Hz-wide passband is far too broad for CW; it admits unnecessary noise and adjacent-signal QRM, defeating the purpose of a narrow CW filter.", "why_tempting": "It includes the common CW beat-note pitch, so it looks 'correct enough' if you forget that narrower is better for CW."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Centred near 2200 Hz, this is above the comfortable CW listening pitch and would reject the usual ~800 Hz beat note.", "why_tempting": "It is a narrow 200 Hz window like the right answer, so the width seems right even though the centre frequency is wrong."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A 300–2700 Hz passband (~2.4 kHz) is an SSB voice bandwidth, not CW; it lets through enormous amounts of noise and interference for a single tone.", "why_tempting": "This is the familiar SSB filter range, so it feels like a standard 'good' filter setting."}], "concept": "CW is detected by beating the incoming carrier against the receiver's BFO/oscillator to produce a single audio tone. Since the desired information is just one tone at the operator's preferred pitch (typically 600–800 Hz), the receiver needs only enough bandwidth to pass that tone plus the keying sidebands. CW keying sidebands are small (a few tens of Hz for typical sending speeds), so a passband of roughly 100–500 Hz is ideal. Narrowing the filter improves the signal-to-noise ratio because received noise power is proportional to bandwidth — halving bandwidth gives ~3 dB less noise. A ~100 Hz window centred on the chosen beat note maximizes this advantage while still passing the keying.", "misconception": "Assuming a wider filter is always better, or confusing CW filter width with SSB voice bandwidth (~2.4 kHz). CW wants the opposite: as narrow as practical around one tone.", "link": "Tools — Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "CW = one tone = narrow filter; centre it where you like to hear the beat (~800 Hz).", "edge_cases": "Very fast CW or high-speed machine keying needs slightly more bandwidth to avoid clipping the keying sidebands, but for normal hand-keyed CW a narrow filter around the beat note is optimum."}

{"core": "Chirp is a momentary shift in a CW transmitter's output frequency that occurs each time a key-down element (dit or dah) is sent, heard as a frequency wobble at the start of each character element.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A slow drift as the oscillator warms up is called frequency drift, not chirp — it happens over minutes, not per keying element.", "why_tempting": "Both involve unwanted frequency change in the transmitter, so they are easy to confuse if you only remember 'frequency moves.'"}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Audio overload on receiving CW is a receiver-side problem unrelated to a transmitter's keying-induced frequency shift.", "why_tempting": "It mentions CW and a defect, which sounds plausible for a CW signal artifact."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A steady high-pitched tone accompanying each element describes a key click or a parasitic/beat tone, not the per-element frequency change that defines chirp.", "why_tempting": "It correctly ties the symptom to each dit and dah, but describes the wrong physical effect (an added tone, not a frequency shift)."}], "concept": "When a keyed oscillator (or one whose load/supply changes with keying) is switched, the loading on the oscillator stage and the supply voltage can momentarily shift, pulling the resonant frequency before it settles. The result is a small frequency glide at the leading edge of each element — the ear perceives it as a 'chirp.' Root causes include poor supply regulation under keying load, the keyed stage directly affecting the oscillator (rather than keying a buffer/amplifier downstream), and inadequate isolation between oscillator and keyed stages. Cures: key a later stage so the oscillator runs continuously, improve voltage regulation, and add buffer amplifiers.", "misconception": "Confusing chirp (per-element, fast, keying-correlated) with drift (slow, thermal) — both are transmitter frequency instabilities but have different time scales and causes.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Chirp = Change per Char­acter; Drift = Drift over time."}

{"core": "Chirp is a small shift in the oscillator's frequency that occurs each time the key is pressed, usually because keying loads the power supply and its voltage sags. Holding the supply voltages rock-steady under load keeps the oscillator frequency constant, eliminating chirp.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter suppresses harmonics and out-of-band emissions; it does nothing to stabilize the oscillator frequency during keying.", "why_tempting": "It's a real transmitter cure-all in other contexts, so it sounds like good RF hygiene."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A key-click filter shapes the rise/fall time of the keying envelope to reduce the broadband 'clicks' from hard switching — a different defect than chirp.", "why_tempting": "Both chirp and clicks are CW keying defects, so the terms get conflated."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "While oscillator loading matters, the standard answer targets the dominant cause — supply-voltage variation under keying load — not the load impedance itself; chirp is fixed by keeping the supply steady (often by keying a later stage or regulating the oscillator's supply).", "why_tempting": "It correctly names the oscillator and 'steady,' and impedance does influence frequency, making it look like the precise technical answer."}], "concept": "An oscillator's frequency depends on its tank/feedback elements, but in practice it also drifts with supply voltage because device junction capacitances and operating point vary with DC bias. When the key closes, downstream stages draw current; an unregulated or poorly regulated supply sags, the oscillator's DC voltage shifts, and its frequency jumps momentarily before settling — heard as a chirp. The fix is to decouple the oscillator from keying transients: key a later (buffer/PA) stage rather than the oscillator, run the oscillator continuously, and/or regulate its supply so its voltage stays constant regardless of load.", "misconception": "Confusing chirp (a keying-induced frequency shift) with key clicks (keying-induced bandwidth from abrupt switching), and reaching for filters when the real cause is supply-voltage instability.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Chirp = Changing supply; Clicks = abrupt Cutoff edges.", "edge_cases": "Keeping the oscillator running and keying a downstream stage is the classic practical implementation of 'steady supply' — it sidesteps the keyed-load voltage swing entirely."}

{"core": "A variable frequency oscillator (VFO) can be tuned continuously across a range, so the operating frequency is no longer fixed to whatever discrete crystal frequencies happen to be on hand.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Keying speed is determined by the keyer/operator and the transmitter's keying circuit rise/fall behaviour, not by whether the frequency source is a VFO or a crystal.", "why_tempting": "'Variable' sounds like flexibility, which a learner may loosely associate with faster sending."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Key clicks come from too-abrupt rise/fall times in the keying envelope; they're cured by waveform shaping, not by the type of oscillator.", "why_tempting": "It's a real CW transmitter problem, so it seems like a plausible 'advantage' to attach to a better oscillator."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Harmonic suppression is a function of output filtering (low-pass/pi-network) and amplifier linearity; a VFO can actually be spectrally noisier than a clean crystal.", "why_tempting": "VFOs are 'fancier', so a learner may assume they improve spectral purity too."}], "concept": "A crystal oscillator resonates at one mechanically-fixed frequency (or close to it with pulling). To change bands or move within a band you'd need a separate crystal for each spot frequency. A VFO uses a tunable LC tank (or equivalent), allowing the operator to set any frequency in the tuning range continuously — essential for netting, calling on any clear spot, or chasing a station that has drifted. The trade-off is stability: crystals offer excellent frequency stability with low phase noise, while early/simple VFOs require careful design (temperature compensation, regulated supply, mechanical rigidity) to avoid drift and chirp.", "misconception": "Assuming a 'better' oscillator improves unrelated transmitter qualities (clicks, harmonics, speed). The VFO's sole advantage here is frequency agility, not signal cleanliness.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance"}

{"core": "Amplitude modulation (AM) encodes information by varying the instantaneous amplitude (envelope) of the RF carrier in proportion to the modulating signal, while the carrier frequency stays constant.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "FM varies the carrier's instantaneous frequency, not its amplitude; the envelope ideally stays constant.", "why_tempting": "FM is the other ubiquitous analogue scheme, so a learner may pick it by familiarity without parsing 'amplitude'."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Phase modulation varies the instantaneous phase of the carrier; amplitude is unchanged. (PM and FM are closely related angle-modulation forms.)", "why_tempting": "PM also conveys information by altering a carrier parameter, and the term sounds technical and plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "FSK shifts the carrier between discrete frequencies to represent digital states — it is a form of frequency, not amplitude, keying.", "why_tempting": "It contains 'shift keying' and is associated with data modes, which can read as 'changes the wave.'"}], "concept": "Any sinusoidal carrier A·cos(2πf·t + φ) has three modulable parameters: amplitude A, frequency f, and phase φ. AM applies the message to A, producing a spectrum of carrier plus upper and lower sidebands at f±f_m, occupying roughly 2×(highest audio frequency) of bandwidth. FM and PM (angle modulation) act on f and φ respectively and are mathematically linked (PM of the integral of a signal equals FM). FSK is digital angle modulation. The key discriminator in this question is which parameter is varied.", "misconception": "Confusing modes that 'carry information' generally with the specific parameter being varied — the question is purely about which scheme manipulates amplitude.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "AM = Amplitude Moves; FM = Frequency Moves; PM = Phase Moves.", "edge_cases": "SSB and CW are derived from/related to AM concepts, but only standard AM is described by 'changes the amplitude' here."}

{"core": "In amplitude modulation (AM) the carrier's instantaneous amplitude (its envelope) follows the modulating audio waveform directly — louder audio means a larger envelope swing.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "FSK shifts the carrier frequency between discrete values; the amplitude stays essentially constant, so the envelope does not track the audio.", "why_tempting": "It is a recognizable digital mode and 'shift' may sound like the signal is changing in a way that affects amplitude."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "In FM the instantaneous frequency varies with the modulating signal while the envelope amplitude remains constant — that constant-amplitude property is exactly why FM is favoured for limiter/discriminator receivers.", "why_tempting": "FM is a very common voice mode, and a learner may confuse 'modulation varies the signal' with amplitude variation."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Pulse modulation encodes information in pulse parameters (width, position, amplitude of discrete pulses), not in a continuous envelope tracking the audio of a CW carrier.", "why_tempting": "Pulse amplitude modulation does involve amplitude, so the word association can mislead."}], "concept": "For a carrier A·cos(ωc·t), AM produces (A + m(t))·cos(ωc·t), where m(t) is the audio. The bracketed term is the envelope — it literally is the audio riding on the carrier, which is what an envelope (diode) detector recovers. Contrast with angle modulation (FM/PM), written A·cos(ωc·t + φ(t)), where the leading A is fixed and the information lives in the phase/frequency term φ(t); the envelope is therefore constant. This distinction is the basis for choosing receiver detector types and explains FM's noise-immunity (a limiter can clip amplitude variations without losing data).", "misconception": "Assuming any 'modulation' alters the amplitude. Only amplitude-type modes vary the envelope; angle-modulation modes (FM, PM, FSK) keep the envelope constant and vary frequency or phase instead.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "AM = Amplitude Moves; FM = Frequency Moves, amplitude flat.", "edge_cases": "SSB is a form of AM (suppressed carrier) where the envelope follows the audio; FSK is effectively two-tone FM, hence constant-amplitude."}

{"core": "Morse code (CW) is sent by keying the transmitter on and off, producing an interrupted carrier — the presence of the carrier is a 'mark' (dot/dash) and its absence is a 'space'.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Phase-shifted keying (PSK) is a distinct digital mode where information rides on phase changes of a continuous carrier; it is not how traditional Morse/CW is transmitted.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a legitimate modulation method, and learners who know about PSK31 may conflate it with hand-keyed Morse."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Key clicks are an undesired artifact — broadband transients caused by too-abrupt rise/fall of the keyed envelope — not the intended signal. Proper CW shaping minimizes them.", "why_tempting": "You do hear clicks on poorly designed transmitters, so it seems plausible that clicks ARE the signal."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A truly continuous (unkeyed) carrier conveys no information — it's just a steady tone. Information requires switching it on and off.", "why_tempting": "CW literally stands for 'continuous wave,' which misleads people into thinking the carrier runs continuously."}], "concept": "CW transmission is On-Off Keying (OOK) of an unmodulated carrier. The RF source runs at a fixed frequency, and the key gates it: carrier present = element (dit/dah), carrier absent = gap. The receiver's BFO heterodynes this against a local oscillator so the interruptions become an audible tone. To avoid splatter (key clicks), the keying envelope is shaped with finite rise/fall times rather than instantaneous switching, since fast transitions generate wide sidebands.", "misconception": "The term 'continuous wave' (CW) tricks learners into picking 'continuous carrier' — but CW refers to the carrier being a continuous, unmodulated sine wave when keyed ON, not to it running uninterrupted.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "CW = carrier switched on and off — the 'continuous' wave is interrupted to make the code.", "edge_cases": "Key clicks (option C) are the failure mode of poor keying-envelope shaping; good CW keeps rise/fall times around a few milliseconds to keep the occupied bandwidth narrow."}

{"core": "Conventional AM (double-sideband full-carrier) produces an upper and a lower sideband, each extending out to the highest modulating frequency. With a 3 kHz top audio frequency the total occupied bandwidth is 2 × 3 kHz = 6 kHz.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "9 kHz would be three times the audio frequency, which has no basis in AM sideband geometry.", "why_tempting": "Multiplying by 3 looks plausible if you confuse the '3 kHz' value with a multiplier rather than the sideband edge."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "3 kHz is the audio bandwidth (and the width of one sideband), not the RF bandwidth — it ignores the second sideband entirely.", "why_tempting": "It is the number given in the question, so it feels like the answer if you forget AM is double-sideband."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "12 kHz is four times the audio frequency; AM only doubles it, not quadruples.", "why_tempting": "Doubling twice (or confusing with some wider mode) gives this incorrect figure."}], "concept": "In amplitude modulation the carrier at f_c is multiplied by the audio, generating sum and difference products: f_c + f_audio (upper sideband) and f_c − f_audio (lower sideband). The spectrum therefore spans from f_c − f_max to f_c + f_max, a total width of BW = 2 × f_max. Here BW = 2 × 3 kHz = 6 kHz. SSB suppresses one sideband (and the carrier) to occupy only ~f_max, which is why SSB is roughly half the bandwidth of AM for the same audio.", "misconception": "Treating the modulating (audio) bandwidth as the transmitted RF bandwidth, forgetting that AM is double-sideband and mirrors the audio on both sides of the carrier.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "AM = Audio Multiplied by two: BW = 2 × highest audio frequency.", "edge_cases": "This 2×f_max rule is specific to AM/DSB. Single-sideband (SSB) occupies about f_max, and FM bandwidth follows Carson's rule, not this simple doubling."}

{"core": "A standard AM signal consists of the unmodulated carrier plus two sidebands (upper and lower), so all three components occupy the signal's bandwidth.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Conventional AM produces BOTH an upper and lower sideband, not just one — single-sideband (SSB) suppresses a sideband, but that is a different mode.", "why_tempting": "Learners confuse AM with SSB, where indeed only a carrier (often reduced) and one sideband remain."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "AM retains the carrier; the sidebands carry the information but the carrier is what makes it 'amplitude modulation' rather than DSB-suppressed-carrier.", "why_tempting": "Since the audio info lives in the sidebands, one might forget the carrier still consumes bandwidth and power."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "AM is not single-sideband; both sidebands are present, plus the carrier.", "why_tempting": "Confusion with SSB transmission, which is more efficient and common on HF voice."}], "concept": "When a carrier of frequency fc is amplitude-modulated by an audio tone fm, the product creates sum and difference frequencies: fc + fm (upper sideband) and fc - fm (lower sideband), with the carrier fc unchanged in between. The occupied bandwidth therefore spans from fc - fm(max) to fc + fm(max), giving BW = 2 × fm(max). The carrier sits at the centre and the two sidebands are mirror images carrying identical information.", "misconception": "Believing AM bandwidth contains only the information-bearing sidebands, or conflating full-carrier AM with SSB which suppresses both the carrier and one sideband.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "AM = All Members: one carrier + two sidebands.", "edge_cases": "This describes conventional double-sideband full-carrier AM. SSB suppresses one sideband (and largely the carrier), and DSB-SC suppresses just the carrier — both reduce occupied spectrum but are distinct emission types."}

{"core": "An RF oscillator's frequency is set by its resonant network (LC, crystal, etc.); electrical and mechanical stability keep those component values constant so the output frequency stays fixed rather than wandering off over time or with vibration/temperature.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Distortion relates to amplifier linearity and waveform purity, not to the time-stability of the oscillating frequency.", "why_tempting": "Distortion is a general 'bad signal' concern, so it sounds like something stability would prevent."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Overmodulation is a property of the modulator/audio stage driving a transmitter, not of the oscillator's mechanical or electrical stability.", "why_tempting": "It's another transmitter fault, and learners may lump all signal-quality issues together."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Key clicks come from abrupt keying (steep rise/fall times) in CW transmission and are cured by waveform shaping, not by oscillator stability.", "why_tempting": "Key clicks are an unwanted emission, which feels related to a 'clean' oscillator."}], "concept": "Oscillation frequency is fixed by the frequency-determining network — for an LC oscillator f = 1/(2\\u03c0\\u221a(LC)). Anything that changes L or C changes f. Mechanical vibration physically alters spacing/dimensions of inductors and capacitor plates; thermal expansion and temperature coefficients shift component values; supply-voltage variations shift junction capacitances and operating points. 'Electrically and mechanically stable' means designing against all of these (temperature-compensated components, rigid construction, regulated supply, buffering) so f stays constant — i.e. it does not drift.", "misconception": "Confusing the oscillator's job (generating a precise, steady reference frequency) with downstream transmitter problems like modulation depth, keying waveform, or amplifier distortion. Stability is specifically about frequency constancy.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Stable oscillator = no DRIFT (Drift Resists with Insulation, Firmness, Temperature control).", "edge_cases": "Crystal oscillators are far more frequency-stable than LC types, but even crystals drift slightly with temperature unless oven-controlled (OCXO) or compensated (TCXO)."}

{"core": "Conservation of energy: the 75 W difference between 200 W DC input and 125 W RF output is lost in the active device and circuit as heat (resistive and switching losses).", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Efficiency is a ratio (output/input), not a consumer of power; the lost power doesn't 'create' efficiency—it lowers it.", "why_tempting": "The numbers do define an efficiency of 62.5%, so the word 'efficiency' feels relevant."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Negative feedback is a signal-routing technique that shapes gain/linearity; it doesn't account for bulk DC power loss in the final stage.", "why_tempting": "Feedback is a real amplifier concept, so it sounds plausible to a learner who knows amplifier theory."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Positive feedback would tend toward oscillation, not power dissipation, and again is a signal concept, not an energy sink.", "why_tempting": "Same trap as C—familiar amplifier jargon dressed up as a power-balance answer."}], "concept": "Power balance: P_DC = P_RF + P_loss. Here 200 W − 125 W = 75 W dissipated, almost entirely as heat in the final device (junction/conduction losses) plus a little in matching and bias circuitry. Efficiency η = P_RF / P_DC = 125/200 = 62.5%, typical of a linear (Class AB) final stage. The dissipated power is why finals need heatsinks.", "misconception": "Treating 'efficiency' or 'feedback' as places power 'goes,' rather than recognizing that any input power not converted to RF output must leave the device as heat (energy conservation).", "link": "Tools — \"Ohm's Law & Power\"", "mnemonic": "In = Out + Heat.", "edge_cases": "A tiny fraction of the missing power may radiate as harmonics or be lost in matching networks, but on the exam the answer is simply heat."}

{"core": "An amplifier is never 100% efficient; the DC power that doesn't become RF output is dissipated within the active device and circuitry as heat (P_loss = P_DC,in − P_RF,out).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Oscillations are an unwanted instability, not the normal accounting for the input–output power gap, and they don't represent the dissipated energy.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a plausible 'where the energy went' answer to someone who associates amplifiers with feedback/oscillation problems."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Only the RF output power reaches the antenna and radiates; the lost power never makes it out of the amplifier as RF.", "why_tempting": "Conflates total power with radiated power, but the radiated portion IS the RF output, not the difference."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Feedline loss is a separate mechanism downstream of the amplifier and is part of the RF output path, not the DC-to-RF conversion deficit.", "why_tempting": "Transmission lines do dissipate some power, so it seems like a place energy is lost."}], "concept": "Energy must balance: P_DC,in = P_RF,out + P_dissipated. Efficiency η = P_RF,out / P_DC,in. For Class A amps η ≈ 25–30%, Class AB ~50–60%, Class C up to ~70–80%. The shortfall (1−η)·P_DC,in is converted to heat in the transistor/tube junction and bias network, which is why power devices need heat sinks and forced cooling. None of this energy is destroyed — it simply takes the thermal path rather than the RF path.", "misconception": "That the 'missing' power must go somewhere useful or escape via the antenna/feedline, rather than being an inherent thermal loss inside the amplifier itself.", "link": "Tools — Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Input = Output + Heat — the amplifier keeps its 'cut' as warmth.", "edge_cases": "Higher-efficiency classes (C, D, E, F) reduce but never eliminate this heat; even switching amplifiers have conduction and switching losses dissipated thermally."}

{"core": "Excessive microphone gain overdrives the SSB transmitter into flat-topping (clipping), generating splatter — spurious sideband products spread across adjacent frequencies that interfere with nearby stations.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Overdriving doesn't selectively shift energy to lower bands; splatter appears immediately adjacent to the operating frequency, not on entirely different bands.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible because intermodulation is sometimes loosely associated with band-wide problems."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Audio overdrive in an RF transmitter produces RF splatter, not 'digital interference' to computers — that's an unrelated mechanism (RFI/EMI).", "why_tempting": "Mixes the familiar idea of RFI to electronics with the audio-gain scenario."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Harmonics are integer multiples of the carrier produced by nonlinearity in the final/output stages, not by excessive audio drive; harmonics land on far-distant frequencies, not adjacent ones.", "why_tempting": "Clipping is a nonlinearity, and learners associate any distortion with harmonics — but here the distortion is in the modulation envelope, producing adjacent splatter."}], "concept": "In an SSB transmitter the speech signal is mixed/balanced-modulated to produce a single sideband. The system is linear only over a limited input range. If mic gain pushes the audio (and thus the modulator/linear PA) beyond its linear region, the envelope clips. Clipping is equivalent to introducing harmonic and intermodulation products of the audio tones; because SSB simply translates audio frequencies up to RF, those new high-order products appear as RF energy extending well beyond the intended ~2.7 kHz bandwidth. The result is 'splatter' — emissions on frequencies adjacent to the transmit channel, degrading the occupied bandwidth and interfering with stations close in frequency. The fix is to set mic gain so ALC just begins to act on voice peaks, keeping the PA in its linear region.", "misconception": "Confusing in-channel/adjacent-channel splatter (caused by audio overdrive in the linear chain) with harmonic emissions (integer multiples on other bands, caused by output-stage nonlinearity).", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Too much mic = too much 'splat' — splatter spreads sideways onto neighbours, not up onto harmonics.", "edge_cases": "ALC limits average overdrive but won't fully prevent transient clipping if gain is grossly excessive; speech processing/compression must also be set conservatively to avoid the same splatter."}

{"core": "Excessive speech processing (heavy compression/clipping) raises the average modulation level and flattens audio peaks, generating distortion products and out-of-channel sidebands (splatter) that spread into adjacent frequencies.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Speech processing is an audio-stage function on the SSB signal; it does not produce broadband digital noise targeting computers.", "why_tempting": "Learners associate 'interference' loosely with any nearby electronics, but splatter is RF spectral spreading, not digital hash."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "It is the opposite effect — too much processing drives modulation too hard, raising average level, not reducing it. Also, SSB suppresses the carrier, so 'modulation of the carrier' is itself a misframing.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a plausible 'something goes wrong with modulation' answer, and reverses the real cause."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Splatter appears in the immediate adjacent-frequency region of the same band, not on an entirely different higher band.", "why_tempting": "Distortion does create harmonics/spurs, so a learner may overgeneralize to other bands rather than nearby frequencies."}], "concept": "A clipper or aggressive compressor sharpens the audio waveform, and any sharp transition is rich in high-frequency harmonics. In an SSB transmitter the modulating audio is mixed up to RF, so harmonic and intermodulation products from over-processed audio translate directly into RF sidebands beyond the intended ~2.5–3 kHz voice passband. The result is occupied-bandwidth growth: energy 'splatters' into adjacent channels. Some processing is beneficial — it raises talk power (higher average-to-peak ratio) for better readability — but the right amount keeps products inside the passband. The trade-off is intelligibility/talk-power versus spectral cleanliness.", "misconception": "Believing more processing always means a louder, better signal, ignoring that over-processing trades bandwidth and linearity for level, producing distortion and adjacent-frequency interference.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Over-process = over-spill: too much punch turns into splatter.", "edge_cases": "Moderate, properly adjusted speech processing is legitimate and improves communication effectiveness; the problem is only when set too high."}

{"core": "Peak envelope power (PEP) is precisely defined as the average power supplied to the antenna over one complete RF cycle taken at the crest (peak) of the modulation envelope — option C.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Average radio frequency power\" is not a standardized term; it would imply averaging over the entire modulation cycle, not just at the envelope crest.", "why_tempting": "PEP does involve averaging power over an RF cycle, so seeing 'average' and 'radio frequency power' looks plausible if you miss the 'crest of the modulation envelope' qualifier."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Input power refers to DC power into the final stage, not the RF output averaged at the envelope peak — and 'peak transmitter input' isn't the standard measure of transmitter capability for SSB.", "why_tempting": "It contains the word 'peak' and 'transmitter,' and older regulations did use input-power limits, so it feels regulatory and correct."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "RMS describes an effective steady-state value of a continuous waveform; it does not capture the peak of a varying modulation envelope, and 'RMS power' is itself a loosely-used (technically sloppy) term.", "why_tempting": "RMS is the proper way to compute average power from voltage/current, so an electronics-strong learner may reflexively pick it."}], "concept": "For an amplitude-varying mode like SSB, the instantaneous RF amplitude rises and falls with the modulating signal. PEP isolates the worst-case loading: take the single RF cycle occurring at the highest modulation peak, and compute the average power over just that one cycle. Mathematically, for a sinusoidal RF cycle at the envelope crest, P_PEP = V_peak²/(2R) = V_rms(of that cycle)²/R. The key distinction is the two-level averaging: you average over ONE RF cycle (fast), but you select the cycle at the SLOW modulation envelope's crest. This is the figure transmitter power limits and output specs are referenced to because it represents the maximum sustained-cycle power the PA and antenna must handle.", "misconception": "Conflating the fast RF cycle with the slow modulation envelope — students either average over the whole envelope (giving a lower 'average' number) or pick RMS of the carrier, missing that PEP combines a per-RF-cycle average evaluated specifically at the modulation peak.", "link": "Tools — \"Ohm's Law & Power\"", "mnemonic": "PEP = Per-cycle average at the Envelope's Peak.", "edge_cases": "For an unmodulated carrier (CW key-down), the envelope is flat, so PEP equals the steady average carrier power — the distinction only matters once amplitude modulation is present."}

{"core": "A typical SSB voice signal carries audio roughly from 300 Hz to 2.7–3 kHz, so its occupied bandwidth falls in the 2–3 kHz range.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "2 kHz is too narrow to pass the full speech band needed for natural intelligibility; it would clip higher voice frequencies.", "why_tempting": "It's near the lower edge of the real range, so it looks plausible if you only recall the rough magnitude."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "3–6 kHz overstates SSB bandwidth; that range is more typical of an AM signal (which carries two sidebands) or wide FM, not single sideband.", "why_tempting": "Doubling the audio bandwidth feels right if you confuse SSB with double-sideband AM."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "1 kHz is far too narrow for voice — it would only pass a fraction of the speech spectrum, more like a CW or very narrow data channel.", "why_tempting": "Sounds 'efficient' and SSB is known for being bandwidth-efficient, so a small number is enticing."}], "concept": "SSB transmits only one sideband and suppresses the carrier and the opposite sideband. The occupied bandwidth equals the highest audio frequency passed, since each audio component maps to one RF component offset from the (suppressed) carrier. Communications-grade voice uses an audio passband of about 300 Hz to 2.7–3 kHz, giving roughly 2.4–3 kHz of RF bandwidth. Compare DSB-AM, which transmits both sidebands and thus needs about twice this (~6 kHz).", "misconception": "Confusing SSB (one sideband) with AM (two sidebands). SSB's whole advantage is occupying about half the bandwidth of AM for the same audio.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Single SideBand ≈ Speech Bandwidth (2–3 kHz); AM doubles it.", "edge_cases": "Narrower 'communications' SSB filters (~1.8 kHz) or wider 'extended SSB' setups exist, but the exam's 'usual' value is 2–3 kHz."}

{"core": "An SSB signal carries voice information as continuously varying amplitude. A linear amplifier reproduces these amplitude variations faithfully; a non-linear stage distorts the envelope and creates intermodulation products, garbling the audio and splattering into adjacent channels.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Linearity has nothing to do with hum or noise reduction; those come from supply filtering, shielding, and good grounding.", "why_tempting": "Linear amplification sounds like a general 'clean signal' quality, so a learner may associate it with lower noise."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Linear amplifiers actually draw current that tracks the voice envelope, so supply demand is anything but constant — a Class C non-linear stage is steadier in some respects.", "why_tempting": "It plausibly mixes 'linear' with 'regulated/steady,' which is a wrong but superficially related idea."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A linear amp must preserve voice-peak amplitude variations, not reduce them; reducing them would destroy the modulation. ALC limits peaks, but that is not the reason for linearity.", "why_tempting": "It uses the correct keyword 'voice peaks' and sounds like it protects the amplifier, baiting the half-informed."}], "concept": "In SSB the transmitted RF envelope is a linear analog of the speech waveform — both amplitude and phase of the modulating signal map onto the carrier sideband. The amplifier transfer function must be a straight line (Vout = A·Vin) across the dynamic range. Any curvature introduces harmonic and intermodulation distortion; with two test tones f1 and f2 you get 2f1−f2 and 2f2−f1 third-order products that fall in-band, both distorting recovered audio and widening occupied bandwidth ('splatter'). This forces Class A, AB, or B operation. Class C, fine for constant-envelope FM/CW, clips the envelope and is unusable for SSB.", "misconception": "That 'linear' refers to a constant or smoothed power output. In fact linearity means faithfully preserving the varying envelope — the opposite of smoothing it.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "SSB = Same Shape, Bigger — the amplifier enlarges the envelope without changing its shape.", "edge_cases": "FM and CW have constant-amplitude envelopes, so they tolerate efficient non-linear (Class C) amplifiers; only amplitude-varying modes like SSB and AM require linearity."}

{"core": "Suppressing the carrier in a double sideband signal means none of the transmitter's power is wasted on a component that carries no information; all available power goes into the two sidebands, which actually contain the modulating intelligence.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Halving the bandwidth describes single sideband (SSB), where one sideband is also removed. DSB suppressed carrier still transmits both sidebands, so the bandwidth is unchanged.", "why_tempting": "It's easy to confuse carrier suppression alone with full SSB, which combines carrier suppression AND sideband removal."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Modulation percentage and distortion are characteristics of the AM modulator and audio chain; removing the carrier doesn't improve the achievable modulation depth or linearity.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a plausible 'cleaner signal' benefit, conflating power efficiency with modulation quality."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The opposite is true: receiving a suppressed-carrier signal requires reinserting a carrier (a product detector/BFO), so the receiver is more complex, not simpler.", "why_tempting": "One might assume that transmitting less means the receiver has less to do, but the missing carrier must be locally regenerated."}], "concept": "In a full-carrier AM signal, the unmodulated carrier consumes at least half of the total transmitter power even at 100% modulation, where each sideband carries only 1/4 of the total. The carrier conveys no information — only the sidebands do. By suppressing the carrier, that power budget is redirected entirely into the sidebands, giving a more effective use of the PA's output for the same total power, or equivalently the same intelligence with far less radiated power. This is the efficiency motivation that leads first to DSB-SC and ultimately to SSB.", "misconception": "That carrier suppression by itself reduces bandwidth. Bandwidth reduction comes from removing a SIDEBAND (SSB), not the carrier; suppressing the carrier is purely a power-efficiency measure.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Carrier = no info, all the power; sidebands = all info, little power. Kill the carrier, feed the sidebands.", "edge_cases": "DSB-SC still occupies the same ~2x audio bandwidth as conventional AM; only when one sideband is also stripped (SSB) does bandwidth halve."}

{"core": "Overmodulating an SSB transmitter drives the audio/RF stages into nonlinear regions, generating harmonic and intermodulation products that distort the signal and spread it across more bandwidth than the intended ~2.4–3 kHz voice channel.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Overmodulation does not extend usable range; the extra spectral content is splatter, not coherent signal power in the wanted channel.", "why_tempting": "Operators sometimes assume 'more modulation = louder = farther,' confusing distortion products with effective signal strength."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "It does the opposite — overmodulation widens occupied bandwidth, it doesn't narrow it.", "why_tempting": "The 'poor high frequency response' phrase sounds plausibly tied to audio problems, masking the inverted bandwidth claim."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Distortion products degrade fidelity and add interference; SNR in the wanted channel is worse, not better.", "why_tempting": "It mistakenly equates aggressive modulation with a stronger, cleaner signal."}], "concept": "In SSB the transmitter's linear amplifier chain must operate within its linear region. When audio drive exceeds the design level, the active devices clip or saturate; their transfer function becomes nonlinear and produces intermodulation distortion (IMD). Two-tone testing reveals these as odd-order products (3rd, 5th, etc.) at frequencies 2f1−f2, 2f2−f1, … that fall just outside the intended passband. These 'splatter' sidebands radiate adjacent to your channel, causing interference and widening the occupied bandwidth well beyond the nominal voice channel. The remedy is proper ALC/mic gain so peaks stay within the linear range.", "misconception": "Believing that pushing modulation harder improves a signal — in reality SSB linearity is paramount, and overdriving trades fidelity and spectral cleanliness for distortion and splatter.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Over-modulate = over-spread (splatter into neighbours).", "edge_cases": "This 'wider bandwidth from overmodulation' applies to all linear analog modes (SSB, AM); the mechanism is amplifier nonlinearity, not the modulation type per se."}

{"core": "On SSB you set mic gain so that voice peaks just reach—but never exceed—the upper limit of the ALC (Automatic Level Control) range, giving maximum clean output without flat-topping and splatter.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Driving the ALC meter to full deflection means the transmitter is being grossly overdriven, producing distortion and adjacent-channel splatter.", "why_tempting": "More ALC action seems like 'using all the power,' and full-scale readings feel like maximum performance."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Frequency deviation is an FM concept; SSB is an amplitude-modulation derivative and has no deviation to set.", "why_tempting": "'100%' and 'voice peaks' sound like proper full modulation if you confuse SSB with FM."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A dip in collector/drain current is a tuning indicator for a resonant amplifier stage, not a microphone-gain criterion.", "why_tempting": "The plate/collector dip is a real adjustment elsewhere, so it can be misremembered as relevant here."}], "concept": "ALC sensing develops a feedback voltage when the drive level approaches the linear PA's clipping point and reduces gain to prevent overdrive. The goal on SSB is to operate the PA in its linear region: peaks should swing the ALC meter up to, but not beyond, its specified range. Beyond that, the envelope flat-tops, generating intermodulation products that spread energy into adjacent channels (splatter). Setting gain so peaks just kiss the top of the ALC range maximizes talk power (peak envelope power) while keeping the signal clean.", "misconception": "That maximizing ALC meter movement maximizes useful output—overdriving an SSB linear actually degrades signal quality, not power.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "ALC: 'Approach the Limit, don't Cross it.'", "edge_cases": "Different rigs mark the ALC zone differently—follow the manufacturer's recommended ALC range rather than a fixed needle position."}

{"core": "A balanced modulator multiplies the audio and carrier so the carrier component cancels (is suppressed) at its output, leaving only the upper and lower sidebands — this is double-sideband suppressed-carrier (DSB-SC).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Single-sideband selection happens AFTER the balanced modulator, done by a filter or phasing network. The balanced modulator alone outputs both sidebands.", "why_tempting": "Because the end goal is SSB, learners assume the balanced modulator does the whole job; in reality it only does the carrier-suppression half."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The sidebands are not phased 180° apart by intent; carrier suppression results from cancellation of the carrier within the mixer, not a deliberate sideband phase relationship.", "why_tempting": "It mentions 180° out of phase, which sounds like the cancellation mechanism — but that cancellation applies to the carrier feedthrough, not the sidebands."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Percentage of modulation is an AM concept; SSB has no carrier to reference, so constant modulation percentage is irrelevant and not the balanced modulator's function.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a transmitter quality-control feature, which a careless reader might attribute to a 'modulator'."}], "concept": "A balanced modulator is a multiplier: output ∝ cos(ω_c t)·cos(ω_a t). Trig expansion gives ½[cos((ω_c+ω_a)t) + cos((ω_c−ω_a)t)] — pure sum and difference frequencies (the two sidebands) with NO term at ω_c. The 'balance' refers to symmetric circuitry (e.g. a diode ring or Gilbert cell) that lets the modulating signal and carrier cancel the carrier component while the product terms survive. To get true SSB you then strip one sideband with a steep filter (filter method) or cancel it via quadrature phasing (phasing method).", "misconception": "Conflating the full SSB generation chain with the single stage. The balanced modulator produces DSB-SC; sideband removal is a separate, downstream operation.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Balanced = Both sidebands, no carrier. The filter that follows picks One.", "edge_cases": "A perfectly balanced modulator still leaves residual carrier in practice; circuits include a carrier-null trimmer to minimize it."}

{"core": "In lower sideband (LSB), the transmitted RF appears below the suppressed carrier frequency by the audio modulating frequency. 7100 kHz − 1 kHz = 7099 kHz.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "7101 kHz is 1 kHz above the carrier — that's the upper sideband result, not lower sideband.", "why_tempting": "Correct arithmetic but wrong direction; easy to confuse which sideband sits above vs. below the carrier."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "6100 kHz subtracts 1000 kHz instead of 1000 Hz — a unit error of three orders of magnitude.", "why_tempting": "Tempting if you mistreat the 1000 Hz tone as 1000 kHz."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "8100 kHz adds 1000 kHz — wrong direction and wrong magnitude.", "why_tempting": "Same unit confusion as C, but adding instead of subtracting."}], "concept": "In an SSB transmitter the carrier is suppressed and only one sideband is transmitted. Modulating a carrier f_c with an audio tone f_a produces sum and difference products at f_c + f_a and f_c − f_a. USB keeps the upper product (f_c + f_a); LSB keeps the lower product (f_c − f_a). Here f_c = 7100 kHz and f_a = 1000 Hz = 1 kHz, so LSB transmits at 7100 − 1 = 7099 kHz. Note the carrier frequency is the dial/reference frequency even though the carrier itself is not radiated.", "misconception": "Believing the radiated RF sits exactly at the displayed 'operating' frequency, or mixing up which sideband lies above versus below the suppressed carrier.", "link": "Tools — Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "LSB = Lower → subtract; USB = Upper → add.", "edge_cases": "A real voice signal occupies a band of audio frequencies (e.g. 300–2700 Hz), so an LSB transmission actually occupies a range of frequencies below the carrier, not a single tone."}

{"core": "ALC monitors the transmitter's output (typically the PA drive level) and feeds back a control voltage that reduces the modulator/IF gain on audio peaks, preventing the final amplifier from being overdriven into flat-topping.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "ALC has nothing to do with acoustic or audio feedback (howl); that's a microphone/gain-staging problem.", "why_tempting": "The word 'feedback' appears in both ALC (a control feedback loop) and audio feedback, inviting confusion between the two unrelated uses."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "ALC's job is precisely the opposite — by limiting peaks it prevents the splatter and harmonic generation that would *widen* the emission, so it tends to keep bandwidth in check.", "why_tempting": "Overdriving an SSB transmitter does increase occupied bandwidth, so a learner who reverses cause and effect may pick this."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "ALC controls gain on signal peaks; it does not affect the noise floor or system noise figure.", "why_tempting": "Any 'control' circuit sounds like it might clean up the signal, and noise reduction is a generically attractive benefit."}], "concept": "In an SSB transmitter the linear PA must stay in its linear region. If audio peaks drive the chain too hard, the PA clips ('flat-tops'), generating intermodulation products that spread energy into adjacent frequencies (splatter). ALC samples the drive or output level, rectifies it to a DC control voltage, and applies that as negative feedback to an earlier gain stage (IF amplifier or modulator), pulling gain down only when peaks exceed the threshold. It's effectively a fast AGC on the transmit side — a peak limiter that keeps the PA operating linearly while leaving normal-level audio untouched.", "misconception": "Thinking ALC processes audio for fidelity or noise, rather than recognizing it as a protective peak-limiting feedback loop tied to RF drive level.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "ALC = 'Avoid Limiting Clipping' — it caps the peaks so the PA never flat-tops.", "edge_cases": "Excessive mic gain that drives ALC hard does not increase your peak power — it just compresses dynamics and can still cause distortion/splatter if the loop is slow; ALC is a safety net, not a substitute for proper drive adjustment."}

{"core": "With no carrier present, the FM receiver's high-gain IF and RF stages amplify thermal noise to near full level, and the discriminator translates these random frequency fluctuations into a loud audio hiss.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Atmospheric noise is not the dominant source; the hiss is internally generated noise amplified by the receiver itself, present even with the antenna disconnected.", "why_tempting": "VHF does have some atmospheric/galactic noise, so it sounds plausible that the environment causes the rush."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The IF value sets selectivity and image rejection, not the noise level; a higher IF doesn't generate the rushing sound.", "why_tempting": "FM does use a relatively high IF, so a learner may conflate that design choice with the noise behaviour."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Post-discriminator audio gain amplifies whatever the detector outputs, but the noise originates upstream — the audio stages only pass it along, they don't create the no-signal condition.", "why_tempting": "It's true the audio amplifier makes it 'loud', so this seems like the proximate cause."}], "concept": "An FM discriminator (or ratio/quadrature detector) converts instantaneous frequency deviation into audio voltage. With no carrier, the limiter/IF chain has nothing to limit to, so it amplifies broadband thermal noise to its full gain. The discriminator then sees rapid random phase/frequency excursions and outputs large random audio — the characteristic loud rush. When a carrier appears, the capture effect / limiter clamps onto it, the noise loses the gain (it's 'captured'), and squelch normally mutes this no-signal hiss. The root cause is the very high front-end/IF gain ahead of the detector.", "misconception": "Believing the loud no-signal noise comes from the environment or from audio-stage gain, rather than recognizing it as amplified internal noise from the high-gain IF chain feeding the discriminator.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "No carrier to capture → full IF gain amplifies noise → discriminator turns it into a roar; that's why FM needs squelch.", "edge_cases": "This is exactly why FM rigs use squelch: it mutes the audio until a carrier raises the signal enough to suppress (capture) the noise."}

{"core": "FM conveys audio amplitude as frequency deviation. If your transmitter only swings ±2.5 kHz when the system expects ±5 kHz, the recovered audio at the receiving discriminator will be proportionally weaker — about half — so listeners hear low audio.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Distortion arises from over-deviation (clipping/splatter as you exceed the receiver's passband), not from under-deviation. Half deviation is perfectly linear, just quiet.", "why_tempting": "Any deviation mismatch sounds like it should 'mangle' the signal, and distortion is a common FM complaint."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Deviation is independent of RF carrier power and link budget. Your coverage/range is set by transmit power, antenna, and path, not by how much you swing the carrier.", "why_tempting": "Learners conflate 'weaker audio' with 'weaker signal,' assuming reduced modulation shortens range."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The repeater keys up on RF carrier presence (and CTCSS if used); a properly tuned 2.5 kHz signal still triggers it normally.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you imagine the repeater 'rejecting' a non-conforming signal, but access depends on carrier/tone, not deviation amount."}], "concept": "In FM, recovered audio amplitude is proportional to peak deviation: V_out ∝ Δf. A discriminator (or quadrature/PLL detector) outputs voltage proportional to instantaneous frequency offset from center. The system's deemphasis and audio gain are calibrated for the standard deviation (here 5 kHz). Feeding only 2.5 kHz halves the detected swing, so audio is roughly 6 dB down — low but clean. Over-deviation is the dangerous direction: it widens occupied bandwidth, spills outside the IF passband, and produces clipping/adjacent-channel splatter. Standard narrowband FM uses ±5 kHz deviation with channel spacing chosen per Carson's rule (BW ≈ 2(Δf + f_max)).", "misconception": "Assuming any deviation error degrades intelligibility the same way. Under-deviation just lowers volume; over-deviation causes distortion and splatter — the two are not symmetric.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Low deviation = low volume; over-deviation = over the edges (splatter).", "edge_cases": "If deviation is extremely low, the audio may drop into the noise and SNR suffers, but the primary, intended answer here is simply reduced (low) audio level."}

{"core": "In FM, the audio signal varies the carrier frequency above and below its rest value; the magnitude of that frequency change is called deviation.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Modulation index is the ratio of peak deviation to the modulating frequency (m = Δf/fm), not the frequency change itself.", "why_tempting": "It's a closely related FM parameter and is built directly from deviation, so it sounds like the right vocabulary."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "\"Spectrum spread\" is not a standard FM term; the actual occupied bandwidth is governed by deviation and modulating frequency (Carson's rule).", "why_tempting": "Wider deviation does spread the spectrum, so the phrase loosely describes a real effect."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "\"Shift\" describes a fixed offset (e.g. FSK or repeater input/output split), not the dynamic carrier swing produced by modulation.", "why_tempting": "\"Shift\" is plausible because it suggests a change in frequency, and FSK/RTTY use the term."}], "concept": "For frequency modulation, the instantaneous frequency is f(t) = fc + Δf·m(t), where Δf (deviation) is the maximum departure of the carrier from its centre frequency. Deviation tracks the amplitude of the modulating signal — louder audio swings the carrier farther. The modulation index m = Δf/fm links deviation to the highest modulating frequency, and Carson's rule (BW ≈ 2(Δf + fm)) uses deviation to estimate occupied bandwidth. So deviation is the fundamental quantity describing the frequency change caused by modulation.", "misconception": "Confusing deviation (the actual frequency excursion) with the modulation index (a dimensionless ratio derived from it), or with a static frequency offset like \"shift.\"", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Deviation = how far the carrier DEVIATES from centre.", "edge_cases": "Narrowband FM voice (typical amateur 2 m FM) limits deviation to about ±5 kHz; exceeding it over-deviates and splatters into adjacent channels."}

{"core": "In FM, the carrier's frequency is shifted by the modulating audio. With no audio input (dead microphone), there is no deviation, so the transmitter emits a steady, unmodulated carrier at its center frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Frequency modulation only occurs when the audio signal drives the modulator; with the microphone failed there is no modulating signal to vary the frequency.", "why_tempting": "It's an FM transmitter, so a learner may assume it always produces an FM carrier regardless of input."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "An FM transmitter does not vary amplitude with the audio; even if it were modulating, it wouldn't produce AM. With no audio it certainly produces no AM.", "why_tempting": "Confusing the modulation types — amplitude modulation is the other 'common' scheme learners mix up with FM."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Phase modulation requires a modulating signal too; no audio means no phase variation, just a plain carrier.", "why_tempting": "PM is closely related to FM (indirect FM is generated via phase modulation), so it seems plausible."}], "concept": "In FM the instantaneous frequency is f(t) = fc + kf·m(t), where m(t) is the modulating audio. Deviation Δf is proportional to the amplitude of m(t). If the microphone fails, m(t) = 0, so f(t) = fc — a constant-frequency, constant-amplitude sine wave: an unmodulated (CW) carrier with no sidebands. The RF stage is still keyed/transmitting; it just carries no information.", "misconception": "Believing that an 'FM transmitter' inherently radiates a frequency-modulated signal at all times. The modulation depends entirely on the presence of a modulating input — no input, no modulation.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "No audio in, no deviation out — just the bare carrier.", "edge_cases": "A small amount of residual hum, noise, or oscillator drift could produce trace modulation, but the intended answer is an unmodulated carrier."}

{"core": "FM has a capture and noise-rejection advantage: above a modest threshold, the limiter/discriminator strips amplitude noise and delivers a clean recovered audio signal, giving a good signal-to-noise ratio at the relatively strong, line-of-sight signal levels typical of local VHF/UHF work.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An FM carrier is just as detectable as any other RF emission; modulation type has nothing to do with carrier detectability, and a constant-envelope FM carrier is in fact easy to spot on a spectrum analyzer.", "why_tempting": "FM's constant amplitude (no envelope variation when unmodulated) might suggest the carrier is 'hidden,' but that confuses amplitude steadiness with detectability."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "FM is actually MORE susceptible to multipath/reflection problems (picket-fencing, flutter) because phase distortion from delayed reflections corrupts the frequency-modulated signal; it is not chosen for reflection resistance.", "why_tempting": "VHF/UHF is dominated by reflections, so a learner may assume the preferred mode must be the reflection-resistant one — but that's backwards for FM."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Frequency stability of the RF carrier is a function of the oscillator/synthesizer design, not the choice of FM versus AM; FM does not inherently hold frequency better.", "why_tempting": "The word 'frequency' in FM invites the false leap that FM somehow keeps the carrier on frequency, conflating modulation method with oscillator stability."}], "concept": "FM's key property is the capture effect and noise quieting. A hard limiter removes amplitude variations (noise rides on amplitude), and the discriminator responds only to instantaneous frequency. Above the FM threshold (~10 dB carrier-to-noise), the output SNR improves dramatically and is enhanced further by the deviation/modulation index — wider deviation trades bandwidth for SNR (the FM improvement factor). On local VHF/UHF links signals are strong and stable line-of-sight paths, so they sit well above threshold, letting FM deliver clean, quiet audio. The trade-off is the wide bandwidth FM occupies and the sharp threshold below which it fails fast (versus AM/SSB which degrade gracefully) — acceptable locally where signals are robust.", "misconception": "That 'best mode' must be the one resistant to reflections or with the most stable carrier, when the real reason is FM's superior recovered SNR under the strong-signal conditions of local line-of-sight communication.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "FM = Fewer-noise, Mostly quiet — limiter kills amplitude noise.", "edge_cases": "FM's advantage collapses below the capture threshold; for weak-signal DX work SSB/CW are preferred because they remain usable far below where FM goes silent."}

{"core": "By Carson's rule, an FM signal's bandwidth is roughly twice the sum of the peak deviation and the highest modulating frequency: BW ≈ 2(Δf + f_m). With 5 kHz deviation and a typical 3 kHz audio bandwidth, BW ≈ 2(5 + 3) = 16 kHz, which lands between 10 and 20 kHz.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "FM bandwidth is always wider than the deviation alone — the sidebands extend well beyond ±Δf, so it can never be less than 5 kHz.", "why_tempting": "A learner might equate bandwidth directly with the 5 kHz deviation figure."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This underestimates the occupied bandwidth; Carson's rule gives roughly 16 kHz, not 5–10 kHz.", "why_tempting": "Doubling just the deviation (2×5=10) without adding the modulating frequency lands here."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "For voice FM with ~3 kHz audio and 5 kHz deviation the bandwidth is around 16 kHz, comfortably below 20 kHz.", "why_tempting": "Knowing FM is 'wide' might tempt picking the largest figure, but that overshoots for these parameters."}], "concept": "Carson's rule: BW ≈ 2(Δf + f_m), where Δf is peak frequency deviation and f_m is the highest significant modulating frequency. FM theoretically produces infinite sidebands (Bessel functions), but Carson's rule captures ~98% of the power. For amateur narrowband voice FM, f_m is typically taken as 3 kHz: BW ≈ 2(5 + 3) = 16 kHz, consistent with standard 12.5–25 kHz channel spacing.", "misconception": "Treating FM bandwidth as equal to (or simply twice) the deviation, ignoring the contribution of the modulating audio frequency.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Carson: 'Twice the sum' — 2 × (deviation + modulation).", "edge_cases": "The exact result depends on the assumed highest audio frequency; with 3 kHz it's ~16 kHz, but the answer band 10–20 kHz holds for any reasonable voice f_m."}

{"core": "In frequency modulation the instantaneous carrier frequency is shifted in proportion to the modulating signal's amplitude, so a louder (higher-level) modulating signal produces a greater frequency deviation from the carrier's rest frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "In FM the carrier amplitude stays constant regardless of modulation level — only frequency varies. Amplitude changes describe AM, not FM.", "why_tempting": "Equating 'louder signal' with 'bigger amplitude' is the intuitive AM answer, and amplitude is the obvious thing to vary."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Pulse-width variation describes pulse-width modulation (PWM), a digital/pulse technique, not analog FM of a continuous carrier.", "why_tempting": "PWM also encodes a level by 'how much' of something, so it can feel analogous if you blur the modulation types."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "PEP is fixed in FM because the envelope amplitude is constant; FM transmitters run at constant output power independent of modulation. Modulation level does not change PEP.", "why_tempting": "PEP sounds like a measure of how 'hard' the transmitter is driven, so it seems to track signal strength."}], "concept": "FM defines instantaneous frequency as f(t) = fc + kf·m(t), where m(t) is the modulating signal and kf is the deviation sensitivity (Hz per volt). The frequency excursion away from fc, called deviation Δf, is therefore directly proportional to the modulating amplitude — peak deviation occurs at the modulating peak. The carrier envelope (amplitude) is constant, which is why FM is largely immune to amplitude noise and why FM rigs run constant-power. Loudness/level → deviation; modulating frequency → rate of deviation swing. Bandwidth follows Carson's rule, B ≈ 2(Δf + fm).", "misconception": "Assuming 'stronger signal = bigger amplitude' for every modulation scheme. That holds for AM, but in FM the amplitude is held constant and information lives entirely in frequency deviation.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "FM: Frequency Moves — amplitude stays put; louder pushes the frequency farther.", "edge_cases": "Deviation is set by modulating amplitude (level), while the modulating frequency sets how fast the carrier swings — don't confuse the two."}

{"core": "Phase modulation (PM) and frequency modulation (FM) both vary the angle of the carrier rather than its amplitude; they are mathematically related because frequency is the time derivative of phase.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "AM varies the carrier's amplitude, not its angle, so it is fundamentally a different class of modulation from FM.", "why_tempting": "AM is the other 'classic' analog mode learners pair with FM, making it an easy default answer."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Pulse modulation encodes information in discrete pulses (their amplitude, width, or position) and is unrelated to continuously varying the carrier angle.", "why_tempting": "It sounds technical and modern, so it can seem like a sophisticated cousin of FM."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Multiplexing combines multiple signals onto one channel; it is a channel-sharing technique, not a carrier-modulation method at all.", "why_tempting": "The word 'modulation' in the name makes it look like a peer of FM and PM."}], "concept": "FM and PM are both forms of angle modulation. The instantaneous phase is θ(t) and the instantaneous angular frequency is ω(t)=dθ/dt. In FM the frequency deviation is proportional to the modulating signal m(t); in PM the phase deviation is proportional to m(t). Because ω is the derivative of θ, an FM signal can be produced by integrating m(t) and then phase-modulating, and a PM signal by differentiating m(t) and then frequency-modulating. This direct mathematical relationship — one is essentially the integral/derivative version of the other — is why PM is the modulation most closely related to FM, while AM (amplitude) and pulse/multiplex techniques are in entirely different categories.", "misconception": "Assuming AM is FM's nearest relative simply because both are common analog voice modes, rather than recognizing that FM's true sibling is the other angle-modulation method, PM.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "FM and PM are both 'angle' modes — frequency is just the rate of change of phase.", "edge_cases": "In practice a transmitter labelled 'FM' may actually use a phase modulator with a pre-emphasis/integrating network, which is why the two are often hard to distinguish on the air."}

{"core": "FM occupies a much wider bandwidth than SSB or CW. On HF bands below 28 MHz the regulatory bandwidth allotments per channel/band segment are narrow, so a typical FM signal would exceed the permitted bandwidth.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Modern HF transceivers have ample frequency stability; stability is not the limiting factor for FM use on HF.", "why_tempting": "FM does require a stable carrier to avoid drift across the discriminator, so it sounds plausible if you focus on the demodulation side."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "FM is actually a constant-envelope mode that allows highly efficient Class-C amplification — efficiency is a strength of FM, not a weakness.", "why_tempting": "Learners may confuse FM with linear modes and assume an efficiency penalty exists."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Harmonic suppression is a function of transmitter filtering and is achievable for any mode at any frequency; it has nothing to do with why FM is avoided on HF.", "why_tempting": "Harmonics are a real design concern, so it reads as a credible engineering limitation."}], "concept": "Occupied bandwidth of FM is governed by Carson's rule: BW ≈ 2(Δf + f_m), where Δf is peak deviation and f_m the highest modulating frequency. For voice FM with ~5 kHz deviation and ~3 kHz audio, BW ≈ 16 kHz — versus ~3 kHz for SSB. Below 28 MHz the HF band segments are crowded and the regulatory occupied-bandwidth limits are too narrow to accommodate a 10–16 kHz FM signal, so SSB/CW dominate. Above ~29 MHz and on VHF/UHF, spectrum is abundant and wider channel spacing makes FM practical.", "misconception": "Assuming the obstacle is a hardware/performance limitation (stability, efficiency, harmonics) when it is purely a spectrum/regulatory bandwidth constraint.", "link": "Canadian Band-Plan Explorer", "mnemonic": "FM = Fat Mode — too wide for narrow HF channels.", "edge_cases": "FM IS allowed and commonly used at the top of 10 m (around 29.6 MHz) where wider channels and higher frequency make the bandwidth acceptable."}

{"core": "In an FM system, audio amplitude controls the frequency deviation.
