Switching supplies achieve the same end via high-frequency conversion but are still called power supplies. The defining feature is the complete AC-to-regulated-DC conversion, not any single internal stage.", "misconception": "Thinking the answer is whichever single internal component (filter) or the reverse-direction device (inverter), rather than the named complete device that performs the full conversion.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "INverter = DC IN to AC; power supply = mains IN, DC out.", "edge_cases": "An inverter and a power supply both bridge AC and DC but in opposite directions — always check which way the conversion runs."}

{"core": "A transceiver on transmit draws substantial current (a 100 W HF rig can pull 20–25 A), so the supply must be rated to deliver at least that peak current continuously. If it can't, voltage sags and the radio malfunctions.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Undervoltage protection is a nice safety feature but irrelevant if the supply simply can't source enough current to run the radio in the first place.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a prudent design spec, and protection circuits are genuinely valuable in regulated supplies."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Connectors can be adapted, re-terminated, or matched with cables; mechanical compatibility never determines whether the supply is electrically adequate.", "why_tempting": "Practical hams worry about plug types, so it feels like a real selection concern."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Metering is convenience/monitoring only — useful for diagnostics but it does not affect the supply's ability to power the rig.", "why_tempting": "A front-panel volt/amp meter looks like a quality, professional feature buyers value."}], "concept": "At 13.8 V the limiting factor is current. A modern 100 W transceiver may draw I = P/(η·V); with roughly 20 A peak on transmit you size the supply with headroom (e.g. 25–30 A continuous) so it never current-limits or droops under the keyed load. Insufficient current rating causes voltage sag below the regulation window, triggering shutdown, distortion (in SSB), or RFI. Voltage is essentially fixed by the radio's design (13.8 V nominal), so the meaningful variable left to specify is maximum deliverable current.", "misconception": "That a supply showing '13.8 V' is automatically adequate — learners forget that a supply also has a current rating, and transmit current is far higher than receive current.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Volts are fixed at 13.8 — pick the supply for the AMPS.", "edge_cases": "Specify continuous (not peak/surge) current rating, and add margin for SSB/CW peaks and accessories sharing the supply."}

{"core": "Linear supplies regulate by dissipating excess voltage across a series pass transistor at DC, so they produce essentially no high-frequency switching transients — meaning far less RF hash to interfere with receivers.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Linear supplies are LESS efficient (often 40–60%) because the pass element burns off the surplus as heat; switchers reach 80–95%.", "why_tempting": "Efficiency is the headline spec people quote, and learners may confuse 'simpler' with 'more efficient'."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Both topologies can deliver tight regulation; regulation quality isn't intrinsically tied to FM operation and isn't the distinguishing advantage here.", "why_tempting": "FM gear sounds demanding, so it seems plausible a 'cleaner' linear supply would regulate it better."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "This is the switcher's advantage — high-frequency operation shrinks the transformer/inductors. Linear supplies need a bulky 50/60 Hz transformer and large filter caps.", "why_tempting": "Size/weight is a real power-supply trade-off, easy to attribute to the wrong side."}], "concept": "A switch-mode supply chops the input at tens to hundreds of kHz and regulates via duty cycle; those fast edges (rich in harmonics) radiate and conduct broadband RF noise unless heavily filtered and shielded. A linear regulator operates at DC with a continuously-conducting pass device — no switching node, no high-dv/dt edges — so its noise floor is just low-level ripple at the mains frequency and thermal noise. The cost is efficiency: P_dissipated = (V_in − V_out) × I_load, all wasted as heat, which is why linears are large and run hot.", "misconception": "Assuming the supply with the best 'modern' specs (efficiency, size, weight) is automatically preferable. For a radio operator, RF cleanliness can outweigh those, which is the whole point of this question.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Linear = Low noise (but Lossy and Large).", "edge_cases": "Well-designed switchers with good filtering/shielding can be acceptably quiet, and many commercial ham switchers are; the linear's RF-noise edge is a general tendency, not a guarantee."}

{"core": "A fuse only protects the wire downstream of itself. Placing it as close to the battery as possible means the entire length of the DC supply cable is protected against a short-to-chassis fault, not just the portion near the radio.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Fuse position has negligible effect on voltage drop; drop is set by conductor cross-section, current and total length, not by where the fuse sits.", "why_tempting": "Voltage drop is a real concern in mobile DC runs, so it sounds plausible to associate cable issues with the fuse."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Ignition/alternator noise is addressed by filtering, bonding and good grounding, not by fuse location; a fuse does no filtering.", "why_tempting": "Mobile noise is a genuine nuisance and learners may link any DC-line measure to noise reduction."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Transient absorption is the job of devices like transient voltage suppressors or capacitors; an ordinary fuse opens on overcurrent and does not clamp voltage spikes.", "why_tempting": "Vehicles do produce transients (load dump), so 'absorb transients' sounds like a protective benefit."}], "concept": "In a vehicle the chassis is the negative return, so the positive supply lead runs unprotected for its whole length until it reaches a fuse. If that lead chafes through to the metal body anywhere ahead of the fuse, the full battery current flows with nothing to interrupt it — potentially hundreds of amps, melting insulation and causing a fire. By inserting the fuse within a few centimetres of the battery's positive terminal, almost the entire cable is on the protected side of the fuse, so any short along its length will blow the fuse and open the circuit. The fuse rating still protects the radio's normal draw, but the rationale for *location* is protecting the wiring itself.", "misconception": "Thinking a fuse protects 'the radio' rather than understanding it protects the conductor downstream of it — fault current upstream of a fuse is never interrupted.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "A fuse guards what's behind it — put it right at the source so the whole cable is behind it.", "edge_cases": "Some installations add a second fuse at the radio end; the battery-end fuse still governs whole-cable protection."}

{"core": "Switching supplies operate at high frequency (tens of kHz to MHz), which lets the transformer and filter components shrink dramatically compared to the bulky 50/60 Hz iron-core transformer of a linear supply, giving smaller size and lighter weight.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Switchers are actually a notorious *source* of RF hash from their fast switching edges; linear supplies are far quieter.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume the 'modern' design is cleaner in every respect, but RF noise is a known drawback of switchers."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Multiple simultaneous output rails are possible in both topologies (a linear supply can have several secondary windings/regulators); it isn't a unique switcher advantage.", "why_tempting": "Multi-rail ATX-style switchers are common, so it sounds like a switcher-specific feature."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Switchers are more complex — many active parts, control ICs, feedback loops — making them harder, not easier, to repair.", "why_tempting": "People conflate 'modern/efficient' with 'simple,' but the higher part count actually complicates servicing."}], "concept": "Transformer core size scales inversely with operating frequency: required core area is roughly proportional to V/(f·N·B). A linear supply rectifies at the 60 Hz mains, so the transformer must handle full power at that low frequency, demanding a large iron core and heavy filter capacitors. A switcher first converts to high-frequency AC (e.g. 100 kHz), at which the same power transfers through a tiny ferrite core, and the higher ripple frequency needs much smaller filter inductors/capacitors. The penalty is switching-frequency noise (the trade-off behind option A).", "misconception": "Assuming a switching supply is superior on every metric — in reality its wins are efficiency and size/weight, while it loses on RF cleanliness and repair simplicity.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "High frequency = small iron: switchers win on Size, Efficiency, Weight.", "edge_cases": "Well-shielded, well-filtered commercial switchers can be acceptably quiet, but inherently they generate more conducted/radiated RFI than a linear supply."}

{"core": "A 100 W transceiver draws roughly 20+ A on transmit at 13.8 V. Heavy-gauge (low-resistance) wire keeps the IR voltage drop along the feed leads small so the radio sees adequate supply voltage.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Shock protection at 13.8 V DC is not a concern; insulation and current capacity matter, not gauge for safety from shock.", "why_tempting": "Heavier wire intuitively feels 'safer,' conflating mechanical/current robustness with shock prevention."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Wire gauge on a DC supply line does not address RF interference; that is handled by bypassing, ferrites/chokes, and good grounding.", "why_tempting": "Learners associate thick conductors with shielding/grounding, which do affect RFI."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Ripple is a function of the power supply's rectifier/filter design, not the gauge of the connecting wire.", "why_tempting": "Ripple is a power-supply term, so it sounds plausibly related to the DC connection."}], "concept": "At high current the lead resistance becomes significant. V_drop = I × R_wire, and R_wire = ρL/A, so reducing gauge number (increasing cross-sectional area A) lowers R and thus the drop. For a 100 W rig pulling ~20 A through, say, 0.05 Ω of round-trip wiring, the drop is 1 V — enough to push a 13.8 V radio below its undervoltage cutoff or cause transmit distortion. Heavier wire also reduces I²R heating and keeps the conductor within its ampacity rating.", "misconception": "Treating wire gauge as a generic 'quality/safety' upgrade rather than recognizing it specifically controls resistive voltage drop under high DC current.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "High current → fat wire → small drop.", "edge_cases": "Drop scales with total loop length, so doubling the run distance doubles the drop; keep DC leads short as well as thick."}

{"core": "In North America, residential service is nominally 120 V (single hot to neutral) and 240 V (across both hot legs of a split-phase service), so the correct pairing is 120 V and 240 V.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "130 V/260 V is above the nominal standard and exceeds the typical service voltage band.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible because actual line voltage can run slightly high, and these numbers preserve the 2:1 split-phase ratio."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "110 V/220 V are obsolete nominal figures no longer used as the official standard in Canada/US.", "why_tempting": "Many people still casually say '110' and '220'—these were older nominal values, so they feel correct from habit."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "100 V/200 V is too low; 100 V is closer to Japanese mains, not North American residential service.", "why_tempting": "Round numbers that keep the 2:1 ratio, which can look like a clean 'standard.'"}], "concept": "North American residential power is a single-phase, three-wire split-phase service: a center-tapped distribution transformer secondary gives two 'hot' legs 180° out of phase relative to the grounded neutral. Each hot-to-neutral measures the nominal 120 V; hot-to-hot adds the two legs (since they are in antiphase, the magnitudes add) for 240 V, used for large loads like dryers and ranges. Nominal values have historically crept upward (32→110→115→120 V) as the standard, but the modern official nominal pairing is 120/240 V.", "misconception": "Confusing the long-retired '110/220' colloquial values with the current official 120/240 V nominal standard.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Double 120 = 240: the two hot legs simply add.", "edge_cases": "Actual measured voltage may legitimately vary roughly ±5% around nominal; commercial three-phase services use different values (e.g., 208 V), but those aren't typical home single-phase service."}

{"core": "With 3 m of wire (and current flowing both ways, so 6 m total of conductor), the total drop must stay under 0.5 V. Number 10 at 0.07 V/m gives 0.07 × 6 = 0.42 V, which is the smallest (lightest) gauge that still falls under 0.5 V.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "At 0.19 V/m, 6 m gives 1.14 V — far over the 0.5 V limit.", "why_tempting": "It's the lightest, cheapest wire and might seem 'enough' if you forget the run is doubled by the return path."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.11 V/m over 6 m is 0.66 V, still exceeding 0.5 V.", "why_tempting": "It's close to the limit and a common automotive gauge, so it looks plausible."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "0.05 V/m × 6 m = 0.30 V works, but #8 is heavier and thicker than needed — the question asks for the MINIMUM (smallest) acceptable gauge.", "why_tempting": "It clearly meets the spec with margin, so it seems 'safe,' but it's oversized and not the minimum required."}], "concept": "Voltage drop along a feed wire is V = I·R, but here losses are pre-tabulated as volts per metre at the stated 22 A. The key subtlety is that current must flow to the radio AND return to the battery, so a 3 m distance means 6 m of total conductor. Multiply each option's V/m by 6 and compare to the 0.5 V budget. Remember in AWG, a SMALLER gauge number = THICKER wire = lower resistance = lower drop. 'Minimum gauge' here means the thinnest (highest-number) wire that still keeps drop under 0.5 V: #10 at 0.42 V passes, #12 at 0.66 V fails, so #10 is the answer.", "misconception": "Two traps: forgetting to double the 3 m run for the return conductor, and misreading 'minimum gauge' — choosing the thickest wire (#8) instead of the thinnest one that meets spec.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Bigger AWG number = smaller wire. Double the distance for the round trip.", "edge_cases": "The 'minimum gauge' is the thinnest acceptable wire; using a thicker (lower-number) wire is always electrically fine but heavier and costlier."}

{"core": "A fuse in the positive supply lead opens if current exceeds its rating — most importantly during a short circuit. A vehicle battery can deliver hundreds of amps into a fault, enough to melt wiring and ignite a fire, so the fuse is a thermal/fire-protection device.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A fuse is essentially a low-resistance wire sized to melt at a set current; it adds negligible resistance and does nothing to reduce voltage drop. Conductor gauge and length govern voltage drop.", "why_tempting": "Fuses sit in the same DC supply path you'd analyze for voltage drop, so a learner may conflate protection with supply quality."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A fuse responds to sustained overcurrent (an I²t heating effect), not to fast voltage transients or spikes. Transient suppression needs TVS diodes, MOVs, or filtering — these act far faster than a fuse can blow.", "why_tempting": "'Protect the radio' sounds like exactly what a fuse does, and vehicles are electrically noisy, so transient protection feels plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Interference (RFI/EMI) is addressed by bonding, shielding, ferrites, and filtering, not by an inline fuse. The fuse carries DC current and has no effect on RF coupling.", "why_tempting": "Automotive RFI is a real concern with mobile installs, so a learner may grab any answer mentioning the vehicle's electronics."}], "concept": "A fuse is a series sacrificial element rated to open above a specific current. The danger it guards against is the immense fault current available from a lead-acid battery: source impedance is milliohms, so a short across the supply lead can draw hundreds or thousands of amps, dumping I²R heat into the wiring insulation and starting a fire long before any other protection acts. The fuse should be placed as close to the battery's positive terminal as practical so that the maximum length of wire is protected — a fault anywhere downstream of the fuse will clear it. Rate the fuse just above the radio's peak transmit current (and below the wire's ampacity).", "misconception": "Thinking the fuse exists primarily to protect the radio. It protects the wiring and vehicle from fire caused by overcurrent/short circuit; the radio surviving is incidental.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Fuse = Fire prevention from a Fault, not filtering.", "edge_cases": "Best practice fuses BOTH the positive and the negative/return leads in a vehicle, since chassis ground paths can also carry fault current; the exam focuses on the positive lead."}

{"core": "A loud low-frequency hum (typically 60 Hz or 120 Hz) almost always traces back to AC ripple from inadequate filtering in the DC power supply, so that is the first place to investigate.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The driver amplifies and shapes the signal but does not generate AC mains-frequency components; any hum there is usually injected from its supply rail.", "why_tempting": "It's an active stage in the chain and a plausible place for noise to enter."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The PA processes RF; it can radiate hum only if its DC supply is dirty, making the supply — not the PA — the root cause.", "why_tempting": "The PA draws the most current, so a learner may assume it's where ripple shows up worst."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A VFO sets transmit frequency and can cause drift or chirp, but it won't produce a strong 60/120 Hz audio hum unless its supply is poorly filtered.", "why_tempting": "Oscillator instability is a known cause of transmit defects, so it seems frequency-related."}], "concept": "Hum frequency is diagnostic. A power supply rectifies AC mains: a half-wave rectifier leaves ripple at the line frequency (60 Hz in Canada), a full-wave/bridge leaves it at twice the line frequency (120 Hz). The filter capacitors and any regulator are meant to smooth this to clean DC. If a filter cap dries out or a rectifier diode opens, ripple voltage rides on the DC rail and modulates every stage fed from it, appearing as audible hum on the transmitted signal. Because all stages share the supply, the supply is the single most likely common cause — fix it once and the hum clears everywhere.", "misconception": "Assuming the stage where the hum is 'heard' (PA output) is where the fault lives, rather than the common DC source feeding all stages.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Hum = humming power supply: 60/120 Hz means look at the rectifier and filter caps first.", "edge_cases": "Ground loops or a hum-injecting microphone/audio line can also cause hum; but for a transmitter's internal LF hum, the supply ripple is the prime suspect."}

{"core": "A key-operated on/off switch in the main power line physically prevents anyone without the key from energizing and operating the station, providing genuine access control.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Fuses protect against overcurrent faults; they do nothing to stop an unauthorized but otherwise normal operator.", "why_tempting": "Fuses are a 'main power line' safety device, so they sound related to controlling the station."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A carrier-operated relay switches on in response to an RF carrier — it automates keying, it doesn't restrict who can use the equipment.", "why_tempting": "It sounds technical and involves switching power, which seems like it could gate access."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A warning sign communicates a hazard but imposes no physical barrier; a determined or careless person can still operate the station.", "why_tempting": "Signs are an obvious deterrent and feel like a reasonable precaution."}], "concept": "The principle is access control through a physical lock. Among the options only a key-operated switch requires possession of a unique physical token (the key) to close the circuit and bring the transmitter to life. The others address fault protection (fuses), automated keying (COR), or hazard notification (signage) — none of which authenticate the operator. The licence holder remains responsible for who uses the station, so a positive lockout is the 'best' answer.", "misconception": "Conflating electrical safety/protection devices with security/access control — they solve different problems.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Only a KEY locks people out.", "edge_cases": "In practice a lockable equipment cabinet or removing a critical component also works, but among the choices the key switch is the intended best answer."}

{"core": "Removing the microphone makes the radio incapable of transmitting, so an unauthorized person physically cannot operate the station on the air even if they power it up.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A powered-off radio is trivially turned back on; nothing prevents an unauthorized person from switching it on and transmitting.", "why_tempting": "It feels like a security step, but it imposes no real barrier to use."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A sign relies on voluntary compliance and provides no physical or functional obstacle to operation.", "why_tempting": "Seems like a deterrent, but anyone ignoring the sign can transmit immediately."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Frequency choice has nothing to do with access control; the user simply retunes to any band they like.", "why_tempting": "Sounds technical, as if leaving it on a 'dead' channel disables it, but it does nothing to stop transmission."}], "concept": "The licensee remains responsible for all transmissions from their station, so the goal is to make unauthorized transmission physically impossible, not merely discouraged. Of the listed choices, only removing the microphone breaks the transmit path — without a mic the transceiver cannot key up and modulate, so the station cannot be operated on the air regardless of power state or frequency.", "misconception": "That deterrence or convenience measures (signs, power-off, retuning) equal security. The exam wants the option that removes operational capability, not one that relies on goodwill or appearance.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "No mic, no mike — no microphone means no talking on the air.", "edge_cases": "For a more robust solution you could also remove a control head or a fuse, but among the given options the microphone is the answer."}

{"core": "A 12 V starter battery can source hundreds of amperes into a low-resistance fault. The danger isn't shock but the massive short-circuit current, which can melt tools, vaporize conductors, and ignite fires.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Low voltage doesn't mean no hazard — the very low internal resistance allows enormous fault currents even at 12 V.", "why_tempting": "12 V is well below the ~50 V threshold for shock danger, so it feels harmless."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A battery supplies DC; steady current produces a static magnetic field, not significant radiated electromagnetic fields, and certainly nothing hazardous here.", "why_tempting": "EMF sounds like a generic 'electrical hazard' buzzword."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "At 12 V the voltage is far too low to drive a dangerous current through dry human skin, so electric shock is not the concern.", "why_tempting": "'Battery + electrical hazard' instinctively suggests shock."}], "concept": "Hazard from a source depends on both voltage AND available current. A lead-acid starter battery has internal resistance on the order of milliohms, so by I = V/R a dead short draws hundreds to over a thousand amperes (cold-cranking amps are specced this high deliberately). That current resistively heats anything in the fault path: a dropped wrench across the terminals can glow red-hot, spray molten metal, or ignite hydrogen gas vented during charging. Voltage (12 V) is too low to push a meaningful current through body resistance (~1–100 kΩ), so shock is negligible — but the short-circuit energy is the real threat.", "misconception": "That 'low voltage = safe.' Safety requires considering current capability, not voltage alone; a low-voltage, low-impedance source can be more destructive at a fault than a high-voltage, current-limited one.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Low volts, big amps — the battery bites by current, not by shock.", "edge_cases": "Series-connected battery banks (e.g., 48 V) or EV traction packs DO add shock risk on top of the short-circuit hazard, but a single 12 V starter battery does not."}

{"core": "An interlock switch automatically removes power when the cabinet is opened, protecting anyone reaching inside from contacting potentially lethal high voltages.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Turning a supply off when idle is an energy/convenience function unrelated to a door-actuated switch, and it doesn't address why the switch is tied to the cabinet being opened.", "why_tempting": "A switch that 'turns off the power' sounds like a general on/off function."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A high-voltage power supply is not an RF source, and an interlock is a safety device, not an RF shield; shielding is provided by the conductive enclosure itself, not a kill switch.", "why_tempting": "Open cabinets and RF leakage are both real concerns elsewhere, so it can seem plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Same reasoning — an interlock cutting DC/AC power does nothing to block incoming RF, and the supply isn't sensitive to it in this way.", "why_tempting": "Mirrors option C and plays on the idea that an open enclosure breaks shielding integrity."}], "concept": "A safety interlock is a switch mechanically actuated by the cabinet door or cover. When the enclosure is open, the contacts open and the primary (or the high-voltage section) is de-energized. The danger in HV supplies is twofold: the live transformer/rectifier outputs, and large filter capacitors that store dangerous charge. Note an interlock alone does NOT discharge stored energy — best practice pairs it with bleeder resistors across the filter capacitors so the charge dissipates after power removal. The hazard scales with stored energy E = ½CV²; even modest capacitance at hundreds or thousands of volts can deliver a fatal jolt.", "misconception": "Confusing electrical-safety interlocks with RF shielding or routine power switching. The question hinges on recognizing the switch is a shock-protection mechanism, not an EMI or convenience feature.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Open the door, kill the power.", "edge_cases": "An interlock removes the source but does not drain charged capacitors — always verify with a meter and use a discharge tool before reaching inside, since bleeder resistors can fail open."}

{"core": "As little as roughly 20 milliamperes through the body — particularly across the chest — can be fatal because it can disrupt breathing or trigger ventricular fibrillation. It is the lowest of the offered values, hence the 'minimum' fatal current.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "500 mA is far above the threshold for lethal effects and is not the minimum; death can occur at currents an order of magnitude lower.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a 'serious' current and learners assume a higher number is needed to kill."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "1 A is enormously above the lethal threshold — well beyond what is needed to stop the heart.", "why_tempting": "A round, large value feels appropriately dangerous, and people overestimate how much current the body can tolerate."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "2 A is the largest value listed and is nowhere near a 'minimum'; lethal effects occur at a tiny fraction of this.", "why_tempting": "Picking the biggest current seems intuitively the most dangerous, but the question asks for the minimum that can kill."}], "concept": "Electrical hazard is about current through tissue, not voltage alone. Effects scale with current: ~1 mA is the perception threshold, ~10–16 mA is the 'let-go' threshold where muscle tetany prevents releasing the conductor, and on the order of 20 mA through the chest can arrest respiration or, with current crossing the heart, induce ventricular fibrillation — the usual cause of death. Current is set by body resistance and applied voltage (I = V/R); with wet-skin or internal resistance dropping to ~1 kΩ, ordinary mains voltages easily push current into this lethal range. The key point: the lethal threshold is in the tens of milliamperes, not amperes.", "misconception": "That it takes a large current (hundreds of mA or whole amperes) to be fatal. In reality, currents two orders of magnitude smaller can stop the heart.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Milliamps kill, not amps — 'it's the current, not the voltage.'", "edge_cases": "The danger depends heavily on path (hand-to-hand or hand-to-foot crossing the heart is worst), duration, and frequency; the exam wants the single fixed answer of 20 mA."}

{"core": "The heart is exquisitely sensitive to electrical current; even a small current (on the order of 100–200 mA, and less if it passes directly through the chest) flowing across it can disrupt the coordinated electrical pacing of the cardiac muscle and induce ventricular fibrillation, which is fatal without immediate intervention.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The lungs are not the primary organ killed by small currents; respiratory arrest from current is usually secondary to paralysis of the diaphragm/chest muscles at higher currents, not a direct low-current fatality.", "why_tempting": "Electrocution can stop breathing, so the lungs seem like a plausible victim."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The brain is well protected and the lethal mechanism of low-current shock is cardiac fibrillation, not direct brain damage.", "why_tempting": "The brain runs on its own bioelectric signals, so it feels like it should be vulnerable to stray current."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The liver has no electrically driven function to disrupt and is not the organ that fails fatally from small currents.", "why_tempting": "It's a vital organ, so it can appear as a generic 'serious' answer."}], "concept": "Cardiac muscle contraction is triggered by precisely timed electrical impulses from the SA node. An external current crossing the heart during the vulnerable repolarization window can desynchronize the myocardium into ventricular fibrillation, where the heart quivers and stops pumping blood. The danger threshold is current, not voltage: ventricular fibrillation can begin around 100 mA through the body, and 'hand-to-hand' or 'hand-to-foot' paths that traverse the chest are the most lethal. Because I = V/R, even modest voltages across low skin resistance (wet skin can drop to a few hundred ohms) can drive lethal current. This is why the rule of thumb in safety is to keep one hand in your pocket when probing live circuits — to avoid creating a current path across the heart.", "misconception": "That voltage alone determines lethality, or that 'small' current is harmless. It is the magnitude AND path of current — specifically through the heart — that matters, and the lethal amount is surprisingly small.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "It's the current that kills, and the heart that stops.", "edge_cases": "The fibrillation threshold depends on current path, duration, and frequency; DC and very high frequencies behave differently, and currents below ~10 mA mainly cause the 'can't let go' muscle tetany rather than immediate cardiac arrest."}

{"core": "Voltages of roughly 30 V and above are generally considered potentially hazardous, because at that level enough current can be driven through the body—especially under low-resistance conditions like wet or broken skin—to cause harm.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "240 V is a common household/mains level and is certainly dangerous, but it's far above the threshold where hazard *begins*.", "why_tempting": "It's a familiar 'high' voltage people associate with deadly shocks, so it feels like the obvious danger level."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "347 V is a specific commercial distribution voltage; it is lethal but nowhere near the lowest hazardous level.", "why_tempting": "An unusual, technical-looking number can seem like a precise 'official' answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "100 V is dangerous, but the recognized onset of hazard is much lower, around 30 V.", "why_tempting": "It's a round number above the 'safe extra-low voltage' range and feels like a sensible cutoff."}], "concept": "The danger of electricity is set by current through the body, not voltage alone (I = V/R). Body resistance ranges from roughly 1 kΩ (wet, internal) to 100 kΩ (dry skin). The hazardous current threshold is low—about 1 mA is perceptible, ~10 mA causes muscle 'let-go' failure, and ~50–100 mA can induce ventricular fibrillation. With worst-case resistance near 1 kΩ, even 30 V can drive ~30 mA, well into the dangerous range. That's why 30 V is the conventional dividing line below which circuits are treated as 'extra-low voltage' / generally safe.", "misconception": "Believing only mains-level voltages (120/240 V) are dangerous and that low-voltage circuits are inherently safe—ignoring that low body resistance under wet conditions makes even tens of volts hazardous.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Think '30 = danger door'—above 30 V, treat it as live and risky.", "edge_cases": "Under exceptionally low-resistance conditions (broken skin, internal contact) even lower voltages can be hazardous; 30 V is a practical convention, not an absolute safety guarantee."}

{"core": "The first and safest action is to remove the hazard by cutting power, then summon professional emergency help and render first aid once it is safe to do so.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Delaying help can be fatal; high-voltage contact can stop the heart or cause severe burns within seconds, and the victim is often unable to release themselves due to muscle tetany.", "why_tempting": "Sounds cautious, and avoiding contact with a live source is genuinely important — but doing nothing wastes critical time."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Touching a person still in contact with a live conductor exposes the rescuer to the same lethal current, creating a second casualty. You must de-energize first.", "why_tempting": "The instinct to pull someone to safety is strong, and it would be correct only after the power is confirmed off."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Abandoning the victim offers no help and the person may die; the correct response addresses the hazard without endangering yourself.", "why_tempting": "Self-preservation is valid since you should never touch a live victim, but you can act safely by killing the power."}], "concept": "High-voltage rescue follows a fixed priority: (1) make the scene safe by removing power — flip the breaker, pull the plug, or have the utility disconnect — because current flowing through a victim will flow through anyone touching them; (2) call emergency services; (3) provide first aid (CPR/treating burns) once the victim is no longer energized. Muscle contraction from AC can 'freeze' a victim to the conductor, so the source must be killed before contact. Only the de-energize-first sequence protects both victim and rescuer.", "misconception": "Believing the heroic move is to immediately grab and pull the person away, when in fact touching an energized victim can electrocute the rescuer.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Power off, Phone, then Patient — the three P's of electrical rescue.", "edge_cases": "If power genuinely cannot be cut, use a dry non-conductive object (e.g., a dry wooden pole) to move the victim or source — but never your bare hands or any conductive material."}

{"core": "The only truly safe action is to remove the source of danger first — shut off or disconnect the power before making any contact, so neither you nor the victim remains in the current path.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A blanket may be damp or contain conductive fibres, and you must still grab the person while they may still be energized — you risk becoming part of the circuit.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a non-conductive barrier and 'pulling to safety' feels like first aid, but it doesn't address the live source."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Waiting for an electrician wastes critical time during which the victim continues to suffer current flow and cardiac/respiratory arrest; the rescuer can and should de-energize immediately.", "why_tempting": "It feels cautious and 'leave it to a professional,' but the immediate priority is breaking the circuit, which anyone can do at the breaker/disconnect."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Directly grabbing a limb of an energized person puts you in series with the high-voltage path and can electrocute you too.", "why_tempting": "It's the instinctive grab-and-pull rescue reaction, but it ignores that the person is still in contact with live voltage."}], "concept": "At high voltage, current can flow through anyone who completes a path to ground or back to the source. A victim 'frozen' to a conductor is energized, so touching them makes you a parallel/series resistance in that circuit — potentially lethal at currents well below 100 mA across the heart. Removing the hazard at its source (open the breaker, pull the disconnect, or kill the supply) eliminates the voltage that drives the current, after which the victim can be safely moved and CPR begun. Only if de-energizing is impossible would you use a dry, non-conductive object (wood, dry rope) to separate them — never bare hands or a possibly conductive blanket.", "misconception": "That the goal is to physically pull the person away as fast as possible; the real goal is to eliminate the voltage source first so contact becomes safe.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Kill the power before the touch.", "edge_cases": "If the source cannot be switched off, use a dry insulating object to push the person clear — never grab them directly while the circuit is live."}

{"core": "The single safest first step before working on any mains-powered equipment is to remove all sources of energy: turn off the supply and physically unplug it so the device is isolated from the AC line.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Deliberately shorting a charged filter capacitor can cause a violent arc, weld your tool, and damage the capacitor; it is also pointless until the unit is unplugged and is never the FIRST action.", "why_tempting": "Discharging a filter cap IS a legitimate safety step, so it feels safety-minded — but it comes after isolating the supply, and should be done with a resistor, not a dead short."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Verifying the bleeder resistor is a diagnostic/confirmation step done after power-off, not the first protective action, and you cannot safely probe it on a live or plugged-in unit.", "why_tempting": "Bleeder resistors are the proper way to drain stored charge, so checking one sounds prudent — but you still must isolate the unit from the mains first."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Pulling the fuse may interrupt one conductor but leaves the unit plugged in with potential mains voltage still present on the line side and large charges stored in the filter capacitors.", "why_tempting": "Removing the fuse seems to 'kill' the circuit, but a fuse only breaks one leg of the supply and does nothing to discharge stored energy."}], "concept": "Mains-operated supplies contain two distinct hazards: live AC at the input and stored DC energy in the filter capacitors (E = ½CV², which for a large electrolytic at hundreds of volts can be lethal). Hazard mitigation follows a fixed order: (1) de-energize and isolate — switch off AND unplug, removing the source; (2) confirm de-energized; (3) discharge stored energy through a bleeder/resistor before touching internals. Only step (1) removes the upstream energy source, so it is always first. Steps that act on stored charge (A, B) are meaningless and dangerous while the unit can still be re-energized.", "misconception": "That 'killing the circuit' by removing a fuse or shorting a capacitor makes it safe — when in fact only physically isolating the unit from the AC mains removes the primary lethal source.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Isolate first, discharge second — unplug before you touch.", "edge_cases": "After unplugging, large filter capacitors can hold a dangerous charge for a long time if the bleeder resistor has failed open, so always discharge through a resistor before contacting internal conductors."}

{"core": "A live power supply has energized terminals — particularly the high-voltage filter capacitors and rectifier outputs — that can deliver a lethal current through the body, so electric shock is the dominant hazard.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A blown fuse is an equipment-protection event, not a personal-safety risk, and is a minor consequence compared to electrocution.", "why_tempting": "Probing live circuits can short something and pop a fuse, so it sounds like a plausible 'risk' of poking around."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Possible collateral damage to attached gear is an economic concern, not the primary safety risk the question asks about.", "why_tempting": "Working on live equipment can indeed damage connected devices, making it a real but secondary worry."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "EMI is an operational/regulatory nuisance unrelated to the physical danger of contacting energized conductors.", "why_tempting": "Switching supplies generate noise, so 'EMI' looks technically relevant to anyone thinking about power-supply behaviour."}], "concept": "Safety questions key on the worst credible outcome to a person. In a linear supply, the transformer secondary and rectifier feed large electrolytic filter capacitors that hold dangerous charge even after power-down; in a switcher, primary-side caps can sit near peak line voltage. Body current of roughly 100–200 mA across the chest can cause ventricular fibrillation, and at mains/HV potentials skin resistance is easily overcome. The principle: de-energize and bleed/discharge filter capacitors before servicing; if live work is unavoidable, use one-hand technique, insulated tools, and an isolation transformer.", "misconception": "Treating a power supply as 'just low-voltage DC' and forgetting that the input side and storage capacitors carry shock-capable potentials whether or not the output is low.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Keep one hand in your pocket — single-hand probing avoids a current path across the heart.", "edge_cases": "Filter capacitors can remain charged for minutes to hours after unplugging, so 'powered off' is not 'safe' until they're verified discharged."}

{"core": "Bonding every chassis and metal enclosure in the station to a common ground means that if a fault energizes any equipment case, the fault current is shunted safely to ground rather than through the operator, so all station equipment should be grounded.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Grounding only the feedline protects against static and RF on that one run but leaves every other piece of gear's chassis able to become hot under fault.", "why_tempting": "The transmission line shield/coax outer is commonly grounded for lightning and RF reasons, so it sounds plausible as the 'safety' ground."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The AC line itself isn't 'grounded' by you — the utility neutral/ground is established at the service panel, and bonding only that wouldn't protect equipment cases.", "why_tempting": "Shock comes from the AC mains, so people assume grounding the power line is the cure."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Grounding the primary winding of a power supply addresses only one component, not the operator's exposure to every other chassis in the station.", "why_tempting": "The primary side carries the dangerous mains voltage, so it seems like the key point to protect."}], "concept": "Electrical-safety grounding works by giving fault current a low-impedance path back to source that trips the breaker or blows the fuse, while holding all touchable metal at (near) zero volts. A single bonded ground point connected to every equipment chassis ensures no potential difference can develop between two cabinets the operator might touch simultaneously, eliminating the shock path through the body. Protecting just one item leaves the others as hazards; the principle is comprehensive bonding of all enclosures.", "misconception": "That protecting the single 'most dangerous' part (mains line or power-supply primary) is enough — when shock protection actually requires every metal enclosure to be at ground potential.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Bond it all to one ground — every box, same potential.", "edge_cases": "Safety (AC/lightning) grounding and RF grounding are related but distinct goals; for shock protection the emphasis is on bonding all equipment chassis to a common ground, ideally tied to the AC service ground."}

{"core": "Bonding all ground electrodes together with heavy, low-impedance conductors forces every grounded point in the station to rise and fall together during a lightning surge, so no large potential difference develops between equipment chassis that could arc or push current through gear.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Continuous static drain is a function of a normal ground connection, not specifically of bonding multiple electrodes together, and it has nothing to do with the transient of a strike.", "why_tempting": "Grounding does bleed static, so a learner may grab the first plausible 'grounding' benefit."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Bonding with heavy conductors deliberately *minimizes* impedance; it does not add impedance to limit induced current. A high-impedance bond would actually worsen voltage differences during a surge.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a sophisticated electronics answer — adding impedance to limit current — but it's backwards for surge bonding."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A station reference (ground) plane is established by a low-impedance ground system generally, but the specific protective purpose of bonding electrodes *together* during a strike is equalizing potentials, not defining a signal reference.", "why_tempting": "It's a true statement about grounding in general, so it reads as correct without parsing the 'in case of a lightning strike' qualifier."}], "concept": "During a lightning event, an enormous di/dt drives huge transient currents through ground conductors. Any inductance L in a conductor develops V = L·(di/dt); with strike currents of tens of kA over microseconds, even a metre of wire can produce thousands of volts. If two grounds connect to earth at separate points, their instantaneous potentials differ wildly, and that difference appears across the equipment bridging them — driving destructive current through it. Single-point bonding with short, heavy (low-L, low-R) conductors ties all electrodes to a common potential so the entire system floats up and down as one, eliminating the difference voltage across gear.", "misconception": "Thinking the benefit is dissipation to earth, when the real protection from *bonding* is equalizing potentials so nothing sees a voltage difference; current still flows, but harmlessly with no gear in a differential path.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Bond = same potential everywhere; lightning can't push current through gear if both ends are at the same voltage.", "edge_cases": "This is about protecting equipment from differential surge, not about safely conducting the strike to earth (that's the role of the down-conductor and grounding electrode itself)."}

{"core": "A fuse is the deliberate weak point that opens before wiring or components overheat. Installing a higher-rated fuse lets fault currents exceed the safe limit of the conductors and parts, so a short can melt insulation, damage equipment, and start a fire.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A fuse opening during normal operation is the symptom of a fuse rated too LOW (nuisance tripping), not too high — the opposite of the scenario asked.", "why_tempting": "It describes a real fuse problem, so it sounds plausible if you don't track which direction of mis-rating causes which fault."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A fuse is essentially a low-resistance link in series; it does not regulate or limit supply voltage. Its rating affects when it melts, not the voltage delivered.", "why_tempting": "Series elements can drop voltage, so a learner may wrongly assume the fuse sets a voltage ceiling."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "An oversized fuse simply fails to protect; it does not prevent a low-current circuit from operating normally. A correctly or even slightly oversized fuse still passes the normal current.", "why_tempting": "It mentions low-current circuits, hinting at a functional issue, but the real danger is loss of protection, not loss of function."}], "concept": "A fuse protects the wiring and components, not itself. The conductor and equipment have a maximum safe current (set by I²R heating). The fuse must blow at or below that limit. If you fit a fuse rated above the wiring's safe ampacity, a fault can draw current that the fuse tolerates but the wire cannot — the conductor heats, insulation melts, and ignition can follow. The rule: size the fuse to protect the weakest current-carrying element, never to accommodate a larger fault current.", "misconception": "Believing a bigger fuse is 'safer' or more convenient because it trips less often — it actually removes the protection the fuse exists to provide.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Fuse protects the WIRE, not the device — rate it down, never up.", "edge_cases": "Replacing a blown fuse with a higher value to stop it 'nuisance tripping' is the classic dangerous mistake; if a correctly rated fuse keeps blowing, find the fault instead of upsizing."}

{"core": "A ground rod must conduct electricity well and resist corrosion in soil; copper-clad steel combines copper's excellent conductivity and corrosion resistance with steel's mechanical strength for driving into the earth.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Hard plastic is an electrical insulator — it cannot carry fault or RF currents to earth at all.", "why_tempting": "Plastic resists corrosion and weather, which sounds desirable for a buried object, but conductivity is the whole point of a ground rod."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Plain iron or steel conducts adequately but corrodes (rusts) rapidly underground, so its connection to soil degrades and ground resistance rises over time.", "why_tempting": "It's strong and cheap and is the structural core of the right answer, so it seems good enough — but bare steel lacks the corrosion protection the copper cladding provides."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Fibreglass is a non-conductive insulator, useless as an electrical path to earth.", "why_tempting": "It's tough and corrosion-proof, which masks the fact that it cannot conduct current."}], "concept": "A good earth ground needs both low contact resistance to soil and long-term durability. Copper offers superb conductivity and forms a stable, corrosion-resistant patina, but pure copper is too soft to hammer into rock-laden ground. Copper-clad steel uses a steel core for mechanical strength while the bonded copper jacket carries current and protects against corrosion — the best engineering compromise. The copper surface also resists galvanic deterioration far better than bare iron or steel.", "misconception": "That mechanical toughness or weather resistance alone makes a good ground rod. The defining requirement is electrical conductivity to earth combined with corrosion resistance, which rules out any insulator.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Conduct + endure = Copper-clad.", "edge_cases": "Solid copper rods exist but are costly and too soft for hard soil; galvanized (zinc-coated) steel is sometimes used but corrodes faster than copper cladding."}

{"core": "Even after a power supply is unplugged, the large electrolytic filter capacitors can retain a lethal charge for a long time. The first action on opening the cabinet is to safely discharge those capacitors before touching any internal circuitry.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Bonding the chassis to ground is good general practice but does nothing about energy stored in the filter capacitors, which are floating across the DC rails — touching them is still lethal.", "why_tempting": "Grounding sounds like a sensible safety-first step and is a real workshop habit."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "An insulating mat protects against ground-referenced shock but offers no protection from a charged capacitor when you bridge its two terminals with a hand or tool.", "why_tempting": "Insulating mats are standard bench safety gear, so it feels like the right precaution."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The unit is already offline/unplugged, so there is no AC present to short; this does nothing for the stored DC charge and is not a meaningful safety step.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a way to 'make safe' the input, mimicking lockout practices."}], "concept": "A capacitor stores energy E = ½CV². A filter cap of, say, 470 µF charged to 300 V holds ½·470e-6·300² ≈ 21 J — enough to seriously injure or kill. With no bleeder resistor (or a failed one) the stored charge can persist for minutes to hours because the RC discharge time constant through any leakage path is long. Discharge it deliberately through an insulated, high-wattage resistor (a few hundred ohms to a few kΩ) to limit peak current, then verify 0 V with a meter before proceeding.", "misconception": "Believing that unplugging the supply makes it safe. The hazard is stored energy in the capacitors, not just live mains.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Open the box → discharge the caps → then touch.", "edge_cases": "Discharge with a resistor, not a screwdriver short — a direct short produces a damaging current spike and can weld the tool or rupture the cap. Always confirm zero volts with a meter afterward, since some caps recover surface charge."}

{"core": "In a standard North American three-wire AC cord, the green wire is the safety/protective ground, and it bonds the equipment chassis to earth ground so a fault cannot leave the chassis at a dangerous potential.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The hot side carries the live ~120 V supply; connecting the safety ground there would short the line to ground and create an immediate hazard/fault.", "why_tempting": "Both involve the power switch area, and a learner may confuse the current-carrying conductors with the protective one."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The fuse goes in series with the hot (line) conductor to interrupt fault current; the green ground wire carries no current in normal operation and is never fused.", "why_tempting": "The fuse is part of the safety scheme, so it sounds protective — but it protects the live path, not the chassis bond."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The white wire is the neutral (current-return) conductor. Tying green to white defeats the independent fault path and can energize the chassis if neutral is interrupted or miswired.", "why_tempting": "Neutral is near ground potential, so it superficially seems interchangeable with safety ground — but they must remain separate downstream of the panel."}], "concept": "Three colour codes: black = hot/line, white = neutral/return, green (or green-yellow) = protective earth. The green bonds exposed metal to ground so that if the hot conductor contacts the chassis, a large fault current flows to ground, blowing the fuse/breaker, rather than leaving the chassis live. Neutral and ground are bonded together only at the service entrance, never inside equipment, so that loss of neutral cannot raise the chassis above ground.", "misconception": "Believing neutral (white) and ground (green) are the same because both sit near 0 V — they serve different functions and bonding them in equipment is unsafe.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Green = Ground = to the Grille (chassis).", "edge_cases": "Colour conventions here are the North American standard; some imported gear uses green/yellow stripe for the same protective-earth function."}

{"core": "A long ground wire (10 m) is an appreciable fraction of an HF wavelength, so it acts as a transmission line/antenna with significant RF impedance rather than a true low-impedance ground — RF voltage appears on the chassis, giving you a burn.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Antenna length affects SWR and radiated efficiency, not whether the chassis carries RF voltage from an ineffective ground path.", "why_tempting": "'Wrong wavelength' sounds like a generic RF fault and the question mentions transmitting."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Wire gauge governs DC/safety-ground current capacity and resistance; the problem here is RF impedance (reactance from length), which thicker wire won't fix.", "why_tempting": "Heavier wire 'fixing' a ground problem is intuitive from mains-wiring habits."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A defective outlet ground is a 60 Hz safety issue and wouldn't selectively cause an RF burn only while transmitting.", "why_tempting": "It plausibly explains 'no good ground,' and faulty outlets are a real hazard."}], "concept": "At RF a ground conductor is not a node at 0 V — it has distributed inductance and behaves as a transmission line. A 10 m wire at, say, 14 MHz (λ≈21 m) is ~0.48λ, near a half-wave, so the far (rod) end's low impedance is transformed toward a high impedance at the rig end. Even a short wire has reactance X_L = 2πfL (a few µH gives hundreds of ohms at HF), so RF current flowing into it develops a voltage V = I·Z that appears on the chassis. The fix is RF grounding technique: short, low-impedance bonding, ground/counterpoise radials, or an RF choke/balun to keep RF off the chassis — a long single wire to a distant rod cannot achieve a true RF ground.", "misconception": "Believing a DC/safety ground (a wire to a rod) is automatically an RF ground; at HF, conductor length and inductance dominate, making a long ground lead a poor RF return.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "For RF ground: short = good, long = an antenna.", "edge_cases": "The severity depends on band — a length near an odd multiple of λ/4 maximizes the high-impedance (and burn) effect, while a much shorter lead reduces it but never eliminates RF reactance entirely."}

{"core": "All chassis grounds should converge at a single common point (single-point ground) to avoid ground loops and keep every chassis at the same reference potential.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Daisy-chaining chassis to chassis creates series ground paths where each connection's resistance/inductance develops differing potentials, producing ground loops and hum.", "why_tempting": "It seems efficient and tidy to just bond nearby boxes together, and physically it 'connects everything to ground.'"}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Multiple separate electrodes at different potentials cause circulating currents between them, and during a transient (lightning) large voltage differences can appear between equipment.", "why_tempting": "More ground rods sounds like 'more grounding,' which intuitively seems safer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The antenna/lightning ground is part of the system but equipment chassis grounds belong at the station single-point bus, which is then bonded to the overall grounding system—not run individually to the antenna ground.", "why_tempting": "Learners conflate RF/antenna grounding with equipment safety grounding."}], "concept": "A single-point (star) ground topology ties every chassis ground lead to one common bus or plate, which in turn bonds to the building/AC safety ground and the lightning/antenna ground system. Because all return leads share one node, no significant voltage difference develops between chassis. Ground loops arise when two grounding paths of differing impedance enclose an area—an EMF (from magnetic coupling or differing potentials) drives a circulating current I = V/Z through the loop, injecting hum and noise. Collapsing all paths to one point eliminates the enclosed area and the differential potential.", "misconception": "That 'more grounds = better grounding'; in reality multiple/series ground paths create the very loops and potential differences good grounding aims to prevent.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Star, not chain: one point to rule them all.", "edge_cases": "The single-point ground must still be properly bonded to the AC service safety ground; an isolated 'station ground' alone can leave dangerous potential differences during faults."}

{"core": "A shorting stick with an in-line resistor lets you safely bleed stored charge through a controlled current path while keeping your hands isolated from the high voltage; the resistor limits the peak discharge current and prevents a violent arc.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A bare screwdriver tip shorts the capacitor directly, producing an explosive arc, welding/pitting the tip, and risking flying metal — no current limiting at all.", "why_tempting": "The insulated handle protects your hand, so it feels 'safe enough', and a screwdriver is the obvious nearby tool."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An insulated wire dead-shorts the capacitor with essentially zero series resistance, causing the same dangerous high-current arc; only the resistor makes a discharge tool safe.", "why_tempting": "Insulated wire with clips lets you connect hands-free, which seems safer than poking with a tool."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Bleeder resistors may be open, missing, or absent on older/faulty supplies, so you can never rely on them — you must verify discharge before touching anything.", "why_tempting": "Bleeders are designed for exactly this, so 'just wait' sounds like the intended engineered solution."}], "concept": "A charged filter cap stores E = ½CV². If you short it with near-zero resistance, peak current is limited only by the tiny parasitic resistance/inductance, giving an instantaneous arc that can vaporize metal and injure you. The in-line resistor sets a controlled time constant τ = RC and limits peak current to I = V/R, dissipating the energy as heat over a few τ instead of in an arc. Choose R high enough to keep peak current and resistor power manageable but low enough to discharge in seconds. Always confirm with a meter afterward, since the cap can recover surface charge.", "misconception": "That any 'insulated' tool is safe — the real hazard is the uncontrolled discharge current, and only series resistance limits it. Insulation protects you from the voltage but does nothing about the arc.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Stick + Resistor = Safe; bare metal = bang.", "edge_cases": "Large electrolytics can 'self-recharge' (dielectric absorption) after discharging, so re-check and short again before working."}

{"core": "Bonding the chassis to the AC safety ground keeps the metal chassis at earth potential. If a live conductor faults to the chassis, the fault current flows to ground and trips the breaker/blows the fuse rather than leaving the chassis at a dangerous voltage for anyone who touches it.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The safety ground does nothing to limit or regulate the DC output voltage delivered to connected equipment; overvoltage protection requires a crowbar/clamp circuit, not chassis bonding.", "why_tempting": "\"Protection\" and \"fault\" sound like they should protect the load, conflating personal safety grounding with output regulation."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The ground path is intended to TRIP the breaker on a fault, not preserve it; the breaker is a sacrificial protective device meant to open.", "why_tempting": "It mentions the breaker, and learners may think grounding spares circuit components from stress."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A proper ground fault deliberately causes high current that should blow the fuse — the grounding promotes the protective action rather than preventing it.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like grounding 'helps' the circuit avoid nuisance trips, but it's the opposite of the safety function."}], "concept": "Equipment (safety) grounding bonds all exposed conductive parts to the supply earth. Under normal operation no current flows in this conductor. If insulation fails and a hot conductor contacts the chassis, the chassis-to-ground bond presents a low-impedance fault path. Ohm's law makes the fault current large, which the overcurrent device (fuse/breaker) interrupts. Crucially, until it opens, the chassis is held near 0 V relative to earth, so touch voltage stays safe. Without the bond, a faulted chassis floats at line potential and electrocutes anyone bridging it to ground.", "misconception": "That the AC safety ground regulates voltage or protects the circuitry/fuse. Its job is human safety: keeping touchable metal at earth potential and forcing protective devices to operate.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Green wire = safe to touch.", "edge_cases": "A GFCI adds further protection by detecting small ground-leakage currents far below the fuse rating, but the chassis bond is the primary defence here."}

{"core": "A fuse's voltage rating specifies the maximum circuit voltage at which the fuse can safely interrupt the current and extinguish the arc that forms as the element melts and the gap opens.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Fuses interrupt overcurrents; they don't absorb or dissipate voltage transients — that's the job of MOVs, TVS diodes, or surge suppressors.", "why_tempting": "It sounds protective and mentions voltage, which is the keyword in the question stem."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The fuse holder's insulation is a mechanical/housing concern, not what the fuse's own voltage rating defines; the rating describes the fuse element's arc-interruption capability.", "why_tempting": "Dielectric breakdown does involve voltage limits, so it superficially connects to a 'voltage rating.'"}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A fuse is a series overcurrent device; it has nothing to do with leakage current to ground — that involves insulation resistance and isolation, not fusing.", "why_tempting": "Leakage and voltage rating both sound like safety specs, blurring the distinction for a careless reader."}], "concept": "When a fuse element melts, the current doesn't stop instantly — the inductance in the circuit tries to maintain current flow, sustaining an arc across the widening gap (V = L·di/dt drives the arc). The voltage rating is the maximum system voltage at which the fuse can reliably quench that arc before it re-strikes or sustains. Operate a fuse above its rated voltage and the arc may not extinguish, leaving the circuit conducting through ionized vapor even after the element has parted. Note the voltage rating is independent of the current rating: a 250 V / 5 A fuse interrupts 5 A overloads and withstands up to 250 V across the open gap. Use a fuse on a circuit at or below its voltage rating; using one rated higher is safe, lower is not.", "misconception": "Assuming the voltage rating limits the operating voltage the fuse can carry (like a conductor's insulation), when it actually defines the open-circuit arc-interruption capability after the fuse blows.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Voltage rating = the voltage it can BREAK without arcing, not the voltage it carries.", "edge_cases": "DC fuses are typically rated for lower voltage than AC fuses of the same size, because DC has no zero crossing to help self-extinguish the arc, making interruption harder."}

{"core": "Grounding antenna and rotator cables when the station is idle gives any induced lightning energy (or static buildup) a low-impedance path to earth instead of through your equipment and house wiring, reducing fire and shock hazards.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Locking a rotator is a mechanical/control function unrelated to grounding the cables; an earth connection does nothing to fix antenna heading.", "why_tempting": "The question mentions 'rotator cables,' so a learner may associate grounding with stopping rotation."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "RFI is addressed by filtering, bonding, and shielding during operation — not by disconnecting and grounding the feedlines while the station is off.", "why_tempting": "Grounding is genuinely part of RFI mitigation, so the word 'ground' makes this plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Physical security against unauthorized use is achieved by locking out the transmitter or controls, not by earthing antenna cables.", "why_tempting": "'When your station is not in use' sounds security-related, nudging toward access control."}], "concept": "Lightning need not strike the antenna directly — a nearby strike or charged cloud induces large transient voltages on long conductors (feedlines, rotator control lines). The protective strategy is twofold: during a storm, disconnect feeds from gear and shunt them to a good ground/single-point ground panel so the surge bypasses sensitive electronics. Bonding all grounds together prevents large potential differences (ground loops) that can arc destructively. The goal is to keep the energy outside, on its way to earth, rather than letting it find a path through your rig and building wiring.", "misconception": "Confusing electrical/lightning safety grounding with operational functions (rotor control, RFI suppression, or station security) just because the word 'ground' or 'not in use' appears.", "link": "section 3", "edge_cases": "Best practice is to physically disconnect cables and route them to an external single-point ground entry panel; merely connecting to a poor or shared ground can still allow damaging voltage differences."}

{"core": "A lightning surge protector belongs outside the building, mounted at the point where the coax enters and bonded to the same ground (earth) system as your station/electrical ground, so surge energy is shunted to earth before it can travel indoors.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Mounting at the antenna gives the diverted surge no low-impedance path to earth there and still lets energy travel down the line into the building.", "why_tempting": "It feels like 'intercept the strike first,' closest to where lightning hits."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "By the time the line is inside and behind the transceiver, the surge is already in the building — protecting after the entry point defeats the purpose and risks routing current through your equipment and house wiring.", "why_tempting": "It seems to directly protect the radio, which is the thing you care about."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Placement is not arbitrary; a protector only works if it has a short, low-inductance bond to an earth ground, which exists at the entry/grounding point.", "why_tempting": "A protector sounds like it works wherever it's in the circuit."}], "concept": "A coax surge protector (gas-discharge tube or similar) clamps and diverts transient overvoltage to ground. Its effectiveness depends on the impedance of the path to earth: at lightning's fast dV/dt the inductance of the ground lead dominates (V = L·di/dt), so the bond to the grounding electrode must be short and direct. The correct topology is single-point grounding at the cable entry panel — all coax, rotor, and control lines pass through protectors bonded to a common ground that ties to the building electrode system. This shunts surge energy to earth at the perimeter and minimizes the potential difference that would otherwise drive current through the station.", "misconception": "That a surge protector 'catches' lightning wherever placed; in reality it only diverts energy effectively when located at the entry point with a low-inductance bond to earth ground.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Stop the surge at the door — protect where the line meets the ground, before it enters.", "edge_cases": "The protector ground, station equipment ground, and AC service ground should be bonded together to avoid dangerous ground-potential differences during a strike."}

{"core": "Physically disconnecting the equipment from both the AC mains and the antenna feedlines removes the conductive paths a lightning surge would otherwise travel into your radios, giving the best protection.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Removing the ground actually makes things worse — a good low-impedance ground bonded to a proper protection system is part of lightning safety, not something to disconnect.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like you're 'breaking a path' for the surge, but the surge will simply find another, more destructive route through your gear."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Lightning involves millions of volts; ordinary 'heavy insulation' on wiring will not stand off that potential and does nothing about surges already coupled onto the antenna or power leads.", "why_tempting": "Insulation intuitively means protection from voltage, so it seems like a reasonable defensive measure."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Whether equipment is powered on or off is irrelevant — if it remains connected to the antenna and mains, a strike can still destroy it.", "why_tempting": "It plays on the vague idea that 'leaving it running' keeps it safe or that switching it off is the danger."}], "concept": "Lightning damage to a station comes from surge energy conducted in along the antenna feedline and the AC power line (and sometimes the ground/telecom paths). No practical in-line device fully clamps a direct or near strike; surge arrestors and bonding reduce risk but the only certain protection is to open every conductive path so there is no circuit for the energy to enter. The standard practice: before a storm, unplug the AC cords and disconnect coax feedlines (ideally routing the disconnected coax to an outside ground point). This galvanically isolates the equipment.", "misconception": "Believing that grounding, insulation, or power state alone protects gear, when in fact only complete disconnection of all conductive paths (power AND antenna) reliably prevents surge entry.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Storm coming? PULL THE PLUG and PULL THE COAX.", "edge_cases": "Bonding and surge arrestors are still valuable for safety and for transients you can't anticipate; full disconnection is the 'best' answer here but is a manual, pre-storm measure, not a substitute for a properly grounded station."}

{"core": "Climbing or working at height on an antenna tower requires approved fall arrest equipment (full-body harness, lanyard, and anchorage) to prevent a fatal fall.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A reflective vest improves visibility but does nothing to stop a fall from height, which is the real hazard on a tower.", "why_tempting": "Reflective vests are standard PPE on many job sites, so it feels like the 'safety gear' answer."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Insulating gloves protect against electrical contact, not falling; they don't address the primary risk of working aloft.", "why_tempting": "Towers can carry RF and power, so electrical protection seems plausible."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A positioning belt only holds you at a work location and is not rated to arrest a free fall — it can cause serious injury if used as fall protection.", "why_tempting": "It physically attaches you to the tower, so it superficially looks like fall protection."}], "concept": "Working at height demands fall protection rated to arrest a fall, not merely position the worker. Approved fall arrest systems use a full-body harness (distributing arrest forces across the body) connected by a shock-absorbing lanyard to a rated anchor point, limiting arresting force and preventing the worker from striking the ground. A positioning belt, by contrast, concentrates force on the abdomen and is only intended to support a worker who is already secured, not to catch a fall.", "misconception": "Confusing positioning/work-support gear (belt) or generic PPE (vest, gloves) with actual fall arrest equipment. The exam tests recognition that height is the dominant hazard.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Arrest the fall, not just position the body — choose the harness.", "edge_cases": "In practice a positioning belt may be worn in addition to a fall arrest harness, but it never substitutes for one."}

{"core": "Fall arrest equipment (harness, lanyard, anchor) exists to catch you and limit injury if you slip or fall from height — its sole purpose is personal fall protection.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Hauling tools is done with a separate rope/bucket system, not your fall arrest harness.", "why_tempting": "Tool handling is a real tower-work concern, so it sounds plausibly related to climbing gear."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A harness anchored to the tower does nothing to stabilize the structure; tower stability comes from guy wires and proper footing.", "why_tempting": "It conflates personal safety equipment with structural integrity."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A tool lanyard or pouch keeps tools from dropping — that's a different piece of equipment from fall arrest gear protecting you.", "why_tempting": "Dropped tools are a genuine ground hazard, blurring with the idea of 'arrest' meaning catching things."}], "concept": "Fall arrest is the last line of personal protection in working-at-height safety. The harness distributes arrest forces across the body and the lanyard/shock absorber limits peak deceleration force, while the anchor point and connectors hold you above the ground. It protects the worker, not the tower or the tools — those have their own controls (guy wires, tool lanyards, hoist lines, ground exclusion zones).", "misconception": "Thinking the gear serves a structural or tool-management role rather than its true single purpose: protecting the person from a fall.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "'Fall arrest' = arrests YOUR fall.", "edge_cases": "Equipment only works if anchored to a sound point and worn correctly; an unrated anchor or excessive free-fall distance can still cause serious injury."}
