{"core": "A horizontal wire antenna carries RF voltage and current, and during transmission can deliver RF burns or shock; it must be high enough that no person can reach any part of it from the ground.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Routing an antenna above high-voltage power lines is extremely dangerous — a broken or sagging wire could contact the mains, energizing your station with lethal voltage.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like 'higher is safer,' but the real danger is proximity to power lines, not height for its own sake."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "If you can easily reach it, so can a bystander; an antenna at hand-height exposes people to RF voltage and burns during transmit.", "why_tempting": "Convenience for maintenance feels practical, but safety, not ease of repair, governs the minimum height."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Mounting close to the ground maximizes the chance of accidental contact and is the least safe option (and also degrades performance).", "why_tempting": "Lower seems easier to install, and a learner may confuse mechanical accessibility with safety."}], "concept": "An antenna is a high-impedance point at voltage maxima and a high-current point at current maxima. On transmit, even modest power develops significant RF potential along the wire. RF energy doesn't trigger the same muscular reflexes as mains AC but readily causes deep thermal burns, and contact can also disrupt your equipment. The safety rule is simple and absolute: keep all parts of the radiating element out of reach of people (and clear of utility lines), so accidental contact is impossible.", "misconception": "That antenna height is chosen mainly for radio performance or installation convenience. Here the question is purely about personal safety — preventing human contact with an energized conductor.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Out of reach, out of danger.", "edge_cases": "Beyond shock/burn risk, keep wire antennas well clear of power lines so a fallen antenna cannot contact the mains — a separate but equally critical hazard."}

{"core": "A hard hat protects a ground worker from tools, hardware, or other objects that can fall from someone working up on the tower — the most likely hazard for a person standing below.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A hard hat offers no meaningful protection against a falling tower; the correct precaution there is to stay clear of the fall radius entirely.", "why_tempting": "Tower collapse is a dramatic, plausible-sounding tower hazard, so it draws attention away from the everyday risk of dropped objects."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A hard hat is a non-shielding insulator/plastic and does nothing to attenuate RF; RF exposure is managed by power, distance, and not transmitting during work.", "why_tempting": "It mixes in an amateur-radio-specific concept (RF safety) to seem like the 'technical' answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Warning passers-by is the job of signage, barriers, and a designated spotter — not personal protective headgear.", "why_tempting": "High-visibility safety gear does sometimes signal active work, blurring the line between PPE and warning devices."}], "concept": "Tower work safety is built on a hazard hierarchy. For the person on the ground, the dominant risk is gravity acting on small objects: a dropped wrench falling even 10–20 m arrives with substantial kinetic energy (E = mgh), enough to cause serious head injury. Personal protective equipment is chosen to match the realistic hazard at each position — the climber uses a harness against falls, the ground crew uses a hard hat against falling objects.", "misconception": "Assuming PPE addresses the most spectacular hazard (tower collapse) or an antenna-specific one (RF), rather than the statistically dominant, position-specific hazard for the ground worker.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Hard hat = falling stuff from above; harness = falling yourself.", "edge_cases": "Best practice also includes keeping the drop zone clear and having ground crew stand outside the area directly beneath the climber whenever possible."}

{"core": "At RF, voltage maxima on a radiating antenna can be very high, and direct contact lets RF current flow through skin/tissue, causing localized heating known as an RF burn. Keeping antennas out of reach prevents this.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "TVI is caused by spurious emissions, fundamental overload, or poor filtering — not by someone physically touching the antenna.", "why_tempting": "Interference is a common antenna-related topic, so it sounds plausible as an antenna hazard."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A hand touching the antenna detunes it and alters SWR, but it does not 'reflect the signal back' in a way that damages the transmitter; this misuses transmission-line reflection concepts.", "why_tempting": "Reflected power and SWR damage are real concerns, so the wording borrows credible jargon."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Harmonics are generated in the transmitter/amplifier by nonlinearity, not by a person touching the antenna.", "why_tempting": "Harmonics are associated with antennas and emissions, so it superficially fits."}], "concept": "On a resonant antenna, the standing-wave pattern produces high RF voltage at current nulls (e.g. wire ends, the tips of a dipole). Touching these points couples your body into the circuit. RF current at these frequencies penetrates tissue and dissipates power as heat (P = I²R through skin contact resistance), producing a deep, painful burn rather than the muscle-tetanizing shock of mains AC. The hazard scales with transmitter power and is worst at high-impedance (high-voltage) points.", "misconception": "Believing the main contact danger is an electric shock or an effect on the signal, rather than thermal RF burns to the person.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Touch the antenna while transmitting = RF Burn.", "edge_cases": "Risk is highest at voltage maxima (antenna tips) and rises with power; even modest power can burn at a high-impedance point."}

{"core": "Open-wire feedline carries the full RF voltage to the antenna; on a high-impedance line this voltage can reach hundreds of volts, and touching it during transmission can cause a painful RF burn.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Light human contact does not mechanically break a feedline; this confuses electrical hazard with physical damage.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you picture someone grabbing or pulling the wire."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Touching the line can detune it and change loading, but the safety concern the question targets is personal injury, not signal purity.", "why_tempting": "Detuning/reflections are real, so 'spurious emissions' feels technically reasonable."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A modern transmitter is protected by SWR/foldback and won't be destroyed by a momentary touch; again the real issue is the person, not the rig.", "why_tempting": "Equipment-damage answers are common and the body does load the line."}], "concept": "On a balanced open-wire line feeding a high-SWR antenna, voltage maxima occur at standing-wave antinodes. Since V = sqrt(P·Zo) scaled by the voltage standing-wave ratio, even modest power on a high-impedance (e.g. 300–600 ohm) line can produce RF potentials of hundreds of volts. RF energy couples into tissue and dissipates as heat, producing deep thermal burns that may not be felt immediately because RF can bypass the pain-sensing nerve response at low frequencies' threshold. The safety priority is always protecting people first.", "misconception": "Assuming the danger of an energized feedline is to the equipment or signal, when the exam's RF-safety emphasis is on bodily harm (RF burns).", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "People before parts: the burn beats the breaker.", "edge_cases": "RF burns differ from low-frequency shock — they are localized heating, can be deep, and the line may feel 'cold' yet still carry hazardous RF voltage."}

{"core": "Before working on an antenna you must remove all RF energy from the system: power down the transmitter and disconnect the feedline so the antenna cannot become energized while you are in contact with it.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Grounding the structure (for lightning/static) is good practice but does nothing to protect you from RF or transmitter output during repair; the live risk is energized RF on the conductor you're touching.", "why_tempting": "Grounding is a real and important antenna safety topic, so it sounds like the correct 'safety' answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Minimizing time addresses fatigue, not the electrical hazard of an energized antenna; speed is not the primary safety control before starting work.", "why_tempting": "Reducing time aloft does reduce fall/fatigue risk, so it reads as sensible safety advice."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Light masts ease handling but say nothing about de-energizing the system; this is a mechanical convenience, not the key electrical precaution.", "why_tempting": "Lighter masts are easier and arguably safer to handle, making it superficially plausible."}], "concept": "An antenna and its feedline are the RF output path. If the transmitter is keyed, the antenna carries RF voltage/current that can cause RF burns or shock. The decisive precaution is to interrupt that path: switch off the transmitter and physically disconnect the transmission line so accidental keying or stored energy cannot energize the conductors you're handling. This is the electrical-isolation principle (lockout-style thinking) applied to RF work.", "misconception": "Assuming 'antenna safety' is mainly about grounding or mechanical handling, when the immediate hazard during repair is an electrically live antenna.", "link": "section 3", "edge_cases": "Disconnecting the feedline also protects against induced voltages from nearby transmitters or static buildup, not just your own rig."}

{"core": "A ground-mounted antenna can produce high RF field intensities and dangerously high RF voltages at its base where people can easily reach it, so keeping people at a safe distance is the key safety precaution.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Burying radials at a particular depth is an installation/performance detail, not a personal safety precaution, and the depth quoted has no special safety significance.", "why_tempting": "Radials are a real part of ground-mounted vertical systems, so it sounds like proper practice."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Putting the feed point at eye level would actually increase exposure risk by placing high RF voltage and field strength right where people are; it is the opposite of good practice.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like it improves accessibility for maintenance or inspection."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A dry location relates to insulation or corrosion, not to the primary RF/contact hazard near a ground-level radiator.", "why_tempting": "Dryness is generally associated with electrical safety, so it feels plausible."}], "concept": "With an antenna mounted at ground level, the high-current/high-voltage regions of the radiator (and the strongest near-field) sit within human reach. The base of a resonant element can develop hundreds of volts of RF, capable of causing RF burns on contact, while the surrounding near-field can exceed exposure limits. Unlike a tower- or roof-mounted antenna where height provides natural separation, a ground mount has none, so the controlling mitigation is physical distance — fencing or otherwise excluding people from the area.", "misconception": "Treating the question as an installation-optimization problem (radials, dryness, mounting height) rather than recognizing it asks specifically about protecting people from RF burns and field exposure.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Ground level = people level — keep them back.", "edge_cases": "Exposure limits depend on frequency, power, and duty cycle; even modest power into a ground-mounted vertical can warrant an exclusion zone during transmit."}

{"core": "At UHF and microwave frequencies RF energy is easily concentrated into a beam, and the eyes are especially vulnerable to thermal heating because they have poor blood circulation to dissipate heat. Keeping the antenna away from your eyes when transmitting avoids this hazard.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Polarization (horizontal vs vertical) is a propagation/antenna-matching concern, not a personal RF-safety measure.", "why_tempting": "Polarization is a real antenna topic, so it sounds plausibly 'technical' in a safety context."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "There is no such device as an 'RF leakage filter' at a feed point; proper safety comes from physical separation and limiting exposure, not a mystery filter.", "why_tempting": "It uses convincing jargon — filters and leakage sound like legitimate engineering fixes."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A low SWR protects the transmitter and improves efficiency, but it does nothing to reduce biological RF exposure to the operator.", "why_tempting": "Checking SWR before transmitting is genuinely good practice, so it feels like the responsible answer."}], "concept": "RF biological hazard is dominated by tissue heating, and the deposited power density rises with frequency for a given field strength because absorption increases as the wavelength shrinks toward body dimensions. At UHF/microwave, high-gain antennas focus power into narrow beams producing high localized power density (W/m\nineye lacks the vascular cooling that other tissue uses to carry heat away, making it the critical organ for microwave exposure — the same physics behind microwave-oven safety. The mitigation is exposure control: distance and aiming the beam away from people.", "misconception": "That RF safety at high frequencies is solved by an electrical fix (filter, low SWR) or an antenna property (polarization), rather than by controlling where the energy is pointed and how close you are to it.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Eyes cook first — keep the beam off your face.", "edge_cases": "The hazard scales with transmit power and antenna gain; low-power handhelds at these frequencies are far less dangerous than a high-gain dish or a transmitting waveguide aperture."}

{"core": "At UHF, RF exposure limits can be exceeded close to a transmitting antenna, so the safe practice is to locate the antenna where people cannot approach it during transmission, keeping anyone in the antenna's near field below safe RF exposure thresholds.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Mounting an antenna near the ground neither makes the radiation pattern 'correct' nor improves safety—it actually puts people closer to the high-field region, increasing exposure risk.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like it addresses directivity and proximity, and learners may conflate antenna height with pattern control."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "There is no such device as an 'RF leakage filter' at a feed point; a properly fed antenna is supposed to radiate, so 'leakage' is a nonsense concept here.", "why_tempting": "The official-sounding term mimics real components like low-pass or harmonic filters, baiting those who don't parse it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "'RF field screens' are not a standard amateur installation practice for an outdoor transmitting antenna; shielding a radiating antenna defeats its purpose.", "why_tempting": "Shielding is a genuine EMC technique, so it feels plausible as a safety measure."}], "concept": "RF exposure scales with power density, which falls off with distance from the radiating element. UHF wavelengths are short, so a given physical antenna is electrically large and concentrates energy; the controlling and uncontrolled exposure limits (in W/m²) are most easily exceeded in the near field. The simplest, most reliable mitigation is separation distance—placing the antenna where no person can be in the high-density region while keying up. This is a physical control rather than a circuit fix.", "misconception": "That RF safety is achieved by adding a filtering/shielding component at the antenna, rather than by managing human proximity and exposure distance.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Distance is your defence—keep people out of the near field.", "edge_cases": "Higher power and higher gain antennas extend the distance over which exposure limits apply, so the required clearance grows with both."}

{"core": "Before opening a UHF power amplifier you must guarantee it cannot be energized — disconnect/lock out the power so no RF voltage, high DC plate voltage, or RF radiation can be present while your hands are inside.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "RF leakage filters do not protect you while the shield is off; they relate to suppressing emissions, not preventing accidental power-up.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a sensible RF-safety precaution and uses familiar shielding/filter vocabulary."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Grounding only the output connector does nothing to remove the hazardous DC supply or RF drive that energize the circuitry you're about to touch.", "why_tempting": "Grounding is a real safety practice, so it feels like the right reflex."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "RF screens at the output address radiation containment, not the shock/RF-burn hazard from a live amplifier with its shielding removed.", "why_tempting": "The question mentions shielding, so 'RF screens in place' seems topically matched."}], "concept": "A UHF PA combines two serious hazards: high DC supply voltages (capable of lethal shock, with charged filter/bypass capacitors retaining voltage) and high-level RF energy (RF burns and tissue heating). Removing the metal shielding exposes both. The only measure that eliminates the root cause is ensuring the unit cannot be powered — pull the plug, remove the supply, and ideally bleed/short any storage capacitors. Containment-type measures (filters, screens, output grounding) assume the device is operating safely behind its enclosure and are irrelevant once you are working inside a de-energized chassis.", "misconception": "Confusing measures that contain RF emissions (filters, screens, grounded connectors) with measures that protect the technician working inside an opened amplifier — the latter requires removing the energy source, not redirecting it.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Open it = OFF it: kill power before you peel the shield.", "edge_cases": "Even after switch-off, treat the PA as live until filter/plate capacitors are confirmed discharged — stored charge can be lethal long after power is removed."}

{"core": "Holding a transmitting antenna close to your head puts tissue in the near field where RF power density is highest, so keeping it away reduces your RF energy exposure.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Static charge buildup is not the concern with a transmitting handheld; the issue is absorbed RF energy, not electrostatic discharge.", "why_tempting": "Antennas and static are vaguely associated in many people's minds, so it sounds plausible."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Your head doesn't make a whip radiate equally in all directions; a nearby lossy body actually distorts and absorbs the pattern rather than improving it.", "why_tempting": "It correctly references radiation patterns, which feels technical and relevant."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Deliberately using your body to beam the signal is not a goal of handheld operation, and tissue absorbs rather than cleanly reflects RF.", "why_tempting": "Bodies do reflect/absorb RF and influence the pattern, so it sounds like a real effect being used purposefully."}], "concept": "In the reactive near field of an antenna, power density falls off rapidly with distance and tissue absorbs RF energy, characterized by Specific Absorption Rate (SAR, W/kg). At VHF/UHF (2 m, 70 cm) the head's dimensions are an appreciable fraction of a wavelength, so coupling and heating can be significant even at a few watts. Maximizing separation drops the field at the body roughly with distance and reduces absorbed power, keeping exposure below safe limits.", "misconception": "That moving the antenna away is about improving radiation efficiency or signal directivity, when it is purely a personal-safety/RF-exposure measure.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Keep RF off your head: distance is your dose-reducer.", "edge_cases": "The effect scales with power and frequency — low-power handhelds are usually within limits, but high duty-cycle or high-power transmissions make separation more important."}

{"core": "A hand-held transmitter's antenna radiates RF energy right next to your body; keeping it away from your head and from bystanders minimizes RF exposure to sensitive tissue.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "For a vertical FM whip, the maximum gain is broadside (toward the horizon) only when the antenna is held vertically; aiming the tip 'at the horizon' tilts it and actually degrades the radiation pattern, and it does nothing for safety.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a sensible way to maximize useful range toward distant stations."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Pointing down does not produce a useful ground-bounce at VHF/UHF; it mainly wastes radiated power into the ground and distorts the pattern.", "why_tempting": "It echoes HF/skywave ideas about reflecting signals, which don't apply to a line-of-sight handheld."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A vertical whip is omnidirectional in the horizontal plane and has a null off its tip, so 'aiming' the end toward a station actually points the weakest part of the pattern at them.", "why_tempting": "Directional antennas like Yagis are aimed at the target, so it seems intuitive to do the same with a whip."}], "concept": "RF exposure falls off rapidly with distance from the radiating element (roughly with the inverse of distance in the near field). With a handheld, the antenna sits centimetres from the operator, so even a few watts can create meaningful field strength at the head and eyes. The mitigation is geometric: increase separation between the antenna and people. Note the antenna-pattern reality too — a short vertical whip radiates omnidirectionally broadside with a null off the tip, so 'pointing' it at a station gives no benefit; the real considerations are vertical orientation for pattern and physical distance for safety.", "misconception": "Treating a handheld whip like a beam antenna that should be 'aimed,' instead of recognizing the dominant concern is RF safety near the body.", "link": "section 3", "edge_cases": "Higher transmit power, longer transmissions, and close proximity all increase exposure — a speaker-mic or remote antenna lets you keep the radiator further from your head."}

{"core": "RF energy absorbed by biological tissue is converted to heat through dielectric and resistive losses, so the dominant non-ionizing hazard of high-level RF exposure is tissue heating.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Paralysis is a neuromuscular effect not produced by RF field absorption; RF does not block nerve conduction in the way implied.", "why_tempting": "People associate strong electrical exposure with shock/paralysis, conflating low-frequency electrocution effects with RF."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "RF does not mechanically constrict vessels; if anything, local heating tends to dilate blood vessels, not restrict flow.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a plausible physiological harm and mirrors heat-related circulatory responses."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "RF exposure has no established mechanism for lowering blood pressure; this is unrelated to field absorption.", "why_tempting": "It's a generic 'bad health effect' that test-takers may pick when unsure."}], "concept": "At RF, the human body acts as a lossy dielectric. Oscillating fields drive conduction currents and dipole reorientation in water and electrolytes; both dissipate power as heat (P = SAR x mass). The key regulatory metric is Specific Absorption Rate (W/kg). Heating is greatest where tissue is poorly perfused (e.g. the eye's lens) because blood flow can't carry heat away, which is why thermal limits and exposure boundaries exist. This is a non-ionizing effect: RF photons lack the energy to break chemical bonds, so the hazard is thermal rather than ionization damage.", "misconception": "Assuming RF harm resembles electrocution or some exotic neurological effect, rather than recognizing it as simple thermal heating of tissue.", "link": "section 3", "edge_cases": "Heating is non-uniform; sensitive low-perfusion tissues like the eyes and testes are most vulnerable, which is why MPE/SAR limits matter even when total absorbed power seems modest."}

{"core": "The eyes are the body part most vulnerable to RF heating because the lens and surrounding ocular tissue have very poor blood circulation, so they cannot dissipate absorbed heat efficiently and can be cooked by relatively modest RF exposure.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Hands are richly vascularized and well able to carry away heat; they are not the most susceptible organ to RF thermal damage.", "why_tempting": "Hands are often the body part physically closest to antennas and feedlines, so they seem like the obvious site of injury."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The heart sits deep within the torso, shielded by tissue, and has abundant blood flow for cooling; it is not the prime target of RF heating.", "why_tempting": "Being a vital organ, the heart feels like the most 'dangerous' thing to damage, but danger of an outcome isn't the same as susceptibility to RF heating."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The liver is deep, heavily perfused, and highly effective at dissipating heat via blood flow, so it is not the most easily damaged by RF.", "why_tempting": "The liver is large and metabolically active, which can be mistaken for thermal vulnerability."}], "concept": "RF energy absorbed by tissue is converted to heat. The body normally regulates temperature by carrying heat away in the bloodstream. Tissues with little or no blood supply cannot shed this heat and so experience a localized temperature rise for a given specific absorption rate (SAR). The lens of the eye is essentially avascular, making it a classic 'hot spot' — chronic or intense RF exposure can produce cataracts. This is why exposure limits and safe-distance practices around transmitting antennas matter.", "misconception": "Assuming the part nearest the antenna (the hands) or the most life-critical organ (heart) is most at risk, rather than the part least able to cool itself.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "No blood flow = no cooling: the avascular eye lens overheats first.", "edge_cases": "This applies to the thermal (heating) hazard of RF; it is distinct from any non-thermal effects, and actual risk depends on frequency, power density, and exposure time."}

{"core": "In free space, radiated power spreads over the surface of an expanding sphere, whose area grows as r². Since the same total power is distributed over an area ∝ r², power density (W/m²) falls off as 1/r² — the inverse-square law.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Free-space propagation by definition has no ground or medium losses; the decrease is purely geometric spreading, not absorption.", "why_tempting": "Real-world paths do involve ground absorption, so it sounds physically plausible if you forget the question specifies free space."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Power density does not fall linearly with distance; that would imply a far slower decay than the actual spherical spreading.", "why_tempting": "Linear decay is the simplest intuitive guess and easy to confuse with the field strength relationship."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Inverse proportion to distance (1/r) describes the electric/magnetic FIELD strength, not the power density. Power density is proportional to E², so it goes as 1/r².", "why_tempting": "The field amplitude really does fall as 1/r, so this is the classic mix-up between field and power."}], "concept": "Consider a point source radiating power P_t isotropically. At distance r the energy is spread over a sphere of area A = 4πr². Power density S = P_t/(4πr²), so S ∝ 1/r². Because power density is proportional to the square of the field strength (S = E²/η₀, with η₀ ≈ 377 Ω), the field E ∝ 1/r while S ∝ 1/r². Doubling distance quarters the power density (−6 dB), but only halves the field strength.", "misconception": "Confusing how field strength decays (1/r, inverse-distance) with how power density decays (1/r², inverse-square). Both are correct statements about different quantities.", "link": "Propagation & the Ionosphere", "mnemonic": "Field falls as 1/r, Power falls as 1/r² — power is field squared, so its exponent is too.", "edge_cases": "This strict 1/r² law applies only in free space (the far field). Near ground, reflections, ducting, or focusing can make real-world power density deviate substantially."}

{"core": "Indoor antennas radiate RF energy in their near field, so they should be installed as far as practical from areas where people will be present during transmission to keep RF exposure low.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Minimizing feedline length puts the radiating element right at your operating position, maximizing your RF field exposure—the opposite of good practice.", "why_tempting": "Short feedlines genuinely reduce line loss, so it sounds like sound engineering advice."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Running an antenna parallel and close to power wiring causes coupling, RFI, and ground-loop problems—there is no useful 'parasitic' benefit, and it raises exposure and interference.", "why_tempting": "'Parasitic effects' echoes real Yagi parasitic-element theory, making it sound technical."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Tucking an antenna into wall/floor/ceiling junctions detunes it, increases nearby coupling and structural losses, and does nothing to 'reduce parasitic radiation,' which isn't a real concern here.", "why_tempting": "It invents plausible-sounding jargon and appeals to the instinct to hide an unsightly indoor antenna."}], "concept": "RF field strength falls off rapidly with distance—roughly as 1/r for the near field of a small antenna and as 1/r² for power density in the far field. Because an indoor antenna can't be elevated away from people the way an outdoor antenna can, the only practical RF-safety lever is horizontal/vertical separation from occupied living space. The goal is keeping human exposure below the applicable RF exposure limits, which depend on power, frequency, duty cycle, and distance.", "misconception": "Assuming antenna installation is purely about electrical performance (feedline loss, tuning) rather than human RF exposure—indoors, safety distance dominates.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Distance is your friend: power density drops fast with separation.", "edge_cases": "Lower transmit power and reduced duty cycle (e.g., SSB/CW vs. continuous carrier) also cut exposure when distance is limited."}

{"core": "A directional high-gain antenna concentrates RF energy into a focused beam; mounting it above nearby structures keeps that main lobe from intersecting occupied spaces, reducing human RF exposure.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Static buildup is managed by bleed resistors, DC grounding paths, and lightning protection — not by mounting height relative to nearby buildings.", "why_tempting": "Height does relate to lightning/static concerns generally, so it sounds plausible if you conflate exposure with electrical safety."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Typical amateur power levels and a fixed antenna structure do not 'damage' buildings; the concern is biological RF exposure to people, not structural harm.", "why_tempting": "It correctly senses RF energy is the issue, but misattributes the hazard to property rather than people."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Sky-wave vs ground-wave performance depends on frequency, takeoff angle, and ground conditions, and isn't the safety rationale the question asks about.", "why_tempting": "Raising an antenna does improve propagation and lower the takeoff angle, so this is a real benefit — just not the answer to the exposure-focused question."}], "concept": "Antenna gain doesn't create power; it redistributes radiated power directionally, raising the effective radiated power (ERP) in the main lobe. Power density falls as 1/r² with distance, but in the near field of a focused high-gain beam the local field strength can be substantial. By placing the antenna above nearby occupied structures, the high-density main lobe passes over people rather than through them, lowering exposure to comply with RF safety limits.", "misconception": "Assuming the height requirement is about propagation or equipment protection, when the exam's safety context targets minimizing human RF exposure in the antenna's concentrated main beam.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "High gain = focused beam; mount it high so the beam clears the people.", "edge_cases": "Mounting higher reduces exposure to nearby occupants but increases ERP toward the horizon; exposure compliance still depends on power, gain, frequency and distance, not height alone."}

{"core": "RF exposure falls off rapidly with distance from the radiating element, so mounting a dipole as high as possible keeps the highest-field regions (the ends, where voltage is greatest, and the centre feedpoint) well above any area where people stand.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Placing the antenna near ground level puts the high-field regions right where people and pets are, maximizing RF exposure — the opposite of safe.", "why_tempting": "Easy mechanical access sounds practical and is genuinely convenient, so a learner may prioritize maintenance over safety."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Soil moisture affects ground conductivity and pattern/efficiency, not whether people are exposed; it has nothing to do with keeping the energetic parts away from bodies.", "why_tempting": "It uses real RF-engineering vocabulary (ground conductivity, radiation), which sounds technically authoritative."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Concentrating RF near the transmitter — and thus near the operator — increases human exposure rather than reducing it.", "why_tempting": "It echoes a vague intuition about 'keeping energy contained,' but that is contrary to RF safety goals."}], "concept": "RF field intensity (and the resulting power density) drops sharply as you move away from a radiator — in the near field roughly with distance, and power density in the far field as 1/r². A dipole's ends are high-impedance, high-voltage points and the centre is the high-current feedpoint, so both are regions of elevated field strength. The safety strategy is simple geometry: increase the separation distance between these strong-field regions and any person. Raising the antenna increases that minimum separation everywhere a person could stand, lowering the exposure they receive without changing the transmitter power.", "misconception": "Thinking RF safety is about ground type, accessibility for tuning, or 'containing' energy near the rig, when it is fundamentally about maximizing the distance between people and the high-field parts of the antenna.", "link": "section 3", "mnemonic": "Higher antenna, lower exposure — distance is the cheapest shielding.", "edge_cases": "Height aids exposure safety but does not eliminate compliance duties; even a high antenna can require distance/power evaluation, and very high installations introduce tower-climbing and structural hazards of their own."}

{"core": "An amplifier is the circuit whose purpose is to increase the amplitude (voltage, current, or power) of an input signal, typically by using active devices to control a larger output drawn from a power supply.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A modulator imposes information onto a carrier by varying one of its parameters (amplitude, frequency, or phase) — it changes the signal's character, not its amplitude as an end goal.", "why_tempting": "AM (amplitude modulation) literally contains 'amplitude,' so it's easy to associate modulation with raising amplitude."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An oscillator generates a periodic signal from DC; it creates a waveform rather than amplifying an existing input.", "why_tempting": "Oscillators use the same active gain devices and feedback as amplifiers, so they feel related to boosting signals."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A frequency multiplier produces an output that is an integer multiple of the input frequency — it operates on frequency, not amplitude.", "why_tempting": "The word 'multiplier' suggests making something larger, which a learner may misread as larger amplitude."}], "concept": "Amplification is the controlled transfer of power from a DC supply into the signal path, governed by an active device (BJT, FET, or tube). Gain is the ratio of output to input — voltage gain Av = Vout/Vin, or power gain often expressed in dB as 10·log10(Pout/Pin). The active device does not 'create' energy; it modulates a larger DC current/voltage in proportion to a small input, so the output is a scaled replica of the input within the linear region. This distinguishes it functionally from oscillators (signal generation), modulators (information encoding), and multipliers (frequency conversion).", "misconception": "Assuming any circuit with a 'bigger/more' connotation (multiplier, modulator) increases amplitude; the defining trait of an amplifier is raising amplitude of an existing input signal.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Amplifier = Amplitude up.", "edge_cases": "Not all gain stages have voltage gain >1 — e.g., an emitter/source follower has near-unity voltage gain but provides power/current gain (impedance buffering), and is still an amplifier."}

{"core": "Linearity means the output is a faithful, scaled copy of the input. When an amplifier loses linearity, the transfer function deviates from a straight line, so the output waveform no longer matches the input shape — it becomes distorted.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Non-linearity changes the waveform shape; it does not eliminate the output. A zero output indicates total cutoff or failure, not non-linear operation.", "why_tempting": "Severe clipping can feel like 'losing' the signal, so a learner may overstate the effect."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Oscillation is caused by unwanted positive feedback (in-phase signal fed back to the input), not by the amplifier's transfer curve being non-linear.", "why_tempting": "Both are 'bad amplifier' symptoms, so they're easily conflated, and non-linearity can coexist with instability."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Non-linearity is about the input-output transfer characteristic, not about drawing excessive current from the supply.", "why_tempting": "'Overload' sounds like a fault condition, and overdriving an amp does increase distortion, blurring cause and effect."}], "concept": "An ideal amplifier obeys Vout = A·Vin, a straight line through the operating region. Real devices deviate when driven into saturation/cutoff or by curvature in the device characteristics, giving Vout = A1·Vin + A2·Vin² + A3·Vin³ + … The higher-order terms (A2, A3 …) generate harmonics and, with multiple input tones, intermodulation products. The result is a waveform whose shape differs from the input — i.e. distortion — including spurious emissions and possible splatter outside the intended channel. Class-A bias and keeping the signal within the linear region minimize these terms.", "misconception": "Equating 'non-linear' with the amplifier failing or going silent, rather than understanding it as a faithfulness problem in the transfer function that distorts the waveform and creates harmonics.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Non-linear = not a straight line = the output bends out of shape = distortion.", "edge_cases": "Some amplifiers (e.g. Class C, frequency multipliers) deliberately run non-linear; there the harmonic content is wanted, but for SSB/linear service that same distortion is harmful."}

{"core": "Signals arriving from an antenna are at radio frequencies, so an RF amplifier is the stage that boosts weak RF before any later processing (mixing, detection).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "An oscillator generates a signal; it doesn't amplify an incoming one, and an audio oscillator operates at AF, not RF.", "why_tempting": "'Oscillator' sounds active and signal-related, but oscillators produce signals rather than strengthen received ones."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An audio amplifier works at audio frequencies (roughly 20 Hz–20 kHz), far below the RF coming off the antenna, so it can't amplify the radio signal itself.", "why_tempting": "It's an amplifier, which is the right action — but the frequency band is wrong; audio amplification happens after detection, not at the antenna."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "An RF oscillator generates an RF signal (e.g. a local oscillator) but does not amplify a received signal.", "why_tempting": "It has 'RF' in it, matching the band, but oscillators create rather than amplify."}], "concept": "A receiver front end places a low-noise RF amplifier right after the antenna because amplification at this stage sets the system noise figure (Friis: total noise is dominated by the first stage's noise and gain). Boosting weak RF before mixing/detection improves sensitivity. Amplifiers increase amplitude of an existing signal; oscillators create a new signal. Match the device by both function (amplify vs generate) and frequency band (RF vs AF).", "misconception": "Confusing 'amplifier' (boosts an existing signal) with 'oscillator' (generates one), and failing to match the frequency band — antenna signals are RF, so audio-frequency stages don't apply yet.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Antenna = RF in; amplify RF first, save audio amps for after detection.", "edge_cases": "Some receivers omit a dedicated RF amplifier and feed the antenna straight to the mixer, but when the goal is raising weak RF levels, the RF amplifier is the answer."}

{"core": "A microphone produces weak audio-frequency (AF) signals, so an audio amplifier is the correct stage to boost them before further processing.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "An oscillator generates a signal; it does not amplify an external input, and RF is the wrong frequency range for a microphone's audio output.", "why_tempting": "The word 'oscillator' sounds like active signal-producing circuitry, and a learner may conflate generating with amplifying."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An RF amplifier boosts radio-frequency signals, not the low audio-frequency band that a microphone outputs.", "why_tempting": "It contains 'amplifier' (the right function) but the wrong frequency domain — easy to grab the first 'amplifier' seen."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "An oscillator produces its own tone; it cannot raise the level of an applied input signal.", "why_tempting": "It correctly identifies 'audio' as the right band, so it pairs the right frequency with the wrong function."}], "concept": "Match the circuit FUNCTION (amplify vs. generate) to the SIGNAL DOMAIN (audio vs. RF). A microphone converts sound into electrical signals in the audio range (roughly 20 Hz–20 kHz, voice ~300–3000 Hz). 'To increase the level' means amplification — an amplifier raises signal amplitude using gain. An oscillator instead generates a periodic waveform on its own and takes no meaningful input to boost. Combining the two axes: weak + microphone + increase level → audio amplifier.", "misconception": "Confusing an oscillator (signal source) with an amplifier (signal booster), and ignoring that a microphone's output is audio, not RF.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Amplifier = make bigger; Oscillator = make from scratch. Mic = audio.", "edge_cases": "In a transmitter chain the microphone's audio is later mixed/modulated up to RF, but the immediate stage that raises the weak mic signal is the audio amplifier."}

{"core": "A speech amplifier in an SSB/AM/FM transceiver is designed to handle the voice band, roughly 300 Hz to 3000 Hz, which carries essentially all the intelligibility of human speech.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "3 Hz to 300 Hz is sub-audio to low-voice range and excludes nearly all speech intelligibility; it's far too low and narrow.", "why_tempting": "It uses '300 Hz' as an endpoint, which looks plausible as one edge of the voice band."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "300 Hz to 1000 Hz captures only the lower portion of speech; the consonants and intelligibility cues above 1 kHz would be lost, making audio muffled.", "why_tempting": "Starts at the correct 300 Hz lower limit, so it appears partially right."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "40 Hz to 40 kHz is essentially the full hi-fi audio range — a transceiver deliberately restricts bandwidth to conserve RF spectrum, so it's wasteful and far wider than needed.", "why_tempting": "It resembles the 'full audio spectrum' that a learner associates with high-quality sound reproduction."}], "concept": "Communications-quality voice is band-limited to about 300–3000 Hz because that 2.7 kHz range carries the overwhelming majority of speech intelligibility (formants and consonant energy). Frequencies below ~300 Hz add power but little intelligibility and waste transmitter energy; frequencies above ~3000 Hz add bandwidth without proportional benefit. In SSB this directly sets the transmitted bandwidth (~2.4–2.8 kHz), so filtering the audio at the speech amplifier conserves RF spectrum and concentrates power where it aids copy. This is why telephone systems use the same nominal band.", "misconception": "Confusing full audio fidelity (hi-fi, ~20 Hz–20 kHz) with the deliberately restricted communications voice band; amateur speech processing trades fidelity for spectral efficiency and intelligibility.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "'300 to 3000' — the same band as a telephone call.", "edge_cases": "Exact filter edges vary by rig (often ~300–2700 Hz for SSB), but 300 Hz to 3000 Hz is the standard nominal answer."}

{"core": "Amplifiers increase signal magnitude, and beyond power and current that magnitude is voltage. A voltage amplifier is explicitly designed to raise the signal's voltage level (its gain is Vout/Vin).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Linearity is a quality/fidelity characteristic, not a signal quantity that is amplified; designers strive to preserve linearity, not 'increase' it.", "why_tempting": "Linearity is a real amplifier design goal and sounds like a desirable property to maximize."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "An amplifier ideally preserves phase relationships; it doesn't multiply or boost phase. Phase shift is usually an unwanted side effect, not an amplified quantity.", "why_tempting": "Amplifiers do affect phase, so it seems plausible they could be designed to increase it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A linear amplifier reproduces the input frequency unchanged; raising frequency requires mixers/multipliers, which are not amplification.", "why_tempting": "Learners confuse frequency multiplier stages with amplification since both are 'increasing' something."}], "concept": "Amplification = multiplying an input quantity by a gain factor. The three quantities that can be directly amplified are voltage (Av = Vout/Vin), current (Ai = Iout/Iin), and power (Ap = Pout/Pin = Av·Ai for matched conditions). Voltage amplifiers (high input impedance, modest current) target Av; current amplifiers target Ai; power amplifiers deliver both into a load. Frequency and phase are properties of the waveform's timing, not its amplitude, so a well-behaved amplifier leaves them intact. Linearity describes how faithfully the output tracks the input — a figure of merit, not an amplified value.", "misconception": "Thinking any electrical 'thing' an amplifier touches can be 'amplified.' Only magnitude quantities (voltage, current, power) are amplified; timing/quality attributes (frequency, phase, linearity) are not.", "link": "Tools — Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Amplifiers boost the three P-V-I magnitudes (Power, Voltage, Current) — never frequency or phase.", "edge_cases": "Frequency multipliers and mixers do shift frequency, but they are separate nonlinear functions, distinct from amplification."}

{"core": "Gain is the term for the increase in signal level produced by an amplifier, expressed as the ratio of output to input (often in dB).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Amplitude describes the magnitude of a signal at a given instant, not the factor by which an amplifier increases it.", "why_tempting": "Amplification changes amplitude, so the word feels related to the process."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Modulation is the process of impressing information onto a carrier, unrelated to raising signal level.", "why_tempting": "It's a common transmitter term that sounds technical and active."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Attenuation is the opposite — a decrease or loss in signal level.", "why_tempting": "It's the natural antonym; if you misread the question as asking about reduction you'd pick it."}], "concept": "Gain G is the ratio of output power (or voltage) to input: G = P_out/P_in. Expressed logarithmically, gain in dB = 10·log10(P_out/P_in) for power, or 20·log10(V_out/V_in) for voltage. A positive dB value means amplification (gain); a negative value means loss (attenuation). The same dB framework unifies amplifiers and lossy components, with sign indicating direction.", "misconception": "Confusing the property being changed (amplitude) or its opposite (attenuation) with the named ratio of increase (gain).", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Gain Goes up; Attenuation = Away (down).", "edge_cases": "Gain can be less than 1 (negative dB) for a passive network, in which case it is effectively a loss; 'gain' strictly means the output/input ratio regardless of sign."}

{"core": "Gain is the ratio of output to input signal power (or voltage/current); a device that increases this ratio amplifies, so gain is the property of amplification.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Attenuation is a reduction in signal level — a loss, i.e. gain less than unity (negative in dB). It is the opposite of what 'gain' describes.", "why_tempting": "Attenuators are characterized by a 'gain' figure too (a negative dB value), so a learner may associate the word 'gain' loosely with any signal-level change."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Oscillation is the generation of a periodic waveform with no input signal; it's a stability/feedback phenomenon, not the definition of gain.", "why_tempting": "High-gain stages with positive feedback can break into oscillation, so the two get linked — but gain itself does not mean oscillation."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Modulation is impressing information onto a carrier by varying amplitude, frequency, or phase — a signal-processing function unrelated to the gain ratio.", "why_tempting": "Modulators and amplifiers both appear in transmitter chains, so they can be confused as related properties."}], "concept": "Gain G is defined as the ratio of output to input: in power terms G = P_out / P_in, or in decibels G(dB) = 10·log10(P_out/P_in) for power, 20·log10(V_out/V_in) for voltage. G > 1 (positive dB) means the device delivers more output than input — amplification. G < 1 (negative dB) is attenuation. An active device (transistor, tube, op-amp) supplies the extra energy from its power supply to achieve G > 1.", "misconception": "Treating 'gain' as a generic term for any signal alteration, rather than specifically an increase in signal level (amplification).", "link": "Tools — \"Decibel Converter\"", "mnemonic": "Gain = Greater output → amplification.", "edge_cases": "A passive device can show 'gain' below 1 (a loss) expressed as negative dB; antenna gain is a relative directivity figure, not power added — but a 'device with gain' in this context means active amplification."}

{"core": "A positive gain in decibels (+10 dB = 10× power gain) means the device increases signal power, which is the function of an amplifier.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "An oscillator generates a signal from DC power rather than amplifying an input, so it isn't characterized by a gain figure between input and output.", "why_tempting": "Oscillators contain active gain stages internally, so a learner may associate them with 'gain.'"}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A fader adjusts/reduces level for mixing; it is a passive or variable-loss control, not a fixed +10 dB gain device.", "why_tempting": "Faders deal in dB markings, but those represent attenuation/level settings, typically 0 dB or negative."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "An attenuator reduces power, which corresponds to negative dB (e.g. −10 dB), the opposite sign of the labelled gain.", "why_tempting": "Attenuators are also rated in dB, so a learner ignoring the sign convention may pick it."}], "concept": "Gain in decibels is dB = 10·log10(Pout/Pin) for power. A value of +10 dB gives Pout/Pin = 10^(10/10) = 10, so the output power is ten times the input — net amplification. A positive dB value always means more power out than in (gain); a negative dB value means loss (attenuation). Only an active device that draws on a power supply can produce true positive gain.", "misconception": "Treating any dB rating as 'gain' regardless of sign; the polarity of the dB value distinguishes amplification (+) from attenuation (−).", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Plus dB = power up = amplifier; minus dB = power down = attenuator.", "edge_cases": "Passive networks can only show loss (≤0 dB); positive gain requires an active device with an external power source."}

{"core": "Efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful RF output power to the DC power drawn from the supply, usually expressed as a percentage: η = (P_out / P_DC) × 100%.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Loss factor\" is not the standard term for this ratio; losses are the difference between input and output, not the ratio itself.", "why_tempting": "The DC power that isn't converted to RF is dissipated as heat (loss), so it sounds like it could describe the relationship."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Current gain is the ratio of output current to input current of a device, not a power ratio against the DC supply.", "why_tempting": "Gain terms relate input and output quantities, so a learner may conflate it with the power conversion ratio."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Dynamic range describes the span between the smallest and largest signals a stage can handle, unrelated to DC-to-RF conversion.", "why_tempting": "It's a common amplifier figure of merit, so it feels like a plausible amplifier specification."}], "concept": "In a power amplifier, the DC supply delivers P_DC = V_supply × I_supply. Only part of this becomes RF output; the rest is dissipated, mostly as heat in the active device. Efficiency η = P_out / P_DC quantifies how much of the DC budget is converted. Class A amps are limited to ~50% theoretical (often ~25–30% real), Class B approaches ~78.5%, and switching classes (C, D, E, F) can exceed 80–90%. Higher efficiency means less heat for a given output, reducing cooling needs and improving battery life in portable rigs.", "misconception": "Confusing efficiency (a power ratio) with gain (an amplification ratio of output to input signal). Efficiency compares RF output to DC supply, whereas gain compares output signal to input signal.", "link": "Tools — Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Efficiency = output OVER intake — what you get out divided by what you feed in.", "edge_cases": "Efficiency is figured against DC input power, not the RF drive power. A high-gain stage can still be inefficient, and a low-gain stage can be highly efficient — they measure different things."}

{"core": "Positive feedback reinforces the input signal in phase. When the loop gain reaches or exceeds unity (Barkhausen criterion), the stage sustains its own output and becomes an oscillator rather than an amplifier.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Reducing voltage gain is the effect of NEGATIVE feedback, which subtracts a portion of the output from the input.", "why_tempting": "Feedback is correctly associated with changing gain, so a learner who confuses positive with negative feedback picks this."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Negative feedback reduces distortion; positive feedback typically increases it as the stage is driven toward instability and clipping.", "why_tempting": "Negative feedback's well-known benefit of low distortion is mistakenly attributed to feedback in general."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Flattening/broadening frequency response is another benefit of negative feedback (gain-bandwidth trade), not a consequence of positive feedback.", "why_tempting": "Again a real feedback benefit, but it belongs to the negative-feedback list."}], "concept": "With feedback factor β and open-loop gain A, the closed-loop gain is A/(1−Aβ). Negative feedback makes the denominator >1 (gain falls, bandwidth and linearity improve). Positive feedback makes Aβ approach +1, so the denominator approaches zero and gain rises without bound. By the Barkhausen criterion, when loop gain |Aβ|=1 with 0° net phase shift, any noise component is regenerated indefinitely — the circuit oscillates. This is exactly how deliberate oscillators are built; in an amplifier it is an unwanted defect.", "misconception": "Lumping all feedback together. The key is sign: negative feedback trades gain for stability/linearity/bandwidth; positive feedback does the opposite and, in excess, causes oscillation.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Positive feedback → Pushes itself → oscillates; Negative feedback → Negates problems (lower gain, less distortion).", "edge_cases": "Controlled positive feedback below unity loop gain is useful (e.g., regenerative receivers, Q multipliers); only excessive positive feedback that reaches unity gain produces sustained oscillation."}

{"core": "A series diode in the positive supply lead only conducts when the supply is connected with correct polarity; if the leads are reversed the diode blocks current, protecting the transceiver from reverse-polarity damage.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A plain series diode does nothing to clamp fast positive voltage spikes; transient suppression needs a shunt device like a TVS, MOV, or zener across the rail.", "why_tempting": "Diodes are associated with 'protection,' so it's easy to assume any diode guards against voltage stress."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A single series diode would half-wave rectify AC and drop ~0.7 V, not enable proper AC operation; transceivers run on DC anyway.", "why_tempting": "Diodes are central to rectifier circuits, so 'AC operation' sounds plausible by association."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A diode does not limit current; it conducts whatever the load draws (up to its rating) and offers no overcurrent cutoff — that's a fuse or breaker's job.", "why_tempting": "It sits in the main power lead like a fuse would, inviting confusion with overcurrent devices."}], "concept": "A diode conducts only in the forward direction (anode positive relative to cathode). Wired anode-to-source, cathode-to-radio in the +12 V lead, it passes current normally but becomes reverse-biased and blocks if the 12 V supply is connected backwards. The trade-off is a forward drop of ~0.7 V (silicon) or ~0.3 V (Schottky), which wastes a little power (P = I·Vf) and slightly lowers the voltage at the rig. An alternative low-loss scheme uses a reverse-biased diode across the input feeding a fuse: normal polarity does nothing, but reversed polarity forward-biases the diode and blows the fuse.", "misconception": "Assuming any diode in a power lead is a general-purpose 'protector.' A series diode's blocking behaviour is polarity-specific — it guards only against reversed connections, not surges or excess current.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Diode = one-way valve: wrong-way battery = valve shut = radio safe.", "edge_cases": "The series diode must be rated for the full load current and its ~0.7 V drop can matter to sensitive radios; many designs prefer the shunt-diode-plus-fuse method or a P-channel MOSFET to avoid the loss."}

{"core": "A diode acts as a rectifier/envelope detector, stripping the modulating information off an RF carrier — this recovery of information is called detection (or demodulation).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Conversion (frequency conversion/mixing) shifts a signal to a different frequency; it doesn't recover the original information.", "why_tempting": "Mixers also use diodes, so 'conversion' sounds like a plausible diode application."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Biasing sets a device's DC operating point; it's a circuit condition, not a process of extracting information.", "why_tempting": "Diodes are central to biasing networks, so the term is associated with diodes."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Regeneration is positive feedback used to increase gain/selectivity in early receivers, not the act of recovering modulation.", "why_tempting": "Regenerative receivers were historically used for detection, blurring the two terms."}], "concept": "In an envelope detector the diode conducts only on one polarity of the RF carrier, rectifying it. A following RC low-pass network charges to the peak envelope and discharges between peaks, so the output tracks the modulation envelope while the RF is filtered out. This nonlinear rectification followed by filtering is the essence of detection (demodulation). The diode's nonlinear V-I curve is what makes the recovery possible.", "misconception": "Confusing the various diode roles — mixing (conversion), feedback (regeneration), and DC operating-point setting (biasing) — with the specific function of pulling the information back out of a modulated carrier.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Detection = un-modulate; the Diode Detects the message.", "edge_cases": "Detection covers several schemes (envelope/diode detection for AM, product/ratio detectors for SSB/FM); the diode-rectifier envelope detector is the classic AM case."}

{"core": "A Zener diode is operated in reverse breakdown, where its voltage stays nearly constant over a wide range of current, making it ideal as a voltage reference or shunt regulator.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Phase shifting is done by reactive components (capacitors/inductors) or RC networks, not by a diode's DC breakdown characteristic.", "why_tempting": "'Phase shift' sounds like a plausible specialized diode function to someone scanning for jargon."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A Zener cannot increase voltage; it clamps/limits it. Boosting requires active circuitry like a boost converter or transformer.", "why_tempting": "Confusion with how regulators are part of power supplies may suggest the diode raises voltage."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "That describes any ordinary conductor or forward-biased diode; it does not capture the Zener's defining regulating behaviour.", "why_tempting": "All diodes do conduct current, so this is technically true but not the 'primary purpose.'"}], "concept": "In reverse bias below the breakdown voltage Vz, a Zener passes negligible leakage current. At Vz, avalanche/Zener breakdown causes the I–V curve to go nearly vertical: large changes in current produce only small changes in voltage. The slope of that region is the dynamic (Zener) resistance rz, so V ≈ Vz + I·rz with rz small. A series resistor sets the current and absorbs the supply variation, while the Zener holds the node at Vz — a classic shunt regulator. Pick R so the Zener stays in breakdown across the expected load and input range without exceeding its power rating (Pmax = Vz·Izmax).", "misconception": "Assuming any diode property (mere conduction) is the answer, rather than recognizing the Zener's unique constant-voltage breakdown behaviour.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Zener = Zero change in voltage (it 'zens' the voltage steady).", "edge_cases": "True Zener mechanism dominates below ~5 V and avalanche above; below the breakdown threshold a Zener behaves like an ordinary diode, so it only regulates when kept in reverse breakdown."}

{"core": "Rectification is the process of converting AC into DC, typically using diodes that conduct only when forward-biased, so current flows in one direction.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Amplification increases the magnitude of a signal (voltage, current, or power) but does not change its AC/DC nature.", "why_tempting": "It's a fundamental active-circuit function, so it sounds like a plausible 'processing' step."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Transformation changes AC voltage/current levels via a transformer's turns ratio; it requires a changing flux and cannot pass DC, so it never produces DC.", "why_tempting": "Transformers are central to power supplies that precede the rectifier, so the term feels associated with the conversion stage."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Modulation impresses an information signal onto a carrier (AM, FM, etc.); it's about encoding, not AC-to-DC conversion.", "why_tempting": "It's another standard signal-processing verb that learners may grab if unsure of the precise term."}], "concept": "A rectifier exploits the unidirectional conduction of diodes. A half-wave rectifier uses one diode passing only one polarity of the cycle; a full-wave bridge uses four diodes to invert the negative half-cycles, doubling ripple frequency and improving efficiency. The pulsating DC output is then smoothed by a filter capacitor (and possibly a regulator) to approach steady DC. The key distinction is direction: AC reverses polarity periodically, DC does not, and the diode's one-way behaviour enforces that.", "misconception": "Confusing the named stages of a power supply — transformation (voltage scaling) precedes rectification (AC→DC) precedes filtering — and assuming the transformer or amplifier does the conversion.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Rectify = make it 'right' (one-direction) current.", "edge_cases": "Rectifier output is pulsating DC, not pure DC; it still contains AC ripple until filtered."}

{"core": "A semiconductor diode has exactly two terminals: the anode (P-side) and the cathode (N-side). Conventional current flows easily from anode to cathode when forward-biased.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Gate and source are terminals of a field-effect transistor (FET), not a diode.", "why_tempting": "These are valid semiconductor electrode names, so they look plausible if you don't recall which device they belong to."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Collector and base are two of the three terminals of a bipolar junction transistor (BJT), not a two-terminal diode.", "why_tempting": "BJT terminology is closely associated with diodes (a BJT contains two junctions), inviting confusion."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Cathode is correct for a diode, but 'drain' is a FET terminal — this mixes two different devices.", "why_tempting": "Half the answer (cathode) is right, making it the strongest distractor for someone who pairs the wrong second term."}], "concept": "A diode is a single PN junction with two electrodes. The P-type side is the anode; the N-type side is the cathode. Forward bias = anode positive relative to cathode, which lowers the depletion-region barrier (~0.6–0.7 V for silicon) and conducts. Reverse bias blocks. Contrast with multi-terminal devices: BJTs use emitter/base/collector, FETs use source/gate/drain — recognizing terminal counts and names tells you the device type at a glance.", "misconception": "Confusing the two-terminal diode's names with the three-terminal transistor families, or mixing a correct term (cathode) with a transistor term (drain).", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "The diode 'AC' device: Anode and Cathode. The arrow in the schematic symbol points from anode toward cathode, the direction of conventional forward current.", "edge_cases": "On the schematic symbol the bar represents the cathode and the triangle the anode; in physical packaging a band marks the cathode end."}

{"core": "A diode conducts only when forward-biased (anode positive relative to cathode), so it passes the positive half-cycles and blocks the negatives — the cathode sees a series of unidirectional current pulses, i.e. pulsating DC.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The diode still conducts on every positive half-cycle, so current does appear at the cathode — it isn't blocked entirely.", "why_tempting": "Learners may overgeneralize that a diode 'blocks' AC, forgetting it only blocks one polarity."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A single diode produces gaps between pulses (it's off during the negative half-cycles), so the output is not smooth or steady — that requires filtering/smoothing capacitors.", "why_tempting": "The output is DC in direction, so 'direct current' feels right; the word 'steady' is the trap since rectified output is anything but steady."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "AC by definition reverses polarity; the diode removes the negative half-cycles, so the current never reverses — it can't still be AC.", "why_tempting": "The waveform looks 'pulsating,' so pairing that with AC seems plausible if you ignore that polarity reversal has been eliminated."}], "concept": "A diode is a one-way valve: it conducts when V_anode > V_cathode + V_forward and is essentially open otherwise. Apply a sinusoid to the anode and only the positive lobes pass — this is half-wave rectification. The resulting waveform is unidirectional (never goes negative) but varies between zero and peak each cycle, hence 'pulsating DC.' Adding a reservoir capacitor smooths these pulses toward steady DC; a full-wave/bridge arrangement uses both half-cycles to reduce the gaps and raise ripple frequency.", "misconception": "Confusing 'DC' (current that never reverses direction) with 'steady DC' (constant amplitude). Rectified output is DC in direction but still pulsates until it's filtered.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Diode = one-way door: positive halves get through, negatives slam shut → pulsating DC.", "edge_cases": "This describes a single (half-wave) diode. A full-wave bridge still gives pulsating DC but with no missing half-cycles and double the ripple frequency; only added filtering makes it approach steady DC."}

{"core": "In a forward-biased semiconductor diode, electron (conventional negative carrier) flow goes from the cathode toward the anode — opposite to the direction of conventional current.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Anode-to-cathode is the direction of conventional current, not electron flow; the two are always opposite.", "why_tempting": "Most circuit conventions and the arrow symbol point anode-to-cathode, so it feels like the 'forward' direction."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Source and drain are the terminals of a field-effect transistor (FET), not a diode.", "why_tempting": "It's a plausible-sounding semiconductor terminal pair if you don't anchor on diode-specific names."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Base and collector are bipolar junction transistor (BJT) terminals, not diode terminals.", "why_tempting": "Another real semiconductor terminal pair that can fool you if you skim the question."}], "concept": "A diode has two terminals: the anode (p-type) and the cathode (n-type). Conventional current (defined as the flow of positive charge) flows from anode to cathode when forward biased — anode positive relative to cathode. Real electrons, being negative, drift in the opposite sense: from the cathode into the device and out the anode. The diode's triangle-and-bar symbol points in the direction of conventional current (anode→cathode), and the bar marks the cathode. So whenever current passes, electron flow is cathode→anode by definition.", "misconception": "Confusing conventional current direction with actual electron flow — they are always opposite. The symbol arrow indicates conventional current, tempting you toward 'anode to cathode.'", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Electrons are negative, so they go AGAINST the arrow: cathode→anode.", "edge_cases": "This holds only under forward bias; reverse-biased a diode passes only tiny leakage current."}

{"core": "A light-emitting diode (LED) is a semiconductor junction that emits photons when forward-biased; the emitted colour (wavelength) is set by the bandgap energy of the semiconductor material chosen.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A neon bulb is a gas-discharge device, not a semiconductor, and emits a fixed orange-red glow from ionized neon — its colour is not set by chemical composition of a semiconductor.", "why_tempting": "It glows and comes in a small package like an indicator LED, so it's easy to confuse for an LED-style indicator."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A vacuum diode is a thermionic tube that rectifies current; it does not emit light by design (only incidental filament glow) and is not a semiconductor.", "why_tempting": "The word 'diode' matches, and LEDs are diodes, so the term feels related."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A fluorescent bulb is a mercury-vapour discharge tube whose phosphor coating sets the colour — not a semiconductor and not varied by semiconductor chemistry.", "why_tempting": "Phosphor colour mixing sounds like 'colour depends on composition,' blurring it with the LED answer."}], "concept": "In an LED the photon energy equals the semiconductor bandgap: E = hf, so wavelength λ = hc/Eg. Different compounds give different Eg and thus different colours — GaAs/GaAsP (red/infrared), GaP (green), GaN/InGaN (blue/violet). White LEDs typically use a blue die plus phosphor. The key idea for the exam: it's a semiconductor, and the colour is a material (chemical composition) property.", "misconception": "Assuming any device that emits light is the same; the question specifically requires a SEMICONDUCTOR whose colour is determined by material composition, which only the LED satisfies.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "LED = Light from Energy of the bandgap → different material, different colour.", "edge_cases": "White LEDs cheat the rule by combining a blue/UV die with a phosphor, but the underlying coloured emission still comes from the semiconductor junction."}

{"core": "A diode conducts current only when forward-biased and blocks it when reverse-biased, so wiring one in series (or shunt) lets normal-polarity current flow while a reversed supply is blocked or fuses, protecting the circuit.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "PIV is the maximum reverse voltage a diode can withstand before breakdown; a value below 1 V would be a fragile, useless diode and has nothing to do with the protection mechanism.", "why_tempting": "Reverse-polarity protection involves reverse voltage, so a parameter named 'peak inverse voltage' sounds directly relevant."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A good diode has LOW forward resistance — that's what lets desired current pass with minimal loss. High forward resistance would defeat the purpose.", "why_tempting": "It pairs 'resistance' with 'forward' and learners may confuse it with the high reverse resistance that actually does the blocking."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Switching/response speed matters for RF detection and fast rectifiers, not for steady DC polarity protection where the diode simply needs to block a reversed supply.", "why_tempting": "Fast diodes sound 'better,' so speed seems like it might enable protection."}], "concept": "A pn junction is a one-way valve: forward bias (anode positive) drops ~0.6–0.7 V (Si) and passes current; reverse bias presents a very high resistance and only a tiny leakage current flows until breakdown (PIV). Two common topologies exploit this: a SERIES diode passes load current only with correct polarity and blocks reversed input (cost: forward voltage drop and power dissipation = Vf × I); a SHUNT diode placed reverse across the supply does nothing normally but conducts heavily on reversed polarity, blowing a series fuse. The defining property in both is unidirectional conduction.", "misconception": "Thinking a specific numeric parameter (PIV, speed, or resistance value) is the active mechanism, rather than the basic rectifying behaviour — conduction in one direction only.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Diode = one-way street: current flows arrow-direction only.", "edge_cases": "Series-diode protection costs a forward voltage drop (use a Schottky for ~0.3 V or a P-channel MOSFET 'ideal diode' for near-zero loss); shunt protection only works if a fuse or current limit is present to clear the fault."}

{"core": "A diode conducts only when forward biased: the anode is made positive relative to the cathode by enough voltage to overcome the junction barrier potential (~0.7 V for silicon), allowing majority carriers to cross the depletion region.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Enhanced\" describes the enhancement mode of a MOSFET, where a gate voltage creates a conductive channel — it has nothing to do with diode conduction.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a state that turns a device 'on,' and a learner mixing up FET and diode terminology might grab it."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Reverse bias (cathode positive relative to anode) widens the depletion region and blocks current — the diode is OFF except for negligible leakage until breakdown.", "why_tempting": "It's the exact opposite of the correct answer, so anyone unsure of polarity has a 50/50 chance of picking it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "\"Close coupled\" refers to magnetic coupling between transformer/inductor windings, not to the electrical state of a PN junction.", "why_tempting": "It's plausible-sounding jargon that a learner may not recognize as belonging to a different topic entirely."}], "concept": "A PN junction has a built-in potential barrier set by the depletion region. Forward bias (V_anode > V_cathode by more than V_f) collapses the depletion region and lets a large current flow with a near-constant ~0.6–0.7 V drop in silicon (~0.3 V Ge, ~1.8+ V for many LEDs). Reverse bias adds to the barrier and only a tiny saturation/leakage current flows until reverse breakdown (avalanche or Zener) occurs. So conduction = forward bias by definition.", "misconception": "Confusing the conducting direction, or conflating diode terminology with MOSFET 'enhancement' and transformer 'coupling' terms.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Forward = anode positive = current flows; arrow of the diode symbol points the direction of conventional current.", "edge_cases": "A reverse-biased diode does conduct in reverse breakdown — this is exploited deliberately in Zener and avalanche diodes — but for normal rectifying operation 'conduct' means forward bias."}
