{"core": "A bipolar junction transistor (BJT) is an active three-terminal device: a small base current controls a much larger collector current, providing current/power gain at low supply voltages — the defining property of an amplifier.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A thyristor is a latching switch — once triggered it conducts fully until current drops below holding level; it has no linear region for proportional signal amplification.", "why_tempting": "It's an active semiconductor that controls large currents, so it superficially resembles a 'gain' device."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An SCR is simply one type of thyristor — a four-layer latching rectifier used for switching/phase control, not linear amplification.", "why_tempting": "Sounds sophisticated and controls power, but it only turns on/off, it doesn't reproduce a small signal at higher amplitude."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A variable resistor is a passive component; it can only attenuate or divide voltage, never add power to a signal, so it cannot amplify.", "why_tempting": "Adjusting it changes signal level, which a learner may confuse with controlling/boosting a signal."}], "concept": "Amplification requires an active device that draws energy from a supply and lets a small input control a larger output, giving power gain >1. In a BJT, IC ≈ β·IB (β often 100+), so a tiny base current commands a large collector current — true gain. Thyristors/SCRs are regenerative latching switches (bistable), and resistors are passive (gain ≤1). Only the transistor provides continuous, low-voltage linear amplification.", "misconception": "Confusing 'controls a large current/power' (switching) with 'amplifies a signal' (proportional, linear gain). Latching switches control power but cannot reproduce an enlarged copy of a small input.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Transistor = transfer-resistor = amplify; Thyristor/SCR = latch-and-switch.", "edge_cases": "FETs also amplify (voltage-controlled); the question specifically names the bipolar transistor among the choices given."}

{"core": "A transistor is the simplest semiconductor device capable of amplification, controlling a larger output current (or voltage) with a small input signal.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A varactor is a voltage-variable capacitance diode used for tuning (e.g., VCOs, PLLs); it stores charge but provides no gain.", "why_tempting": "It is an active-sounding, less-common device, so it can seem like a specialized amplifying part."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A P-N junction is the building block inside diodes and transistors but by itself only rectifies — a single junction has no mechanism to control current with a separate input.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like the most 'basic' semiconductor element, matching the word 'basic' in the question."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A diode (one P-N junction) conducts in one direction only and cannot amplify; it rectifies, clips, or detects.", "why_tempting": "It is the simplest two-terminal semiconductor, so 'most basic' lures learners toward it."}], "concept": "Amplification requires a three-terminal device where a small control input modulates a larger output. A bipolar transistor (NPN/PNP) uses two back-to-back P-N junctions: a small base current controls a much larger collector current (β = Ic/Ib). An FET uses a gate field to control channel conduction. Either way, the key is gain — a property neither a single junction nor a two-terminal diode possesses. The varactor exploits the junction's depletion-region capacitance versus reverse bias, again no gain.", "misconception": "Confusing 'most basic' with 'simplest possible junction.' The simplest semiconductor structure is the P-N junction/diode, but the simplest one that *amplifies* is the transistor — read the verb 'amplify' carefully.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Three terminals to transform a tiny trickle into a torrent — Transistor.", "edge_cases": "Tunnel diodes and other negative-resistance devices can produce gain in special circuits, but the conventional 'most basic amplifying component' answer is the transistor."}

{"core": "A bipolar junction transistor (BJT) has three terminals: the collector, emitter, and base. The base controls current flow between the collector and emitter.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Source and drain are FET terminals, not BJT terminals; only 'collector' belongs to a BJT.", "why_tempting": "It includes the genuine BJT term 'collector,' so it looks partly correct."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Gate, source, and drain are all field-effect transistor (FET/MOSFET) electrodes, not BJT ones.", "why_tempting": "These are the correct electrodes for a different device, so a learner confusing transistor types may pick it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Drain and source are FET terminals; mixing them with 'base' creates an electrode set that exists in no real device.", "why_tempting": "It includes 'base,' a real BJT term, blended with FET terminals to look plausible."}], "concept": "A BJT is a current-controlled device with two junctions: base-emitter and base-collector. A small base current modulates a much larger collector-emitter current (Ic = β·Ib). Contrast with FETs, which are voltage-controlled and use gate, source, and drain — the gate's electric field, not a gate current, controls conduction through the channel. The naming difference is the key discriminator on this question.", "misconception": "Confusing BJT terminology (collector/emitter/base) with FET terminology (gate/source/drain). The exam deliberately mixes the two sets in the distractors.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "BJT = 'CEB' (Collector, Emitter, Base); FET = 'GSD' (Gate, Source, Drain). 'B' for Base and BJT go together.", "edge_cases": "Loosely, the FET gate↔BJT base, source↔emitter, and drain↔collector serve analogous roles, but the devices operate on different principles (voltage vs. current control)."}

{"core": "Amplification is the process where a small input signal controls a larger output signal, increasing its level. A transistor uses a small base/gate current or voltage to control a larger collector/drain current drawn from the supply, producing a magnified replica of the input.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Rectification converts AC to DC by passing current in only one direction — it does not increase signal level.", "why_tempting": "Transistors and diodes both involve semiconductor junctions, so a learner may conflate junction conduction with rectifying behaviour."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Detection (demodulation) recovers the information signal from a modulated carrier; it extracts content rather than boosting amplitude.", "why_tempting": "Detector stages often follow or include amplification, blurring the distinction between the two functions."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Modulation impresses an information signal onto a carrier by varying its amplitude, frequency, or phase — it changes the carrier, not the signal's level.", "why_tempting": "Modulation is a familiar transmitter-side process and sounds like it could 'add' to a signal, but it alters character, not magnitude."}], "concept": "In a transistor amplifier the gain is the ratio of output to input. For a common-emitter BJT stage, voltage gain Av ≈ -gm·RC where gm = IC/VT; a small change in base current ib produces a much larger collector current change ic = β·ib. The energy for the larger output comes from the DC supply, not the input — the input merely controls how much supply current/voltage reaches the load. Gain greater than 1 (positive dB) is the defining feature of amplification.", "misconception": "Thinking that producing a larger output 'creates' energy. The transistor doesn't generate the extra power; it modulates power drawn from the bias supply, with the weak input acting as the control signal.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Amplify = Ample (more) output from a small input.", "edge_cases": "A transistor can also attenuate or merely buffer (gain ≈ 1, e.g. an emitter follower). The term 'amplification' specifically requires output level greater than input."}

{"core": "NPN and PNP transistors require opposite supply polarities and bias-current directions, so swapping one for the other without reversing all bias and supply polarities will not work.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Frequency response is set by device construction and parasitics, not by the NPN/PNP distinction, and is irrelevant to why a direct substitution fails.", "why_tempting": "Frequency limits are a real device parameter, so it sounds like a plausible compatibility issue."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Current gain (hFE) varies between part numbers regardless of type; it is not the fundamental barrier to substituting PNP for NPN.", "why_tempting": "Gain mismatch is a genuine concern when swapping any transistor, so it feels like the right reason."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Both types use the same electrode names — emitter, base, collector — so labelling is not the problem.", "why_tempting": "Pinouts do differ between packages, which can be confused with a labelling difference."}], "concept": "In an NPN device the collector is biased positive relative to the emitter and base current flows into the base; in a PNP device every polarity and current direction is reversed (collector negative, base current flows out). The two are charge-carrier complements: NPN conducts mainly by electrons, PNP by holes. A circuit designed around one biasing scheme delivers reverse-polarity bias to the other, so the substitute transistor stays cut off or is damaged. Substitution is only possible if you also reverse the supply rail and all bias polarities (a complementary redesign).", "misconception": "Assuming transistors of equivalent gain/frequency are interchangeable, overlooking that NPN and PNP are polarity complements requiring opposite bias.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "NPN: arrow Not Pointing iN (points out, electron device, positive collector); PNP is the mirror image.", "edge_cases": "Complementary pairs (e.g. matched NPN/PNP) are deliberately used in push-pull output stages, but only because the surrounding circuit supplies each with its correct polarity."}

{"core": "\"NPN\" describes a three-layer junction arrangement (N-type/P-type/N-type) that is the defining structure of a bipolar junction transistor. The labelling \"general purpose audio NPN\" is therefore a bipolar transistor.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A thyristor (SCR) is a four-layer PNPN latching device used for switching/power control, not described by the NPN designation and not a general-purpose audio amplifier.", "why_tempting": "It is also a multi-layer semiconductor, and the layer-letter naming can look superficially similar."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A FET is a unipolar, voltage-controlled device with terminals named gate/source/drain; it is described by channel type (N-channel/P-channel), never as NPN.", "why_tempting": "FETs are common in audio too, and 'N-channel' can be confused with 'NPN'."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A triac is a bidirectional AC switching device (essentially two SCRs back-to-back); it is a power-control part, not an NPN audio amplifier.", "why_tempting": "Like the thyristor it's a multi-layer device, so it tempts those grouping all multi-junction parts together."}], "concept": "Bipolar junction transistors come in two polarities defined by their doping sandwich: NPN (N-emitter, P-base, N-collector) and PNP. Current flow uses both carrier types (hence 'bipolar'), and the device is current-controlled — collector current is roughly β times base current. The 'NPN/PNP' nomenclature is exclusive to BJTs: FETs use channel-type naming, while thyristors/triacs use PNPN four-layer structures. So the moment a datasheet says 'NPN' (or 'PNP'), the part is a bipolar transistor.", "misconception": "Assuming any multi-letter/multi-layer semiconductor label could be a thyristor or triac, rather than recognizing NPN as the unique signature of a BJT.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "NPN / PNP = Bipolar; channel = FET; PNPN = thyristor family."}

{"core": "Bipolar junction transistors come in exactly two polarities determined by their doping arrangement: NPN (an N-doped emitter and collector sandwiching a thin P base) and PNP (the complement). These are the two basic types.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Diode and triode are not transistor types — a diode is a two-terminal junction and 'triode' is a vacuum-tube term.", "why_tempting": "A triode has three electrodes like a transistor has three terminals, so it sounds plausible by analogy."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Varicap (variable-capacitance diode) and varistor (voltage-dependent resistor) are two-terminal devices, not transistors at all.", "why_tempting": "Both are real semiconductor component names, so they look like legitimate device categories."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "P-channel and N-channel describe FETs (field-effect transistors), not bipolar transistors.", "why_tempting": "These genuinely are the two basic types of the OTHER transistor family (FETs), making them an easy mix-up."}], "concept": "A BJT is a three-layer doped structure. NPN devices conduct via electrons as majority carriers and turn on with a positive base-emitter voltage; PNP devices use holes and turn on with the emitter positive relative to the base. The choice is purely about carrier polarity and biasing convention. Contrast this with FETs, which are unipolar (one carrier type) and are classified by channel type (N-channel/P-channel) and gate structure (JFET/MOSFET).", "misconception": "Confusing BJT classification (NPN/PNP) with FET classification (N-channel/P-channel) — both families have two complementary types but use different terminology.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "BJT = polarity (NPN/PNP); FET = channel (N-ch/P-ch).", "edge_cases": ""}

{"core": "Transistors are thermally limited devices; exceeding the maximum junction temperature causes runaway and permanent damage, making excessive heat the most likely failure cause.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Standard transistors are encased in opaque packages, and light is only relevant to photo-sensitive devices (phototransistors), not a general failure mode.", "why_tempting": "Light affects semiconductor junctions, so it sounds plausible if you recall that photons can generate carriers."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Saturation is a normal, safe operating state (used in switching) where the device is fully on; it does not inherently destroy the transistor.", "why_tempting": "'Saturation' sounds like overload or being pushed past limits, suggesting stress."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Cut-off is the fully-off state with essentially no current flowing; it dissipates almost no power and is harmless.", "why_tempting": "It's an extreme operating point, so it may seem like an abnormal or damaging condition."}], "concept": "Power dissipated in a transistor is P = V_CE * I_C, and this heats the junction. Junction temperature rises as T_j = T_a + P*(theta_jc + theta_ca), where theta is thermal resistance. If T_j exceeds the rated maximum (often ~150 C for silicon), the device degrades or fails. Worse, silicon's current increases with temperature, so heat raises I_C, which raises P, which raises temperature further — positive feedback known as thermal runaway. Heat sinks lower theta_ca to keep T_j safe.", "misconception": "Thinking that the operating-region labels (saturation/cut-off) are inherently dangerous, when in fact the destructive variable is power dissipation converted to heat, not the logic state.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Transistors die hot — heat is the killer.", "edge_cases": "Excessive voltage (breakdown) and overcurrent are also failure causes, but they ultimately destroy the device by the heat they generate, so heat remains the root mechanism."}

{"core": "In a bipolar junction transistor (BJT), a small current injected into the base controls the much larger collector-emitter (output) current. The base is the controlling electrode.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The emitter is the carrier-source electrode common to both input and output loops; it conducts the output current but does not control it.", "why_tempting": "The emitter handles nearly all the device current (Ie = Ic + Ib), so it seems central to operation."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The collector carries the controlled output current — it is the output electrode, not the control electrode.", "why_tempting": "Since 'output current' literally flows in the collector, learners conflate where the current appears with what controls it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The source is an electrode of a field-effect transistor (FET/MOSFET), not a BJT, which has base, emitter and collector.", "why_tempting": "Source/gate/drain are easily mixed up with the BJT's three terminals when the device types are studied together."}], "concept": "A BJT is a current-controlled device. In active mode the output (collector) current is Ic = β·Ib, where β (hFE) is the current gain, typically 50–500. The base–emitter junction is forward-biased; the small base current sets the level of injected carriers that diffuse across to the reverse-biased collector junction, so Ib governs Ic. Contrast this with a FET, where the gate VOLTAGE (not current) controls the drain current — that's the source/gate/drain family in option D.", "misconception": "Confusing the electrode where the output current flows (collector) with the electrode that controls it (base), and mixing BJT terminal names with FET terminal names.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "BJT = Base controls (current-driven); FET = Gate controls (voltage-driven).", "edge_cases": "This holds in the active region. In saturation the base loses control of Ic (the transistor is fully 'on' and Ic is set by the external circuit), and in cutoff there is no significant output current."}

{"core": "In a bipolar junction transistor (BJT), the base current controls the conduction between collector and emitter. Driving base current turns the transistor on (saturation); removing it turns it off (cutoff), so the base controls the switch state.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The collector is the controlled output terminal where the switched load current flows; it does not control the transistor's state.", "why_tempting": "It's the terminal that visibly carries the switched current, so it can seem like the 'active' control point."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "'Gate' is the control electrode of a field-effect transistor (FET/MOSFET), not a BJT — a BJT has no gate.", "why_tempting": "Both devices are switching transistors, and learners conflate FET gate control with BJT control."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The emitter is the common reference terminal (often grounded) through which both base and collector currents return; it's not the control input.", "why_tempting": "Emitter current equals base plus collector current, so it appears central to operation."}], "concept": "A BJT in switch mode operates between two regions: cutoff (I_B ≈ 0, transistor off, V_CE high) and saturation (I_B large enough that I_C is limited only by the external load, V_CE ≈ 0.2 V). The base–emitter junction is forward-biased to inject minority carriers; the base current I_B controls collector current via I_C = β·I_B until saturation. For reliable switching you drive enough base current that β·I_B exceeds the load-determined I_C, forcing saturation regardless of β tolerances.", "misconception": "Assuming the terminal carrying the main current (collector) or the common terminal (emitter) is the control electrode, and confusing BJT terminology (base) with FET terminology (gate).", "link": null, "mnemonic": "BJT = Base controls (current-driven); FET = Gate controls (voltage-driven)."}

{"core": "Saturation drives the transistor fully ON (collector-emitter near 0 V, like a closed contact); cut-off drives it fully OFF (no collector current, like an open contact). Toggling between these two extremes is exactly switch behaviour.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An inverter (logic NOT gate) is a *function* implemented with switching, not the fundamental behaviour described; the question asks what the device acts like, and the two-state ON/OFF action is generically a switch.", "why_tempting": "A common-emitter stage driven between saturation and cut-off does invert the input, so 'inverter' feels half-right."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Linear amplification requires biasing in the *active* region between cut-off and saturation, where collector current varies proportionally with base drive. Pinning the device at the two extremes destroys linearity.", "why_tempting": "Transistors are most associated with amplification, so it's the reflexive answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A transistor alone has no inherent timing element; a timer needs an RC network or oscillator to set intervals. Switching states says nothing about time.", "why_tempting": "Switching transistors are used inside timer circuits, blurring the distinction."}], "concept": "A bipolar transistor has three operating regions. In the active region (forward-biased B-E, reverse-biased B-C) Ic ≈ β·Ib — analog gain. Saturation occurs when base drive is large enough that Vce collapses to Vce(sat) (~0.2 V) and Ic is limited only by the external load; further base current no longer raises Ic. Cut-off is Ib ≈ 0, so Ic ≈ 0 and Vce ≈ supply. Operating only at these endpoints gives two states — closed (low resistance, low voltage drop) and open (high resistance, no current) — which is the definition of an electronic switch. Bonus: dissipation P = Vce·Ic is low in both states (one term near zero), which is why switching mode is efficient.", "misconception": "Assuming a transistor is 'always an amplifier.' Linear amplification only happens in the active region; deliberately bouncing between saturation and cut-off is digital/switch operation, not analog gain.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Saturated = ON, Cut-off = OFF → ON/OFF = switch.", "edge_cases": "Real switching transistors spend brief time traversing the active region during transitions, where momentary dissipation spikes occur — important for high-speed or high-power switching but not for this conceptual question."}

{"core": "FETs conduct through a channel of doped semiconductor between source and drain. That channel is either N-type or P-type material, giving the two basic families: N-channel and P-channel.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Gallium arsenide (GaAs) is a substrate material used in some high-frequency FETs, but 'gallium' and 'arsenide' are not channel types — they're constituents of one compound.", "why_tempting": "GaAs FETs (MESFETs) are real RF devices, so the words sound legitimate to someone who's heard of them."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "NPN and PNP describe bipolar junction transistors (BJTs), which are current-controlled and have three doped regions, not a single source-drain channel.", "why_tempting": "It's the most common transistor classification, so it's easy to misattribute it to FETs."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Silicon and germanium are semiconductor base materials, not a way of categorizing the channel between source and drain.", "why_tempting": "These are classic semiconductor materials, so they feel like a plausible 'two types' answer."}], "concept": "A FET is a voltage-controlled, majority-carrier device. The conduction path between source and drain is a doped channel: an N-channel uses electrons as the majority carriers (N-type silicon), a P-channel uses holes (P-type silicon). The gate voltage modulates the channel's conductivity via an electric field — hence 'field-effect.' This channel-polarity distinction is the fundamental dividing line, paralleling how N-type/P-type doping defines the device, regardless of whether it's a JFET or MOSFET, enhancement or depletion mode.", "misconception": "Confusing the BJT's NPN/PNP junction nomenclature with the FET's channel-type nomenclature — both are bipolarity classifications but for different device families.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "FET = channel (N or P); BJT = junctions (NPN or PNP).", "edge_cases": "Within each channel type, FETs subdivide into JFET vs MOSFET and depletion vs enhancement mode, but those are mode/structure distinctions, not the basic source-to-drain material classification asked here."}

{"core": "A field-effect transistor (FET) controls current flowing between source and drain via the voltage applied to its gate terminal, so its three electrodes are named gate, drain and source.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The point-contact transistor was an early bipolar-type device with emitter, base and collector contacts — no gate, drain or source.", "why_tempting": "It sounds exotic and unfamiliar, so a learner might guess it has unusual terminal names."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A bipolar junction transistor has three terminals named emitter, base and collector, not gate/drain/source.", "why_tempting": "It's the most common transistor, and current-control through a control terminal feels analogous to the gate."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A unijunction transistor has an emitter and two base terminals (base-1 and base-2), not a gate, drain and source.", "why_tempting": "The 'unijunction' name implies a single-junction control device, which could be confused with a gated FET structure."}], "concept": "FETs (JFET and MOSFET) are voltage-controlled, majority-carrier devices: a depletion or inversion region in the channel between source and drain is modulated by the electric field from the gate. The gate draws essentially no DC current (high input impedance), distinguishing it from the current-driven base of a BJT. Terminal naming is the quick exam discriminator — gate/drain/source uniquely identifies the FET family; emitter/base/collector identifies bipolar devices.", "misconception": "Confusing FET terminal names with BJT terminal names; both are three-terminal transistors but the control mechanism (voltage vs current) and the nomenclature differ.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "FET = 'Gate-Drain-Source'; BJT = 'Base-Emitter-Collector'. Gate guards the channel."}

{"core": "In an FET the gate's electric field modulates the conductivity of the channel between source and drain; varying the gate-to-source voltage changes the depletion (or inversion) region and thus the channel resistance.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The source is the channel terminal where carriers enter; it carries current but does not control channel conductivity.", "why_tempting": "In a JFET the controlling voltage is referenced to the source (V_GS), so the source seems involved in control."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "'Collector' is a bipolar junction transistor terminal and does not exist on an FET at all.", "why_tempting": "It's a transistor electrode name, so it sounds plausible to someone mixing BJT and FET terminology."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The drain is the other channel terminal where carriers exit; it conducts current but doesn't set the channel resistance.", "why_tempting": "Drain current responds to gate voltage, so it can be confused as the controlling element rather than the controlled output."}], "concept": "An FET is a voltage-controlled device: the gate forms a reverse-biased junction (JFET) or an insulated capacitor (MOSFET) over the channel. The transverse electric field from V_GS widens or narrows the conducting channel, changing its cross-section and hence its resistance. Because the gate draws essentially no DC current (very high input impedance), control is by voltage/field rather than current — contrasting with the BJT, where base current (collector/emitter terminals) controls conduction.", "misconception": "Confusing FET terminals (gate, source, drain) with BJT terminals (base, collector, emitter), and assuming the current-carrying terminals do the controlling.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "FET = Gate controls (like a valve's handle); BJT = Base controls. Source/Drain carry the flow, Gate gates it.", "edge_cases": "In both JFETs and MOSFETs the gate is the control electrode, but the mechanism differs: JFET uses a reverse-biased PN junction, MOSFET uses an insulated gate capacitor."}

{"core": "In a FET, the source is the terminal where charge carriers (electrons in an N-channel, holes in a P-channel) enter the conductive channel; they exit at the drain. The names literally describe the function — the source 'sources' the carriers.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The drain is where carriers leave the channel, not where they enter — it's the output end of the current path.", "why_tempting": "Source and drain are physically symmetric in many FETs, so it's easy to confuse which one carriers enter versus leave."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Emitter is a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) terminal, not a FET terminal at all.", "why_tempting": "The emitter is the carrier-injecting electrode in a BJT, so the analogy seems to fit, but the question is specifically about FETs."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The gate controls the channel's conductivity via an electric field but carries essentially no current and is not where carriers enter.", "why_tempting": "The gate is the control terminal and the most 'active' electrode conceptually, so it draws attention."}], "concept": "A FET is a unipolar, voltage-controlled device. Current flows along the channel between source and drain; the gate–channel voltage modulates channel conductivity (depletion or enhancement) without contributing current. By definition the source is the terminal at which majority carriers originate and enter the channel, and the drain is where they are collected. The FET source/drain map functionally onto the BJT emitter/collector, while the gate maps onto the base as the control terminal — but FETs and BJTs use distinct terminal names.", "misconception": "Mixing FET terminology (source, gate, drain) with BJT terminology (emitter, base, collector), and reversing the carrier-flow direction between source and drain.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Source = where carriers SOURCE/start; Drain = where they DRAIN away.", "edge_cases": "Source and drain are often physically interchangeable in construction; which is which is defined by biasing and carrier-flow direction rather than fixed geometry."}

{"core": "In an FET, charge carriers enter the channel at the source and exit at the drain; the drain is where carriers leave the channel.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "'Collector' is a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) terminal, not an FET electrode. FETs use source, gate, and drain.", "why_tempting": "The collector is the carrier-output terminal in a BJT, so it seems like the analogous 'output' electrode."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The source is where charge carriers enter the channel, not where they leave it — it's the opposite end of the conduction path.", "why_tempting": "The name 'source' suggests origin, and learners may confuse entry with exit when the roles are reversed in their mind."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The gate controls the channel's conductivity via an electric field but, in normal operation, carries essentially no channel current — carriers do not flow out of it.", "why_tempting": "As the control electrode it feels central, but it modulates rather than conducts the carrier stream."}], "concept": "An FET is a three-terminal device: source (S), gate (G), and drain (D). Conduction occurs through a channel of one carrier type (majority carriers — electrons in N-channel, holes in P-channel). The source injects carriers into the channel and the drain collects them, so by definition carriers leave at the drain. The gate, separated by an insulator (MOSFET) or a reverse-biased junction (JFET), sets up a transverse electric field that widens or pinches the channel, controlling drain current with negligible gate current. The FET analogy to a BJT: source↔emitter, drain↔collector, gate↔base — but the BJT terms (emitter/collector/base) are not used for FETs.", "misconception": "Mixing FET and BJT terminology, or assuming 'source' is where carriers exit because the word implies origin/output.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Carriers go Source → Drain: they 'drain out' the far end, just as current 'collects' at a BJT's collector.", "edge_cases": "For symmetric MOSFETs the physical source/drain can be interchangeable; the drain is simply whichever terminal is biased so carriers exit toward it."}

{"core": "A FET's input is the gate, which is separated from the conducting channel either by a reverse-biased junction (JFET) or an insulating oxide layer (MOSFET), so essentially no DC current flows into the gate — that near-zero gate current is what defines its very high input impedance.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Operating voltage has nothing to do with input impedance; FETs work across a wide voltage range and impedance is set by gate isolation, not supply level.", "why_tempting": "FETs are often used in low-power/low-voltage circuits, so a learner may conflate operating conditions with the impedance property."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "FETs are voltage-controlled and typically handle modest gate current of essentially zero; 'high current' actually describes the opposite of high impedance (high current implies low impedance).", "why_tempting": "Power FETs can switch large drain currents, so the word 'high current' sounds plausible if you ignore that impedance refers to the input/gate."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The high impedance comes from the gate's physical isolation (insulating oxide or reverse-biased junction), not from using a high-resistivity semiconductor material in the channel.", "why_tempting": "It links 'high impedance' to 'high resistance' semiconductor, which is a superficially logical but mechanically wrong cause."}], "concept": "Input impedance Z_in ≈ V_gate / I_gate. Because the gate draws virtually no current (MOSFET gate is capacitively coupled through an oxide; JFET gate is a reverse-biased PN junction with only tiny leakage), I_gate → 0 and Z_in → very large (megohms to gigohms). This contrasts with the bipolar transistor, whose base–emitter junction is forward-biased and continuously draws base current, giving a much lower input impedance. The FET is therefore a voltage-controlled device: gate voltage modulates channel conductivity without sourcing current.", "misconception": "Believing high input impedance comes from resistive channel material or supply conditions, rather than from the gate being electrically isolated so it conducts no DC current.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "FET = 'Field' controls, no current in the gate → near-infinite input impedance.", "edge_cases": "At high frequencies the gate's input capacitance lowers the effective impedance, and MOSFET gates do draw transient charging currents during switching — the 'no gate current' rule is a DC approximation."}

{"core": "In a field-effect transistor (FET), the gate is the control electrode: the voltage applied between gate and source sets up an electric field that modulates the conductivity of the channel, controlling current flow.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The base is the control electrode of a bipolar junction transistor (BJT), not a FET — a FET has no base.", "why_tempting": "BJT and FET both have three terminals and the base/gate play analogous control roles, so learners blur the two terminologies."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The source is the channel terminal from which carriers enter; it's the reference for the gate voltage but does not itself control the device.", "why_tempting": "The source is part of the controlling junction (V_GS is measured to it), so it feels involved in control."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The drain is the output terminal where current exits the channel — it carries the controlled current but does not control it.", "why_tempting": "It's analogous to the BJT collector and is where the controlled current appears, which can be confused with controlling."}], "concept": "A FET is a voltage-controlled device. The gate–channel structure (an insulated gate in a MOSFET, or a reverse-biased p-n junction in a JFET) creates a transverse electric field. Varying V_GS changes the depletion region (JFET) or inversion layer (MOSFET), modulating channel conductance between source and drain. Crucially, the gate draws essentially no DC current (high input impedance), contrasting with the BJT base, which is current-controlled. Terminal mapping: gate↔base (control), source↔emitter (carrier origin/reference), drain↔collector (carrier collection).", "misconception": "Mixing FET and BJT terminal names — assuming the control terminal is always called the 'base.' The base belongs only to bipolar transistors.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Gate Guards the channel — G for Gate, G for Guard (it controls flow without touching it).", "edge_cases": "Source and drain in a symmetrical MOSFET can be physically interchangeable, but the gate is always the control electrode regardless of orientation."}

{"core": "In a junction FET (JFET), the gate-channel junction is reverse-biased. The reverse-bias voltage creates a depletion region that pinches the conducting channel; decreasing that reverse bias narrows the depletion region, widens the channel, and lets more drain current flow.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "JFET gates are operated reverse-biased, not forward-biased; applying forward bias would forward-conduct the gate junction and destroy normal FET operation.", "why_tempting": "In a BJT, forward-biasing the base junction turns the device on, so a learner may transfer BJT logic to the FET."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "There is normally no forward bias on a JFET gate to remove; the parameter that controls channel current is the magnitude of the reverse bias.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like the inverse of the BJT 'turn on' idea, and removing a bias vaguely suggests letting current flow."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Increasing reverse bias widens the depletion region, further pinches the channel, and reduces (eventually cuts off) the current — the opposite of what's asked.", "why_tempting": "It's the symmetric counterpart of the correct answer, and learners may guess the direction backwards."}], "concept": "A JFET is a voltage-controlled device: drain current I_D depends on gate-source voltage V_GS, which sets the reverse bias on the gate-channel junction. The depletion-region width scales with the square root of reverse-bias voltage, so increasing |V_GS| (more reverse bias) expands depletion, narrows the channel cross-section, and reduces I_D toward pinch-off (V_GS = V_P). Conversely, decreasing the reverse bias toward zero maximizes channel width and I_D approaches I_DSS. Unlike a BJT, the controlling junction draws essentially no gate current.", "misconception": "Treating the FET like a BJT — assuming forward-biasing a junction is what increases current. FETs control current by modulating channel width via reverse bias, not by forward-conducting a junction.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Less reverse bias = wider channel = more current; more reverse bias = pinch = less current.", "edge_cases": "This describes a JFET (and depletion-mode MOSFET). Enhancement-mode MOSFETs work differently — they require a gate voltage to create (enhance) the channel rather than relying on reverse-bias depletion."}

{"core": "In a BJT the emitter is the carrier-injecting terminal, functionally analogous to the FET's source which is the terminal from which charge carriers enter the channel.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The base controls current flow and corresponds to the FET's gate, not the source.", "why_tempting": "Both are the 'control' terminal, so learners may pair base↔source if they only remember it's the odd one out."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The drain is the carrier-exit terminal of the FET, which maps to the BJT collector, not to itself.", "why_tempting": "Naming confusion — drain is an FET terminal, so it can't be the BJT analog being asked for."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The collector is where carriers exit the BJT, corresponding to the FET drain.", "why_tempting": "Collector and emitter sit at opposite ends of the device, so it's easy to swap which one matches the source."}], "concept": "The standard terminal correspondence between device types: BJT emitter ↔ FET source (carriers originate here), BJT base ↔ FET gate (control terminal), BJT collector ↔ FET drain (carriers collected/drained out). The key cue is the prefix S/E both relate to the carrier-injection end of the channel/junction.", "misconception": "Confusing which end carriers enter versus exit, or mismatching the control terminal with a current-carrying terminal.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Source = Emitter, Gate = Base, Drain = Collector — the order S-G-D matches E-B-C end-to-end.", "edge_cases": "FETs are symmetric in many cases (source and drain can be physically interchangeable), but the functional 'source supplies carriers' analogy to the emitter still holds for exam purposes."}

{"core": "In a BJT, the collector receives the carriers that flow through the device under the controlling action of the base, just as the FET drain collects carriers steered by the gate-controlled channel. So the collector is the functional analogue of the drain.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Source is an FET terminal, not a BJT electrode, so it cannot be the BJT counterpart to the drain.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a transistor terminal and the question mixes both device families, inviting a slip."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The emitter supplies the carriers into the device; it corresponds to the FET source, not the drain.", "why_tempting": "Emitter and drain are both endpoints of the main current path, so they feel interchangeable if you forget the direction of carrier flow."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The base is the control electrode and corresponds to the FET gate, not the drain.", "why_tempting": "Base is the 'special' third terminal, so a learner may map it to whatever FET terminal they recall least."}], "concept": "Map the three terminals by function. Control electrode: BJT base ↔ FET gate. Carrier entry: BJT emitter ↔ FET source. Carrier exit: BJT collector ↔ FET drain. The BJT is current-controlled (base current sets collector current via β), while the FET is voltage-controlled (gate voltage modulates the channel), but the topological roles line up the same way: input/source end, control terminal, output/collector end.", "misconception": "Assuming carriers 'flow into' the collector the way they flow into a drain, but actually the analogy is by role (output terminal) — and confusing emitter (the source analogue) with the collector.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "G-S-D ↔ B-E-C in order: Gate=Base, Source=Emitter, Drain=Collector.", "edge_cases": "The analogy is functional, not electrical: a BJT is current-driven and a FET is voltage-driven, so biasing and input impedance differ greatly despite the terminal correspondence."}

{"core": "In a FET the conductive channel runs between the source and the drain; the gate modulates the channel's conductivity but is not at either end of the channel.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A FET has no base — that's a BJT electrode — and only the source sits at a channel end.", "why_tempting": "Mixes BJT and FET terminology; 'base' sounds like a transistor electrode."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The gate is the control electrode, capacitively/junction-coupled to the channel, not connected to an end of it.", "why_tempting": "Source is correct, so pairing it with the prominent gate seems plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Drain is one channel end, but the gate controls the channel rather than terminating it.", "why_tempting": "Drain is a real channel end, so the pairing looks half-right."}], "concept": "A FET is a three-terminal device: source, drain, and gate. Current carriers enter at the source and leave at the drain, so the channel physically spans source-to-drain. The gate voltage sets up an electric field (via a reverse-biased junction in a JFET or an insulated oxide in a MOSFET) that widens or pinches the channel, controlling drain-to-source current — analogous to the BJT's emitter, collector, and base, but voltage-controlled rather than current-controlled.", "misconception": "Confusing the control terminal (gate) with the current-carrying ends, or importing BJT terms (base) into FET nomenclature.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Source→Drain is the current path; the Gate is the 'valve' in between.", "edge_cases": "In a MOSFET a fourth terminal (body/substrate) exists but is usually tied to the source; it still isn't a channel end."}

{"core": "Vacuum tubes can dissipate large amounts of power and tolerate high voltages and temperatures, so high-power RF amplifiers (e.g. transmitter finals) still often use tubes where a single transistor would fail.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Tubes require heater (filament) current plus relatively high plate currents, so they generally draw more current, not less.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume the 'old' technology is more frugal, but the opposite is true."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A vacuum tube is physically much larger than a transistor — semiconductors are prized precisely for miniaturization.", "why_tempting": "Easy to confuse 'older' with 'smaller' if you don't picture the actual glass envelope."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Tubes typically need high plate voltages (hundreds to thousands of volts), whereas transistors run on low supply voltages.", "why_tempting": "The phrase 'lower voltages' sounds like a modern efficiency benefit, but it describes transistors, not tubes."}], "concept": "The key engineering advantage of a thermionic device is its ability to withstand high anode voltages and dissipate heat from a large physical structure. A transistor's junction breaks down at modest voltages and its small die overheats quickly under high dissipation. For a given RF output power, the tube's robustness against voltage breakdown and thermal stress makes it the survivable choice in high-power linear and final amplifier stages. Tradeoffs: tubes need filament power, high-voltage supplies, are bulky, and have finite emission life.", "misconception": "Believing that newer (transistor) technology is superior in every metric. Tubes still win on raw power-handling and overvoltage survivability.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Tubes = Tough on high Tension (voltage/power), transistors = tiny & thrifty.", "edge_cases": "Modern LDMOS/GaN transistors now handle kilowatts in many HF/VHF designs, so this advantage is narrowing — but for the exam, tube = higher power handling."}

{"core": "In a triode vacuum tube, electrons are emitted by the heated cathode and collected by the plate (anode); the output (plate) current flows through these two electrodes.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The grid does not carry the main output current — it is a control electrode that modulates the electron stream via its bias voltage, ideally drawing negligible current.", "why_tempting": "The cathode is correct, and the grid is a real triode element, so pairing them feels plausible."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Emitter and collector are terminals of a bipolar junction transistor, not a vacuum tube.", "why_tempting": "They are the current-carrying pair in a BJT, the solid-state analogue, so the function maps to the right idea but wrong device."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Source and drain are terminals of a field-effect transistor (FET/MOSFET), not a triode.", "why_tempting": "In an FET the source–drain channel carries the output current, paralleling the cathode–plate path conceptually."}], "concept": "A triode has three active electrodes: cathode (electron source), grid (control), and plate/anode (electron collector). Electrons boiled off the heated cathode are attracted to the positively biased plate, forming the plate current. A small voltage on the intervening grid controls this current with little grid current — that is the amplifying action. The cathode↔plate path is the output current loop; the grid↔cathode path is the (high-impedance) input. The vacuum-tube triode maps directly onto solid-state devices: cathode≈emitter≈source, grid≈base≈gate, plate≈collector≈drain.", "misconception": "Confusing the control electrode (grid) with the current-carrying electrodes, or mixing tube terminology with transistor terminology.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Cathode-to-Plate Carries Power; the Grid just Guards the Gate.", "edge_cases": ""}

{"core": "Both triode vacuum tubes and bipolar/field-effect transistors are active three-terminal devices: a small input signal on the control terminal (grid or base/gate) modulates a larger output current, producing gain. That ability to amplify is their shared defining function.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Neither device dissipates appreciable heat when cut off — power dissipation (P = V_CE·I_C or V_a·I_a) requires current flow, so when not conducting, dissipation approaches zero.", "why_tempting": "Tubes obviously run hot and transistors need heatsinks, so a learner may wrongly generalize heat to the non-conducting state."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "This describes thermionic emission, which is true ONLY for tubes (the heated cathode boils off electrons). Transistors use doped semiconductor junctions and carrier injection, not heat, to move charge.", "why_tempting": "It's a real mechanism for tubes, so it sounds plausible until you remember transistors don't have a heater."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Electrons drifting through a vacuum is the operating principle of the tube alone; transistor current flows through solid semiconductor material, not a vacuum.", "why_tempting": "It's the textbook description of a triode and the word 'common' baits you into applying it to both."}], "concept": "An amplifying device must control a large output with a small input. In a triode, the grid voltage controls plate current (transconductance g_m = ΔI_a/ΔV_g); in a transistor, base current controls collector current (β = I_C/I_B) or gate voltage controls drain current. The physical conduction medium differs — vacuum vs. semiconductor — and the carrier source differs — thermionic emission vs. junction injection — but the functional outcome, signal gain, is identical. That functional equivalence is why transistors directly replaced tubes in most circuits.", "misconception": "Confusing the shared FUNCTION (amplification) with the device-specific physical MECHANISMS (vacuum conduction, thermionic emission), which are precisely what differ between the two.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Different guts, same job: both AMPLIFY.", "edge_cases": "Both can also act as switches/oscillators, but among the options offered, amplification is the only feature genuinely common to both."}

{"core": "In a vacuum tube the plate (anode) collects electrons emitted from the heated cathode, so it must be held at the highest positive potential to attract that electron stream across the tube.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The grid is operated near or below the cathode potential (usually slightly negative as a control bias); a positive grid would draw grid current and disrupt control.", "why_tempting": "The grid is an active control electrode, so learners may assume it carries the high voltage that does the 'work'."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A collector is the terminal of a transistor (or the output element of some other devices), not an electrode found in a conventional vacuum tube.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like it would 'collect' electrons, mimicking the plate's actual role, and the term is familiar from semiconductors."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The cathode is the electron source and serves as the reference (often near ground); it sits at the most negative potential relative to the plate.", "why_tempting": "Being the emitter of electrons, it seems central, but emission requires it to be negative relative to the plate, not positive."}], "concept": "A thermionic vacuum tube works by space-charge-limited electron flow: the heated cathode emits electrons, and a positive plate-to-cathode voltage establishes the field that accelerates electrons to the plate. Electrons are negative, so they move from low (cathode) to high (plate) potential. The control grid, placed between cathode and plate, modulates this current with a small (typically negative) bias, controlling a large plate current — the basis of the tube's amplification. The order of increasing positive potential is roughly cathode (reference) → grid (slightly negative) → plate (most positive).", "misconception": "Confusing tube terminology with transistor terminology (cathode/plate/grid vs emitter/collector/base) and assuming the control element or electron source carries the highest voltage rather than the electron-collecting plate.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Electrons flow TO the most Positive Plate — 'P for Plate, P for Positive.'", "edge_cases": "In some tubes the screen grid is held positive too, but the plate is still normally the highest-potential electrode."}

{"core": "A field-effect transistor (FET) is controlled by a voltage applied to its high-impedance gate, just as a triode is controlled by the voltage on its grid — both modulate current flow without drawing significant input current.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A triac is a bidirectional thyristor for AC power switching; it latches on once triggered and has no proportional voltage-controlled amplifying region.", "why_tempting": "It's a three-terminal semiconductor, so it superficially resembles a three-element triode."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A thyristor (SCR) is a latching switch triggered by gate current; once on it conducts until the current drops, with no linear amplification analogous to a triode.", "why_tempting": "It has a 'gate' terminal, and the name suggests grid-like control."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A bipolar transistor is current-controlled — base current governs collector current — and presents a low input impedance, unlike the high-impedance voltage control of a triode grid.", "why_tempting": "BJTs are the classic amplifying transistor, so they seem the obvious analog to an amplifying tube."}], "concept": "A triode amplifies by using the grid-to-cathode voltage to control plate current, drawing essentially no grid current (high input impedance). The FET mirrors this: gate-source voltage controls drain current through the channel, with negligible gate current because the gate is reverse-biased (JFET) or insulated (MOSFET). Functionally grid↔gate, plate↔drain, cathode↔source. Transconductance gm = ∂I_out/∂V_in characterizes both. BJTs differ fundamentally because they are current-driven devices with low input impedance.", "misconception": "Assuming the most 'transistor-like' amplifier (the BJT) is the tube analog, when the defining triode trait is voltage (not current) control via a high-impedance input.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Gate–Drain–Source = Grid–Plate–Cathode; both are voltage-controlled.", "edge_cases": "This analogy is about control mechanism, not biasing — tubes and depletion-mode FETs share negative-bias operation, but enhancement-mode MOSFETs differ in polarity while still being voltage-controlled."}

{"core": "In a vacuum triode the grid sits between cathode and plate; a small voltage on the grid controls the much larger electron current flowing from cathode to plate, making it the control element.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The cathode is the electron source (emitter), heated to release electrons by thermionic emission; it does not modulate the current.", "why_tempting": "It is the active source of the electrons, so it feels central to operation — but it emits rather than controls."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The plate (anode) is the positively biased collector of electrons; it is the output electrode, not the controlling one.", "why_tempting": "It carries the main current and is where the amplified signal appears, so it can seem to be 'in charge.'"}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "'Emitter' is a bipolar transistor terminal, not a triode electrode; the triode's three elements are cathode, grid, and plate.", "why_tempting": "The cathode does emit electrons, so 'emitter' sounds like a plausible synonym to someone mixing tube and transistor vocabulary."}], "concept": "A triode has three electrodes: cathode (emitter of electrons), grid, and plate (anode). The grid is a fine wire mesh placed close to the cathode. A small negative bias on the grid repels electrons and throttles the space-charge-limited current to the plate; because the grid is near the cathode where electron velocities are low, a tiny grid voltage swing produces a large plate current change. This is the basis of voltage amplification, characterized by transconductance gm = ΔIplate/ΔVgrid and amplification factor μ. The grid functions like a gate/base analog in semiconductor terms.", "misconception": "Confusing which electrode does the work: students may think the plate (large current) or cathode (electron source) is the controller, when control comes from the low-power grid voltage.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Triode = Cathode-Grid-Plate; the Grid in the middle Guards the Gate.", "edge_cases": "In a tetrode or pentode there are additional grids (screen, suppressor), but the control grid is still the primary control element analogous to a transistor's gate or base."}

{"core": "In a vacuum tube the cathode is heated and emits electrons via thermionic emission; these electrons then flow toward the positive plate (anode).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The plate (anode) is the positive electrode that collects electrons, not the one that emits them.", "why_tempting": "The plate carries the main electron current, so a learner may confuse 'where electrons end up' with 'where they start'."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The grid is a control electrode that modulates electron flow with a small voltage; it neither emits nor collects significant current.", "why_tempting": "The grid is central to amplification, so it can seem like the active source."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "'Collector' is a transistor (BJT) terminal, not a vacuum-tube electrode at all.", "why_tempting": "It sounds plausible as the destination of electrons and borrows from semiconductor terminology."}], "concept": "Thermionic emission: a heated cathode gains enough thermal energy for surface electrons to escape into the vacuum (governed by the Richardson–Dushman relation, where current density rises steeply with temperature). The plate is held positive relative to the cathode, so the freed electrons are accelerated toward it, forming plate current. The grid, sitting between cathode and plate, controls this current with relatively little power — the basis of tube amplification. Electron flow is therefore always cathode → plate inside the tube.", "misconception": "Assuming the electrode carrying the most current (plate) must be the emitter, or mixing vacuum-tube terminology with transistor terms like 'collector'.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Cathode = Cooks electrons (it's heated); plate = Pulls them in.", "edge_cases": "In directly-heated tubes the filament itself is the cathode; in indirectly-heated tubes a separate heater warms a coated cathode sleeve — either way the cathode is the emitting surface."}

{"core": "A triode is a vacuum tube — the glass envelope is evacuated so electrons can travel freely from cathode to plate without colliding with gas molecules.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Argon is an inert gas used in gas-filled tubes (e.g. some voltage regulators and discharge tubes), not in an amplifying triode.", "why_tempting": "Argon's inertness sounds like it would protect the hot filament, and gas-filled tubes do exist."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Air contains oxygen, which would oxidize and burn out the hot cathode/filament almost immediately, and gas molecules would impede electron flow.", "why_tempting": "It seems simplest to assume the glass just contains ordinary air."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Neon is used in indicator/discharge lamps where ionized gas glows, not in a triode where free electron travel is needed.", "why_tempting": "Neon is associated with glowing glass tubes, so it gets confused with electron tubes."}], "concept": "Thermionic emission requires a heated cathode to boil off electrons that are then accelerated toward the plate (anode), with the grid modulating the flow. Any residual gas would (1) be ionized by the electron stream, distorting characteristics and possibly causing runaway, and (2) chemically attack the hot emissive surfaces. So the envelope is evacuated to a hard vacuum, often with a 'getter' (the silvery deposit on the glass) that absorbs remaining gas after sealing.", "misconception": "Assuming a vacuum tube contains some special gas; the defining feature is precisely the absence of gas (a vacuum), distinguishing it from gas-filled discharge devices.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "'Vacuum tube' literally names what's inside — vacuum.", "edge_cases": "Gas-filled tubes (thyratrons, neon lamps, VR tubes) do contain gas deliberately, but those are not amplifying triodes."}

{"core": "A triode is named for its three active electrodes: cathode, a single control grid, and plate (anode). The single grid is the defining feature.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A tetrode has two grids (control grid plus screen grid), giving four electrodes total.", "why_tempting": "It's another common multi-grid tube, and learners may confuse the Greek prefixes if they don't tie 'tetra' to four."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A pentode has three grids (control, screen, and suppressor), for five electrodes.", "why_tempting": "Pentodes are the most common power amplifier tubes, so they come to mind readily."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A diode has only two electrodes, cathode and plate, with no grid at all.", "why_tempting": "It's a vacuum tube and the question mentions cathode and plate, but the single grid rules it out."}], "concept": "Vacuum-tube naming counts the number of active electrodes using Greek number prefixes: di-=2 (diode: cathode+plate), tri-=3 (triode: cathode+grid+plate), tetra-=4 (tetrode: +screen grid), penta-=5 (pentode: +suppressor grid). Each added grid solves a problem—the control grid (triode) modulates plate current, the screen grid (tetrode) reduces grid-to-plate capacitance, and the suppressor grid (pentode) suppresses secondary-emission electrons.", "misconception": "Miscounting electrodes or mismatching the Greek prefix to the wrong number—especially forgetting that the count includes cathode and plate, not just the grids.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Count electrodes, not just grids: Di=2, Tri=3, Tetra=4, Penta=5. 'Triode = three terminals = one grid.'", "edge_cases": "The count refers to active signal electrodes; the heater/filament is not counted as one of them (in indirectly-heated tubes)."}

{"core": "On a four-band resistor, the fourth band gives the tolerance (e.g. gold = ±5%, silver = ±10%). The first three bands encode the resistance value.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The third band is the multiplier (power-of-ten factor), not the tolerance.", "why_tempting": "It's the last of the 'value' bands, so a learner counting carelessly might stop here thinking it's the final band."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The second band is the second significant digit of the resistance value.", "why_tempting": "Easy to confuse band positions if reading the resistor from the wrong end."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The first band is the first significant digit of the resistance value.", "why_tempting": "Reading direction confusion — but the value always starts at the digit bands grouped together."}], "concept": "A four-band code reads: band1 = first digit, band2 = second digit, band3 = multiplier (×10^n), band4 = tolerance. So value = (10·d1 + d2) × 10^(band3) ohms, with the final band being a percentage spread. The tolerance band is usually offset slightly toward one end and is gold (±5%) or silver (±10%); the gap between band 3 and band 4 tells you the correct reading orientation. Five- and six-band resistors add a third significant digit (and temperature coefficient), shifting tolerance to band 5.", "misconception": "Confusing which band is the multiplier versus the tolerance, or reading the resistor from the wrong end so the tolerance band is mistaken for the first digit.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "On a 4-band part, the lone gold/silver band is the tolerance — start reading from the end farthest from it.", "edge_cases": "No fourth-band colour (only three bands) implies ±20% tolerance. On 5/6-band resistors the tolerance is the 5th band, not the 4th."}

{"core": "On a four-band resistor, the first two bands are significant digits and the third is the decimal multiplier, which together encode the resistance value in ohms. The fourth band gives tolerance.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Power rating is not colour-coded; it's inferred from physical body size or printed on the part.", "why_tempting": "Learners know resistors have a power rating and may assume the bands encode every spec."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Tolerance is the FOURTH band (e.g. gold = ±5%), not the first three.", "why_tempting": "Tolerance is genuinely a band — but it's the last one, so it's an easy off-by-one trap."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Composition (carbon, metal film, wirewound) is not indicated by the colour bands at all.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible since 'material' affects behaviour, but it isn't part of the colour code."}], "concept": "The standard scheme: band1 = first digit, band2 = second digit, band3 = multiplier (×10^n), band4 = tolerance. Value = (10·d1 + d2)·10^(d3). Example: brown(1) black(0) red(2) = 10·10^2 = 1000 Ω = 1 kΩ. The digit-to-colour mapping follows the BB ROY GBVGW sequence (Black=0 … White=9).", "misconception": "Confusing which band carries tolerance versus value — the third band is still part of the value (the multiplier), while tolerance lives in the fourth band.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "BB ROY of Great Britain had a Very Good Wife: Black0 Brown1 Red2 Orange3 Yellow4 Green5 Blue6 Violet7 Grey8 White9.", "edge_cases": "Five- and six-band resistors add a third significant digit (and a temperature-coefficient band), so the 'first three = value' rule is specific to the four-band type."}

{"core": "On a standard four-band resistor, the fourth band gives the tolerance — the percentage by which the actual resistance may deviate from the nominal value (e.g. gold = ±5%, silver = ±10%).", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The ohmic value comes from the first three bands (two significant digits plus a multiplier), not the fourth band.", "why_tempting": "It's the resistor's headline spec, so a learner may assume the last band still encodes value."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Power rating is not colour-coded at all; it's determined by the resistor's physical size and is found in the datasheet, not on the body bands.", "why_tempting": "Power is another key rating, so it seems plausible one band would carry it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Resistor construction (carbon, metal film, wire-wound) is never indicated by a colour band.", "why_tempting": "Material genuinely affects performance, making it a believable—but invented—band meaning."}], "concept": "Four-band code: band1 = first significant digit, band2 = second significant digit, band3 = decimal multiplier (×10^n), band4 = tolerance. So bands 1–3 set the value and band4 sets precision. Tolerance colours: brown ±1%, red ±2%, gold ±5%, silver ±10%, no band ±20%. Five-band parts add a third significant-digit band, shifting multiplier and tolerance one position right, but the principle that the LAST band is tolerance still holds.", "misconception": "Assuming every band contributes to the numeric resistance value; in fact the trailing band steps out of the value calculation to express manufacturing tolerance.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Read left-to-right: digits, digits, multiply, then 'how close' (tolerance) at the end.", "edge_cases": "Gold and silver as the THIRD band act as fractional multipliers (×0.1, ×0.01), but in the FOURTH position they mean ±5% and ±10% tolerance—don't confuse the two roles."}

{"core": "A 10% tolerance means the actual resistance may vary by ±10% of the nominal value: 100 Ω × 0.10 = 10 Ω, giving a range of 90 Ω to 110 Ω.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "80–120 Ω corresponds to ±20% tolerance, not ±10%.", "why_tempting": "It is symmetric about 100 Ω like the correct answer, so it looks right if you misremember the percentage."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This only applies the tolerance downward (90 Ω) but caps the top at the nominal 100 Ω, ignoring the +10% high end.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume tolerance only means a part is 'no better than' nominal, forgetting it deviates both ways."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "10–100 Ω is a huge spread that has nothing to do with a 10% tolerance; it confuses '10%' with '10 ohms' as a lower bound.", "why_tempting": "The '10' in the range echoes the 10% figure, which can mislead."}], "concept": "Tolerance is expressed as a percentage of the nominal value and applies symmetrically: range = nominal ± (tolerance × nominal). Here ±10% of 100 Ω = ±10 Ω, so the resistor is guaranteed only to fall between 90 Ω and 110 Ω. Tolerance describes manufacturing spread, not drift or operating variation.", "misconception": "That tolerance applies in only one direction, or that the percentage maps directly to an ohm-value as a bound rather than being computed from the nominal resistance.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Tolerance swings both ways: nominal minus AND plus the percentage.", "edge_cases": "Real resistors are sorted by tolerance, so a 10% part may actually be a unit that failed the 5% bin—but for the exam, just compute the full ±10% window."}

{"core": "In a 33 Ω resistor the digits are 3-3 and in a 39 Ω resistor they are 3-9; the first significant digit (3) is identical, so the second band (3 vs 9) is what differs.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The first band encodes the first significant digit, which is 3 for both 33 Ω and 39 Ω — identical, so it cannot distinguish them.", "why_tempting": "People assume the first band always carries the most meaningful difference, but here both values start with 3."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The third band is the multiplier. For 33 Ω (3-3) and 39 Ω (3-9) the digits 33 and 39 both use the same multiplier (×1, black), so the third band is identical.", "why_tempting": "The multiplier band changes for different decades, and a learner might think 33 and 39 fall in different ranges — they don't."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The fourth band is tolerance (e.g., gold ±5%), which has nothing to do with the nominal value and is typically the same for both.", "why_tempting": "It's the last band, and someone scanning left-to-right for a difference might overshoot to it."}], "concept": "Four-band code: band1 = first significant figure, band2 = second significant figure, band3 = decimal multiplier (×10^n), band4 = tolerance. Value = (10·d1 + d2) × 10^band3. For 33 Ω: d1=3 (orange), d2=3 (orange), mult=×1 (black). For 39 Ω: d1=3 (orange), d2=9 (white), mult=×1 (black). Only d2 changes (orange vs white), so it's the second band that differentiates them.", "misconception": "Assuming that two close resistor values must differ in their first or multiplier band, rather than recognizing that 33 and 39 share the same first digit and same multiplier.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Bands read left→right: digit, digit, multiply, tolerance. Two numbers sharing a leading digit differ in the SECOND digit band.", "edge_cases": "This logic assumes both resistors carry the same tolerance band; if tolerances differed, band four could also differ, but the question concerns the value 33 vs 39, which is purely the second digit."}

{"core": "A tolerance of 0.1% means the actual resistance deviates at most 0.1% from its nominal value — the smallest allowed deviation in the list, hence the highest precision.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "5% allows the value to wander up to 5% either side of nominal — far looser than 0.1%.", "why_tempting": "5% is a very common everyday resistor grade, so it may feel like a 'good' standard precision."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "10% permits twice the deviation of 5% and 100× that of 0.1% — low precision.", "why_tempting": "Learners may pick a mid-range number rather than thinking 'smallest = best'."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "20% is the coarsest tolerance shown, the lowest precision, not the highest.", "why_tempting": "Someone misreading the question as 'widest range' or 'least precise' would choose this."}], "concept": "Tolerance is the guaranteed maximum fractional error: R_actual lies in R_nom·(1 ± t). Precision is inversely related to tolerance — a smaller percentage band means a tighter, more repeatable value. So 0.1% < 5% < 10% < 20% in deviation, meaning 0.1% is the most precise. Note tolerance describes manufacturing spread, not temperature stability (that's the temperature coefficient).", "misconception": "Confusing a larger tolerance number with higher quality; in fact a larger percentage means a looser, less precise part.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Lower % = tighter fit = higher precision.", "edge_cases": "Tolerance only bounds the static value; a low-tolerance resistor can still drift with temperature or aging unless it also has a low temperature coefficient."}

{"core": "Tolerance is the maximum allowed deviation from the nominal value; a larger tolerance percentage means the actual value may stray further from nominal, so 20% is the loosest spec and therefore the lowest precision.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "5% is a moderately tight tolerance — far more precise than 20%.", "why_tempting": "5% is the most common resistor value, so it feels like a 'default' answer if you confuse 'common' with 'low precision'."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "10% allows half the deviation of 20%, so it is more precise.", "why_tempting": "It's a relatively loose tolerance and could be mistaken for the worst if you don't compare it directly against 20%."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "0.1% is the tightest tolerance listed — the highest precision, the exact opposite of what's asked.", "why_tempting": "The small number can trick you into reading 'lowest number = lowest precision' instead of largest tolerance = lowest precision."}], "concept": "Precision in a resistor is inversely related to its tolerance band. Tolerance defines the guaranteed range: a 1 kΩ ±20% part may measure anywhere from 800 Ω to 1200 Ω, while a ±0.1% part stays within 999 Ω–1001 Ω. Ranking by precision (best to worst): 0.1% > 5% > 10% > 20%. The colour-band/E-series also reflects this — E6 (20%), E12 (10%), E24 (5%) use progressively finer value spacing because tighter tolerance lets adjacent nominal values not overlap.", "misconception": "Reading the smallest numeric value (0.1%) as 'least precise.' In tolerance, bigger percentage = wider allowed error = lower precision.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Bigger tolerance %, sloppier resistor — 20% is the loosest, 0.1% the tightest.", "edge_cases": "'Lowest precision' here means worst accuracy spec; don't confuse with power rating or temperature coefficient, which are separate specs."}

{"core": "A resistor's resistance change with temperature is governed by its temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR), which can be positive, negative, or near-zero, so the direction and magnitude of change depend on that coefficient.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Resistance only decreases with rising temperature if the device has a negative temperature coefficient (like an NTC thermistor or carbon composition); it is not universally true.", "why_tempting": "NTC thermistors and some semiconductor-based resistive elements do decrease, so a learner familiar with those parts may overgeneralize."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Resistance only increases for materials with a positive temperature coefficient (most metals); it is not true for all resistor types.", "why_tempting": "Pure metallic conductors (copper, tungsten) and many wirewound resistors do increase with temperature, which is the most commonly taught case."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Real resistors are never perfectly temperature-independent; even precision metal-film parts have a small nonzero TCR (e.g. ±25 to ±100 ppm/°C).", "why_tempting": "Ideal-component thinking and high-stability resistors with very low TCR make this seem plausible."}], "concept": "Resistance varies approximately linearly over modest temperature ranges: R(T) = R0[1 + α(T − T0)], where α is the temperature coefficient of resistance in ppm/°C (or per °C). Metals have α > 0 (resistance rises as lattice vibrations scatter electrons more). Carbon composition and many thermistors have α < 0. Metal-film and metal-foil parts are engineered for very small |α| for stability. Since α can have either sign and varies by material, the only correct general statement is that the change 'depends on the temperature coefficient.'", "misconception": "Assuming all resistors behave like a single class — that resistance always rises (metals) or always falls (semiconductors) with heat — when the behavior is material-specific and captured by the TCR sign and magnitude.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "TCR is the answer: 'It depends on the coefficient' — the coefficient is literally what quantifies the dependence.", "edge_cases": "Over wide temperature ranges the linear approximation breaks down and a second-order term may be needed; thermistors are deliberately strongly nonlinear and used as sensors rather than fixed resistors."}
