{"core": "The temperature coefficient describes how much a resistor's value changes per degree Celsius, expressed as a fractional change (often in parts-per-million, ppm/°C) for each 1 °C change in temperature.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Power rating is specified in watts and defines how much heat the resistor can dissipate before damage — it has nothing to do with per-degree value drift.", "why_tempting": "Power and heat are temperature-related, so it sounds plausible that it would be quoted per °C."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Ohmic value is the nominal resistance in ohms at a reference temperature; it is a single fixed figure, not a rate of change per degree.", "why_tempting": "It is the most fundamental resistor parameter, so a learner might assume it carries the temperature spec itself."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Tolerance is the allowable percentage deviation from the nominal value (e.g. ±5%) due to manufacturing spread, expressed as a percent, not per °C.", "why_tempting": "Tolerance is also a fractional/percentage figure, so it's easy to confuse with a fractional-per-degree spec."}], "concept": "Real resistors drift with temperature. The temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) quantifies this: ΔR/R = α·ΔT, where α is the coefficient. Because α is a fractional change per degree, it is given in ppm/°C (e.g. 100 ppm/°C means R changes 0.01% per °C). Metal-film resistors have low α (~25–100 ppm/°C); carbon-composition types are much higher and can be negative. The key phrase 'a given fraction per degree Celsius' maps exactly to this definition — a normalized fractional change scaled by temperature.", "misconception": "Confusing the four independent resistor ratings: value (ohms), tolerance (% spread), power (watts), and temperature coefficient (fraction/°C). Each answers a different question; only the coefficient is referenced to temperature.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Coefficient + Celsius — the two C's go together."}

{"core": "On a four-band resistor, the third band is the multiplier. 120 Ω and 1200 Ω share the same two significant digits (1, 2), so only the multiplier band differs — 120 = 12×10¹, 1200 = 12×10².", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The fourth band is the tolerance (e.g. gold = ±5%), which has nothing to do with the nominal resistance value.", "why_tempting": "It's the last band, and learners sometimes confuse 'last' with 'final scaling'; tolerance and multiplier both feel like 'adjustment' bands."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The first band is the first significant digit. Both values begin with 1, so this band is identical (brown) for both resistors.", "why_tempting": "It's the most visible band, and people assume the leading digit must change between such different values."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The second band is the second significant digit. Both values have 2 as the second digit, so this band (red) is also identical.", "why_tempting": "Since the values look very different numerically, it seems a digit must differ — but the digits 1 and 2 are unchanged."}], "concept": "Four-band code: Band1 = first digit, Band2 = second digit, Band3 = multiplier (power of ten), Band4 = tolerance. Value = (10·D1 + D2) × 10^Band3. For 120 Ω: 12 × 10¹ (Band3 = brown, 1). For 1200 Ω: 12 × 10² (Band3 = red, 2). The two-digit base '12' (brown-red) is shared, so changing the value by a factor of 10 only shifts the multiplier band by one increment.", "misconception": "Believing that a tenfold change in resistance must change one of the digit bands, when in fact a clean ×10 change only moves the multiplier band.", "link": "section 4", "mnemonic": "Digit, Digit, Multiplier, Tolerance — a ×10 jump only nudges the multiplier.", "edge_cases": "This holds only when the two values share the same significant figures (here 12). If the digits also differed, more than one band would change."}

{"core": "The first two bands are significant digits (red=2, violet=7 → 27), and the third band is the decimal multiplier (yellow=4 → ×10⁴). So 27 × 10⁴ = 270,000 Ω = 270 kΩ.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This reverses the digit order (giving 72) and uses kilohms arbitrarily; the multiplier yellow=10⁴ doesn't produce 72 kΩ.", "why_tempting": "It uses the right two digits but transposed, and lands in the kΩ range, which feels plausible."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "27 MΩ would require a multiplier of 10⁶ (blue), not yellow's 10⁴. This is off by two orders of magnitude.", "why_tempting": "The 27 digits are correct, so a learner who guesses the multiplier may jump to megohms."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "This treats yellow as a third significant digit (274 Ω) rather than a multiplier, ignoring how the three-band code works.", "why_tempting": "It uses all three numbers literally (2,7,4) as if they were digits, a natural but wrong reading."}], "concept": "In a 3-band (or the first three bands of a 4-band) resistor code: band1 = first digit, band2 = second digit, band3 = ×10^(band3). Here digits = 27, multiplier = 10⁴, so value = 27 × 10,000 = 270,000 Ω. Convert: 270,000 Ω = 270 kΩ. The multiplier band is an exponent of ten, not an appended digit.", "misconception": "Reading the third band as another significant figure (yielding 274) instead of as a power-of-ten multiplier.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "\"Big Boys Race Our Young Girls But Violet Generally Wins\" — Black0, Brown1, Red2, Orange3, Yellow4, Green5, Blue6, Violet7, Grey8, White9.", "edge_cases": "The third band only acts as a multiplier on standard resistors; on 5-band precision resistors the third band is a third significant digit and the fourth is the multiplier."}

{"core": "1 MHz = 1000 kHz, so 3.525 MHz × 1000 = 3525 kHz.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This divides by ~100 instead of multiplying by 1000 — three orders of magnitude wrong.", "why_tempting": "Reaches for a smaller number assuming kHz must be 'less' and shifts the decimal the wrong way."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "3 525 000 is the value in hertz, not kilohertz; that's multiplying by 1 000 000 (MHz→Hz).", "why_tempting": "Correct conversion for MHz→Hz, easy to overshoot by one prefix step."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Off by a factor of 10 — the decimal was moved only two places instead of three.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible decimal shift if you miscount the powers of ten."}], "concept": "Metric prefixes step by powers of ten: mega = 10^6 Hz, kilo = 10^3 Hz. Converting between adjacent named prefixes mega→kilo is a factor of 10^6/10^3 = 10^3 = 1000. So multiply by 1000 (shift decimal three places right): 3.525 → 3525.", "misconception": "Confusing the prefix step size (×1000 between kHz and MHz) or moving the decimal the wrong direction when going to a smaller unit.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Going to a SMALLER unit, the NUMBER gets BIGGER: MHz→kHz means ×1000.", "edge_cases": "Same factor of 1000 applies kHz→Hz and Hz→kHz; only the direction (×/÷) changes."}

{"core": "3000 milliamperes equals 3 amperes, since 1 ampere = 1000 mA, so an ammeter scaled in amperes reads 3.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.003 A is 3 mA, not 3000 mA — this divides by 1,000,000 instead of 1,000.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a milli-to-base conversion if you misplace the decimal by three extra places."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "0.3 A is 300 mA; this divides by 10,000 — an incorrect scaling.", "why_tempting": "Plausible if you shift the decimal one place too far."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "0.03 A is 30 mA; this divides by 100,000 — wrong by orders of magnitude.", "why_tempting": "Another decimal-shift error that superficially resembles a metric conversion."}], "concept": "The SI prefix 'milli' (m) means 10⁻³, so a value in milliamperes is converted to amperes by dividing by 1000: 3000 mA × (1 A / 1000 mA) = 3 A. The decimal point moves three places left.", "misconception": "Confusing the direction or magnitude of the prefix conversion, moving the decimal the wrong way or too far.", "link": "Formula-sheet trainer", "mnemonic": "milli = thousandths: 1000 mA = 1 A.", "edge_cases": ""}

{"core": "The SI prefix 'kilo' means 1000, so 1 kHz = 1000 Hz exactly.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "10 000 Hz is 10 kHz, an order of magnitude too high.", "why_tempting": "Confusing kilo (10^3) with an extra factor of ten, or misremembering the prefix as 10 000."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "10 Hz is off by a factor of 100; 'kilo' is never 10.", "why_tempting": "Picking the smallest plausible 'round' number without applying the prefix."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "100 Hz is the prefix 'hecto' (10^2), not kilo.", "why_tempting": "Recalling that the prefix scales up but underestimating by one power of ten."}], "concept": "SI prefixes scale by powers of ten: kilo = 10^3, mega = 10^6, milli = 10^-3, micro = 10^-6. Thus 1 kHz = 1 × 10^3 Hz = 1000 Hz. The same conversion applies to any unit (1 kΩ = 1000 Ω, 1 kW = 1000 W), which is worth internalizing since the exam frequently chains these (e.g., MHz↔kHz↔Hz).", "misconception": "Treating 'kilo' as a factor of 10 or 10 000 instead of exactly 1000.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "k = 3 zeros (kilo→thousand).", "edge_cases": "Step up another 1000× for MHz: 1 MHz = 1000 kHz = 1 000 000 Hz."}

{"core": "1 picofarad = 10⁻¹² F and 1 microfarad = 10⁻⁶ F, so 1 µF = 10⁶ pF. Therefore 1 000 000 pF = 1 µF.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "0.01 µF = 10 000 pF, off by a factor of 100.", "why_tempting": "Plausible if you misplace the decimal by guessing rather than tracking the 10⁶ ratio."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.001 µF = 1 000 pF (i.e. 1 nF), off by a factor of 1000.", "why_tempting": "1000 pF→0.001 µF is a real common conversion, so it looks familiar even though the count of zeros is wrong here."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "10 µF = 10 000 000 pF, ten times too large.", "why_tempting": "Comes from adding one extra zero when counting the six places between pico and micro."}], "concept": "The SI prefix steps: pico (10⁻¹²) → nano (10⁻⁹) → micro (10⁻⁶). Pico to micro spans 6 decades, so divide picofarads by 10⁶ (or shift the decimal 6 places left). 1 000 000 pF / 1 000 000 = 1 µF. Equivalently, build it up: 1 000 000 pF = 1000 nF = 1 µF.", "misconception": "Miscounting the number of decade steps between pico and micro — treating it as 3 places (pico↔nano) instead of the full 6 (pico↔micro).", "link": "Formula-sheet trainer", "mnemonic": "pico → nano → micro, each step ×1000; pico to micro is ×1000×1000 = 10⁶.", "edge_cases": "Watch that nano (10⁻⁹) sits between them; many datasheets quote capacitors in nF, so 1000 pF = 1 nF = 0.001 µF is the adjacent conversion that traps people."}

{"core": "500 milliwatts = 500 × 10⁻³ W = 0.5 W, since 1 milliwatt is one-thousandth of a watt.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "50 W would be 50000 mW — off by a factor of 100 in the wrong direction (multiplying instead of dividing).", "why_tempting": "Looks plausible for a transmitter power level and uses the digits 5 and 0."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.05 W = 50 mW, which divides by 10000 — one extra factor of ten too far.", "why_tempting": "Correct direction (smaller number) but an extra decimal place slipped in."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "5 W = 5000 mW, ten times the actual output (divided by only 100).", "why_tempting": "Common HT high-power figure, so it feels like a realistic answer."}], "concept": "The milli- prefix means 10⁻³. To convert milliwatts to watts, divide by 1000 (shift the decimal three places left): 500 mW → 0.500 W. The general rule: value(W) = value(mW)/1000. Conversely W→mW multiply by 1000.", "misconception": "Misplacing the decimal point or confusing whether to multiply or divide when moving between a unit and its milli- subunit.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "milli = move the decimal 3 left (÷1000); 500 → 0.5", "edge_cases": "Note many HTs labelled '5 W' actually default to lower 'low-power' settings like 0.5 W or 1 W; don't confuse the spec with the active power level."}

{"core": "The SI prefix \"kilo\" means 1000, so one kilohm (kΩ) equals 1000 ohms.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "10 ohms has no corresponding standard prefix; \"deka\" (10) is essentially never used in electronics.", "why_tempting": "A learner might vaguely associate the 'k' with a small multiplier rather than recalling its exact value."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "0.1 ohms would be a sub-unit (decihm), the opposite direction from kilo, which scales up.", "why_tempting": "Confusing prefixes that scale down (milli, deci) with those that scale up."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "0.001 ohms is a milliohm (mΩ), a thousandth of an ohm — the reciprocal of a kilohm.", "why_tempting": "\"Milli\" (10⁻³) and \"kilo\" (10³) share the digit-3 magnitude, so swapping the sign of the exponent is an easy slip."}], "concept": "SI multiplier prefixes scale a base unit by powers of ten. Common ones in radio: kilo (k) = 10³, mega (M) = 10⁶, milli (m) = 10⁻³, micro (µ) = 10⁻⁶, pico (p) = 10⁻¹². Thus 1 kΩ = 10³ Ω = 1000 Ω, and 1 MΩ = 10⁶ Ω. Resistor and impedance values are routinely written this way (e.g. 4.7 kΩ = 4700 Ω).", "misconception": "Treating 'kilo' as a small or downward scaling factor, or confusing it with 'milli', which scales in the opposite direction.", "link": "Formula-sheet trainer", "mnemonic": "Kilo = thousand (think kilometre = 1000 m); the capital-vs-lowercase k/M distinguishes thousands from millions.", "edge_cases": "Note the case sensitivity: lowercase 'k' is kilo (×1000) but uppercase 'K' is reserved for kelvin; 'm' is milli while 'M' is mega — mixing them changes the value by a factor of a million."}

{"core": "1 MHz = 1000 kHz, so dividing 7040 kHz by 1000 gives 7.040 MHz.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "This would require dividing by about 9514, not a power of ten — it has no consistent unit basis.", "why_tempting": "It superficially resembles a plausible 'shifted decimal' answer if you misplace digits."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This multiplies by 10 instead of dividing by 1000, moving the decimal the wrong direction.", "why_tempting": "Reading 7040 and just inserting a decimal as 70.40 feels intuitive but ignores the kHz→MHz scale."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "This divides by 100000, off by two orders of magnitude.", "why_tempting": "It looks like a 'small number for big unit conversion,' tricking those who over-shift the decimal."}], "concept": "Frequency unit prefixes are powers of ten: kilo = 10^3 Hz, mega = 10^6 Hz. To convert kHz to MHz you divide by 10^3 (1000), equivalently shift the decimal point three places left: 7040. → 7.040. The 7040 kHz value sits in the 40-metre amateur band, so 7.040 MHz is the expected magnitude.", "misconception": "Confusing the direction or magnitude of the decimal shift when stepping between metric prefixes — moving the decimal too few/many places or the wrong way.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "k→M = three zeros gone: divide by 1000, decimal hops 3 left.", "edge_cases": "Same factor-of-1000 logic chains: Hz→kHz→MHz→GHz each step is ÷1000."}

{"core": "One quarter ampere = 0.25 A. Since 1 A = 1000 mA, 0.25 A × 1000 = 250 mA.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "2.5 mA = 0.0025 A, which is 100× too small.", "why_tempting": "Picks the right digits (2 and 5) but misplaces the decimal point by two orders of magnitude."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.25 mA = 0.00025 A — this is a quarter of a milliampere, not a quarter of an ampere.", "why_tempting": "It literally reads '0.25', matching 'one quarter', but applies the value to mA instead of A."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "250 µA = 0.00025 A, off by a factor of 1000 from 0.25 A.", "why_tempting": "Has the correct '250' figure but uses micro- (10⁻⁶) instead of milli- (10⁻³)."}], "concept": "Metric prefix conversion: milli- = 10⁻³, micro- = 10⁻⁶. To convert amperes to milliamperes multiply by 10³. 0.25 A × 10³ = 250 mA. The decimal moves three places right going A→mA, and a further three places right mA→µA.", "misconception": "Confusing the prefix scale (which power of ten) and slipping the decimal point — the trap mixes correct digits with wrong prefixes.", "link": "Formula-sheet trainer", "mnemonic": "milli down three, micro down six — moving to smaller units multiplies the number.", "edge_cases": ""}

{"core": "One millivolt is one-thousandth of a volt, so 2 V × 1000 = 2000 mV.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "0.002 mV would be 2 microvolts — this divides by 1000 instead of multiplying, treating volts as the smaller unit.", "why_tempting": "Learners who confuse the direction of the prefix conversion may divide by 1000."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "0.000 002 mV is 2 nanovolts — a double error, dividing by a million.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a 'small' answer for a 'small' prefix; tempting if you over-apply scaling steps."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "2 000 000 mV equals 2000 V — this multiplies by a million (the micro factor) instead of a thousand.", "why_tempting": "Reasonable if you mix up milli (10⁻³) with micro (10⁻⁶) when going the other way."}], "concept": "Metric prefixes scale by powers of ten: milli = 10⁻³, meaning 1 mV = 0.001 V. To express a quantity in a smaller unit you multiply by the inverse of the prefix factor: V → mV multiplies by 1000. Thus 2 V = 2 × 10³ mV = 2000 mV. Check the dimension: moving to a smaller unit always yields a larger number, so the answer must exceed 2.", "misconception": "Confusing the multiply/divide direction when converting between base units and prefixed units — going to a smaller unit gives a bigger number, not a smaller one.", "link": "Formula-sheet trainer", "mnemonic": "Smaller unit, bigger number: V→mV climbs three zeros (×1000).", "edge_cases": "Don't confuse milli (m, 10⁻³) with micro (µ, 10⁻⁶); the µV conversion would be ×1 000 000."}

{"core": "One gigahertz equals 1000 megahertz, so to convert MHz to GHz you divide the MHz value by 1000.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Multiplying by 1 000 000 would convert MHz to Hz scaled up enormously — it moves in the wrong direction by six orders of magnitude.", "why_tempting": "1 000 000 is the MHz-to-Hz factor, so it feels like a 'big unit' conversion if you confuse which prefixes are involved."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Multiplying by 1000 goes the wrong way — it would give a number 1000× larger, not the smaller GHz figure.", "why_tempting": "The factor of 1000 between adjacent metric prefixes is correct, but the operation should be division when moving to a larger unit."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Dividing by 1 000 000 would understate the value by three orders of magnitude; that factor relates Hz and MHz, not MHz and GHz.", "why_tempting": "It pairs 'divide' (correct direction) with a million, mixing up the prefix step size."}], "concept": "Metric prefixes step in factors of 1000: kilo (10³), mega (10⁶), giga (10⁹). Going from a smaller unit (MHz, 10⁶ Hz) to a larger unit (GHz, 10⁹ Hz) means the numeric value shrinks, so you divide by 10⁹⁻⁶ = 10³ = 1000. Example: 1296 MHz ÷ 1000 = 1.296 GHz.", "misconception": "Confusing the direction of conversion (multiply vs divide) and which prefix gap applies — learners often grab the Hz↔MHz factor of a million instead of the MHz↔GHz factor of a thousand.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Bigger unit, smaller number — divide. Each prefix jump (M→G) is ÷1000.", "edge_cases": "Reverse the operation (×1000) to go GHz→MHz; the same 1000-step applies between any two adjacent metric prefixes."}

{"core": "1 millihenry = 1000 microhenries, so 10 000 µH ÷ 1000 = 10 mH.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "100 mH would be 100 000 µH — off by a factor of 10.", "why_tempting": "Looks plausible if you misplace one decimal place during conversion."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "1 mH equals only 1000 µH, ten times too small.", "why_tempting": "Tempting if you confuse the milli/micro relationship and divide by 10 000."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "1000 mH = 1 000 000 µH, a factor of 100 too large.", "why_tempting": "Picks the literal '1000' figure from the conversion factor without applying it correctly."}], "concept": "Milli (m) = 10⁻³ and micro (µ) = 10⁻⁶, so 1 mH = 10⁻³ H and 1 µH = 10⁻⁶ H. The ratio is mH/µH = 10⁻³/10⁻⁶ = 10³, meaning 1 mH = 1000 µH. To convert microhenries to millihenries, divide by 1000: 10 000 µH / 1000 = 10 mH. Equivalently, 10 000 × 10⁻⁶ H = 0.01 H = 10 × 10⁻³ H = 10 mH.", "misconception": "Confusing which way to apply the 1000 factor — multiplying instead of dividing when going from the smaller unit (µH) to the larger unit (mH).", "link": "Formula-sheet trainer", "mnemonic": "Going from micro to milli (smaller prefix to larger), the number gets smaller — divide by 1000.", "edge_cases": "Same 1000-factor logic applies to other milli/micro pairs (mA↔µA, mF↔µF, mV↔µV)."}

{"core": "Gold, silver and aluminum are all metals with abundant free conduction electrons, making them excellent electrical conductors; option D is the only group where every listed material is a good conductor.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Wood is an insulator (especially when dry), so the group is not all conductors despite gold and silver being excellent conductors.", "why_tempting": "Gold and silver are correctly recognized as top conductors, so the eye stops before scrutinizing wood."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Paper is an insulator; only copper and aluminum here actually conduct well.", "why_tempting": "Copper and aluminum are the two most common practical conductors, lending false credibility to the whole list."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Mica is a classic insulator/dielectric (used in capacitors and as a thermal-conductive electrical insulator), so the trio fails.", "why_tempting": "Copper and gold are genuine conductors, and mica's metallic-sounding name and use in electronics make it seem conductive."}], "concept": "Electrical conductivity depends on the density of mobile charge carriers. In metals the outer valence electrons are delocalized into a 'sea' of free electrons, giving conductivities on the order of 10^7 S/m. Silver is the best (~6.3×10^7 S/m), copper next (~5.96×10^7), gold (~4.1×10^7), aluminum (~3.5×10^7). Insulators like wood, paper and mica have electrons bound in covalent/ionic bonds with a large band gap, so virtually no free carriers exist at room temperature — their conductivities are many orders of magnitude lower (mica is used precisely because it blocks current while passing heat).", "misconception": "Assuming a list is 'all conductors' because it leads with familiar metals like gold and silver, without checking the third item — the trap material in each wrong option is a common insulator (wood, paper, mica).", "link": null, "mnemonic": "'Silver, Copper, Gold, Aluminum' — the standard conductivity ranking; if a list contains wood, paper, glass, mica, rubber or plastic, it's not all conductors.", "edge_cases": "Silver actually conducts better than gold, but gold is preferred for connectors because it doesn't corrode/oxidize — conductivity ranking isn't the only criterion in real hardware."}

{"core": "Glass, air and porcelain are all dielectric materials with no free charge carriers, so they resist current flow — all three are genuine insulators.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Carbon is a conductor (graphite conducts well), so the group fails despite plastic and wood being insulators.", "why_tempting": "Plastic and wood are obvious insulators, so a quick reader may not scrutinize carbon."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Teflon and mica are excellent insulators, but aluminum is a metal and a good conductor, disqualifying the group.", "why_tempting": "Teflon and mica are textbook RF dielectrics, lulling you into accepting the third item."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Copper is one of the best conductors there is, so this group cannot be all insulators.", "why_tempting": "Wood and porcelain are clearly insulators, and the conducting item is buried in the middle."}], "concept": "An insulator (dielectric) has its electrons tightly bound with a large band gap, leaving essentially no free carriers to support conduction; conductors (most metals plus carbon/graphite) have abundant mobile electrons. The trick to every 'pick the insulators' question is to scan for the one conducting impostor: metals (aluminum, copper) and carbon are conductors, while ceramics (porcelain, mica), glass, most plastics/Teflon, dry wood, and air are insulators. Only option D contains no conductor.", "misconception": "Assuming a group is all insulators because two of the three are familiar dielectrics — exam options deliberately hide a single conductor (carbon, aluminum, copper) among good insulators.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Spot the conductor: metals + carbon = current carriers; everything ceramic, glassy, gaseous or plastic blocks it.", "edge_cases": "Air is an insulator only until ionized — a high enough voltage causes dielectric breakdown (arcing), after which it conducts."}

{"core": "Electric current is by definition the rate of flow of electric charge through a conductor, measured in amperes (1 A = 1 coulomb per second).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Capacitance is the ability of a component to store charge per unit voltage (C = Q/V), measured in farads — not the flow itself.", "why_tempting": "It involves charge (Q) in its definition, so a quick read may conflate stored charge with moving charge."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Voltage is the potential difference (energy per unit charge, joules/coulomb) that drives the flow — the cause, not the flow.", "why_tempting": "Voltage and current are intimately linked via Ohm's law, so the driving force is easily mistaken for the flow it produces."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Resistance opposes the flow of charge (R = V/I, in ohms); it impedes current rather than being current.", "why_tempting": "Resistance is defined in terms of current, so it sits right next to the concept being asked."}], "concept": "Formally, current I = dQ/dt — the time rate of charge transport. One ampere equals one coulomb (~6.24×10^18 electron charges) passing a point each second. This is distinct from the related triad: voltage V (the EMF/potential difference driving the charge), resistance R (opposition to flow), and capacitance C (charge storage per volt). Ohm's law V = IR ties three of these together, but only current is the *flow* of charge.", "misconception": "Treating the four core electrical quantities as interchangeable. Each describes a different aspect: current = flow, voltage = drive, resistance = opposition, capacitance = storage.", "link": "Tool: Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Current = Charge in motion — both start with the idea of something moving.", "edge_cases": "Conventional current is defined as the direction positive charge would flow, opposite to actual electron drift in a metal conductor."}

{"core": "Among these materials, copper has the lowest resistivity (about 1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m), making it the best electrical conductor listed.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Silicon is a semiconductor, not a good conductor — its conductivity is many orders of magnitude lower than any metal here.", "why_tempting": "It's ubiquitous in electronics, so learners associate it with conducting current, forgetting it only conducts well when doped or biased."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Aluminum conducts well (resistivity ~2.65×10⁻⁸ Ω·m) but is still roughly 60% as conductive as copper for a given cross-section.", "why_tempting": "It's a genuine conductor used in power transmission, so it seems plausible — but per unit area copper still wins."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Carbon (graphite) has far higher resistivity than metals and is used for resistive elements and brushes, not as a low-loss conductor.", "why_tempting": "Graphite does conduct and is used in electrical contacts, which can mislead."}], "concept": "Conductivity is the reciprocal of resistivity (ρ). Lower ρ means better conduction. Silver (~1.59×10⁻⁸ Ω·m) is actually the best metal, but among the options copper (~1.68×10⁻⁸) edges out aluminum (~2.65×10⁻⁸); carbon and silicon are far worse. Aluminum is often chosen for weight/cost despite needing larger cross-sections to match copper.", "misconception": "Assuming a material common in electronics (silicon, carbon) must be a good conductor — conductivity is a specific physical property, not 'used in circuits.'", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "Silver > Copper > Gold > Aluminum (best to worse among common metals).", "edge_cases": "Silver is a slightly better conductor than copper but isn't listed; if it appeared, it would be the correct answer."}

{"core": "A conductor contains abundant free (mobile) charge carriers, typically delocalized electrons in a metallic lattice, so it readily allows current to flow with very low resistance.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An insulator has essentially no free carriers; its electrons are tightly bound, giving very high resistivity and blocking current under normal voltages.", "why_tempting": "It's the direct opposite of the answer, so a learner reading quickly might pick the extreme they happen to focus on."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A semiconductor conducts only moderately and conditionally — its conductivity sits between conductor and insulator and depends on doping, temperature, and applied bias, so it does not 'readily' allow flow in raw form.", "why_tempting": "The word 'conduct' is in the name, and electronics learners associate semiconductors with active devices, mistaking that for ready conduction."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A dielectric is an insulating material used between capacitor plates to store charge by polarization; it specifically opposes DC current flow.", "why_tempting": "Familiar from capacitor theory, and a strong-electronics learner may overthink and choose the more technical-sounding term."}], "concept": "Conductivity (σ) is set by carrier density n, carrier charge q, and mobility μ: σ = nqμ. Metals have n on the order of 10^28–10^29 free electrons per m³, so σ is very high (resistivity ~10^-8 Ω·m). Insulators have n near zero (resistivity ~10^12 Ω·m and up). Semiconductors sit between (~10^16–10^19 carriers/m³, tunable by doping/temperature). The defining trait of a conductor is this large pool of delocalized carriers free to drift under an applied field.", "misconception": "Confusing the everyday meaning of words like 'semiconductor' (contains 'conduct') or 'dielectric' (a technical term) with the basic material category that simply passes current best.", "link": "section 5"}

{"core": "A good conductor offers little opposition to current flow, which is precisely what low resistance means — current passes easily for a given applied voltage.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Capacitance describes the ability to store charge across a dielectric, not the ease of conducting steady current; it has no direct bearing on DC conductivity.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like an 'electrical property,' and 'low' anything can seem like it removes a barrier."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Admittance (Y = 1/Z) is the reciprocal of impedance — its conductance part actually rises for good conductors. LOW admittance means POOR conduction, the opposite of what's asked.", "why_tempting": "Admittance is a real measure of how readily AC flows, so it looks correct until you notice the question pairs it with 'low.'"}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Reluctance is the magnetic-circuit analogue of resistance (opposition to magnetic flux), not electrical conduction, so it doesn't describe how well something carries current.", "why_tempting": "It's the direct magnetic counterpart of resistance and 'low reluctance' parallels 'low resistance,' inviting confusion between magnetic and electric circuits."}], "concept": "Conductivity is governed by resistance: R = ρL/A, where ρ is resistivity. Materials with low ρ (copper, silver) yield low R and thus high current for a given V (I = V/R). Conductance G = 1/R and admittance Y = 1/Z generalize this for AC — a good conductor has HIGH conductance/admittance and LOW resistance/impedance. The trap is the inverse relationship: 'good conduction' maps to LOW resistance but HIGH admittance.", "misconception": "Confusing reciprocal quantities (admittance vs resistance) or magnetic-circuit terms (reluctance) with simple electrical resistance.", "link": "Tools — Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Low Resistance = current Runs free; admittance is the opposite (high = good).", "edge_cases": "For AC, the relevant quantity is impedance/admittance; but for the DC conduction this question targets, resistance is the correct property."}

{"core": "In electrical notation, R is the standard symbol for resistance, measured in ohms, representing the in-phase (real) opposition to current flow.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Impedance is symbolized by Z, the complex sum of resistance and reactance.", "why_tempting": "Impedance is also measured in ohms and represents opposition to current, so it feels interchangeable with resistance to the inattentive."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Reluctance is the magnetic-circuit analog of resistance and is symbolized by R-script or ℛ, not plain R; it opposes magnetic flux, not current.", "why_tempting": "The word begins with 'R' and is conceptually parallel to resistance, inviting a wrong match."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Reactance uses the symbol X (X_L for inductive, X_C for capacitive) and is the out-of-phase opposition due to inductors and capacitors.", "why_tempting": "Reactance starts with 'R' phonetically (rea-) and is measured in ohms like resistance."}], "concept": "The standard set of symbols: R = resistance, X = reactance, Z = impedance, where Z = R + jX. Resistance is the real, frequency-independent component dissipating power as heat; reactance is the imaginary, frequency-dependent component storing and returning energy. Keeping these symbol-to-quantity mappings straight is foundational for any AC analysis on the exam.", "misconception": "Assuming any ohm-valued opposition term can be labelled R, when each distinct quantity (R, X, Z) has its own dedicated symbol.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "R = Resistance (Real), X = reactance (the 'unknown'/imaginary part), Z = impedance (the Zum of both).", "edge_cases": "In magnetic circuits, reluctance is sometimes written R or ℛ — context (electrical vs. magnetic) disambiguates, but on this electrical-quantity question R means resistance."}

{"core": "Conductance (G) is the reciprocal of resistance, G = 1/R, measured in siemens (formerly mhos). It quantifies how easily current flows for a given voltage.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Reluctance is the magnetic-circuit analog of resistance (opposition to magnetic flux), not the inverse of electrical resistance.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a resistance-related term and shares the 'opposition' concept, so it can be mistaken for a resistance counterpart."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Permeability describes a material's ability to support a magnetic field — a magnetic property unrelated to the reciprocal of resistance.", "why_tempting": "It's the magnetic 'ease' parameter, which superficially parallels conductance being an electrical 'ease,' luring the careless."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Reactance is the opposition to AC current from inductance or capacitance; it is a form of opposition like resistance, not its inverse.", "why_tempting": "Reactance shares units (ohms) and the role of opposing current, so it can be confused with a resistance-derived quantity."}], "concept": "Resistance R opposes current; its reciprocal, conductance G = 1/R, expresses ease of conduction in siemens (S). The analogous AC pair is reactance X and susceptance B = 1/X, and the complex generalizations are impedance Z = R + jX and admittance Y = 1/Z = G + jB. Note that for a complex impedance, G ≠ 1/R in general because Y = 1/Z mixes the real and imaginary parts; the simple G = 1/R holds for a purely resistive element.", "misconception": "Confusing the magnetic-circuit terms (reluctance, permeability) or the AC opposition term (reactance) with the true electrical reciprocal of resistance.", "link": "Tools — \"Ohm's Law & Power\"", "mnemonic": "conductance = 1/Resistance, in Siemens — 'easy flow' is the opposite of 'opposition.'", "edge_cases": "For a series RC/RL or any complex Z, conductance is the real part of admittance Y = 1/Z, which is not simply 1/R unless reactance is zero."}

{"core": "A voltage drop is the potential difference that develops across any circuit element when current flows through its resistance (or impedance) — that is, the loss of voltage attributable to current flow through that part of the circuit.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A step-down transformer's output is a transformed voltage by turns ratio, not a loss caused by current flowing through resistance.", "why_tempting": "A transformer does produce a lower output voltage, so 'drop' loosely suggests a reduced voltage, conflating turns-ratio reduction with resistive drop."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A node at zero volts (e.g., circuit ground/reference) is a defined potential point, not the change in potential across an element.", "why_tempting": "Beginners associate 'drop' with voltage 'going to zero,' so a zero-volt point seems related."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "That describes a transformer's terminal voltage difference, again a transformer characteristic rather than the general definition of voltage drop from current through circuit resistance.", "why_tempting": "It uses the words 'difference in voltage,' which superficially matches the idea of a drop."}], "concept": "By Ohm's law, V = I·R: whenever current I flows through a resistance R, a potential difference V appears across it. In a series circuit the source voltage is fully accounted for as the sum of the individual drops (Kirchhoff's voltage law: ΣV_drops = V_source). Conductors, contacts, and components all have finite resistance, so each carries a drop proportional to current — which is why feedline and connector resistance 'eats' voltage and dissipates power (P = I²R) before it reaches the load.", "misconception": "Thinking 'voltage drop' is a transformer property or a specific zero-volt node, rather than the universal V = I·R potential difference that current produces across any resistance.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Current through resistance → voltage drops (V = I·R).", "edge_cases": "The same principle extends to AC, where the 'drop' is across impedance Z (V = I·Z) and can be out of phase with the current."}

{"core": "The resistance of a metallic conductor varies with temperature: as temperature rises, lattice vibrations increase, scattering electrons more and raising resistance.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Surface humidity may affect leakage paths or corrosion over time, but it does not change the bulk resistance of the conductor itself.", "why_tempting": "Humidity is associated with electrical problems generally, so it seems plausible as an environmental factor."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "For an ohmic conductor, resistance is the constant of proportionality between V and I; changing voltage changes current proportionally but leaves R unchanged.", "why_tempting": "Ohm's law links all three, so a learner may wrongly assume R depends on the applied voltage."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Current does not define resistance; it results from the applied voltage divided by resistance. Indirectly, large currents heat the wire, but that is a temperature effect, not current per se.", "why_tempting": "High current heating wires is real, blurring the line and making current look like the cause."}], "concept": "For metals, resistance rises roughly linearly with temperature over normal ranges: R = R0[1 + α(T − T0)], where α is the temperature coefficient of resistance (positive for metals like copper). Physically, higher temperature increases thermal vibration of the crystal lattice, increasing electron scattering and reducing mobility, hence higher resistivity. Note semiconductors behave oppositely (negative α). Resistance is a material/geometry property (R = ρL/A) and the resistivity ρ is the temperature-dependent term — not voltage or current.", "misconception": "Because Ohm's law ties V, I, and R together, learners assume changing V or I changes R. In a linear resistor R is fixed by material and temperature; V and I merely track each other through it.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Hot metal = harder for electrons = higher R.", "edge_cases": "This applies to metals (positive temperature coefficient); semiconductors and some components (thermistors, carbon) can show decreasing resistance with temperature."}

{"core": "In a DC circuit, current flows in one fixed direction; the term that describes this fixed sense of flow (and the corresponding + and − terminals) is polarity.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Phase describes the timing relationship between AC sinusoidal quantities; it has no meaning for steady DC current direction.", "why_tempting": "Phase is a common circuit term and sounds technical, so it can be mistaken for anything describing 'direction.'"}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Polarization refers to the orientation of an electromagnetic wave's electric field (e.g. vertical/horizontal antenna polarization), not to current direction.", "why_tempting": "It looks almost identical to 'polarity' and shares the same root word."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Directivity is an antenna property describing how an antenna concentrates radiation in a given direction; unrelated to DC current.", "why_tempting": "The word literally contains 'direction,' which baits an answer-by-keyword guess."}], "concept": "DC by definition is unidirectional flow: charge moves consistently from one terminal to the other. 'Polarity' names which terminal is positive and which is negative, and therefore the direction of conventional current (from + to − externally). Contrast with AC, where current periodically reverses and is characterized by amplitude, frequency, and phase rather than a fixed polarity.", "misconception": "Confusing similar-sounding wave/antenna terms (polarization, directivity) or AC timing terms (phase) with the simple concept of which way DC flows.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Polarity = Poles (+/−) → which way DC goes."}

{"core": "Power is defined as the rate of energy transfer or consumption per unit time (P = energy/time, measured in watts = joules/second). It directly answers \"rate at which energy is used.\"", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Resistance (ohms) opposes current flow and relates voltage to current via Ohm's law; it is not a rate of energy use.", "why_tempting": "Resistance does dissipate energy as heat, so it feels associated with energy consumption, but it's a property, not a rate."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Current (amperes) is the rate of charge flow (coulombs/second), not the rate of energy use.", "why_tempting": "It is also a 'rate' quantity, so the word 'rate' in the question lures you here — but it's the rate of charge, not energy."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Voltage (volts) is energy per unit charge (joules/coulomb) — a potential difference, not a rate over time.", "why_tempting": "Voltage involves energy in its definition, but it lacks the per-time element that defines a rate of use."}], "concept": "Power P = work/time = energy/time. In circuit terms P = VI, and combining with Ohm's law gives P = I²R = V²/R. The unit watt (W) is one joule per second, making power inherently a rate. Voltage (J/C) and current (C/s) each capture only one factor; their product reintroduces time through the charge term: V·I = (J/C)(C/s) = J/s = W.", "misconception": "Confusing a 'rate' (per-second) quantity with energy itself, or assuming current — the other obvious rate — is the answer because both involve 'per second.'", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Watts = work per second; P = VI is the rate, V and I are the ingredients.", "edge_cases": "For AC, true (real) power in watts accounts for the power factor (P = VI·cosθ); apparent power in VA is not the actual rate of energy consumed."}

{"core": "The watt is the unit of power — the rate at which energy is consumed. The bulb rated highest in watts (100 W) consumes electrical energy fastest.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "60 W is a higher rate than 40 or 50 W but still less than 100 W.", "why_tempting": "It's a 'middle-high' value that might seem like a plausible compromise if you misread the question."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "50 W is below 60 W and 100 W, so it is not the highest rate.", "why_tempting": "Could be picked by someone scanning the list without comparing all values."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "40 W is the lowest rating, hence the slowest energy consumption.", "why_tempting": "A learner inverting the concept (thinking lower wattage = more energy over time, like a slow drain) might wrongly pick it."}], "concept": "Power P is energy per unit time: P = E/t, measured in watts (1 W = 1 joule/second). A bulb's wattage rating is precisely this instantaneous rate of energy conversion at its rated voltage. Total energy used = P × t, so over the same interval the highest-wattage device consumes the most energy. Don't confuse rate (watts) with accumulated energy (watt-hours).", "misconception": "Confusing the rate of energy use (power, watts) with total energy consumed over time (energy, watt-hours). The question asks for rate, which the watt directly expresses.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Watt = rate. More watts now = more energy per second.", "edge_cases": "Ratings assume operation at rated voltage; a bulb run at lower voltage draws less than its nameplate power."}

{"core": "The watt is the SI unit of power — the rate of energy transfer (one joule per second). In electrical terms, P = V × I, so one watt equals one volt driving one ampere.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The ohm is the unit of resistance (opposition to current), not power.", "why_tempting": "It's a fundamental electrical unit covered alongside power in the same study section, so it's easy to confuse the role of each unit."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The ampere measures current — the rate of charge flow — not the rate of energy transfer.", "why_tempting": "Current is a factor in the power equation (P = V × I), so it feels associated with power."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The volt measures potential difference (energy per unit charge), not power.", "why_tempting": "Voltage is the other factor in P = V × I, so learners who half-remember the formula may pick it."}], "concept": "Power is the rate of doing work or transferring energy: 1 W = 1 J/s. In a DC or resistive circuit, P = V·I, and substituting Ohm's law gives the equivalent forms P = I²R and P = V²/R. Each base unit has a distinct job: volts (potential), amperes (current), ohms (resistance), and watts (power = volts × amperes). Keeping that mapping straight is the whole point of this question.", "misconception": "Treating the volt, ampere, ohm, and watt as interchangeable 'electrical units' rather than recognizing that only the watt quantifies power (energy per unit time).", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Watts = Work rate. The 'W' in watt ties to Work (energy) over time.", "edge_cases": "In AC circuits, watts measure true (real) power only; apparent power is measured in volt-amperes (VA) and reactive power in volt-amperes reactive (var), but the watt remains the unit of actual power delivered."}

{"core": "An open circuit has a break in the conductive path (infinite resistance), so no current can flow and none is drawn from the battery.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Reactance limits AC current but doesn't stop DC flow in a battery/resistor circuit; pure reactance still passes current and isn't a 'malfunction' here.", "why_tempting": "Sounds technical and impedance-related, attractive to someone overthinking the AC angle."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A closed circuit is the normal complete path — it allows current to flow, the opposite of the fault described.", "why_tempting": "The word 'closed' can be misread as 'blocked/shut off,' suggesting no current."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A short circuit is near-zero resistance across the source, which causes excessive (maximum) current, not zero current.", "why_tempting": "It's clearly a malfunction, so it grabs attention — but it produces too much current, not none."}], "concept": "Current requires a complete conductive loop. With I = V/R, an open circuit means R → ∞, so I = V/∞ → 0. A short circuit is the inverse: R → 0, giving I = V/0 → very large current limited only by source/wiring resistance. 'Closed' = intact loop (normal operation); 'open' = broken loop (no flow).", "misconception": "Confusing the everyday meanings of 'open' and 'closed' (a closed door blocks you) with circuit terminology, where 'closed' means the path is complete and 'open' means it's broken.", "link": "Tools — Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Open = no flow (like an open drawbridge stops traffic); Closed = current goes.", "edge_cases": "A short circuit doesn't draw zero current — it draws dangerously high current and can damage the battery or wiring."}

{"core": "A short circuit provides a near-zero-resistance path that bypasses the intended load, so by Ohm's law the current rises to a dangerously high value limited only by source and wiring resistance.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A closed circuit is simply a complete, normal current path through its intended load; current is whatever the load resistance dictates — not excessive.", "why_tempting": "'Closed' sounds like current is flowing, and learners may equate 'current flowing' with 'too much current.'"}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An open circuit is broken, so no current flows at all (I = 0) — the opposite of drawing too much.", "why_tempting": "'Open' can be confused with a fault condition, and faults are associated with abnormal current."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A 'dead' circuit has no source or is de-energized, so essentially no current flows.", "why_tempting": "It sounds abnormal/faulty, so it could be mistaken for a current problem."}], "concept": "With the load short-circuited, the effective resistance collapses toward the small series resistance of the source and conductors (R → R_source + R_wire). From I = V/R, as R approaches zero the current spikes toward a maximum set only by those parasitic resistances. This is why fuses and breakers exist: they open before the short-circuit current damages wiring or the supply.", "misconception": "Confusing the four circuit states — assuming any 'flowing' or 'faulty' condition means high current. Only the short (low-resistance bypass) forces excessive current; open and dead carry essentially none.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Short = low R = high I (low resistance lets current 'short-cut' the load).", "edge_cases": "Real current isn't infinite — it's bounded by source internal resistance and conductor resistance, which is why short-circuit current is finite but still large enough to cause heating, arcing, or fire."}

{"core": "Power — the rate of energy transfer per unit time — is measured in watts (W), defined as one joule per second, or equivalently one volt-ampere in DC/resistive circuits.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The ampere is the unit of electric current (charge flow per second), not power.", "why_tempting": "Current appears in P = VI, so it's easy to grab a factor of the power equation instead of the result."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The ohm measures resistance/impedance, the opposition to current — a different quantity entirely.", "why_tempting": "Resistance also appears in power forms like P = I²R, tempting you to confuse the variable with the answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The volt is the unit of potential difference (energy per unit charge), not power.", "why_tempting": "Voltage is the other factor in P = VI, so it looks like a plausible 'electrical' unit for power."}], "concept": "Power P is energy per unit time. In electrical terms P = VI, and 1 W = 1 V × 1 A = 1 J/s. The three common algebraic forms — P = VI, P = I²R, P = V²/R — all resolve to watts because volts×amperes, amperes²×ohms, and volts²/ohms each reduce to joules per second dimensionally. Distinguish the dimensions: V (potential), A (current), Ω (resistance), W (power).", "misconception": "Treating any variable that shows up inside a power formula as if it were the unit of power itself.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Watts = Work per second (both start with W).", "edge_cases": "In AC circuits 'watts' specifically means real (true) power; apparent power is in volt-amperes (VA) and reactive power in VAR — same dimensions, different names to flag power factor."}

{"core": "Power is the rate of energy transfer; in a DC or resistive circuit it equals the product of voltage and current, P = V × I.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Inductance and capacitance store energy in magnetic and electric fields respectively; their product (related to LC resonance) has units of time², not watts.", "why_tempting": "Both are energy-related reactive quantities, so a learner may assume their combination yields power."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Voltage times inductance has no meaningful power interpretation; inductance is not a current-like quantity, so the product is dimensionally wrong for watts.", "why_tempting": "It pairs the correct voltage term with a plausible-looking second quantity, mimicking the right answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Resistance times capacitance gives an RC time constant (seconds), not power.", "why_tempting": "Both appear in circuit analysis, and a learner may confuse the RC product with an energy/power relationship."}], "concept": "The fundamental power relation is P = V × I (watts = volts × amperes). Combined with Ohm's law (V = I × R), this yields the derived forms P = I²R and P = V²/R. Note that V × I gives instantaneous or DC/resistive power; in AC circuits with reactance you must account for the power factor (P = V × I × cosθ), since only the in-phase component delivers real (dissipated) power.", "misconception": "Assuming that any two electrical quantities multiply to give power, rather than recognizing that power is specifically the product of the through variable (current) and the across variable (voltage).", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "PIV: Power = I × V — picture the pyramid with P on top, I and V below.", "edge_cases": "For reactive AC loads, P = VI only when voltage and current are in phase; otherwise multiply by the power factor (cosθ) to get real power, while VI alone gives apparent power (VA)."}

{"core": "Power equals voltage times current: P = V × I, so volts multiplied by amperes yields watts.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Henries are the unit of inductance, not part of the basic power equation; amperes × henries gives volt-seconds (flux linkage in webers), not watts.", "why_tempting": "Amperes is genuinely a factor in P = V·I, so seeing it paired with another unit looks plausible."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Farads measure capacitance. Volts × farads gives coulombs (charge, Q = CV), not power.", "why_tempting": "Volts is correct half of the answer, and capacitor energy formulas involve V and C, blurring the distinction."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Farads × henries gives seconds-squared (LC has units of time², the basis of resonance), with no relation to power.", "why_tempting": "Both are common reactive component units, so a learner unsure of the power formula may guess them together."}], "concept": "The defining relation for electrical power is P = V·I. Dimensionally, a volt is a joule per coulomb and an ampere is a coulomb per second, so V·A = (J/C)(C/s) = J/s = watt. This is the conduction-power form of Ohm's law family; substituting V = IR or I = V/R also gives P = I²R and P = V²/R, but the fundamental units that directly multiply to watts are the volt and the ampere.", "misconception": "Confusing the units tied to energy storage (farads for charge, henries for flux) with the units that define instantaneous power dissipation.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "\"Watt = Volt-Amp\" — power is pressure (volts) times flow (amperes).", "edge_cases": "Volt-amperes (VA) equals watts only for purely resistive or DC loads; with AC reactance, true power (W) = V·I·cos φ, where φ is the phase angle."}

{"core": "A resistor that overheats and burns is converting electrical energy into heat faster than it can shed it — that rate of energy conversion is power, P = I²R, measured in watts. Exceeding the resistor's power rating causes thermal damage.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Voltage is electric potential difference, not a quantity that is 'dissipated.' A resistor can have a high voltage across it without overheating if the current (and thus power) is low.", "why_tempting": "Voltage drop does appear across the resistor and feels like a stress on it, so learners conflate it with the heating cause."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Resistance is a fixed property of the component, not something dissipated. It influences power (P = I²R) but is not consumed or given off.", "why_tempting": "The word 'resistor' makes resistance the intuitive answer, and resistance does set how much heat develops."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Current alone isn't 'dissipated' — it flows through. Heating depends on current AND resistance together (I²R); current is a factor, not the dissipated quantity itself.", "why_tempting": "Excess current does drive the overheating, so it seems like the direct culprit — but the dissipated quantity is power."}], "concept": "Power dissipation in a resistor is P = I²R = V²/R = VI, expressed in watts — the rate at which electrical energy becomes heat. Every resistor has a power (wattage) rating; exceeding it raises the device's temperature beyond what its body and any heatsinking can radiate/conduct away, leading to scorching or open failure. Voltage, current, and resistance are inputs to this rate, but only power (energy per unit time) is what is actually 'dissipated' as heat.", "misconception": "Thinking the cause of overheating is a single electrical variable like current or voltage, rather than recognizing that heating is fundamentally about power — the product/combination of those variables over time.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Resistors are rated in WATTS, not volts or amps — what burns is power.", "edge_cases": "A resistor can survive brief overpower if it doesn't reach failure temperature; ratings often assume free-air cooling, so enclosed or bunched resistors burn at lower dissipation than their nameplate suggests."}

{"core": "\"Continuity\" means there is an unbroken conductive path — a closed circuit through which current can flow.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Backup power describes a redundant supply, not the integrity of the conductive path.", "why_tempting": "\"Continuity\" sounds like \"continuous power,\" inviting confusion with uninterrupted supply."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A continuous-duty rating concerns thermal/operational tolerance over time, not whether the path is electrically intact.", "why_tempting": "The word \"continuous\" overlaps semantically with \"continuity.\""}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Continuity is independent of current type — it applies equally to AC and DC circuits.", "why_tempting": "Continuity testers use a small DC source, suggesting a false DC association."}], "concept": "Continuity testing verifies a low-resistance, unbroken connection. A multimeter in continuity mode injects a small current and measures resistance: near 0 Ω (often with a beep) confirms a closed path; an open (broken wire, bad solder joint, blown fuse) reads infinite resistance and no current flow. It is the practical complement to checking for shorts — you confirm intended paths conduct and unintended ones don't.", "misconception": "Reading \"continuity\" as referring to power supply continuity or duty rating rather than the physical integrity of the current path.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Continuity = Closed path, Current can flow.", "edge_cases": "A continuity test should be done with power removed; a high-resistance or partially corroded joint may still buzz yet cause problems under load."}

{"core": "A fuse blows from excessive current. A short circuit provides a near-zero-resistance path, so current spikes far above the fuse rating and it opens immediately.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A 'closed circuit' is simply a complete, normally operating circuit — that's the intended state, not a fault.", "why_tempting": "The word 'closed' sounds like something went wrong (slammed shut), but in circuit terms it just means current flows normally."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "An open circuit breaks the current path, so current drops to zero — a fuse cannot blow with no current flowing through it.", "why_tempting": "'Open' sounds like a break or failure, so a learner may assume any abnormality blows the fuse, ignoring that zero current can't overload anything."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A resonant circuit is a deliberate L-C arrangement tuned to a frequency; it's a design feature, not a DC fault that draws excess supply current.", "why_tempting": "It's a familiar radio term and sounds technical, which can lure someone away from the simpler correct answer."}], "concept": "By Ohm's law I = V/R, fuse current is inversely proportional to load resistance. A short reduces R toward 0 Ω, so I → very large, exceeding the fuse's interrupting rating and melting its element almost instantly. The fuse is the deliberate weak link that opens to protect wiring and the supply from this overcurrent. Compare: open circuit R → ∞, so I → 0 (nothing blows); normal closed circuit R is sized so I stays under the fuse rating.", "misconception": "Confusing the everyday meanings of 'open' and 'closed' with their electrical definitions — and assuming any 'abnormal-sounding' condition (open, resonant) causes overcurrent, when only a low-resistance fault (short) does.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Short = small resistance = surging current = snapped fuse.", "edge_cases": "An immediate blow points to a hard short (e.g. reversed polarity or a pinched wire). A fuse that blows after some delay or under load often indicates an overload rather than a dead short."}

{"core": "By Ohm's law, V = I × R = 0.5 A × 2 Ω = 1.0 V.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "0.25 V comes from dividing 0.5 by 2 instead of multiplying — that would be current divided by resistance, which is dimensionally meaningless here.", "why_tempting": "A learner who recalls 'divide' from Ohm's law (I = V/R) may apply division to the wrong quantities."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "2.5 V results from misreading the numbers, e.g. treating current as 1.25 A or adding rather than multiplying.", "why_tempting": "It contains the digits 2 and 5 from the given values, so it looks plausible at a glance."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "1.5 V doesn't follow from any correct combination of 0.5 A and 2 Ω.", "why_tempting": "It's near the correct 1.0 V and serves as a close numerical decoy."}], "concept": "Ohm's law in the form V = I × R directly relates voltage drop across a resistor to the current through it and its resistance. Here the multiplication 0.5 × 2 = 1.0, so 1.0 V appears across the 2 Ω resistor. The key is choosing the correct rearrangement: V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I.", "misconception": "Confusing which operation Ohm's law calls for — multiplying I and R for voltage, versus dividing in the other forms. The fractional current (0.5 A) tempts learners toward division.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "V = I·R: 'Very Important Resistor' — voltage is current times resistance.", "edge_cases": "This assumes a purely resistive element; for reactive components you'd use impedance, not plain resistance."}

{"core": "Ohm's Law states I = V/R: current equals the applied voltage divided by the circuit resistance.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "I = R×V is dimensionally meaningless (Ω·V ≠ amperes) and predicts higher current for higher resistance, which is backwards.", "why_tempting": "It uses both known quantities and looks like a valid rearrangement if you forget which operation Ohm's Law requires."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "I = R/V inverts the relationship — it makes current rise with resistance and fall with voltage, the opposite of reality.", "why_tempting": "It's a near-mirror of the correct V/R form, so a hasty reader can flip numerator and denominator."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "I = P/V is a correct power relation, but it requires power, not resistance — it doesn't use the quantities the question specifies.", "why_tempting": "It is a genuinely true formula (from P = VI), so it looks right until you notice it needs P, not R."}], "concept": "Ohm's Law links the three core quantities: V = I·R. Solving for current gives I = V/R. The triangle/algebra also yields R = V/I and the other rearrangement. Since the question supplies V and R, you isolate I directly. Note the inverse relationship to R: for a fixed voltage, doubling resistance halves current. Distinguish this from the power family (P = VI, P = I²R, P = V²/R), which option D draws from.", "misconception": "Confusing which variable goes in the numerator versus denominator, or substituting a power equation when only voltage and resistance are given.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "V-I-R triangle: cover the quantity you want — covering I leaves V over R, so I = V/R.", "edge_cases": "Valid only for ohmic (linear) DC resistances; for reactive AC circuits use impedance Z instead of R."}

{"core": "Ohm's Law rearranged for resistance gives R = V/I, so resistance equals voltage divided by current.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This inverts the relationship — I/V gives conductance (in siemens), not resistance.", "why_tempting": "Both V and I appear, so a learner who forgets which goes on top can pick the reciprocal."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "V×I yields power (watts), not resistance.", "why_tempting": "It's the correct combination of the same two quantities, just the wrong operation."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "P/V gives current (I = P/V), not resistance.", "why_tempting": "It resembles a valid power-formula rearrangement and uses a familiar division form."}], "concept": "From V = IR, isolating R gives R = V/I. The three core Ohm's Law forms are V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I. Combined with P = VI, you can derive any quantity from two knowns — e.g. R = V²/P or R = P/I². The key distinction: division of voltage by current yields ohms, multiplication yields watts, and the inverse (I/V) yields siemens.", "misconception": "Confusing which variable is the numerator, or mixing the resistance formula with the power formula since both use only V and I.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "In the V-I-R triangle, cover R and you're left with V over I.", "edge_cases": "Holds exactly for purely resistive DC circuits; in AC with reactance you'd use impedance Z, not simple resistance."}

{"core": "Ohm's Law in its voltage form: V = I × R. With current and resistance known, multiplying them gives the voltage across the element.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "V = P/I is a valid power-law rearrangement, but it requires power and current, not the current and resistance specified in the question.", "why_tempting": "It is a genuine correct relationship (P = VI rearranged), so it looks right if you don't read which quantities are given."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "I/R has units of A/Ω, which is not volts; it inverts resistance's role in Ohm's Law.", "why_tempting": "It mixes the right variables (I and R) but with the wrong operation, easy to pick if you guess at the arrangement."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "R/I gives Ω/A, dimensionally wrong for voltage and physically meaningless here.", "why_tempting": "Uses the correct two variables, so it's plausible to anyone who hasn't memorized the exact form V = IR."}], "concept": "Ohm's Law links the three quantities: V = IR, I = V/R, R = V/I. A quick dimensional check resolves any ambiguity — volts = amperes × ohms (since 1 Ω = 1 V/A, A × V/A = V). Both division forms (C and D) fail this units test immediately, leaving only the product. Option A is a different law (P = VI) and applies only when power is the known quantity.", "misconception": "Confusing which two variables are supplied. The question gives current and resistance, so only the V = IR form applies; the power-based formula and the inverted divisions are decoys.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "The 'VIR' triangle: cover V → I×R; cover I → V/R; cover R → V/I.", "edge_cases": "Holds for DC and instantaneous values; for AC reactive circuits replace R with impedance Z."}

{"core": "R = V / I = 12 V / 0.25 A = 48 ohms.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "12 ohms ignores the current entirely — it's just the source voltage value treated as a resistance.", "why_tempting": "The 12 from '12-volt source' is the most prominent number and gets grabbed directly."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.25 ohms is just the current value restated as resistance, no division performed.", "why_tempting": "Picking the small given number feels safe when unsure which operation applies."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "3 ohms is V × I (12 × 0.25), i.e. the power dissipated (3 W), not resistance.", "why_tempting": "Multiplying the two givens is a common reflex, and 3 is a clean-looking result."}], "concept": "Ohm's law rearranged for resistance: R = V / I. With V = 12 V and I = 0.25 A, R = 12 / 0.25 = 48 Ω. Note dividing by a fraction less than 1 increases the result — small current at a fixed voltage implies high resistance. As a check, P = VI = 3 W and P = V²/R = 144/48 = 3 W confirms consistency.", "misconception": "Confusing which quantity divides which — and the intuition that a 'small' current (0.25) should give a small resistance, when in fact small current means large resistance.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "R = E/I: voltage on top, current on the bottom — current over 1 makes R smaller, current under 1 makes R bigger.", "edge_cases": "Watch the fractional divisor: dividing by 0.25 is the same as multiplying by 4, which is why the answer jumps well above the 12 V figure."}

{"core": "R = V/I = 9 V / 0.010 A = 900 ohms.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This treats the current as 0.1 A (100 mA): 9/0.1 = 90, off by a factor of 10.", "why_tempting": "A single decimal-place slip in the milliamp-to-amp conversion lands here."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "This treats current as 1 A: 9/1 = 9, ignoring the milli prefix entirely.", "why_tempting": "Dividing 9 by a 'rounded' 10 without converting units gives 9, then carelessly dropping ohms scale."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This uses 0.001 A (1 mA): 9/0.001 = 9000, an extra factor-of-10 error in the conversion.", "why_tempting": "Over-correcting the milli conversion (moving the decimal one place too many) yields 9000."}], "concept": "Direct application of Ohm's law solved for resistance: R = V/I. The only real work is unit handling — 10 mA = 10 × 10⁻³ A = 0.010 A. Then R = 9 / 0.010 = 900 Ω. A quick sanity check: 900 Ω × 0.010 A = 9 V, confirming the result. Carrying the prefix algebraically (9 V ÷ 10 mA = 0.9 V/mA = 0.9 kΩ = 900 Ω) avoids decimal slips.", "misconception": "The trap is mishandling the milli (10⁻³) prefix on the current. Each wrong option corresponds to a different power-of-ten error in converting milliamperes to amperes.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Volts over Amps gives Ohms — and milli means move the decimal three places (÷1000), so divide by 0.010, not 10.", "edge_cases": "Keep the prefix consistent: V/mA gives kΩ directly (9/10 = 0.9 kΩ = 900 Ω), which is often faster and less error-prone than full conversion to base units."}

{"core": "By Ohm's law, V = I × R = 0.44 A × 50 Ω = 22 V.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "2.2 V is the product divided by 10 — an order-of-magnitude error.", "why_tempting": "Looks right if you misplace the decimal in 0.44 × 50 and get 2.2 instead of 22."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.22 V is off by a factor of 100; it resembles the bare current value (0.44) halved or a dropped exponent.", "why_tempting": "Tempting if you confuse the small current value with the answer or mishandle decimals."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "222 V is ten times too large — a decimal slip the other way (treating 0.44 as 4.4).", "why_tempting": "Arises from multiplying 4.44 × 50 or shifting the decimal in the wrong direction."}], "concept": "Direct application of Ohm's law: V = I·R. With I = 0.44 A and R = 50 Ω, the multiplication 0.44 × 50 = 22.0 V exactly. The only real risk here is decimal placement: 0.44 × 50 = 0.44 × 5 × 10 = 2.2 × 10 = 22. Keeping the powers of ten explicit prevents an order-of-magnitude slip.", "misconception": "The trap is not the formula but careless decimal arithmetic — every distractor is the correct digits (22) shifted by a power of ten.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "V = IR: 'Very Important Rule.'", "edge_cases": "Holds for DC or RMS AC across a pure resistance; for reactive loads you'd use impedance Z instead of R."}

{"core": "By Ohm's law, I = V/R = 6 V / 30 Ω = 0.2 A.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "0.5 A would require R = 12 Ω (6/0.5), not 30 Ω.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible 'half-amp' round number if you misdivide or invert the ratio."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.005 A is off by a factor of 40; it implies a 1200 Ω resistor or a misplaced decimal.", "why_tempting": "Mistaking 6/30 = 0.2 for a milliamp-scale answer leads to a decimal/units slip."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "2 A is the result of dividing R/something incorrectly; 6/30 is 0.2, not 2 — a factor-of-10 error.", "why_tempting": "Dropping the decimal place in 0.2 gives 2, an easy slip under time pressure."}], "concept": "Ohm's law in current form: I = V/R. With V = 6 V across R = 30 Ω, I = 6/30 = 0.2 A = 200 mA. Keep units consistent: volts over ohms yields amperes directly. Watch the decimal — 6/30 reduces to 1/5 = 0.2.", "misconception": "Misplacing the decimal point or inverting the V/R ratio (computing R/V instead of V/R).", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "V over R for I — 'voltage on top, resistance below.'", "edge_cases": "Assumes an ideal resistor and a battery with negligible internal resistance; real internal resistance would slightly lower the actual current."}

{"core": "By Ohm's law, V = I × R = 0.200 A × 25 Ω = 5 V.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "8 V doesn't result from any correct combination of 200 mA and 25 Ω; it's an arbitrary value.", "why_tempting": "Looks plausible if you mis-multiply or guess a 'round-ish' answer near the correct one."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "0.5 V comes from dividing 25 by 50 or other mishandling — it inverts the relationship; current through 25 Ω at 0.5 V would be only 20 mA.", "why_tempting": "Shares the digits '5' and looks like a scaled-down version, tempting if you drop a factor of 10."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "50 V results from forgetting that 200 mA = 0.200 A and instead treating it as 2 A (or multiplying 25 × 2). It's off by a factor of 10.", "why_tempting": "The classic milliamp-to-amp conversion slip: 25 × 2 = 50 if you misplace the decimal."}], "concept": "Direct application of Ohm's law in the form V = I·R. The only real work is unit conversion: 200 mA = 200 × 10⁻³ A = 0.2 A. Then V = 0.2 × 25 = 5 V. Keeping current in amperes (not milliamperes) before multiplying is the whole game here.", "misconception": "Treating 200 milliamperes as 200 amperes or 2 amperes — failing to convert mA → A correctly, which throws the answer off by a factor of 10 or more.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "'milli means move the decimal three left': 200 mA → 0.200 A before plugging into V=IR.", "edge_cases": "Relay coil resistance is the DC resistance used here; in a real relay the coil also has inductance, but that only affects transient/AC behaviour, not the steady-state DC operating voltage this question asks about."}

{"core": "Ohm's law in resistance form is R = E / I — dividing the voltage across a component by the current through it yields its resistance.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This inverts the ratio, giving the reciprocal (conductance-like) value, not resistance; units would be siemens-equivalent, not ohms.", "why_tempting": "It contains the same two variables E and I divided, so it looks like a plausible rearrangement if you forget which goes on top."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "E squared divided by I is dimensionally wrong for resistance (volts²/amp = volt·ohm, not ohm); it resembles a power expression, not Ohm's law.", "why_tempting": "Squared-voltage forms appear in power formulas (P = E²/R), so it borrows familiar-looking algebra."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "E × I is the formula for power (P = EI in watts), not resistance.", "why_tempting": "It's a real and commonly memorized formula, so it's easy to grab the wrong one from the Ohm's-law/power family."}], "concept": "Ohm's law links the three quantities: E = I·R. Solving for resistance gives R = E / I. The three forms (E = IR, I = E/R, R = E/I) are algebraic rearrangements of a single relation. Dimensional check: volts / amperes = ohms, confirming D is correct. Keep this distinct from the power relations P = EI, P = I²R, P = E²/R, which share the same variables but answer a different question.", "misconception": "Confusing the resistance form of Ohm's law with the power formula (E × I) or inverting the voltage/current ratio.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Cover R in the E/I/R triangle (E on top, I and R on the bottom): covering R leaves E over I, so R = E / I.", "edge_cases": "Valid for purely resistive DC circuits; with AC reactive components you'd use impedance Z = E/I instead of pure resistance."}

{"core": "R = V/I = 3 V / 0.3 A = 10 Ω.
