Convert milliamperes to amperes first.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "9 Ω comes from multiplying 3 V × 3 (using the bare '3's), not dividing voltage by current.", "why_tempting": "Both numbers in the problem are 3, inviting a 3×3 multiplication mistake."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "5 Ω has no valid derivation; it doesn't match any combination of the given values.", "why_tempting": "It's a plausible mid-range round number for a guess."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "3 Ω results from treating I as 1 A (or ignoring current entirely) so R equals the voltage value.", "why_tempting": "Equals the battery voltage, so it looks 'natural' if you skip the current term."}], "concept": "Ohm's law solved for resistance: R = V/I. The only real work is unit conversion — 300 mA = 0.300 A. Then 3 / 0.3 = 10 Ω. Forgetting the milli-prefix (treating 300 mA as 300 A) would instead give 0.01 Ω, and treating it as 3 A gives 1 Ω — neither is offered, so the conversion to 0.3 A is the intended path.", "misconception": "Dividing by the raw number 300 (or 3) instead of converting milliamperes to 0.3 A before applying R = V/I.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "milli = ÷1000: 300 mA → 0.3 A", "edge_cases": "Always normalize prefixes (mA→A, mV→V) before plugging into V=IR; the algebra is trivial, the unit handling is where errors hide."}

{"core": "In a parallel network each branch sees the same source voltage and draws its own current; by Kirchhoff's Current Law the current leaving the source must equal the sum of all the branch currents.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The total is the SUM of branch currents, not their average; averaging would massively understate the real draw.", "why_tempting": "Learners confuse the parallel-resistance result (total R is less than the smallest branch, a sort of 'averaging-down') with the current behaviour."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Adding parallel resistors lowers total resistance, so total current INCREASES, not decreases, for a fixed voltage.", "why_tempting": "It echoes the true fact that resistance drops as branches are added — the learner applies that trend to current by mistake."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This mixes units nonsensically — multiplying voltage drops by a count gives neither a current nor any meaningful quantity. In parallel every branch has the same voltage anyway.", "why_tempting": "It sounds formula-like and uses 'sum' and 'number of resistors', superficially resembling a real relationship."}], "concept": "For N parallel branches across source V: each branch current is I_k = V/R_k, and KCL at the node gives I_total = ΣI_k = V·Σ(1/R_k) = V/R_total. This is the dual of the series case, where current is shared (same) and voltages add; here voltage is shared (same) and currents add. Conductances (G=1/R) add directly in parallel, which is why total current scales with the sum of conductances.", "misconception": "Treating parallel current like parallel resistance — assuming it averages or decreases — instead of recognising current is additive (KCL) while voltage is the shared quantity.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Parallel = currents Pile up; Series = voltages Stack.", "edge_cases": "Holds for ideal DC resistive branches from a stiff voltage source; with AC and reactive branches you sum currents as phasors, not arithmetic magnitudes."}

{"core": "Four 100 Ω resistors in parallel give 25 Ω total; I = V/R = 12 V / 25 Ω = 0.48 A = 480 mA.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "48 mA is 480 mA off by a factor of ten — a decimal slip, not a valid result of any correct combination.", "why_tempting": "Looks plausible if you mis-place the decimal when converting amps to milliamps."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "120 mA is the current through a single 100 Ω resistor (12 V / 100 Ω), ignoring that there are four parallel paths.", "why_tempting": "You compute the current for one resistor and forget the parallel branches multiply the total."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "240 mA corresponds to only two parallel resistors (50 Ω → 240 mA), so it under-counts the branches.", "why_tempting": "Halving the count of branches or assuming 50 Ω total gives this seductive midpoint answer."}], "concept": "For N equal resistors R in parallel, total resistance is R/N = 100/4 = 25 Ω. Equivalently, total current is the sum of the branch currents: each branch draws 12 V / 100 Ω = 120 mA, and four identical branches give 4 × 120 mA = 480 mA. Both approaches must agree — that consistency check is the fastest way to catch errors.", "misconception": "Treating parallel resistance like series (adding to 400 Ω) or forgetting that parallel branches add current rather than reduce it. Parallel resistors lower total resistance and raise total current.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Parallel = more paths = more current; equal resistors → divide R by how many.", "edge_cases": "This shortcut (R/N) only works when all resistors are equal; for unequal values use 1/R_total = Σ(1/Rᵢ) or sum the individual branch currents."}

{"core": "For resistors in parallel, conductances add (G_total = G1+G2+...), so the total resistance is always smaller than any single resistor — including the smallest one.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Parallel resistance never exceeds the smallest resistor, let alone the largest; adding parallel paths only increases conductance and lowers resistance.", "why_tempting": "Learners confuse the parallel rule with series, where total resistance does exceed the largest element."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The reciprocal sum has nothing to do with a simple arithmetic average; the parallel result is pulled well below the mean toward the smallest value.", "why_tempting": "Averaging feels like a reasonable 'compromise' between values, but it ignores the reciprocal relationship."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "There is no square-root-of-sum relationship for resistors; that form doesn't arise anywhere in DC resistance combinations.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like an exotic formula and may be confused with RMS or impedance magnitude calculations."}], "concept": "Parallel resistors combine via 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... + 1/Rn. Because every added term increases the right-hand side (total conductance), 1/R_total is always larger than any individual 1/Ri, which means R_total is always smaller than the smallest Ri. For two resistors the product-over-sum form R_total = (R1·R2)/(R1+R2) makes this clear: with R1=10Ω and R2=10Ω you get 5Ω, and adding any further parallel path only lowers it more. A useful limiting case: n equal resistors of value R in parallel give R/n.", "misconception": "Treating parallel like series (or assuming the answer lands 'between' the values, e.g. an average) instead of recognizing that conductances add and total resistance drops below the smallest element.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Parallel = more paths = less resistance; the total is always under the smallest.", "edge_cases": "If one parallel resistor is 0Ω (a short), total resistance is 0Ω; if any branch is open (∞Ω) it simply drops out of the reciprocal sum."}

{"core": "Two 1000 Ω resistors in parallel give 500 Ω total; I = V/R = 12 V / 500 Ω = 24 mA.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "60 mA would require ~200 Ω total, which doesn't follow from two 1000 Ω resistors in any standard configuration.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible round number if you mis-combine the resistances."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "120 mA implies 100 Ω total — off by a factor that doesn't match either series or parallel results here.", "why_tempting": "Doubling something from another wrong answer (e.g. mistaking total R as 100 Ω) gives this."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "12 mA is the current through ONE 1000 Ω resistor alone (12 V / 1000 Ω). The total is the sum of both branch currents.", "why_tempting": "Easy to compute current through a single resistor and forget the second branch doubles it."}], "concept": "Parallel resistors: R_total = (R1·R2)/(R1+R2) = (1000·1000)/2000 = 500 Ω. Equivalently, two equal resistors in parallel halve to R/2. Then total current I = V/R_total = 12/500 = 0.024 A = 24 mA. Cross-check via branch currents: each branch carries 12/1000 = 12 mA, and the two branches sum to 24 mA — KCL confirms the result.", "misconception": "Confusing parallel with series (which would give 2000 Ω → 6 mA) or computing only one branch's current (12 mA) instead of the total drawn from the source.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Two equal resistors in parallel = half the resistance, double the single-branch current.", "edge_cases": "For N equal resistors in parallel, R_total = R/N — generalize before plugging into Ohm's law."}

{"core": "Series resistances simply add (R_total = R1 + R2 + ...), so the sum is necessarily larger than any single resistor in the chain.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This describes parallel resistors, where the total is always less than the smallest branch — the opposite topology.", "why_tempting": "Learners who confuse series with parallel behaviour swap the two rules."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The total equals the SUM of all resistors, not just the largest one; every resistor contributes.", "why_tempting": "In a series chain the largest resistor dominates the value, so it feels close to the answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The lowest value is a parallel-circuit limiting case; in series it understates the total badly.", "why_tempting": "Mixing up the parallel 'less than smallest' intuition with series."}], "concept": "In series, the same current flows through every element, and voltages add per KVL. Since V_total = I·R_total = I·(R1+R2+...), dividing by I gives R_total = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn. Each term is positive, so the sum strictly exceeds any individual term. Contrast with parallel: 1/R_total = Σ(1/Ri), giving a result below the smallest branch.", "misconception": "Confusing the series 'add' rule with the parallel 'reciprocal' rule, and assuming a single resistor sets the total.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Series resistors Stack up; Parallel resistors Pare down.", "edge_cases": "Holds for any number of positive resistors; the same additive rule applies to inductive reactances and to capacitive reactance magnitudes in series."}

{"core": "Resistors in series simply add: 5 × 10 Ω = 50 Ω.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "10 ohms is the value of a single resistor, not the total of five in series.", "why_tempting": "Looks right if you forget you have five of them and just read off one resistor's value."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "2 ohms is 10 ÷ 5, which would only apply to a parallel combination of equal resistors — and even then, parallel gives 10/5 = 2 Ω for this case, so this is the parallel answer, not series.", "why_tempting": "Confusing the series rule with the parallel rule (dividing instead of adding)."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "5 ohms is just the count of resistors, not a resistance; it ignores the 10 Ω value entirely.", "why_tempting": "Mixing up the quantity (five) with the resistance value."}], "concept": "For resistors in series, total resistance R_total = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn. With identical resistors this reduces to n × R. Here n = 5 and R = 10 Ω, so R_total = 5 × 10 = 50 Ω. Contrast with parallel, where 1/R_total = Σ(1/Ri); for n equal resistors that gives R/n = 10/5 = 2 Ω — note that 2 Ω appears as a distractor precisely to catch series/parallel confusion.", "misconception": "Treating a series string with the parallel formula (or vice versa). Series resistances always add and the total is larger than any single resistor; parallel resistances always come out smaller than the smallest branch.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Series = Sum (both start with S).", "edge_cases": "Always sanity-check: a series total must be greater than the largest individual resistor. 50 Ω > 10 Ω, so it passes."}

{"core": "Resistors in series add directly: five 24-ohm resistors give 5 × 24 = 120 Ω, exactly replacing the single 120-ohm resistor.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Six 22-ohm resistors in series total 6 × 22 = 132 Ω, not 120 Ω.", "why_tempting": "6 × 22 is close to 120 and looks plausible if you don't multiply carefully."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Two 240-ohm resistors in series total 480 Ω; 240/2 = 120 Ω would be the parallel result, not series.", "why_tempting": "The number 240 is exactly twice 120, so a learner confusing series with parallel grabs this — but halving only happens for equal resistors in parallel."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Five 100-ohm resistors in series total 500 Ω, far above 120 Ω.", "why_tempting": "Five resistors and a round 100 Ω value look tidy, mirroring the correct option's 'five resistors' structure."}], "concept": "For series resistors the total is the simple sum R_total = R1 + R2 + ... + Rn. Contrast with parallel, where 1/R_total = Σ(1/Ri), and for n equal resistors parallel gives R/n while series gives R×n. Here n=5, R=24: series = 120 Ω. Note that series resistance always exceeds the largest single element, while parallel always falls below the smallest.", "misconception": "Mixing up series (values add) with parallel (reciprocals add / equal values divide). Option C is the classic bait for that swap.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Series Sums, Parallel Plummets.", "edge_cases": "Equal-value division (R/n) is the parallel rule, not the series rule; series only divides if you're finding each element's value from a known total."}

{"core": "For N identical resistors of value R in parallel, the total resistance is R/N. With ten equal resistors, that is R/10.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This inverts the relationship — dividing the count by the resistance has no physical basis and gives wrong units behaviour.", "why_tempting": "It contains both 10 and R in a division, so it looks like a plausible 'parallel divides' formula if you forget which way the division goes."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Multiplying scales resistance UP, which is what series stacking trends toward, not parallel. Parallel always lowers total resistance below the smallest branch.", "why_tempting": "10×R is the natural 'ten of them' instinct, and it's correct for total resistance only in the series case (N·R)."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Addition (R₁+R₂+…) is the series rule, and here it's also dimensionally wrong since 10 is a count, not a resistance.", "why_tempting": "'Adding resistors together' is the most memorable rule, and learners misapply the series sum to parallel."}], "concept": "The general parallel formula is 1/R_total = Σ(1/Rᵢ). For N equal resistors each of value R, 1/R_total = N/R, so R_total = R/N. The key insight: paralleling identical resistors divides the resistance by the number of paths because you're adding equal conductances (G = 1/R) in parallel — conductances add, resistances don't. Ten equal branches give ten times the conductance, hence one-tenth the resistance.", "misconception": "Confusing the series rule (resistances add, total goes up) with the parallel rule (conductances add, total goes down). The trap rewards the 'multiply by ten' or 'add them up' reflex.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Parallel = Pour through more pipes: more paths, less resistance — divide by the count for equal resistors.", "edge_cases": "R/N only works when all resistors are EQUAL. For unequal values you must use 1/R_total = Σ(1/Rᵢ). Total parallel resistance is always less than the smallest individual resistor."}

{"core": "For N equal resistors in parallel, the total is R/N. Four 68 Ω resistors give 68/4 = 17 Ω.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "12 Ω doesn't correspond to any correct combination of 68 Ω and four resistors.", "why_tempting": "It's a plausibly small number, and parallel combinations do yield values smaller than the individual resistor, so it 'feels' right."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "34 Ω is 68/2, the result for only TWO in parallel, not four.", "why_tempting": "Halving 68 is an easy mental step and looks like a clean parallel answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "272 Ω is 68×4 — that's the series total, not parallel.", "why_tempting": "Multiplying by the count is the series rule; mixing up series and parallel is the classic slip."}], "concept": "Parallel resistance: 1/R_total = Σ(1/Rᵢ). With four identical resistors, 1/R_total = 4/68, so R_total = 68/4 = 17 Ω. The shortcut R/N applies only when all resistors are equal. General principle: parallel always yields a total smaller than the smallest branch resistor.", "misconception": "Confusing the parallel rule (divide by N for equal resistors) with the series rule (multiply/add). The parallel total must be LESS than any single resistor — 272 Ω violates that immediately.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Parallel = smaller; equal resistors → just divide the value by how many there are.", "edge_cases": "R/N is valid only for identical resistors. For unequal values you must sum reciprocals, and the result is still always below the smallest branch."}

{"core": "In parallel, both resistors see the same voltage. Since I=V/R, the resistor carrying twice the current (A) must have half the resistance of the other (B). Therefore A has half the resistance of B.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This reverses the relationship: B carries less current, so B has the larger resistance, not the smaller. B has twice the resistance of A.", "why_tempting": "The names get swapped easily; 'half' sounds right but is attached to the wrong resistor."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "In a parallel connection the voltage across both branches is identical by definition, so neither voltage can be larger.", "why_tempting": "In a series circuit voltage divides, so a learner conflating series and parallel might expect unequal voltages."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Same flaw as C — parallel branches share one common voltage; it cannot differ between them.", "why_tempting": "Since A carries more current, it feels intuitive that 'more' should also apply to its voltage, but current differs because resistance differs, not voltage."}], "concept": "Parallel elements share the same node pair, so V_A = V_B = V. From I=V/R, I_A/I_B = R_B/R_A. Given I_A = 2·I_B, we get R_B/R_A = 2, i.e. R_A = R_B/2. Current is inversely proportional to resistance when voltage is held constant — the branch with lower resistance hogs the current.", "misconception": "Confusing parallel (equal voltage, divided current) with series (equal current, divided voltage). Here voltage is fixed common to both, so the current split is purely a resistance ratio.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Parallel = same Voltage; more current = less resistance (I and R go opposite ways).", "edge_cases": "Holds for ideal ohmic resistors; with nonlinear devices the simple inverse current–resistance ratio breaks down."}

{"core": "In a parallel circuit every branch shares the same voltage but carries its own current; by Kirchhoff's Current Law the total current entering the node equals the sum of all branch currents.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "V divided by one resistor gives only that single branch's current, not the total.", "why_tempting": "It looks like Ohm's law applied to the source, but it ignores all the other parallel paths."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Dividing V by the arithmetic sum of resistances gives a current smaller than any branch — that's how series resistance behaves, not parallel.", "why_tempting": "Summing resistances feels right, but parallel resistances combine reciprocally (1/Rt = Σ1/Ri), not additively."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "One branch current is only a fraction of the total unless there is just a single branch.", "why_tempting": "True for voltage (which is common in parallel), so a learner may confuse the shared quantity."}], "concept": "Kirchhoff's Current Law: charge is conserved at a node, so I_total = I1 + I2 + ... + In. Each branch obeys I_i = V/R_i with the same V, giving I_total = V·(1/R1 + 1/R2 + ...) = V/R_parallel. Note the contrast with series circuits, where current is the shared quantity and voltages add.", "misconception": "Swapping the parallel-circuit rules: in parallel, voltage is common and currents add; in series, current is common and voltages add. This question tests that you don't reverse them.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Parallel = currents Pile up; Series = voltages Stack up.", "edge_cases": "Holds for DC resistive branches; with reactive branches you must add currents as phasors, not simple arithmetic."}

{"core": "Physical size of a resistor is governed primarily by its power (wattage) rating, not its resistance. A physically larger resistor has more surface area to radiate heat, so it can dissipate more power without overheating.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A resistor is essentially a non-reactive element at DC and low frequencies; physical size does not improve switching or 'response' speed and can actually add parasitic inductance/capacitance.", "why_tempting": "In other components (capacitors, batteries) size sometimes correlates with performance, so a learner may assume 'bigger = faster.'"}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Conductance (G = 1/R) is fixed by the resistance value. Two resistors of the same resistance have identical conductance regardless of physical size.", "why_tempting": "The phrase 'same resistance' is easy to skim past, leading one to think size somehow changes the electrical value."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Impedance of a resistor is its resistance (plus tiny unavoidable parasitics); a larger body does not lower impedance and may slightly raise stray inductance.", "why_tempting": "'Less impedance' sounds like a desirable benefit, but it confuses physical size with electrical value."}], "concept": "Resistor power dissipation is P = I²R = V²/R. The component must convert that electrical power into heat and shed it to the surroundings. A larger package (e.g. 1 W or 5 W vs 1/4 W) has greater thermal mass and surface area, lowering the temperature rise for a given dissipation and keeping the part within its rated junction/body temperature. The ohmic value, tolerance, and conductance are independent design parameters set during manufacture — only the wattage rating tracks physical size.", "misconception": "Confusing a resistor's electrical value (resistance/conductance/impedance) with its physical attribute that scales with size — power-handling capacity.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Bigger body = more watts, not more ohms.", "edge_cases": "At RF, larger resistors can have more parasitic inductance/capacitance, so for high-frequency work a smaller (or non-inductive) part is preferred even if it handles less power."}

{"core": "Power equals voltage times current: P = V × I = 12 V × 5 A = 60 W, so the transformer must be rated for at least 60 watts.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "2.4 W comes from dividing 12 by 5 (12/5 = 2.4), which is dimensionally wrong for power.", "why_tempting": "Picks the division operation instead of multiplication and lands on a clean number."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "6 W would result from misreading the values or halving the product; it has no basis in P = V × I here.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible 'small power' figure for a low-voltage supply."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "17 W ≈ 12 + 5 is the sum of the two numbers, not their product — adding volts and amps is meaningless.", "why_tempting": "Adding the given numbers is a common shortcut error and yields a 'middle' value."}], "concept": "DC power delivered to a load is P = V × I. A transformer (with its rectifier/regulator) must supply at least the load's power demand, so its minimum VA/watt rating is the product of the output voltage and current: 12 V × 5 A = 60 W. In practice you'd derate further for transformer/rectifier losses and headroom, but the theoretical minimum is the load power itself.", "misconception": "Confusing which arithmetic operation links volts and amperes to power — learners may divide or add instead of multiply.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "PIE: P = I × E (power = current × voltage).", "edge_cases": "For AC mains a transformer is rated in VA, not watts; with a resistive/DC load VA ≈ W, but a real supply needs extra capacity for losses, so 60 W is a minimum, not the recommended size."}

{"core": "DC input power is P = V × I = 12 V × 0.5 A = 6 watts.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "24 W doesn't follow from the given values by any correct operation; it appears to be V × 2 or a doubled error.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible mid-range answer if you mis-multiply or misread the current."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "60 W results from treating 500 mA as 5 A (12 × 5), a decimal-place error.", "why_tempting": "Easy to get if you forget to convert milliamperes to amperes by the wrong factor."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "600 W comes from using 500 directly (12 × 500) without converting mA to A at all.", "why_tempting": "The raw multiplication 12 × 500 = 6000, or a slip gives 600 — a classic unit-conversion trap."}], "concept": "Power in a DC circuit is P = V·I. The only catch here is unit conversion: 500 mA = 500 × 10⁻³ A = 0.5 A. Then P = 12 × 0.5 = 6 W. Always normalize prefixes to base units (volts, amperes) before multiplying.", "misconception": "Forgetting to convert milliamperes to amperes, leaving current as 500 and inflating the answer by a factor of 1000 (or partial slips giving ×10 or ×100 errors).", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "milli means ÷1000: 500 mA → 0.5 A before you multiply.", "edge_cases": "This is DC *input* power to the final stage, not RF output power — output is always less due to efficiency losses."}

{"core": "Each resistor can safely dissipate 1 watt, and in series both carry the same current, so the combination can dissipate up to 1 W + 1 W = 2 watts before either reaches its rating.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "1 watt is the rating of a single resistor, not the pair; combining two identical devices doubles the total power-handling capacity.", "why_tempting": "Learners may think the rating doesn't change or that the 'lowest' rating limits the whole network to 1 W."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "0.5 W would only be relevant if you incorrectly divided one resistor's rating, treating series connection as halving capability.", "why_tempting": "Series resistance adds, so some assume the power rating must shrink, confusing resistance addition with power."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "4 watts would require each resistor to handle 2 W; the rating per device is fixed at 1 W regardless of how they're wired.", "why_tempting": "It looks like a 'double the doubling' answer if you wrongly multiply effects of series connection."}], "concept": "Power dissipation limits are additive for identical resistors in any series or parallel arrangement, because the total power capacity is simply the sum of each device's rating: P_total = P1 + P2 = 1 W + 1 W = 2 W. In series, the same current I flows through both; each dissipates I²R. The limiting condition is reached when each individual resistor hits 1 W simultaneously (since they are equal). At that point total power = 2 W. Note the total resistance becomes 1000 Ω, but resistance value does not change the summed power-handling figure.", "misconception": "Believing that connecting resistors in series changes (raises or lowers) each resistor's individual power rating, when in fact the total safe dissipation is just the sum of the individual ratings.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Two 1-W parts = 2 W total — power ratings just add up.", "edge_cases": "Adding ratings directly works cleanly only for identical resistors; with unequal values in series the resistor with the higher resistance reaches its limit first, so the safe total is less than the naive sum."}

{"core": "Each resistor is independently rated for 1 watt, so two of them together can dissipate 2 watts regardless of how they are wired.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Halving the rating has no physical basis; parallel connection lowers total resistance but never reduces each resistor's individual power capacity.", "why_tempting": "Parallel resistance is half the single value (250 Ω), so a learner may carelessly apply 'half' to the power rating too."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "1 watt is the rating of a single resistor; combining two devices adds their capacities.", "why_tempting": "It echoes the printed rating on each resistor, so it looks like a 'safe' answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "There is no multiplication factor of four; power ratings add linearly, not by the number squared.", "why_tempting": "Doubling resistance ideas or confusing with V²/R scaling can suggest a factor-of-four jump."}], "concept": "Power dissipation rating is a per-component thermal limit. When components are combined, their maximum dissipation capabilities simply add: P_total = P1 + P2 = 1 W + 1 W = 2 W. The resistance arrangement (parallel gives 250 Ω, series gives 1000 Ω) changes the equivalent resistance and how current/voltage divide, but it does NOT change how much heat each individual resistor can shed. In parallel with equal resistors the applied voltage is shared identically and each dissipates the same power, so both reach their 1 W limit simultaneously — total 2 W.", "misconception": "Believing the parallel resistance formula (which halves resistance) also halves or otherwise alters the combined power rating. Resistance combines by network rules; power-handling combines by simple addition.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Power ratings ADD — two 1 W parts handle 2 W, no matter series or parallel.", "edge_cases": "This works cleanly only for identical resistors that share dissipation equally. With unequal resistors in parallel, the lower-resistance one carries more power and may hit its limit first, so the safe total is less than the simple sum."}

{"core": "Power varies with the square of voltage (P = V²/R) for a fixed resistance, so doubling the voltage multiplies the total power by 2² = 4.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Power does not scale linearly with voltage; doubling V quadruples P, not doubles it.", "why_tempting": "Learners assume a proportional relationship—double the cause, double the effect—forgetting the square term."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Power increases, not decreases, when voltage rises; one-quarter would require halving the voltage.", "why_tempting": "It's the inverse of the correct factor, attractive if you confuse the direction of the change or mix up V²."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Power rises sharply, not halves; halving would correspond to reducing voltage, not raising it.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you wrongly think series resistors 'split' the increased voltage and reduce power."}], "concept": "With resistance R fixed, P = V²/R. Replacing V with 2V gives P' = (2V)²/R = 4V²/R = 4P. The series combination's total resistance R_total = R1 + R2 is unchanged, so the same square law applies to the whole circuit. Equivalently, doubling V doubles the current (I = V/R), and P = VI scales as the product of two doubled quantities = 4×.", "misconception": "Treating power as linearly proportional to voltage instead of proportional to V². The square relationship is the key trap.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Double the volts, quadruple the watts—square the voltage ratio.", "edge_cases": "This holds only if resistance stays constant. Real resistors heat up and may change value slightly, but for exam purposes R is treated as fixed."}

{"core": "Four 200-Ω resistors in parallel give 200/4 = 50 Ω, and their combined power rating is 4 × 2 W = 8 W, which safely exceeds the required 5 W.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Ten 500-Ω resistors in parallel give 500/10 = 50 Ω (correct resistance), but the total power handling is only 10 × 0.25 W = 2.5 W — far short of 5 W, so it would overheat.", "why_tempting": "The resistance works out to exactly 50 Ω, so a learner who stops after checking ohms is fooled; the power math is the real gate."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Two 100-Ω resistors in series give 100 + 100 = 200 Ω, not 50 Ω, even though the 10 W total power would be more than adequate.", "why_tempting": "The generous 5-W ratings draw the eye, and '100' near '50' invites a careless guess — but series adds, giving 200 Ω."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Two 25-Ω resistors in series give 25 + 25 = 50 Ω (correct), but total power is only 2 × 2 W = 4 W, below the 5 W needed.", "why_tempting": "The resistance is spot-on at 50 Ω, so it looks right until you total the wattage and find it falls just short."}], "concept": "Two independent checks must both pass. Resistance: parallel of N equal resistors R is R/N; series is R1+R2. Power: identical resistors sharing current/voltage equally split the load, so combined rating is N × (per-resistor rating). For B: 200/4 = 50 Ω and 4×2 = 8 W ≥ 5 W. The current/voltage divides evenly only because the resistors are equal, so each dissipates an equal share — that's why the total rating scales linearly with count.", "misconception": "Assuming the combination only has to hit 50 Ω, ignoring that the aggregate power rating must also meet or exceed the dissipation requirement. Both conditions are mandatory.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Check BOTH: 'right ohms AND enough watts' — a dummy load that's the wrong wattage still burns out.", "edge_cases": "Equal-share power scaling assumes matched resistors; with unequal values the smallest/hottest element limits real power handling, so you cannot simply add the ratings."}

{"core": "Power, voltage and current relate by P = V·I, so I = P/V = 30 W / 12 V = 2.5 A.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "18 A comes from subtracting (30 − 12) instead of dividing, which has no physical basis.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a quick arithmetic shortcut if you forget the power formula and just combine the two numbers."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "4.8 A would result from inverting the ratio incorrectly or using a different (wrong) voltage; it's not P/V.", "why_tempting": "It's a plausible mid-range value that could come from a slipped decimal or mis-set calculator."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "0.4 A is V/P (12/30) — the reciprocal of the correct ratio.", "why_tempting": "Easy to flip the formula and divide voltage by power instead of power by voltage."}], "concept": "The three-way power relation P = V·I rearranges to I = P/V and V = P/I. Here the bulb dissipates 30 W at 12 V, so I = 30/12 = 2.5 A. As a sanity check, that current implies a hot-filament resistance R = V/I = 12/2.5 = 4.8 Ω, or equivalently R = V²/P = 144/30 = 4.8 Ω — consistent, confirming the answer.", "misconception": "Confusing which quantity goes in the numerator: current is power divided by voltage, not voltage divided by power, and never the difference of the two numbers.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "\"PIV pie\": cover I in P = V·I to get I = P/V.", "edge_cases": "Rated wattage is the steady-state value at rated voltage; an incandescent bulb's cold-filament resistance is far lower, so inrush current at switch-on briefly exceeds 2.5 A."}

{"core": "Two 10-Ω resistors in series give 20 Ω total; with 10 V the current is 0.5 A, so total power P = VI = 10 × 0.5 = 5 W (answer D).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "2 W comes from no consistent calculation matching this circuit.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible 'small' number if you mis-multiply current and voltage."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "20 W would require treating total resistance as 10 Ω (P = V²/R = 100/10 = 10 W is still wrong, and 20 W is the resistance value used as wattage).", "why_tempting": "20 is the total series resistance, easily confused with the answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "0.5 is the current in amps, not the power; mistaking I for P.", "why_tempting": "0.5 A is a real value computed mid-problem, so it sticks in mind."}], "concept": "Series resistors add: R_total = 10 + 10 = 20 Ω. Current is the same through both: I = V/R = 10/20 = 0.5 A. Total power dissipated equals P = V·I = 10 × 0.5 = 5 W, or equivalently P = V²/R = 100/20 = 5 W, or I²R = 0.25 × 20 = 5 W. Each resistor dissipates 2.5 W, summing to 5 W.", "misconception": "Forgetting that series resistors add (using 10 Ω instead of 20 Ω) or confusing the computed current (0.5 A) with the answer in watts.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Series: resistances add; same current flows through all.", "edge_cases": "If asked per-resistor instead of total, each dissipates 2.5 W — check whether the question wants total or individual power."}

{"core": "Two 100-Ω resistors in parallel give 50 Ω (the same resistance), but because the load splits across two components each dissipating half the total power, the combination tolerates twice the power of a single resistor.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Parallel identical resistors share the dissipated power, so the combination's rating goes up, not down.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume splitting current somehow weakens the assembly, but more components sharing the load means more total capacity."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Two 100-Ω resistors in parallel equal 50 Ω, not a greater resistance.", "why_tempting": "100 Ω is bigger than 50 Ω, so it's tempting to forget that paralleling halves the value back to 50 Ω."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The resistance is unchanged at 50 Ω, and the power rating is doubled, not unchanged.", "why_tempting": "Half-right on resistance feel, but ignores that two parts each rated for the same wattage double the handling."}], "concept": "For two equal resistors in parallel, R_total = R/2, so 100/2 = 50 Ω — resistance is preserved. Power capacity, however, adds: each resistor of rating P can dissipate P, so the pair can dissipate 2P. With equal resistors the current splits equally, so each carries half the total current and dissipates a quarter of (I²R_total)·... more simply, total dissipation 2P is shared as P each. The net effect: identical 50-Ω load with double the safe wattage.", "misconception": "Confusing how resistance combines (parallel halves it) with how power rating combines (it adds). The two follow different rules.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Parallel equals: half the ohms, double the watts.", "edge_cases": "This works cleanly only when the resistors are equal and identically rated; mismatched values share current unequally and one may overheat first."}

{"core": "A resistor's wattage rating is set by how much power (heat) it can dissipate before overheating, which depends on its ability to shed that heat to the surroundings.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Tolerance is a separate spec describing resistance accuracy (e.g. ±5%) and has nothing to do with power handling; physical size correlates with wattage but is a consequence of dissipation capacity, not a defining calculation.", "why_tempting": "Larger resistors do handle more power, so size feels relevant, and tolerance is a familiar rating that gets conflated."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Joules measure energy, not power. A wattage rating is a rate of energy dissipation (1 W = 1 J/s), so the unit is the watt, not the joule.", "why_tempting": "Joules and watts are closely related units of the energy/power family, making this a plausible-sounding swap."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Standard power ratings come in values like 1/8, 1/4, 1/2, 1, 2, 5 W — not fixed 100-unit steps. The claim is simply fabricated.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like an arbitrary 'standard step' rule someone might half-remember."}], "concept": "Power dissipated in a resistor is P = I²R = V²/R = VI, all in watts. That power becomes heat. The wattage rating is the maximum continuous P the device can dissipate while staying below its safe temperature, governed by thermal resistance to ambient (surface area, body material, airflow, heatsinking). Exceed it and the part overheats and fails. Note this is independent of the ohmic value — a 100 Ω and a 10 kΩ resistor can share the same 1/4 W rating.", "misconception": "Confusing the power rating (a thermal limit, in watts) with energy (joules), resistance accuracy (tolerance), or physical dimensions as the defining factor.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Watts = how much heat it can throw off; joules are energy in the tank, watts are how fast it pours.", "edge_cases": "Ratings assume free-air at a stated ambient (often 25 °C/70 °C); they must be derated at higher temperatures or when packed tightly, so the 'safe' wattage in a real enclosure is often well below the nameplate."}

{"core": "Frequency is defined as the number of complete cycles an alternating waveform passes through per second, measured in hertz (Hz).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Phase describes the position within a cycle (an angular offset in degrees or radians) or the timing relationship between two signals, not a count of cycles per second.", "why_tempting": "Phase is a closely related AC parameter and sounds technical, so it can pass as the 'rate' descriptor to someone not parsing the definition carefully."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Speed is not a defined property of an AC waveform; propagation velocity of the wave is a different concept and is not cycles per second.", "why_tempting": "Casually, people equate 'fast' signals with high frequency, so 'speed' feels intuitively right."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Pulse rate applies to repetitive pulse/digital streams, not the continuous sinusoidal positive-to-negative cycling described here.", "why_tempting": "It contains 'rate' (a per-second idea) and superficially mirrors the question's wording about counting events per second."}], "concept": "One full cycle of a sinusoidal AC waveform goes 0 → positive peak → 0 → negative peak → 0. Frequency f counts how many such cycles occur each second: f = 1/T, where T is the period in seconds. Units are hertz (Hz), with 1 Hz = 1 cycle/s. Angular frequency ω = 2πf relates this to phase, since phase advances 360° (2π rad) per cycle — which is precisely why phase and frequency are distinct: phase is the instantaneous angle, frequency is the rate at which that angle accumulates.", "misconception": "Conflating the rate at which cycles repeat (frequency) with where you are within a cycle (phase), or loosely associating high frequency with 'speed'.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Frequency = how Frequently the cycle repeats — counted in hertz (cycles per second).", "edge_cases": "Note that one positive-to-negative excursion plus return constitutes a single complete cycle; don't double-count the positive and negative halves as two cycles."}

{"core": "The human auditory range spans roughly 20 Hz at the low (bass) end to about 20 kHz at the high (treble) end, so 20 Hz to 20 000 Hz is correct.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "30 kHz exceeds the upper limit of human hearing; even young, healthy ears top out near 20 kHz.", "why_tempting": "It gets the low end right and only inflates the top, so it looks like a near-miss rounding of the true range."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "200 kHz is well into the ultrasonic region, and 200 Hz is too high a lower bound; this range describes nothing human.", "why_tempting": "It mirrors option A's pattern scaled up by 10, which can fool someone who misremembers the order of magnitude."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "300 Hz to 3000 Hz is the voice communications/telephone bandwidth, not the full hearing range.", "why_tempting": "This band is heavily emphasized in radio (SSB voice passband), so it's a familiar number that feels relevant."}], "concept": "Audio frequencies are the band of vibrations the human ear transduces into perceived sound, conventionally cited as 20 Hz–20 kHz. The upper limit declines with age (presbycusis), often falling below 15 kHz in adults, but the nominal textbook figure remains 20 kHz. In radio this matters because audio modulates RF carriers: the 300–3000 Hz communications passband (option D) is a deliberately narrowed subset chosen for speech intelligibility while conserving bandwidth, distinct from the full audible range.", "misconception": "Confusing the restricted voice/communications bandwidth (300–3000 Hz) or an ultrasonic range with the complete span of human hearing.", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "\"20 to 20k\" — both ends carry a 20.", "edge_cases": "The 20 kHz upper bound is an idealized maximum; practical hearing rolls off well below it with age and noise exposure."}

{"core": "The band 20 Hz–20 kHz is called \"audio\" because it spans the range of mechanical vibrations the healthy human ear can perceive as sound.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An SSB receiver's audio output is deliberately filtered to roughly 300–3000 Hz for voice intelligibility — far narrower than 20 Hz–20 kHz, so speaker response does not define the term.", "why_tempting": "Receivers do produce audio, so a learner might link the audio band to receiver output bandwidth."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The defining feature is human hearing, not a comparison to RF. Frequencies in this range are audio because we hear them, independent of whether RF can or cannot exist there.", "why_tempting": "It correctly notes the range is low compared to RF, which sounds plausible as a reason."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "RF signals are not 'directly converted to sound' by being in this range; audio frequencies are themselves acoustic/electrical signals matching human hearing, not converted RF.", "why_tempting": "Detection does turn RF into audio in a receiver, blurring the cause-and-effect for the unwary."}], "concept": "\"Audio frequency\" (AF) is defined physiologically: the nominal limits of human auditory perception, conventionally 20 Hz (low rumble) to 20 kHz (upper limit, which declines with age). It is a perceptual definition, not a circuit or RF definition. Contrast with radio frequency (RF), which begins well above this range. In a receiver the detector/demodulator recovers a baseband AF signal that is then amplified and fed to a speaker — but the band is named for what the ear can hear, not for any equipment characteristic.", "misconception": "Believing the audio band is defined by equipment (speaker/receiver bandwidth) or by its relationship to RF, rather than by the limits of human hearing.", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "AF = 'Audible to Folks' — 20 to 20k is what people can hear.", "edge_cases": "The 20 kHz upper limit is nominal; real hearing rolls off with age, and communications voice audio occupies only a few hundred Hz to ~3 kHz within this band."}

{"core": "7125 kHz = 7.125 MHz, which falls within the 3–30 MHz band designated as High Frequency (HF).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "LF is 30–300 kHz, three orders of magnitude below 7125 kHz.", "why_tempting": "The frequency is given in kHz, so a learner skimming the units might associate 'kHz' with the low end of the spectrum."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "MF is 300–3000 kHz (0.3–3 MHz); 7125 kHz exceeds the 3000 kHz upper limit.", "why_tempting": "7125 kHz is still expressed in kHz and is close enough in magnitude that someone forgetting the 3000 kHz ceiling might pick MF."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "VHF is 30–300 MHz; 7.125 MHz is far below 30 MHz.", "why_tempting": "VHF is a very familiar amateur range (2 m), so it can be chosen reflexively if the magnitude isn't checked."}], "concept": "The ITU spectrum is divided in decade bands by frequency: VLF 3–30 kHz, LF 30–300 kHz, MF 0.3–3 MHz, HF 3–30 MHz, VHF 30–300 MHz, UHF 0.3–3 GHz. Convert first: 7125 kHz ÷ 1000 = 7.125 MHz. Since 3 ≤ 7.125 < 30 MHz, it is HF. This is the 40 m amateur band.", "misconception": "Confusing the kHz unit label with a 'low' band, instead of converting to MHz and checking against the decade boundaries.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Each band spans a decade (×10): MF tops out at 3 MHz, HF tops out at 30 MHz.", "edge_cases": "Band names are defined by frequency boundaries, not by which amateur allocations sit inside them; HF spans far more than just the ham bands."}

{"core": "Wavelength is by definition the physical distance a wave travels during one complete cycle (one period) of oscillation.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Wave speed\" denotes propagation velocity (c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s in free space), not a distance.", "why_tempting": "Speed and wavelength are linked through λ = v/f, so it sounds related to motion of the wave."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A waveform describes the shape of the signal over time (sine, square, etc.), not a length.", "why_tempting": "It's a legitimate term containing \"wave\" and is used constantly in signal analysis."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "\"Wave spread\" is not a standard term and has no defined physical meaning here.", "why_tempting": "It superficially sounds like it could describe how far a wave extends."}], "concept": "For a periodic AC signal, in one period T the wavefront advances by a distance equal to the wavelength: λ = vT = v/f. In free space v = c, so λ(m) = 300/f(MHz). This is the spatial counterpart of the period (the temporal length of one cycle), and it sets antenna dimensions, element spacing, and resonant lengths.", "misconception": "Confusing the temporal description of one cycle (period/frequency) with its spatial extent (wavelength), or grabbing a similar-sounding term.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "λ = 300 / f(MHz) gives metres in free space.", "edge_cases": "In media or on transmission lines the velocity factor reduces v below c, so the physical wavelength is shorter than the free-space value for the same frequency."}

{"core": "Wavelength and frequency are inversely related through the wave equation λ = c/f, so as frequency rises, wavelength falls.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "This states a direct relationship, but λ and f are inversely proportional — increasing f shortens λ.", "why_tempting": "A learner who hasn't internalized the inverse relationship may assume both quantities track together."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The decrease is proportional to 1/f, not 1/f². There is no square term in λ = c/f.", "why_tempting": "The correct direction (decrease) plus a plausible-sounding power law can lure someone unsure of the exact relationship."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Doubly wrong: wrong direction (increase) and wrong power law (f²). λ neither increases with f nor depends on f².", "why_tempting": "Combines two distractor ideas, which can seem 'more precise' to someone overthinking it."}], "concept": "For an electromagnetic wave in free space, λ = c/f, where c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s. This is a simple inverse (1/f) relationship — doubling frequency halves the wavelength. A handy amateur form is λ(m) = 300 / f(MHz). The c is fixed, so the only trade-off is between λ and f; there is no quadratic dependence anywhere in the equation.", "misconception": "That wavelength and frequency move together, or that the relationship involves a squared term — the trap mixes a correct/incorrect direction with a fictional f² law.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "λ = 300/f(MHz): higher f, shorter wave.", "edge_cases": "In a medium other than vacuum the speed (and thus λ) is reduced by the velocity factor, but the inverse-with-frequency relationship still holds."}

{"core": "Frequency and wavelength are inversely related (f = c/λ). As wavelength gets shorter, frequency increases.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The relationship is a simple inverse (f ∝ 1/λ), not a squared dependence — and there is no f² term in the wave equation.", "why_tempting": "The 'squared' phrasing mimics power or energy relationships and sounds more rigorous, luring those who overcomplicate a simple proportionality."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Frequency moving the same direction as wavelength contradicts the inverse relationship; shorter λ means higher f, not lower.", "why_tempting": "A reader might assume 'shorter' and 'lower' naturally go together if they don't recall the inverse link."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Same inverse-not-squared error as A, plus the wrong direction — frequency rises as wavelength shrinks.", "why_tempting": "Combines two plausible-sounding ideas (decrease + squared) for someone guessing at a fancy formula."}], "concept": "For an electromagnetic wave in free space, c = f·λ, where c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s is constant. Rearranged, f = c/λ. Since c is fixed, f and λ are inversely proportional — halving the wavelength doubles the frequency. The handy amateur shortcut is λ(metres) = 300/f(MHz). The dependence is strictly first-order inverse; there is no square term anywhere in the relation.", "misconception": "That the f–λ relationship might involve a power law (squared) or that 'shorter' wavelength intuitively pairs with 'lower' frequency.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Short waves wiggle faster: short λ → high f.", "edge_cases": "In a medium other than vacuum, the propagation velocity (and thus the constant) is lower, but the inverse f–λ relationship still holds; only the numeric constant changes."}

{"core": "Hertz is the SI unit of frequency, defined as one cycle (complete oscillation) per second; therefore 60 Hz means 60 complete cycles every second.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Metres per second is a unit of speed/velocity, not frequency — hertz never expresses a distance over time.", "why_tempting": "Wavelength is measured in metres and learners conflate the metre dimension of radio waves with frequency units."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Same dimensional error: m/s is velocity, not the cycles-per-second meaning of hertz.", "why_tempting": "The number 60 matches the question, so it looks right if you ignore the wrong units."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The cycles-per-second part is correct, but the count is wrong — 60 Hz is 60 cycles/s, not 6000.", "why_tempting": "It pairs the correct unit concept with an inflated number, baiting a careless reader who locks onto 'cycles per second' without checking the count."}], "concept": "Frequency f = (number of cycles)/(time). 1 Hz = 1 cycle/second. The unit replaced the older 'cycles per second' (cps); they are identical. Note that hertz describes a temporal rate only — wavelength λ (metres) and velocity (m/s) are separate quantities linked by v = fλ, where v ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s for radio waves in free space. Mixing those dimensions is the core error in the distractors.", "misconception": "Believing hertz can describe a wave's speed or physical length, when it strictly counts oscillations per second.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Hertz = 'how many per second' — H for 'How often.'", "edge_cases": "60 Hz is also the North American AC mains frequency, a handy real-world anchor for this value."}

{"core": "Two waveforms of equal frequency whose cycles start at different instants differ in phase; the angular separation between them is the phase difference.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Polarity refers to the sign or direction of a voltage/current (positive vs negative), not to a timing offset between periodic waveforms.", "why_tempting": "A phase shift can invert a waveform (180° looks like reversed polarity), so the two ideas seem related."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "'Offset' usually denotes a DC level added to a waveform (vertical shift), not a horizontal timing/angular displacement.", "why_tempting": "The everyday meaning of 'offset' loosely suggests 'a difference,' which fits the casual reading of the question."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Delta is a generic symbol for 'a change in' a quantity; it is not the specific term for the timing relationship between two AC waveforms.", "why_tempting": "Δ is used in math/physics for differences, so it superficially matches 'a difference in timing.'"}], "concept": "For sinusoids of the same frequency, each can be written v(t)=Vsin(ωt+φ). The constant φ is the phase angle; the difference φ₁−φ₂ is the phase difference, measured in degrees or radians. Because the period maps to 360°, a time delay Δt corresponds to a phase shift of (Δt/T)×360°. Phase is meaningful only when frequencies are equal — otherwise the relative angle drifts continuously.", "misconception": "Confusing phase (an angular/timing relationship) with polarity (sign) or with a DC offset (vertical shift).", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Same frequency, different start = 'out of phase.'", "edge_cases": "Phase comparison is only stable when both waveforms share the same frequency; with different frequencies the phase relationship is not fixed."}

{"core": "AC mains from a household outlet is generated by rotating machinery, producing a smoothly varying sinusoidal voltage at the line frequency (60 Hz in Canada).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A pulse wave is a series of discrete on/off transitions, not the continuous smooth variation of mains power.", "why_tempting": "Pulse waveforms are common in digital and switching circuits, so a learner might associate 'electricity' broadly with them."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A complex wave is one composed of a fundamental plus harmonics; ideal mains is intended to be a pure single-frequency sinusoid.", "why_tempting": "Real-world mains does carry small harmonic distortion, so 'complex' sounds plausible to someone thinking about non-ideal power quality."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A modified square wave is the stepped approximation produced by cheap inverters, not the true sine wave delivered by the utility grid.", "why_tempting": "Anyone who has used an inverter or UPS may recall this term and confuse inverter output with grid output."}], "concept": "Utility power is generated by alternators whose rotating windings cut a magnetic field; the induced EMF follows v(t) = Vpeak·sin(2πft). In Canada f = 60 Hz, and the nominal 120 V is the RMS value, where Vpeak = Vrms·√2 ≈ 170 V. The defining feature of a sinusoid is that it contains only one frequency component — its derivative and integral are also sinusoids — which is why it is the canonical AC waveform.", "misconception": "Confusing the ideal utility sine wave with the stepped/approximated waveforms produced by inverters, or assuming 'electricity' implies a digital pulse shape.", "link": "Tools — \"Wavelength ↔ Frequency\"", "mnemonic": "Grid = Genuine sine; inverters = imitation (modified) square.", "edge_cases": "Real distribution lines carry minor harmonic distortion, but for exam purposes the answer is an ideal sine wave."}

{"core": "A harmonic is an integer multiple of the fundamental frequency. 4 kHz is exactly 2× the 2 kHz fundamental, so it is the second harmonic.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Aliasing is a sampling artifact in digital systems where frequencies above the Nyquist rate fold back to lower apparent frequencies — unrelated to a simple integer multiple of a fundamental.", "why_tempting": "The term sounds plausible to anyone with DSP background, and 'alias' implies a derived frequency."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A sub-harmonic is a frequency BELOW the fundamental (an integer division, e.g. f/2). 4 kHz is above 2 kHz, not below.", "why_tempting": "It contains 'harmonic' and a learner may pick it without noting that 'sub' means lower."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Intermodulation products arise from mixing TWO or more different frequencies in a nonlinear device, producing sums and differences (f1±f2). A single fundamental and its multiple is not a mixing product.", "why_tempting": "IMD is a real spurious-emission problem, and the question mentions two frequencies together."}], "concept": "For a periodic signal with fundamental f, the harmonics occur at integer multiples: 2f, 3f, 4f… The nth harmonic is n×f. Here f = 2 kHz, so the harmonics are 4, 6, 8 kHz… and 4 kHz = 2×2 kHz is the second harmonic. Harmonics arise naturally from any non-sinusoidal periodic waveform (Fourier series) and from nonlinearities in transmitters, which is why harmonic suppression (low-pass filtering) matters for spectral purity.", "misconception": "Confusing harmonics (integer multiples ABOVE the fundamental) with sub-harmonics (below) or with intermodulation (sum/difference of two distinct signals).", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "Harmonic = whole-number multiple UP; Sub-harmonic = down.", "edge_cases": "The fundamental itself is the 1st harmonic; 4 kHz here is the 2nd harmonic specifically."}

{"core": "Decibels for power are 10·log₁₀(P₂/P₁). For a ratio of 2, 10·log₁₀(2) ≈ 3.01 dB, so doubling power is +3 dB.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "+6 dB corresponds to a power ratio of 4× (10·log₁₀4 ≈ 6.02 dB), or a voltage doubling, not a power doubling.", "why_tempting": "6 dB is the well-known 'double the voltage/field strength' figure, and learners conflate the voltage rule (20·log) with the power rule (10·log)."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "+12 dB is roughly a 16× power increase (4 successive doublings), far more than 2×.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible 'larger' step and is a multiple of 6, fitting the wrong voltage-doubling pattern."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "+1 dB is a power ratio of about 1.26×, not a doubling.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a small, modest change that a learner might guess for 'a bit more power.'"}], "concept": "dB(power) = 10·log₁₀(P₂/P₁). Key anchor values: ×2 → +3 dB, ×10 → +10 dB, and these add — ×4 = ×2×2 → 3+3 = 6 dB, ÷2 → −3 dB. Because power ∝ V²/R, the voltage form is 20·log₁₀(V₂/V₁); a 2× voltage gives 20·log₁₀2 ≈ 6 dB while only doubling... actually quadrupling power. Keep the two log multipliers (10 for power, 20 for voltage/current) straight.", "misconception": "Mixing up the power formula (10·log) with the voltage formula (20·log), which makes learners assign +6 dB to a power doubling.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "'Double the power, plus three; ten times, plus ten.'", "edge_cases": "The exact value is 3.01 dB; '3 dB' is the rounded convention used throughout amateur radio (e.g. the −3 dB half-power bandwidth point)."}

{"core": "A 3 dB decrease corresponds to halving the power, because dB = 10·log₁₀(P₂/P₁), and 10·log₁₀(0.5) ≈ −3.01 dB.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Dividing by 1.5 gives 10·log₁₀(1/1.5) ≈ −1.76 dB, not −3 dB.", "why_tempting": "Learners who don't recall the log relationship may guess a small divisor for a 'small' 3 dB change."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Dividing by 3 gives 10·log₁₀(1/3) ≈ −4.77 dB.", "why_tempting": "The '3' in '3 dB' invites a false match with dividing by 3 — a number-pattern trap."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Dividing by 4 gives 10·log₁₀(1/4) ≈ −6 dB; that's a 6 dB decrease, not 3 dB.", "why_tempting": "Confuses the power-quadrupling/quartering ratio (6 dB) with the doubling/halving ratio (3 dB)."}], "concept": "For power ratios, dB = 10·log₁₀(P₂/P₁). The key anchors: a factor of 2 → ±3 dB, a factor of 4 → ±6 dB, a factor of 10 → ±10 dB. Since log₁₀(2) ≈ 0.301, 10×0.301 ≈ 3.01 dB. A decrease means the negative direction, so dividing power by 2 yields −3 dB. (Note the factor of 10 is for power; voltage/current use 20·log₁₀ because power ∝ V².)", "misconception": "Assuming the numeric value in 'X dB' maps directly to a divide-by-X factor. dB is logarithmic, so 3 dB ↔ ×2, not ×3.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "3 dB = double (or halve) the power; 10 dB = ten times the power.", "edge_cases": "This 3-to-2 rule applies to power. For voltage or current the same ±3 dB corresponds to a factor of √2 ≈ 1.414, since those use 20·log₁₀."}

{"core": "Decibels for power follow dB = 10·log₁₀(P₂/P₁). For +6 dB, P₂/P₁ = 10^(6/10) = 10^0.6 ≈ 3.98 ≈ 4, so the power must be multiplied by 4.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Doubling power is only +3 dB (10·log₁₀ 2 = 3.01 dB), not +6 dB.", "why_tempting": "3 dB ↔ ×2 is the most-memorized pair, and learners reach for it whenever dB appears."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "×1.5 gives 10·log₁₀ 1.5 ≈ +1.76 dB, far short of 6 dB.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a 'small' increase to pair with a single-digit dB number, but the scale is logarithmic, not linear."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "×3 gives 10·log₁₀ 3 ≈ +4.77 dB, not 6 dB; the number 3 is confused with the 3 in '6 dB'.", "why_tempting": "Tempting to map the digit 6 onto 'times 3' or to think dB scales proportionally with the multiplier."}], "concept": "Power decibels use the 10·log₁₀ ratio (field/voltage quantities use 20·log₁₀). Key shortcut: each +3 dB doubles power and each +10 dB tens it. So +6 dB = +3 dB + +3 dB = ×2 × ×2 = ×4. The exact factor is 10^0.6 = 3.981, which rounds to 4. Conversely −6 dB = ×¼.", "misconception": "Treating dB as a linear scale (assuming 6 dB means ×6 or relating it to ×3), instead of recognizing that dB adds while the underlying power ratios multiply.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "+3 dB doubles, +10 dB ×10. Stack two 3 dB steps: 6 dB = ×2×2 = ×4.", "edge_cases": "This ×4 factor is for power. If you were given a voltage or current ratio, +6 dB corresponds to ×2 because those use 20·log₁₀."}

{"core": "Reducing power from 200 W to 20 W is a 10:1 power decrease, which equals 10·log10(20/200) = 10·log10(0.1) = −10 dB. Starting at 10 dB over S9 and dropping 10 dB lands exactly on S9.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "S9 +3 dB would correspond to only a 2:1 power decrease (−3 dB), not 10:1.", "why_tempting": "3 dB is the familiar 'half power' figure, so a learner reaching for any standard dB number may grab it."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This subtracts 10 dB from a plain S9 reading, ignoring that the start point was 10 dB ABOVE S9; the result should be back AT S9, not below it.", "why_tempting": "The −10 dB change is correct, but it's applied to the wrong reference level, producing a sub-S9 answer that looks plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "+5 dB implies the signal got stronger, but cutting power must reduce the received level.", "why_tempting": "Pulls in the '5' digit and a positive sign for anyone confusing magnitude with direction of change."}], "concept": "Power ratios in decibels: dB = 10·log10(P2/P1). A factor-of-10 reduction in power is −10 dB regardless of the absolute power level (200→20, or 50→5, both give −10 dB). The S-meter is calibrated so that '10 dB over S9' is a defined level 10 dB above S9; subtracting the 10 dB loss returns the reading precisely to S9. Note S-units below S9 are nominally 6 dB each, but above S9 the scale is read directly in dB, which is why this problem stays in the linear dB region above S9.", "misconception": "Confusing the −10 dB change (correct) with where it is applied. The change must be subtracted from the actual starting reading (S9+10 dB), not from a bare S9.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "×10 power = +10 dB, ÷10 power = −10 dB.", "edge_cases": "Below S9 the meter uses ~6 dB per S-unit, so a 10 dB drop there would not equal a clean number of S-units; this problem works neatly only because both points sit in the dB-calibrated region above S9."}

{"core": "Reducing power from 150 W to 15 W is a factor-of-10 decrease, which equals 10 dB. So the reading drops from 20 dB over S9 to 10 dB over S9, i.e. S9 plus 10 dB.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A drop to plain S9 would require removing the full 20 dB, which corresponds to a factor of 100 reduction (150 W to 1.5 W), not 10.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume any power cut just collapses the over-S9 margin to baseline S9."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "5 dB corresponds to a power ratio of about 3.16:1, not 10:1, so this doesn't match a 10x reduction.", "why_tempting": "It looks like a 'partial' reduction and 5 is half of the 10 dB answer, inviting a guess."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "3 dB is a 2:1 power ratio (halving), but power was cut by a factor of 10, not 2.", "why_tempting": "The 3 dB = half-power rule is heavily drilled, so it gets reflexively applied here."}], "concept": "Decibels for power: dB = 10·log10(P2/P1). Here P2/P1 = 15/150 = 0.1, so 10·log10(0.1) = 10·(-1) = -10 dB. The S-meter is logarithmic, with each S-unit nominally 6 dB and 'over S9' marked directly in dB. Subtracting 10 dB from '20 dB over S9' leaves '10 dB over S9.' Note this is the voltage-based '6 dB/S-unit' standard, but the over-S9 portion is already calibrated in dB, so you simply subtract the dB change.", "misconception": "Confusing the 3 dB (half-power) rule with a 10x change. A 10:1 power ratio is always 10 dB; only a 2:1 ratio is 3 dB.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Power: ×10 = +10 dB, ×2 = +3 dB. Tenfold means ten dB.", "edge_cases": "This assumes a linear path with no fading; real S-meters are loosely calibrated and 6 dB/S-unit is only an IARU convention many radios don't honour."}

{"core": "The decibel is a logarithmic unit expressing the ratio between two power (or voltage/current) levels — it is inherently a relative measurement, not an absolute quantity.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Waveform shape on an oscilloscope is described by amplitude, period, and frequency in linear units — the dB plays no role in time-domain waveform display.", "why_tempting": "An oscilloscope deals with signal levels, so a learner may loosely associate dB with anything signal-related."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "VHF radio waves are described by frequency or wavelength, not by a decibel — the dB has no units of frequency.", "why_tempting": "'Decibel' sounds technical and radio-related, so it may seem to label a band of waves."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "SSB describes a modulation/emission mode; while you can measure an SSB signal's strength in dB relative to something, the dB itself doesn't 'measure SSB' specifically.", "why_tempting": "It pairs a real signal type with measurement, sounding plausible."}], "concept": "The bel is log10(P2/P1); the decibel is one-tenth of that: dB = 10·log10(P2/P1) for power, or 20·log10(V2/V1) for voltage/current (since P∝V²). Because it is logarithmic, ratios multiply by adding dB: cascaded gains/losses simply sum. The factor of 10 vs 20 is the most common slip — use 10 only when comparing powers and 20 when comparing field quantities into equal impedances. Absolute references turn dB into an absolute scale (dBm = relative to 1 mW, dBW = relative to 1 W), but the unit's nature is always a ratio.", "misconception": "Thinking a decibel is an absolute unit of signal strength or a property of a wave, rather than a relative comparison of two levels.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "dB = 'difference between' two signals; 10-log for power, 20-log for voltage.", "edge_cases": "Once a reference is attached (dBm, dBW, dBμV), the value becomes absolute — but only because the second term of the ratio is a fixed, agreed reference."}

{"core": "A doubling of power is a 3 dB increase, since dB = 10·log₁₀(P₂/P₁) = 10·log₁₀(2) ≈ 3.01 dB.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "10 dB corresponds to a 10× power ratio, not 2×.", "why_tempting": "The factor 10 in the formula invites confusing the multiplier with the result; learners may equate '10 dB' with 'doubling' from rote habit."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "6 dB is a 4× power ratio (or a doubling of voltage/field strength using 20·log). Doubling power once is only 3 dB.", "why_tempting": "6 dB is the classic 'double the voltage' figure, and 2× sounds like it should pair with 6 dB if you misapply the 20·log formula."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "1 dB is roughly a 1.26× ratio, far less than a doubling.", "why_tempting": "A '1 watt to 2 watt' change feels small, so a small-looking number like 1 dB seems plausible."}], "concept": "The decibel for a power ratio is dB = 10·log₁₀(P₂/P₁). For P₂/P₁ = 2, log₁₀(2) ≈ 0.301, so dB ≈ 3.01, rounded to 3 dB. Key benchmarks worth memorizing: 2× power = 3 dB, 4× = 6 dB, 10× = 10 dB, 100× = 20 dB. These add: 4× = 2×2× = 3+3 = 6 dB, consistent with the log identity log(ab)=log a+log b. Note that VOLTAGE or current ratios use 20·log₁₀ because power ∝ V², so doubling voltage = 6 dB.", "misconception": "Mixing up the power formula (10·log) with the voltage formula (20·log), or memorizing the wrong benchmark (thinking 2× = 6 dB).", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "'Double the power, three dB louder' — 3 dB per doubling, 10 dB per ten-fold.", "edge_cases": "This 3 dB rule is for POWER ratios. If the question gave a voltage ratio of 2:1 into the same impedance, the answer would be 6 dB."}

{"core": "A tenfold increase in power (5 W to 50 W) is a power ratio of 10, and 10·log₁₀(10) = 10 dB.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "45 dB would be a power ratio of about 31623:1, vastly larger than 10:1.", "why_tempting": "Looks like it could come from adding/subtracting the wattage figures (50−5=45) and mislabeling them as dB."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "20 dB corresponds to a power ratio of 100:1, not 10:1.", "why_tempting": "A learner who confuses the voltage dB formula (20·log) with the power formula (10·log) lands here, since 20·log₁₀(?) thinking creeps in."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "30 dB is a power ratio of 1000:1.", "why_tempting": "Mistaking the absolute power difference (50−5=45, or loosely 'a big jump') for a large dB value, or confusing dBm/absolute-level reasoning."}], "concept": "Power gain in decibels is dB = 10·log₁₀(P_out/P_in). Here P_out/P_in = 50/5 = 10, so dB = 10·log₁₀(10) = 10·1 = 10 dB. Note the key distinction: power ratios use the factor 10, whereas voltage or current ratios use 20 (because power ∝ V², and log of a square brings the exponent of 2 out front: 10·log(V²)=20·log(V)). Memorize the anchors: ×2 ≈ 3 dB, ×10 = 10 dB, ×100 = 20 dB, ×1000 = 30 dB.", "misconception": "Treating the difference in watts (45 W) as if it were the dB value, or applying the 20·log voltage formula to a power ratio.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Every ×10 in POWER = +10 dB; every ×2 ≈ +3 dB. Power uses 10·log, voltage uses 20·log.", "edge_cases": "The 10·log rule applies to power ratios. If the problem gave voltage or current ratios you'd use 20·log instead — confirm whether the quantity is a power or an amplitude before choosing the multiplier."}

{"core": "9 dB of gain is a power ratio of about 8×. 2 W × 8 = 16 W, so the combination outputs 16 watts.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This adds 9 to 2 as if decibels were watts (9 + 2 = 11), but dB is a logarithmic ratio, not an additive number of watts.", "why_tempting": "It looks like a simple sum and gives a 'nearby' answer."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "This treats 9 dB as a ×10 ratio (2 × 10 = 20), confusing 9 dB with the cleaner 10 dB.", "why_tempting": "10 dB = ×10 is a memorized landmark, and 9 is close to 10."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This uses a ×9 multiplier (2 × 9 = 18), mistaking the '9' in '9 dB' for the power ratio itself.", "why_tempting": "Multiplying 2 by 9 feels intuitive and is right next to the correct 16 W."}], "concept": "Decibels relate power as dB = 10·log₁₀(P_out/P_in). To invert: P_out/P_in = 10^(dB/10). For 9 dB that is 10^0.9 ≈ 7.94 ≈ 8. Using the building-block ratios: 3 dB ≈ ×2, so 9 dB = 3+3+3 dB = 2×2×2 = ×8. Thus 2 W × 8 = 16 W. Adding gains in dB corresponds to multiplying the linear ratios.", "misconception": "Believing decibels add to or multiply the wattage directly, rather than representing a logarithmic power ratio that must be converted to a linear multiplier first.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "3 dB = double, 9 dB = three doublings = ×8 (×2×2×2).", "edge_cases": "9 dB is exactly ×7.94, so 2 W gives 15.9 W — rounded to 16 W. The 3-dB-per-doubling shortcut is an approximation (3.01 dB precisely) but is plenty accurate for exam multiple-choice."}

{"core": "A loss of 6 dB corresponds to a power factor of 4 (since 6 dB = two 3-dB halvings, or 10^(6/10) ≈ 3.98). Dividing 100 W by 4 gives 25 W delivered to the antenna.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "33 W would require a loss of about 4.8 dB (a factor of ~3), not 6 dB.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a plausible 'roughly a third' answer if you wrongly equate 6 dB with a factor of 3."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "50 W is a factor-of-2 reduction, which is only 3 dB of loss, not 6 dB.", "why_tempting": "Tempting if you remember 3 dB = half power and mistakenly apply it to 6 dB."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "17 W implies a factor of ~6 (about 7.7 dB), more loss than stated.", "why_tempting": "Easy to pick if you confuse the dB value (6) with the linear power-division factor."}], "concept": "Decibels are logarithmic: dB = 10·log10(Pout/Pin). For loss, Pout = Pin / 10^(dB/10). Here 10^(6/10) = 10^0.6 ≈ 3.98 ≈ 4, so 100 W / 4 = 25 W. The handy shortcut: each 3 dB halves the power, so 6 dB = halve twice = 100→50→25 W.", "misconception": "Treating the dB number as a direct linear divisor (6 dB ≠ divide by 6) or mixing up the 3 dB-per-halving rule.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "3 dB = half, so 6 dB = quarter (half of a half).", "edge_cases": "The 3 dB ≈ half approximation (10^0.3 = 1.995) is what makes 6 dB give exactly /4; the true factor is 3.98, close enough for exam purposes."}

{"core": "Each S-unit is 6 dB; 30 dB over S9 is 30 dB above S9, so you must drop the received signal by 30 dB. A 30 dB reduction is a factor of 1000 in power, so 100 W ÷ 1000 = 0.1 W.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "1 W is only a 20 dB reduction from 100 W (100/1 = 100× = 20 dB), leaving the reading at S9 + 10 dB.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a clean round-number drop and learners may stop one decade short."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "10 W is just a 10 dB reduction (100/10 = 10×), so the signal would still read about 20 dB over S9.", "why_tempting": "A single power-of-ten cut feels intuitive but only removes 10 dB, not 30."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "33.3 W is roughly a 4.8 dB reduction (10·log(100/33.3) ≈ 4.8 dB) — barely under one S-unit, nowhere near 30 dB.", "why_tempting": "It mimics the '100/3' arithmetic and looks like a plausible 'about a third' answer."}], "concept": "On HF/VHF S-meters one S-unit is defined as 6 dB, but the practical key here is the dB-to-power-ratio conversion: dB = 10·log₁₀(P1/P2). To shed 30 dB of received power you solve 30 = 10·log₁₀(100/P2) → log₁₀(100/P2) = 3 → 100/P2 = 10³ = 1000 → P2 = 0.1 W. Received signal strength tracks transmitter power directly (same path, same antennas), so a 30 dB cut at the transmitter yields a 30 dB drop at the receiver.
