Remember the shortcuts: 10 dB = 10×, 20 dB = 100×, 30 dB = 1000× in power.", "misconception": "Confusing dB with linear ratios — thinking '30 dB' means dividing power by 30, or stopping at a factor of 10 or 100 instead of the full 1000× that 30 dB represents.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Every 10 dB = ×10 in power. 30 dB = three tens = ÷1000.", "edge_cases": "S-meter calibration varies by radio, but the dB-above-S9 figure is meant to be read directly as a power-ratio change, which is what this question tests."}

{"core": "Inductors in series add directly, just like resistors. Two equal inductors L in series give L+L = 2L, twice the value of one.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Halving applies to equal inductors in PARALLEL, not series.", "why_tempting": "Learners confuse series/parallel rules and recall that something gets halved."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Series inductance is a sum, not a product; doubling doesn't yield ×4.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a 'compound' effect for someone misremembering combination rules."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Dividing by 4 has no basis in either series or parallel inductor math.", "why_tempting": "Pairs with option C as a plausible-looking 'fraction' answer for the unsure."}], "concept": "For series inductors with no mutual coupling, total inductance is the simple sum: L_total = L1 + L2 + … + Ln. This mirrors series resistors because the same current flows through each and the induced voltages add (V = L·di/dt summed). For n equal inductors, L_total = n·L, so two equal inductors give 2L. (Parallel inductors instead follow the reciprocal rule, 1/L_total = Σ1/Li, giving L/2 for two equal ones.)", "misconception": "Mixing up the series rule (add) with the parallel rule (reciprocal), or assuming inductors combine multiplicatively.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Inductors combine like resistors: series adds, parallel reciprocates.", "edge_cases": "The simple sum assumes no mutual inductance between the coils. If they are magnetically coupled, L_total = L1 + L2 ± 2M depending on field orientation."}

{"core": "Inductors in parallel combine like resistors in parallel; two equal inductors L give L_total = L/2.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Dividing by 4 has no basis — parallel combination of two equal elements halves, not quarters, the value.", "why_tempting": "Confusion with squaring effects or mixing up the n=2 case with some other factor."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Doubling describes inductors in SERIES, not parallel.", "why_tempting": "Series inductors add (L+L=2L), and learners often swap the series/parallel rules."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Multiplying by 4 is the inverse of dividing by 4 and is simply wrong for any combination.", "why_tempting": "Looks like the 'opposite' trap answer paired with option A."}], "concept": "For inductors (no mutual coupling) the parallel formula mirrors resistors: 1/L_total = 1/L1 + 1/L2. For two equal values, 1/L_total = 2/L, so L_total = L/2. The general N-equal case gives L/N. This contrasts with series, where L_total = L1 + L2 = 2L. The reason inductors follow the resistor rules (and capacitors the opposite) is that inductive reactance X_L = 2πfL is proportional to L, so impedances combine the same way resistances do.", "misconception": "Believing inductors combine like capacitors. In fact inductors behave like resistors: add in series, reciprocal-sum in parallel — capacitors are the reverse.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Inductors act like resistors: series adds, parallel halves (for equal values).", "edge_cases": "This assumes no magnetic coupling between the inductors; mutual inductance would alter the result."}

{"core": "Capacitors in series combine like resistors in parallel: 1/C_total = 1/C1 + 1/C2. For two equal capacitors C, 1/C_total = 2/C, so C_total = C/2 — half the value of either one.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Doubling occurs for equal capacitors in parallel, not series.", "why_tempting": "Learners confuse series/parallel capacitor rules, which are inverted relative to resistors."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "No series or parallel combination of two equal caps multiplies value by 4.", "why_tempting": "The '4' invites guessing via a half-remembered squaring or doubling-twice notion."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Dividing by 4 has no basis for two equal capacitors; series of two halves the value.", "why_tempting": "Looks like the 'series divides' intuition pushed too far."}], "concept": "For series capacitance, charge Q is common to both plates while voltages add: V = Q/C1 + Q/C2, so C_total = Q/V = 1/(1/C1+1/C2). With C1=C2=C this gives C/2. Physically, stacking dielectric gaps in series increases effective plate separation, lowering capacitance (C = εA/d). Note this is the opposite of resistors, where series adds.", "misconception": "Assuming capacitors follow the same series/parallel arithmetic as resistors — in fact the rules are swapped.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Caps in Series = Smaller (reciprocal sum); caps in Parallel = Plus (add directly).", "edge_cases": "Only true for equal values; for unequal series caps the total is always smaller than the smallest capacitor. Voltage rating also matters — series equalizes voltage only if values match."}

{"core": "Capacitors in parallel add directly, so two equal capacitors of value C give C + C = 2C — twice the value of either one.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Halving the value is the result for two equal capacitors in SERIES, not parallel.", "why_tempting": "Learners often confuse the series/parallel rules for capacitors versus resistors and pick the inverse behaviour."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "There is no factor-of-4 multiplication; parallel capacitance is a simple sum, giving 2C for two equal units.", "why_tempting": "Looks like an answer for combining four capacitors, or a guess that 'more capacitors' means a large multiplier."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Dividing by 4 has no basis in either series or parallel combination of two equal capacitors.", "why_tempting": "Pairs with option B as a 'reciprocal' trap for someone unsure which way the value moves."}], "concept": "Capacitors add by plate area. Putting them in parallel effectively increases total plate area, so capacitances sum: C_total = C1 + C2 + ... For two equal values, C_total = C + C = 2C. This is opposite to resistors, where parallel values combine reciprocally. In series, capacitors combine like parallel resistors: 1/C_total = 1/C1 + 1/C2, giving C/2 for two equal units.", "misconception": "Treating capacitors like resistors — assuming parallel means the value goes down. For capacitors the rules are swapped: parallel adds, series reduces.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Capacitors are 'backwards' from resistors: parallel = add (bigger), series = reciprocal (smaller).", "edge_cases": "Adding directly only holds for parallel; for series-equal capacitors the total would be C/2."}

{"core": "For an air-core solenoid, inductance is set purely by its geometry: the coil diameter (cross-sectional area), its length, and the number of turns. No magnetic core means permeability is fixed at μ₀.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Operating frequency does not change a coil's intrinsic inductance; L is a geometric property. Frequency only affects reactance (X_L = 2πfL), not L itself.", "why_tempting": "Learners conflate inductive reactance (frequency-dependent) with inductance (a fixed property)."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The wire material/gauge affects DC resistance and current rating, but not inductance — it replaces the relevant 'diameter' parameter with an irrelevant one.", "why_tempting": "Thicker wire feels like it should matter, and 'type of wire' sounds plausibly relevant to an electrical component."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Coil orientation in space has no effect on inductance; it only affects mutual coupling with nearby coils/fields. It substitutes orientation for the needed length parameter.", "why_tempting": "Orientation matters for mutual inductance and interference, so it can seem like a self-inductance factor too."}], "concept": "The textbook solenoid approximation is L = μ₀·N²·A / ℓ, where N is turns, A = π(d/2)² is the cross-sectional area (diameter dependence), and ℓ is the coil length. With an air core, μ = μ₀ is constant, so the three free variables are exactly diameter, length, and number of turns. Inductance scales with N² and with area, and inversely with length.", "misconception": "Believing frequency (which governs reactance) or wire/conductor properties change the inductance itself, rather than recognizing L as a fixed geometric quantity.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "DLN — Diameter, Length, Number of turns.", "edge_cases": "With a ferromagnetic core, relative permeability μ_r dominates and can swamp the geometric terms; that's why this question specifies air-core."}

{"core": "Parallel-plate capacitance depends on the plate area, the gap between plates, and the dielectric — so for a fixed air dielectric, area and spacing are the determining geometric factors.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Applied voltage doesn't change capacitance; it sets the stored charge via Q=CV but C itself is purely geometric/material-dependent.", "why_tempting": "Voltage feels relevant because it appears in capacitor equations and affects energy and breakdown."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Capacitance is a static structural property independent of operating frequency; frequency only affects reactance Xc=1/(2πfC).", "why_tempting": "Frequency dominates AC behaviour, so learners conflate the capacitor's reactance with its intrinsic capacitance."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Plate conductivity/metal type is irrelevant to capacitance as long as the plates are conductors; what matters is the dielectric between them.", "why_tempting": "Spacing is correctly listed, and 'type of metal' sounds plausibly material-related — but the relevant material is the dielectric, not the plate metal."}], "concept": "For a parallel-plate capacitor, C = εrε0·A/d, where A is plate overlap area, d is plate separation, and εr is the relative permittivity of the dielectric (≈1 for air). C rises with area and falls as the gap widens. The plate conductor material drops out entirely (assuming a good conductor), and neither applied voltage nor frequency appear — those govern stored charge and reactance respectively, not the capacitance value itself.", "misconception": "Confusing factors that determine the capacitance (geometry + dielectric) with factors that determine charge, energy, or reactance (voltage, frequency).", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Capacitance = A over d (Area up, distance down — more C).", "edge_cases": "With air as dielectric εr≈1; insert a dielectric and C scales by εr. Edge fringing makes the formula approximate for small plates."}

{"core": "Polarized electrolytic capacitors have a defined positive and negative terminal; applying voltage of the wrong polarity (reverse voltage) breaks down the oxide dielectric, causing the capacitor to overheat, leak, or rupture. So you must never apply reverse voltage.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Electrolytics can be paralleled with any capacitor type (film, ceramic, etc.); doing so is common practice for broadband decoupling and adds nothing dangerous.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a plausible 'special handling' rule, and mixing capacitor types may seem risky to a beginner."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Electrolytics are actually poor at RF due to high internal series inductance/ESR — they're used for low-frequency filtering and power-supply smoothing, not exclusively at RF. The opposite of the truth.", "why_tempting": "Specifies a frequency-domain rule that could be confused with the real-world limitation that electrolytics work badly at high frequencies."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Electrolytics can be placed in series (sometimes done to share voltage or even to make a non-polar pair); series use is permitted with proper balancing.", "why_tempting": "Series capacitor rules are a real exam topic, so a 'forbidden series' claim feels familiar."}], "concept": "An aluminum electrolytic forms its dielectric from a thin oxide layer grown anodically on the positive plate. Correct polarity maintains this oxide. Reverse bias drives current through and chemically reduces the oxide, dropping its resistance; current rises, internal heating boils the electrolyte and pressure builds until the vent or case fails. This is why they carry a polarity stripe and why bipolar/non-polar electrolytics are built as two cells back-to-back. Equally, never exceed the rated working voltage even with correct polarity.", "misconception": "Thinking the key hazard relates to how electrolytics combine with other components, when the real constraint is polarity orientation of the applied DC.", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "'Electrolytic' = 'electro-LIMITED' to one direction: positive to plus, or it pops.", "edge_cases": "Non-polarized (bipolar) electrolytics exist for AC/crossover use; the reverse-voltage rule applies only to the polarized type. Also respect the voltage rating and ripple-current limits even when correctly polarized."}

{"core": "Inductors in series add directly (assuming no mutual coupling), so 12 mH + 12 mH = 24 mH.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "6 mH is half of one choke's value — that's the result for two equal inductors in parallel (12/2), not series.", "why_tempting": "Learners who confuse series and parallel rules grab the parallel result."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "48 mH would be quadrupling a single value, which has no basis here; series simply sums to 24 mH.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a 'big number' answer for series combination and is a multiple of 12."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "3 mH is far too small; combining inductors in series can only increase total inductance, never reduce it to a quarter.", "why_tempting": "Resembles a parallel-style division and is a plausible-looking small fraction."}], "concept": "Inductors behave like resistors topologically. In series, L_total = L1 + L2 + ... (when there is no mutual inductance M). In parallel, 1/L_total = 1/L1 + 1/L2. Here both are 12 mH in series: L_total = 12 + 12 = 24 mH. If the chokes were magnetically coupled, you'd add ±2M, but exam questions assume no coupling.", "misconception": "Swapping the series/parallel rules — applying the parallel reciprocal formula to a series circuit, which yields a smaller value when series should give a larger one.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Inductors add like resistors: SERIES = SUM.", "edge_cases": "The simple sum holds only when the inductors are not mutually coupled; with coupling, L_total = L1 + L2 ± 2M depending on winding orientation."}

{"core": "Equal inductors in parallel halve the total inductance, so two 20 mH coils give 10 mH.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "40 mH is the sum, which is the rule for inductors in SERIES, not parallel.", "why_tempting": "Adding values feels natural and matches the series case learners memorize first."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "80 mH multiplies the sum by two — no formula produces this; it's a dimensional trap.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a 'big' answer for combining two coils, and 4×20 might seem like a plausible combination."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "5 mH would result from dividing 20 mH by 4; the correct division is by the number of equal coils (2), not by 4.", "why_tempting": "Picks the smallest value, and learners who over-apply 'parallel makes it smaller' may overshoot."}], "concept": "Inductors combine like resistors. In parallel: 1/L_total = 1/L1 + 1/L2 = 1/20 + 1/20 = 2/20, so L_total = 10 mH. For N equal inductors in parallel, L_total = L/N — here 20/2 = 10 mH. This assumes no mutual coupling between the coils (negligible shared magnetic flux); coupling would alter the result.", "misconception": "Confusing the parallel rule with the series rule. Series inductors add (like series resistors); parallel inductors combine via reciprocals (like parallel resistors).", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Inductors behave like resistors: series adds, parallel uses the reciprocal/product-over-sum.", "edge_cases": "The L/N shortcut only works for equal values, and the whole formula assumes the inductors are not magnetically coupled (mutual inductance = 0)."}

{"core": "For capacitors in series the reciprocals add; two equal 20 µF caps give 20/2 = 10 µF.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "40 µF is the sum of the two values, which is the rule for parallel capacitors, not series.", "why_tempting": "Adding values feels intuitive and matches how resistors add in series."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "80 µF has no physical basis here — it's roughly the product (20×20=400) misremembered or a doubling error.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a 'bigger combination' answer for someone guessing series increases capacitance."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "5 µF would result from quartering (20/4), an over-application of the divide rule.", "why_tempting": "It's smaller than the originals — correct direction — so a careless divide gives this."}], "concept": "Capacitance in series combines like resistance in parallel: 1/C_total = 1/C1 + 1/C2. For two equal caps C, C_total = C/2. Physically, stacking capacitors in series increases the effective plate separation, which lowers total capacitance. Here 1/C = 1/20 + 1/20 = 2/20 = 1/10, so C_total = 10 µF. (Note: series capacitors share the same charge and divide the applied voltage, which is why series stacking raises the combined voltage rating.)", "misconception": "Treating capacitors like resistors — assuming series adds and parallel divides. It's the opposite: capacitors ADD in parallel and combine reciprocally (decrease) in series.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Capacitors are 'backwards' from resistors: parallel adds, series shrinks. Two equal in series = half.", "edge_cases": "The shortcut C/n only works for n identical capacitors; for unequal values you must use the full reciprocal sum."}

{"core": "Capacitors in parallel add directly, so 24 µF + 24 µF = 48 µF (option C).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "96 µF would be 4× a single capacitor; that's not produced by any series or parallel rule for two equal 24 µF units.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a 'doubling then doubling' guess, or confusing parallel addition with some multiplied result."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "4 µF is far below either capacitor's value; the only way to drop below 24 µF is a series connection, and even then two equal 24 µF give 12 µF, not 4 µF.", "why_tempting": "A learner mixing up the resistor/capacitor series formula and miscalculating could land here."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "12 µF is the result of wiring the two equal capacitors in SERIES (24/2), not in parallel.", "why_tempting": "This is the classic trap: applying the series rule when the question clearly says parallel. Capacitors behave opposite to resistors."}], "concept": "For capacitors the parallel/series rules are inverted relative to resistors. In parallel the plate areas effectively combine, so capacitances add: C_total = C1 + C2 + … = 24 + 24 = 48 µF. In series the reciprocals add: 1/C_total = 1/C1 + 1/C2, giving 12 µF for two equal 24 µF units. Physically, parallel connection increases total stored charge for a given voltage, raising capacitance.", "misconception": "Assuming capacitors follow the same combination rules as resistors. They are reversed: capacitors ADD in parallel and combine reciprocally in series.", "link": "Series / Parallel R & C", "mnemonic": "Capacitors are the 'opposite' of resistors: parallel adds, series shrinks.", "edge_cases": "Equal-value series capacitors always give half the value; equal-value parallel capacitors always give double."}

{"core": "Inductive reactance is X_L = 2πfL, so it rises in direct proportion to frequency. Higher AC frequency means greater opposition to current flow.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Reactance depends on frequency and inductance, not on signal amplitude; X_L = 2πfL contains no amplitude term.", "why_tempting": "Confuses amplitude with frequency, and inverts the relationship — sounds like a plausible 'opposite' of the right answer."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Amplitude does not appear in X_L = 2πfL, so changing the voltage/current level alone leaves reactance unchanged.", "why_tempting": "Has the correct 'increases' direction but pins it to amplitude instead of frequency."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This is the behaviour of a capacitor (X_C = 1/(2πfC)), not an inductor. For an inductor, reactance rises with frequency.", "why_tempting": "Correctly cites frequency as the variable, but states the inverse relationship that actually belongs to capacitive reactance."}], "concept": "For an ideal inductor, the voltage leads current by 90° and the magnitude of opposition is the inductive reactance X_L = 2πfL = ωL. Reactance is purely a function of frequency f and inductance L — it scales linearly with both. Amplitude (the size of the applied voltage or current) sets how much current flows for a given X_L but never changes X_L itself. Physically, a faster-changing current produces a larger back-EMF (V = L·di/dt), so higher frequency yields more opposition. Contrast with a capacitor, where X_C = 1/(2πfC) falls as frequency rises.", "misconception": "Mixing up the two distinct variables: thinking reactance tracks signal amplitude, or applying the capacitor's inverse-with-frequency rule to an inductor.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "\"Inductors Increase\" — X_L goes up with frequency; capacitors do the opposite.", "edge_cases": "Real inductors have series resistance and self-capacitance; near self-resonance the simple X_L = 2πfL no longer holds and the component eventually behaves capacitively."}

{"core": "Capacitive reactance Xc = 1/(2\\u03c0fC), so it is inversely proportional to frequency — raise f and Xc falls. As frequency rises a capacitor passes AC more readily.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "This states the opposite of the inverse relationship; Xc actually decreases with frequency.", "why_tempting": "It mirrors inductors, where Xl = 2\\u03c0fL rises with frequency, so learners who confuse L and C pick it."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Reactance depends only on f and C, not on signal amplitude/voltage — Xc is independent of how large the AC swing is.", "why_tempting": "In a resistive intuition, bigger voltage feels like it should change something; 'amplitude' sounds relevant."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Same flaw: amplitude does not appear in Xc = 1/(2\\u03c0fC), so changing amplitude does not change reactance.", "why_tempting": "It pairs the correct direction word 'decreases' with the wrong variable, baiting partial recall."}], "concept": "Xc = 1/(2\\u03c0fC) in ohms. The 1/f term means as f \\u2192 0 (DC) Xc \\u2192 \\u221e (capacitor blocks DC), and as f \\u2192 \\u221e Xc \\u2192 0 (acts like a short). Physically, higher frequency means the plates charge/discharge faster, so more charge flows per second for the same voltage — i.e. lower opposition. Reactance is purely a function of f and C; the current's amplitude scales with applied voltage but does not alter the reactance itself.", "misconception": "Believing reactance depends on signal amplitude, or swapping the capacitor's inverse-f behaviour for the inductor's direct-f behaviour.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Capacitor: C for 'Conducts at high f' (Xc down). Inductor is the opposite.", "edge_cases": "At DC (f = 0) Xc is theoretically infinite — the capacitor blocks steady current; only the transient charging current flows."}

{"core": "Capacitive reactance Xc = 1/(2\\u03c0fC) is inversely proportional to frequency, so as frequency decreases, Xc rises.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Reactance depends only on frequency and capacitance, not on the applied voltage level.", "why_tempting": "Learners may confuse the voltage-current relationship in a capacitor with a dependence of reactance on voltage."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "As with B, applied voltage does not appear in Xc = 1/(2\\u03c0fC); reactance is voltage-independent.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you wrongly assume lower voltage means less current 'opposition.'"}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "This is the inverse of the truth: increasing frequency lowers Xc, not raises it.", "why_tempting": "Easy to swap the relationship if you confuse it with inductive reactance, which does increase with frequency."}], "concept": "For an ideal capacitor, Xc = 1/(2\\u03c0fC). The frequency f sits in the denominator, so Xc and f are inversely related: halve f and Xc doubles; at DC (f=0) Xc is infinite (open circuit), and at very high frequency Xc \\u2192 0 (short). This is the mirror image of inductive reactance Xl = 2\\u03c0fL, which rises with frequency. Note reactance is purely a function of f and C \\u2014 amplitude of applied voltage never enters the expression.", "misconception": "Believing reactance scales with applied voltage, or confusing capacitive behaviour with inductive (where reactance rises with frequency).", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Capacitors 'pass the highs, block the lows' \\u2014 high f = low Xc, low f = high Xc.", "edge_cases": "Holds for ideal capacitors; real capacitors have parasitic series inductance (ESL) causing self-resonance, above which they behave inductively."}

{"core": "Impedance (Z) is the total opposition to AC, combining resistance (R) and reactance (X). It is the AC analog of resistance but includes the phase-shifting reactive component.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Conductance (G) is the reciprocal of resistance (G = 1/R), measuring how easily DC current flows, not the combined AC opposition.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a fundamental electrical quantity and is related to current flow, but it's the inverse of opposition, not opposition itself."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Reluctance is the opposition to magnetic flux in a magnetic circuit, not opposition to electric current.", "why_tempting": "The word resembles 'resistance' and appears in magnetics, so it can feel like a circuit-opposition term."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Admittance (Y) is the reciprocal of impedance (Y = 1/Z), describing how readily AC flows, not the opposition.", "why_tempting": "It's a legitimate AC quantity that pairs with impedance, so it's easy to confuse the two."}], "concept": "In AC circuits the total opposition is impedance Z, a complex quantity Z = R + jX, where R is resistance and X = X_L − X_C is net reactance. Its magnitude is |Z| = √(R² + X²), and it carries a phase angle θ = arctan(X/R) describing the lead/lag between voltage and current. Resistance dissipates power; reactance stores and returns energy (inductive X_L = ωL is positive, capacitive X_C = 1/ωC is negative). The reciprocals — conductance G, susceptance B, and admittance Y = 1/Z = G + jB — describe ease of flow rather than opposition.", "misconception": "Confusing impedance with its reciprocal (admittance) or with resistance/conductance, or mixing in the magnetic-circuit term reluctance because the names sound similar.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Z combines R and X: think 'iZ everything together.' Admittance/conductance start the back half of the alphabet of reciprocals.", "edge_cases": "When X = 0 (purely resistive, e.g. at resonance), impedance equals resistance and is purely real; only then do Z and R coincide."}

{"core": "Impedance (Z) is defined as the ratio of AC voltage to AC current, V/I, and generalizes resistance to AC circuits by including both resistive and reactive opposition.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Conductance (G) is the reciprocal of resistance (1/R), measured in siemens — it's a ratio of current to voltage, not voltage to current.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a fundamental circuit ratio, and learners may forget it's the inverse relationship."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Resistance is the V/I ratio only for the purely resistive (real) part of opposition; it ignores the frequency-dependent reactive component present in AC circuits.", "why_tempting": "Ohm's law (R=V/I) is drilled so heavily that 'resistance' feels like the universal answer, but the question specifically says AC."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Reactance (X) is only the opposition contributed by inductors and capacitors (the imaginary part); it's not the total V/I ratio.", "why_tempting": "It's clearly AC-related, so it looks plausible if you don't recall that reactance is only one component of impedance."}], "concept": "In AC analysis impedance is the complex quantity Z = R + jX, where R is resistance and X is net reactance (X_L − X_C). Its magnitude |Z| = √(R² + X²) equals the ratio of voltage magnitude to current magnitude, and its angle gives the phase shift between them. Thus Z is the AC generalization of Ohm's law: V = I·Z. Resistance and reactance are merely the real and imaginary components, while conductance (G) and susceptance (B) belong to the reciprocal quantity, admittance Y = 1/Z.", "misconception": "Confusing the total AC opposition (impedance) with one of its constituent parts (resistance or reactance), or with the reciprocal family of quantities (conductance/admittance).", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Z is the whole pie: R + jX. Reactance and resistance are slices; impedance is the full V/I ratio.", "edge_cases": "In a purely resistive circuit Z = R (X = 0), so impedance and resistance coincide — which is why DC and resistive-only cases hide the distinction."}

{"core": "Inductive reactance is X_L = 2πfL, so it rises directly with frequency. Increasing the applied frequency increases the reactance.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Reactance depends only on f and L, not on the magnitude of applied voltage. Voltage sets the current through a given reactance, not the reactance itself.", "why_tempting": "Learners conflate 'more drive' with 'more opposition,' but reactance is a circuit property independent of excitation amplitude."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Lowering frequency reduces X_L = 2πfL — it moves reactance toward zero (a short at DC), the opposite of the asked effect.", "why_tempting": "It's the exact inverse of the correct answer, so it looks plausible if you forget the direction of proportionality."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Like voltage, current amplitude doesn't alter reactance; X_L is fixed by f and L. Current is a *result* of reactance, not a cause.", "why_tempting": "Ohm's-law intuition (V, I, R coupled) bleeds over, suggesting current must influence the opposition."}], "concept": "For an ideal inductor, X_L = 2πfL = ωL, measured in ohms. The reactance is linear in frequency: double f, double X_L. Physically, a higher rate of change of current induces a larger back-EMF (v = L di/dt), so the inductor opposes AC more strongly as frequency climbs. At DC (f = 0) X_L = 0 — the inductor looks like a wire. Note reactance is purely a function of f and L; the excitation level (V or I) never enters the formula. Contrast with capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(2πfC), which falls with frequency.", "misconception": "Believing reactance is governed by the applied voltage or current rather than purely by frequency and inductance.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "'Inductors block the fast' — X_L grows with frequency; capacitors do the opposite.", "edge_cases": "This assumes an ideal inductor; real coils add series resistance and self-resonance from parasitic capacitance, above which they behave capacitively."}

{"core": "A ferrite-cored coil presents high inductive reactance at radio frequencies, so it chokes off (blocks) the offending RF current while letting lower frequencies pass — the basis of a ferrite RF choke.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Low RF reactance would let the offending RF signal pass straight through, doing nothing to suppress it.", "why_tempting": "Inverts the goal — a learner might think the choke should 'conduct' the signal away rather than block it."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Audio frequencies are not the offending signal here, and low reactance at any frequency means no blocking action.", "why_tempting": "Mixes the wrong frequency band with the wrong (low) reactance, sounding plausible if you focus on 'passing audio.'"}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The choke must block RF, not audio; high reactance at audio would interfere with the desired audio signal instead of suppressing RFI.", "why_tempting": "Right idea (high reactance blocks) but applied to the wrong band — confuses the interfering RF with the wanted audio."}], "concept": "Inductive reactance rises with frequency: X_L = 2\\u03c0fL. A ferrite core raises the effective permeability, boosting L (and often adding loss that dissipates RF as heat). At RF the reactance becomes large compared with the circuit impedance, so RF current is strongly impeded; at audio/DC the reactance is small and those signals pass. This is exactly how ferrite beads and chokes on power and audio leads cut RFI.", "misconception": "Thinking the choke 'absorbs' or routes the signal via low impedance, rather than blocking it with high reactance — and confusing which frequency band needs blocking.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Choke = high X_L at high f: it 'chokes' the high frequencies.", "edge_cases": "Beyond pure reactance, ferrites also add resistive loss at RF (the bead becomes lossy), so real-world suppression is a mix of reactance and dissipation, which is why ferrites work over a broad RF range rather than a single resonant point."}

{"core": "A bypass capacitor presents low reactance at radio frequencies, so unwanted RF energy is shunted (bypassed) to ground rather than passing through the audio stage. Because Xc = 1/(2πfC), reactance falls as frequency rises.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "At audio frequencies the capacitor's reactance is comparatively high, so it does NOT shunt audio — which is exactly what you want, but it's not the property that diverts the RF.", "why_tempting": "Learners confuse 'audio circuit' with 'acts on audio'; they assume the cap works on the band it sits in."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "High reactance at audio means the cap is nearly open to audio, leaving audio undisturbed — but it does nothing to divert RF, which is the question's actual goal.", "why_tempting": "It correctly notes the cap doesn't affect audio, so it sounds half-right."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "This is the opposite of how a capacitor behaves: reactance DECREASES with increasing frequency, so a cap has low (not high) reactance at RF.", "why_tempting": "Inductors show high reactance at RF, so learners who swap L and C behaviour pick this."}], "concept": "Capacitive reactance Xc = 1/(2πfC) is inversely proportional to frequency. For a small bypass cap, at audio (say 1 kHz) Xc is large, but at RF (say 14 MHz) Xc is a tiny fraction of an ohm — effectively a short to ground for RF while remaining an open circuit for audio. Placed across a stage or from a signal/supply line to ground, it forms a low-pass shunt that drains RFI energy without loading the wanted audio.", "misconception": "That the relevant property is defined by the circuit's name ('audio circuit') rather than by the frequency of the interfering signal being diverted — and forgetting that capacitor reactance falls, not rises, with frequency.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Cap = 'short to fast' — high frequency sees low reactance.", "edge_cases": "Lead inductance and self-resonance mean a real capacitor stops looking capacitive above its SRF; at VHF/UHF you choose small-value, low-ESL caps so the bypass remains effective."}

{"core": "An RF bypass capacitor is small, so at audio frequencies its capacitive reactance is high — it looks like a near-open circuit and barely loads the audio signal, while at RF it presents low reactance and shunts the unwanted RF to ground.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "High reactance at RF would prevent the cap from bypassing RF at all, defeating its purpose; it actually has LOW reactance at RF.", "why_tempting": "Mentions RF, the frequency the cap is meant to act on, so it feels relevant if you forget which way reactance goes."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Low reactance at audio would shunt and attenuate the audio signal — the exact harmful effect we want to avoid.", "why_tempting": "It correctly pairs audio frequencies with the cap's interaction, but inverts the reactance, sounding plausible if you misremember Xc."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "True statement, but low RF reactance describes WHY it bypasses RF, not why it leaves the audio circuit unaffected — wrong scope for the question.", "why_tempting": "It's a factually correct property of the cap, so it's easy to pick without noticing the question asks about non-interference with audio."}], "concept": "Capacitive reactance is Xc = 1/(2\\u03c0fC). For a fixed small C, Xc varies inversely with frequency. At low (audio) f, Xc is large → high impedance path → negligible signal diverted. At high (RF) f, Xc is small → low impedance path to ground → RF is shunted. A bypass cap is chosen small so its corner is above the audio band, isolating audio from RF effects.", "misconception": "Confusing which frequency the cap 'affects.' The question asks why it leaves AUDIO alone, so you must reason about reactance at audio frequencies, not at RF.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Cap = 'high-pass to ground': blocks lows (audio), passes highs (RF) to ground.", "edge_cases": "Real caps add series inductance (ESL) and self-resonance, so at very high RF the effective reactance can rise again above resonance."}

{"core": "An RF choke is meant to pass low-frequency (or DC) signals while blocking RF. Because inductive reactance X_L = 2πfL falls toward zero as frequency drops, the choke presents low reactance at low frequencies and lets those signals flow through with little impediment.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "High reactance at low frequencies would obstruct the very signals the choke is supposed to pass — the opposite of an RF choke's purpose.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you confuse which frequencies the choke is meant to block versus pass."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "An inductor's reactance rises with frequency, so it cannot have low reactance at high frequencies; that's also exactly what makes it useful for blocking RF, not passing it.", "why_tempting": "The phrase 'high frequencies' matches the word 'RF' in the name, baiting a careless reader."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "High reactance at high frequencies is true of the choke, but that's the property that BLOCKS RF, not the one that lets desired (low-frequency) signals pass — the question asks about signals 'meant to flow through.'", "why_tempting": "It's a factually correct statement about the choke, so it feels right until you read the question carefully."}], "concept": "For an ideal inductor X_L = 2πfL. Reactance is directly proportional to frequency: at DC (f=0) X_L=0, and it grows without bound as f increases. An RF choke exploits this frequency dependence — it acts nearly as a short to low-frequency and DC currents (low X_L, little effect) while presenting a large series impedance to RF (high X_L, strong attenuation). So the property enabling desired signals to pass is its low reactance at low frequencies.", "misconception": "Reading 'RF choke' and assuming the question is about what it blocks. The question specifically asks about the signals 'meant to flow through' — i.e. the low-frequency/DC signals — so the relevant property is the low reactance at those frequencies.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Choke = 'high f gets choked, low f flows free.'", "edge_cases": "At self-resonance the choke's parasitic capacitance can make its behaviour deviate from the simple X_L=2πfL model, but for exam purposes treat it as an ideal inductor."}

{"core": "Inductive reactance is given by X_L = 2πfL, so reactance rises in direct proportion to AC frequency. As f increases, X_L increases.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Reactance depends only on frequency and inductance, not on applied voltage; voltage changes current but not X_L.", "why_tempting": "Learners may confuse reactance (impedance to AC) with current flow, which does scale with voltage via Ohm's law."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This is the inverse relationship — decreasing frequency lowers X_L for an inductor. This describes capacitive behavior, not inductive.", "why_tempting": "It's the opposite of the right answer and matches how capacitors behave (X_C = 1/2πfC), so it's an easy swap."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Voltage has no role in determining reactance; X_L is fixed by f and L for a given inductor.", "why_tempting": "Pairs with the false notion that supply voltage governs an inductor's opposition to current."}], "concept": "Inductive reactance: X_L = 2πfL (ohms), where f is in hertz and L in henries. It is the opposition an inductor presents to alternating current, arising from the back-EMF (V = L di/dt) — faster current changes (higher f) produce larger opposing EMF, hence higher reactance. Contrast with capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(2πfC), which falls as frequency rises. At DC (f=0), X_L = 0 (a short), while X_C → ∞ (open).", "misconception": "Believing applied voltage affects reactance, or confusing the inductor's directly-proportional frequency response with the capacitor's inversely-proportional one.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "'ELI the ICE man' — for inductors (L), voltage E leads current I; and remember X_L goes UP with frequency, X_C goes DOWN.", "edge_cases": "This is the ideal-inductor relationship; real inductors have parasitic resistance and self-capacitance that cause deviations near self-resonant frequency."}

{"core": "For an ideal (lossless) transformer, input power equals output power, so the primary current is simply the total power divided by the primary voltage: I = P/V = 250/120 ≈ 2.08 A, which rounds to 2.1 A.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "0.48 A would result from dividing 250 by an assumed secondary voltage near 520 V or inverting the ratio (120/250).", "why_tempting": "Looks plausible if you mistakenly compute V/P instead of P/V."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "1.4 A doesn't follow from any correct relation here; it may come from using ~180 V or an erroneous turns-ratio guess.", "why_tempting": "Falls in a believable mid-range current band for a small transformer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "3.1 A would arise from using a lower primary voltage (e.g. ~80 V) or miscalculating; with 120 V the answer is ~2.1 A.", "why_tempting": "Close to the correct figure, so it feels like a rounding alternative."}], "concept": "In an ideal transformer P_primary = P_secondary (no core, copper, or hysteresis losses). Since P = V·I on the primary side, I_primary = P / V_primary = 250 W / 120 V = 2.083 A ≈ 2.1 A. You never need the secondary voltage or turns ratio for this calculation — the power and primary voltage fully determine primary current. The turns ratio only matters when you want secondary voltage or current.", "misconception": "Thinking you need the secondary voltage or the turns ratio. 'Neglecting losses' is the key phrase: it lets you equate input and output power and solve directly from primary V and total P.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "I = P/V — power in equals power out for an ideal transformer.", "edge_cases": "Real transformers have efficiency below 100%, so actual primary current would be slightly higher than the ideal value to cover core and winding losses."}

{"core": "A transformer transforms impedance because each winding operates at a different voltage/current ratio set by the turns ratio. Since impedance is V/I, presenting different V and I on each side makes the apparent impedance differ between primary and secondary.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Core losses are an inefficiency to be minimized, not the mechanism of impedance transformation; a perfect (lossless) transformer still transforms impedance.", "why_tempting": "It sounds plausible that 'losing' part of the energy could account for a difference, but transformation is about ratio, not dissipation."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Winding resistance is parasitic and ideally negligible; it does not set the transformed impedance, which depends on the turns ratio.", "why_tempting": "The word 'resistance' near 'impedance' invites confusion, but DC winding resistance is unrelated to the reflected AC impedance."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Tight magnetic coupling is required for the transformer to work at all, but coupling quality doesn't determine the impedance ratio — the turns ratio does.", "why_tempting": "Magnetic coupling is genuinely essential to transformer action, so it feels like the right cause."}], "concept": "For an ideal transformer the voltage ratio equals the turns ratio: Vp/Vs = Np/Ns, and current ratio is inverse: Ip/Is = Ns/Np. Impedance seen at a winding is Z = V/I. The reflected (primary) impedance Zp = (Np/Ns)^2 * Zs. So the impedance transforms as the SQUARE of the turns ratio, precisely because each winding carries a different voltage and a different current. Power is conserved: Vp*Ip = Vs*Is (ideal), so raising voltage lowers current and vice-versa, which is exactly what changes the V/I ratio.", "misconception": "Believing impedance matching comes from resistance, losses, or coupling quality, rather than from the inherent voltage/current scaling produced by the turns ratio.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Z transforms as the square of the turns ratio: Zp = n^2 * Zs.", "edge_cases": "This assumes an ideal/near-ideal transformer; real units have leakage inductance, finite coupling, and losses that degrade the match, especially at RF."}

{"core": "Power in equals power out for an ideal transformer: P = 120 V × 0.5 A = 60 W. The secondary delivers the same 60 W at 12 V, so I = 60 W / 12 V = 5 A.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "2.5 A would result from dividing the primary current by the turns ratio instead of multiplying it — backwards application.", "why_tempting": "Learners know the ratio is 10:1 and apply it, but invert the relationship for current versus voltage."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "25 A is a magnitude error (off by 10×) from misplacing a decimal or multiplying 0.5 by 50.", "why_tempting": "Looks like a clean turns-ratio result and is in the right ballpark of 'much larger current.'"}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "50 A ignores the 0.5 A primary current entirely and just applies the 10:1 ratio to a presumed 5 A primary, or multiplies wrongly.", "why_tempting": "50 is the turns ratio (10) times 5, an easy spurious product."}], "concept": "For an ideal (lossless) transformer, conservation of power gives Vp·Ip = Vs·Is. The voltage and current scale inversely with the turns ratio n = Np/Ns = Vp/Vs: Vp/Vs = Is/Ip. Here n = 120/12 = 10, so the secondary current is 10× the primary current: Is = n·Ip = 10 × 0.5 = 5 A. The step-down in voltage produces a proportional step-up in current.", "misconception": "Confusing the direction of the current transformation — current goes UP on the side where voltage goes DOWN, opposite to the voltage ratio.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Step voltage down, step current up — power stays the same.", "edge_cases": "Real transformers have copper, core, and leakage losses, so the actual secondary current is slightly less than the ideal value; the 'no losses' assumption is what makes the simple power-balance exact."}

{"core": "The voltage ratio equals the turns ratio. With Ns/Np = 500/250 = 2, the secondary voltage is 120 V × 2 = 240 V (a step-up transformer).", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "620 V doesn't follow from any turns ratio; it appears to be 500+120 added together.", "why_tempting": "Mixing units by adding the secondary turn count to the voltage looks numerically plausible if you forget the ratio relationship."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "60 V is 120 ÷ 2, which would apply if the ratio were inverted (250/500). That's a step-down result, but here the secondary has more turns.", "why_tempting": "Easy to flip the ratio and divide instead of multiply when you don't track which winding is larger."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "480 V is 120 × 4, treating the ratio as 4:1 instead of 2:1.", "why_tempting": "Misreading 500/250 as a factor of 4, or doubling twice, gives this."}], "concept": "An ideal transformer obeys Vs/Vp = Ns/Np, derived from the same flux linking both windings (each turn sees the same dΦ/dt, so induced voltage scales linearly with turn count). Here Ns/Np = 500/250 = 2, so Vs = Vp × (Ns/Np) = 120 × 2 = 240 V. Note current ratio is inverse (Is/Ip = Np/Ns), conserving power Vp·Ip = Vs·Is in the ideal case.", "misconception": "Confusing step-up vs step-down — students may divide when they should multiply, or invert the turns ratio. The winding with more turns is the higher-voltage side.", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "More turns = more volts. Secondary side of the ratio goes on top: Vs = Vp × (Ns/Np).", "edge_cases": "This assumes an ideal lossless transformer; real units have copper/core losses and leakage that slightly reduce secondary voltage under load."}

{"core": "The magnetic field surrounding a straight conductor is produced by the current flowing through it, so field strength is directly proportional to that current.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Conductor diameter does not set field strength; for a given current the field at a fixed radius from the wire's center is unchanged by wire thickness.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume a 'bigger' wire produces a 'bigger' field, conflating physical size with magnetic effect."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The magnetic field arises from moving charge (current), not from potential difference; voltage alone with no current produces no surrounding magnetic field.", "why_tempting": "Voltage feels like the 'driving force,' so an inverse relation sounds plausible if you don't separate the magnetic (current) effect from the electric (voltage) effect."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Same as A — diameter is not the controlling variable; the field depends on current and distance, not on the wire's diameter.", "why_tempting": "Sounds technical and 'inverse proportionality' appears in many physics formulas, baiting an over-clever guess."}], "concept": "For an infinitely long straight conductor, Ampère's law gives B = μ0·I / (2πr): the flux density B at radial distance r is directly proportional to the current I and inversely proportional to distance r from the conductor's axis. Note that B depends on r (distance from center), not on the conductor's diameter, and not on voltage at all — magnetism is a current phenomenon. Doubling the current doubles the field; the wire's thickness and the voltage on it do not enter the relationship.", "misconception": "Confusing the magnetic field (set by current) with electric-field/voltage effects, or assuming the conductor's physical dimensions govern field strength.", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "Magnetism follows current — 'no current, no magnet.'", "edge_cases": "B does fall off with distance r from the wire, so 'strength of the field' is meaningful only at a stated distance; near a thick conductor the surface field is lower because the surface sits farther from the axis, but the controlling proportionality is still current."}

{"core": "An inductor's voltage is V = L(di/dt), so the induced voltage is maximum exactly when the rate of change of current is greatest.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Least rate of change means di/dt approaches zero, so V = L(di/dt) is at its minimum, not maximum.", "why_tempting": "It pairs 'maximum voltage' with a current condition, and learners who confuse current magnitude with rate of change may pick it."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A non-changing magnetic field means di/dt = 0, producing zero induced voltage — the exact opposite of maximum.", "why_tempting": "Faraday's law links flux to EMF, so 'magnetic field' sounds relevant, but it must be a *changing* field to induce voltage."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Steady DC has di/dt = 0, so once current is constant no voltage is induced across the inductor's inductance.", "why_tempting": "DC is associated with steady current flow, and learners may wrongly assume steady current produces a steady induced voltage."}], "concept": "Inductor voltage follows V = L(di/dt). The induced EMF opposes the *change* in current (Lenz's law), so it depends on the slope of the current waveform, not its instantaneous amplitude. For a sinusoidal current i = I·sin(ωt), di/dt = ωI·cos(ωt), which peaks at the zero-crossings of the current — meaning the inductor voltage leads the current by 90°. Maximum voltage therefore coincides with maximum di/dt, which occurs where the current is changing fastest (and is momentarily zero in a sine wave).", "misconception": "Confusing maximum current magnitude with maximum rate of change of current. Induced voltage tracks the slope (di/dt), not the value, of the current.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "ELI the ICE man: in an inductor (L), voltage E leads current I — voltage peaks when current is changing fastest.", "edge_cases": "This assumes ideal inductance; in a real coil, series resistance adds an in-phase IR voltage term that does not depend on di/dt."}

{"core": "In an ideal lossless transformer, input power equals output power. So 10 W on the primary means 10 W on the secondary, and I = P/V = 10 W / 5 V = 2 A.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "1 A at 5 V is only 5 W, not the 10 W delivered.", "why_tempting": "Looks right if you mistakenly equate primary voltage or current to the secondary value of 5."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "5 A is the secondary voltage value misused as current; 5 A × 5 V = 25 W, far above input.", "why_tempting": "The number 5 is staring at you, so it's easy to grab it as the answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "0.5 A gives only 2.5 W; this comes from dividing 5/10 instead of 10/5.", "why_tempting": "Inverting the P=VI rearrangement (V/P instead of P/V) yields this."}], "concept": "An ideal transformer conserves power: P_pri = P_sec (losses neglected). The turns ratio sets the voltage and current relationship — V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p and I_p/I_s = N_s/N_p — but you don't need the ratio here. Just apply P = V·I on the secondary: I_s = P/V_s = 10/5 = 2 A. The transformer steps voltage and current in opposite directions while keeping their product (power) constant.", "misconception": "Thinking you need the turns ratio or primary voltage to find secondary current — when conservation of power lets you solve it directly from secondary voltage alone.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Power in = power out; then I = P/V.", "edge_cases": "Only valid because losses are neglected; a real transformer has core and copper losses, so secondary power (and thus current at a given voltage) would be slightly less."}

{"core": "In a transformer, current ratio is the inverse of the turns ratio. With a 1:5 step-up, the secondary current is 1/5 of the primary, so the primary current = 5 × 50 mA = 250 mA.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "This multiplies by 50 instead of 5, an order-of-magnitude error.", "why_tempting": "Looks like 50 mA scaled by the ratio if you confuse the load current value with the multiplier."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "This divides the secondary current by 5, which is what you'd do for voltage (step-down side) or current in a step-down transformer — wrong direction for current here.", "why_tempting": "Applying the turns ratio directly (50/5) feels natural but ignores that current scales inversely to turns."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "50 mA divided by 200; no correct relationship gives this, it just mixes scaling errors.", "why_tempting": "Small-number bias and confusing milliamp/amp conversions."}], "concept": "Conservation of power (100% efficiency) means P_pri = P_sec, so V_p·I_p = V_s·I_s. Voltage scales with turns: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p = 5. Therefore current scales inversely: I_p/I_s = N_s/N_p = 5, giving I_p = 5 × I_s = 5 × 50 mA = 250 mA. The step-up that boosts voltage by 5× must draw 5× the current on the primary to keep VA constant.", "misconception": "Assuming current follows the turns ratio the same way voltage does. Voltage goes up with more secondary turns; current goes down — the two are inverse.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Volts up, amps down: a transformer is a power see-saw — VA in equals VA out.", "edge_cases": "This inverse-current relationship assumes ideal 100% efficiency; real transformers have core and copper losses so primary current is slightly higher."}

{"core": "Inductive coupling depends on shared magnetic flux. Two wires that are close and parallel maximize the mutual flux linkage between them, giving maximum coupling.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "At right angles the magnetic field lines of one wire run essentially perpendicular to the other conductor, so net flux linkage and induced EMF drop toward zero even when close.", "why_tempting": "\"Close\" sounds good, but geometry (orientation) matters as much as distance, and right angles actually minimize coupling."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Parallel orientation favors coupling, but increasing separation rapidly weakens the magnetic field at the second wire (field falls off with distance), reducing mutual inductance.", "why_tempting": "Parallel is the right orientation, so it looks correct until you account for distance."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "This is the worst case: both separated (weak field) and at right angles (poor flux linkage), giving minimum coupling.", "why_tempting": "Easy to confuse if you don't remember which orientation maximizes linkage."}], "concept": "Mutual inductance M sets the coupling: induced voltage = M·di/dt. M scales with the fraction of one conductor's magnetic flux that links the other. Flux density falls off with distance, so closeness raises M. Orientation governs the dot product of the field with the second conductor's loop area: parallel wires let the field lines thread fully (maximum linkage), while perpendicular wires present near-zero linkage. Hence maximum coupling = close AND parallel; minimum = separated AND perpendicular. This is the same principle exploited in transformers (tight, aligned windings) and avoided in cabling by spacing and crossing wires at right angles.", "misconception": "Thinking only distance matters, or only orientation matters — both must be optimized. Coupling needs close spacing for strong field AND parallel alignment for full flux linkage.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Close + parallel = maximum coupling; far + crossed = minimum (cross wires to cut crosstalk).", "edge_cases": "Crossing signal wires at right angles is a real-world technique to reduce unwanted inductive crosstalk; capacitive coupling follows similar close-spacing logic but depends on conductor area and dielectric rather than flux."}

{"core": "Permanent magnets are made from ferromagnetic materials that retain magnetization after the magnetizing field is removed; steel (an iron alloy) is ferromagnetic and exhibits high remanence and coercivity, so it holds a permanent magnetic field.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Aluminum is paramagnetic — only very weakly attracted to a field and unable to retain any usable magnetization.", "why_tempting": "Aluminum is a common, familiar metal and learners may assume any shiny metal can be magnetized."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Brass is a copper-zinc alloy and is essentially non-magnetic; it cannot be permanently magnetized.", "why_tempting": "It is a hard, solid-looking metal, which superficially resembles a magnetic material."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Copper is diamagnetic — it actually opposes an applied field weakly and retains no magnetization.", "why_tempting": "Copper is central to electromagnetics (windings, coils), so its strong association with electricity and magnetism misleads learners."}], "concept": "Ferromagnetism requires unpaired electron spins that align into domains and stay aligned after the external H field is removed. The key parameters are remanence (residual flux density Br) and coercivity (Hc, the reverse field needed to demagnetize). 'Hard' magnetic materials like hardened steel and alloys (alnico, etc.) have high Hc and a wide B-H hysteresis loop, making them suitable for permanent magnets. Iron, nickel, and cobalt and their alloys are the practical ferromagnets; only steel (iron-based) appears in this list. Note copper's role in electromagnetism is as a conductor carrying current to create a field — it is not itself magnetic.", "misconception": "That 'used in electrical work' or 'metallic' implies magnetic. Conductivity (copper, aluminum) is unrelated to ferromagnetism; only iron-group metals and their alloys retain magnetization.", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "Magnets love Iron, Nickel, Cobalt — 'INC.' Steel is iron-based, so it's in the club.", "edge_cases": "Soft iron is magnetic but makes a poor permanent magnet (low coercivity); high-coercivity hardened steel or alloy magnets are needed to stay magnetized."}

{"core": "Real transformers dissipate energy in the core and windings; warm iron laminations are direct evidence of core losses (hysteresis and eddy currents), confirming the energy transfer is less than 100% efficient.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Acoustic noise (magnetostriction hum) is largely a mechanical/vibration effect and is not a reliable measure of energy-transfer inefficiency; a silent transformer can still have substantial losses.", "why_tempting": "Noise feels like 'wasted' output and many transformers do audibly hum, so it seems like a loss indicator."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A large secondary current reflects the load demand and turns ratio, not inefficiency; an ideal lossless transformer would also deliver large secondary current into a low-impedance load.", "why_tempting": "High current sounds like strain or loss, but it is just normal power transfer scaled by the turns ratio."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Primary voltage is set by the source; a high applied voltage says nothing about how well energy crosses to the secondary.", "why_tempting": "Learners may associate 'high voltage' with stress or breakdown, mistaking it for a loss symptom."}], "concept": "Transformer losses split into copper losses (I\\u00b2R in the windings, load-dependent) and core (iron) losses. Core losses have two parts: hysteresis loss, proportional to the area of the B-H loop and frequency, and eddy-current loss, induced circulating currents in the core that scale roughly with (frequency\\u00d7B\\u00d7thickness)\\u00b2. Both convert magnetic energy into heat in the iron \\u2014 hence warm laminations. Efficiency \\u03b7 = P_out/P_in = P_out/(P_out + P_copper + P_core) < 1. Laminating the core (thin insulated sheets) limits eddy-current paths to reduce, but never eliminate, these losses, so some heating always remains.", "misconception": "Confusing normal operating quantities (load current, source voltage, audible hum) with actual energy dissipation. Only heat generation in the core/windings is unambiguous proof that input energy was lost rather than transferred.", "link": "section 5", "mnemonic": "Heat = lost watts. If the iron is warm, energy went into the core instead of the load.", "edge_cases": "Laminating the core suppresses eddy currents (the reason cores are thin stacked sheets rather than solid iron); at higher frequencies powdered-iron or ferrite cores are used to keep core losses manageable."}

{"core": "Resonance occurs when inductive reactance equals capacitive reactance (X_L = X_C), so they cancel and the circuit appears purely resistive.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "At resonance X_L is cancelled by X_C, leaving net reactance zero — inductive reactance is not the sole opposition.", "why_tempting": "Inductors are central to many resonant circuits, so a learner may overweight X_L."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Real circuits always have some resistance; resonance is defined by reactance cancellation, not by absence of resistance.", "why_tempting": "The 'ideal' resonant tank with infinite Q feels like it has no resistance."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Resistance equalling reactance describes a 45° phase / half-power condition, not resonance.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a balanced 'equality' condition similar to the correct answer's phrasing."}], "concept": "Inductive reactance X_L = 2πfL rises with frequency; capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(2πfC) falls with frequency. Setting X_L = X_C gives the resonant frequency f = 1/(2π√(LC)). At that point the two reactances are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign, so they sum to zero net reactance — the impedance is minimum (series) or maximum (parallel) and purely resistive, with voltage and current in phase.", "misconception": "Confusing resonance (reactance cancellation) with the condition where resistance equals reactance, or assuming resonance requires zero resistance.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Resonance = Reactances Reconcile: X_L = X_C.", "edge_cases": "Series and parallel resonant circuits behave oppositely (minimum vs maximum impedance), but both share the same X_L = X_C condition."}

{"core": "At resonance a parallel LC (tank) circuit presents its maximum, purely resistive impedance — ideally very high — because the inductive and capacitive branch currents cancel, leaving only the small loss current.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Low impedance at resonance describes a SERIES tuned circuit, not a parallel one.", "why_tempting": "Series and parallel resonance are easily confused, and 'resonance' loosely suggests minimum impedance."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "At resonance the net reactance is zero (XL and XC cancel), so the impedance can't equal a reactance that has vanished; the impedance is resistive.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible because reactance dominates a tuned circuit off-resonance, and the phrasing seems technical."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "While the impedance is resistive at resonance, in a parallel tank it equals the dynamic (resonant) resistance R_dyn = L/(C·R), which is very LARGE, not the small series loss resistance R. The exam answer they want is the qualitative 'high impedance.'", "why_tempting": "It correctly notes the impedance is resistive, but mislabels which resistance and misses the key point that it's high."}], "concept": "In a parallel LC circuit each branch carries current 180° out of phase: I_L lags, I_C leads. At f0 = 1/(2π√(LC)), |XL| = |XC| so the two reactive currents are equal and cancel in the supply line. The only line current left supplies the resistive losses, so impedance peaks. The dynamic resistance is R_dyn = L/(C·R) = Q·X0, which for a high-Q tank is very large. Note the contrast with a series circuit, where the same cancellation makes voltages (not currents) cancel, giving minimum impedance ≈ R.", "misconception": "Treating series and parallel resonance as identical, or assuming 'resonance' always means low/minimum impedance.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Parallel = Peak (high Z); Series = Small (low Z).", "edge_cases": "An ideal lossless tank has infinite impedance at resonance; real circuits are limited by component resistance, giving a finite but high R_dyn proportional to Q."}

{"core": "The range of frequencies over which a tuned circuit responds significantly—conventionally the span between the half-power (−3 dB) points—is called the bandwidth.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Shape factor describes the steepness of the skirts (ratio of the −60 dB width to the −6 dB width), not the resonant range itself.", "why_tempting": "It is a real selectivity metric that sounds like it could describe a frequency span."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The response curve is the entire plot of amplitude versus frequency, not the specific range of significant response.", "why_tempting": "The bandwidth is read off the response curve, so the terms are easily conflated."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Quality factor (Q) is a dimensionless ratio of stored to dissipated energy; it determines bandwidth but is not itself a frequency range.", "why_tempting": "Q and bandwidth are inversely related (BW = f₀/Q), so they're tightly linked in memory."}], "concept": "For a resonant circuit, bandwidth is defined between the two frequencies where output power falls to half the peak (−3 dB, or 0.707 of peak voltage/current). It relates to resonant frequency and Q by BW = f₀ / Q. A high-Q circuit has a narrow bandwidth (sharp selectivity); a low-Q circuit has a wide bandwidth. Q itself = X/R (reactance of L or C at resonance over the effective series resistance), so adding loss broadens the response.", "misconception": "Confusing the descriptors of a tuned circuit: Q sets how sharp the response is, the response curve is the whole picture, shape factor measures skirt steepness, but only bandwidth names the range of effective response.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "BW = f₀/Q — high Q, narrow band.", "edge_cases": "The −3 dB convention is standard, but some contexts define bandwidth at other points (e.g. −6 dB in filter specs); the exam intends the half-power definition."}

{"core": "A tuned (resonant) circuit requires an inductor (L) and a capacitor (C); their opposing reactances cancel at the resonant frequency, producing a sharp impedance peak or null.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A resistor dissipates energy and a transistor is an active device — neither stores reactive energy, so they cannot resonate.", "why_tempting": "These are common circuit parts and a transistor is associated with RF amplifiers/oscillators, but the resonance itself comes from the LC tank, not the transistor."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A resistor has no reactance to cancel the capacitor's reactance, so no resonance occurs — only an RC time constant results.", "why_tempting": "It correctly includes a capacitor, and RC circuits do shape frequency response (filtering), so it looks half right."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Diodes and transistors are non-resonant semiconductor devices; they store no oscillating energy and cannot form a tuned circuit.", "why_tempting": "Both appear in RF and oscillator stages, tempting learners who associate any RF block with tuning."}], "concept": "Resonance occurs when inductive reactance equals capacitive reactance: X_L = X_C, i.e. 2πfL = 1/(2πfC). Solving gives f = 1/(2π√(LC)). The inductor stores energy in a magnetic field, the capacitor in an electric field, and they exchange energy back and forth each cycle — a 'tank' circuit. Series LC gives minimum impedance at resonance; parallel LC gives maximum impedance. Resistance only adds loss, lowering Q and broadening the response.", "misconception": "Believing that any pair of common components can 'tune' a circuit, or confusing energy-dissipating/active devices (R, diode, transistor) with the energy-storing reactive pair (L and C) that actually produces resonance.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "LC = resonance: the two reactive storage elements.", "edge_cases": "Real circuits always include some resistance (wire/ESR), which sets the Q and bandwidth, but only L and C are required to establish the resonant frequency itself."}

{"core": "At resonance in a parallel LC circuit, the inductive and capacitive susceptances cancel, leaving (ideally) only the high resistive impedance of a tank circuit — so impedance peaks at the resonant frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "\"Reactive frequency\" is not a defined term; reactance varies continuously with frequency rather than occurring at one special point.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible because the circuit's behaviour is governed by reactance (XL and XC)."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "\"Maximum frequency\" describes a frequency limit, not the condition of peak impedance; impedance maximum has nothing to do with the highest frequency applied.", "why_tempting": "The phrase pairs the words 'maximum' and 'frequency,' and the question mentions impedance being highest, inviting a loose word-match."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "\"Inductive frequency\" is not a standard term; below resonance the parallel tank looks inductive and above it looks capacitive, so no single frequency is uniquely 'inductive.'", "why_tempting": "A coil is involved, so 'inductive' feels related to the circuit's makeup."}], "concept": "For a parallel LC tank, the resonant frequency is f = 1/(2\\u03c0\\u221a(LC)). At f, XL = XC so the inductive and capacitive branch currents are equal and oppositely phased, circulating between L and C. The net line current drops to a minimum (only that needed to feed losses), so the impedance seen by the source is maximum — Zmax \\u2248 L/(C\\u00b7R) = Q\\u00b7X for a coil with series resistance R. Contrast this with a SERIES LC circuit, where the same resonance gives minimum impedance. The parallel-resonant impedance peak is the basis for tuned tank circuits used as oscillator and amplifier loads.", "misconception": "Confusing parallel resonance (impedance maximum, current minimum) with series resonance (impedance minimum, current maximum) — both occur at f = 1/(2\\u03c0\\u221aLC) but the impedance behaviour is opposite.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Parallel = Peak impedance; Series = Small impedance.", "edge_cases": "The impedance is only infinite for an ideal lossless tank; real coil resistance limits it to a finite high value set by the circuit Q."}

{"core": "At parallel resonance the inductive and capacitive branch currents are equal and opposite, nearly cancelling the net line current, so the tank presents maximum (high) impedance to the source.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Low impedance describes the series-resonant case, not parallel; the parallel tank is maximum impedance at f0.", "why_tempting": "Learners conflate series and parallel resonance — series resonance does dip to minimum impedance, so the wrong rule is easy to apply."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Mutual inductance is a coupling property between two windings and is irrelevant to a single L-C tank's resonant impedance.", "why_tempting": "The word 'inductance' appears, and 'low' pairs with the series-resonance intuition, making it a plausible-sounding answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Mutual inductance plays no role in the impedance of a parallel-resonant tank; the answer concerns impedance, not coupling.", "why_tempting": "'High' sounds right because parallel resonance is a 'high' something — but it's high impedance, not high mutual inductance."}], "concept": "At resonance XL = XC, so f0 = 1/(2π√(LC)). In the parallel (tank) configuration the L and C branches each draw current but those currents are ~180° out of phase, so they circulate within the tank and the net current drawn from the source falls to a minimum. By Z = V/I, minimum line current means maximum impedance. Ideally (lossless) Z→∞; in practice Q and the coil's series resistance limit it, with dynamic resistance Rp ≈ L/(C·R) for a coil of resistance R. Compare series resonance, where XL and XC cancel as voltage drops, leaving only R and thus minimum impedance / maximum current.", "misconception": "Assuming both resonance types behave the same, or swapping the series (low-Z) and parallel (high-Z) impedance results.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Parallel = Peak impedance; Series = Small impedance.", "edge_cases": "Only ideal tanks reach truly infinite impedance; real components with finite Q peak at the dynamic resistance L/(CR), and the exact maximum-impedance frequency shifts slightly from f0 with losses."}

{"core": "At resonance in a series RLC circuit, the inductive and capacitive reactances are equal and cancel, leaving only the resistance — so impedance is at its minimum (low) and current is maximum.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Mutual inductance is a property of coupled coils, not a defining condition of a single series resonant circuit; it has nothing to do with the impedance behaviour at resonance.", "why_tempting": "'Mutual inductance' sounds like it belongs to a resonant LC discussion and may seem like a sophisticated answer."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Same category error as A — mutual inductance is irrelevant to whether a series circuit is at resonance, and 'high' does not describe resonant behaviour.", "why_tempting": "Resonance involves an inductor, so a learner may grab any inductance-related term."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "High impedance describes a *parallel* resonant circuit at resonance, not a series one. In series resonance, X_L and X_C cancel so Z drops to just R.", "why_tempting": "Series and parallel resonance are easily swapped; parallel resonance does present high impedance, so the answer is a correct fact about the wrong configuration."}], "concept": "Series resonance occurs when X_L = X_C, i.e. 2*pi*f*L = 1/(2*pi*f*C), giving f = 1/(2*pi*sqrt(LC)). The total impedance is Z = sqrt(R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2); when the reactances cancel, the term (X_L - X_C) goes to zero and Z = R, its minimum value. Minimum impedance means maximum current for a given source voltage. Contrast with a parallel (tank) circuit, where the same resonance condition produces maximum impedance and minimum line current.", "misconception": "Confusing series resonance (minimum Z, maximum current) with parallel resonance (maximum Z, minimum current).", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Series = Small impedance; Parallel = Plenty of impedance.", "edge_cases": "The impedance at series resonance is purely resistive (the residual R), not literally zero — circuit Q determines how sharp the dip and how high the current is."}

{"core": "Resonant frequency depends only on inductance L and capacitance C (f = 1/(2π√(LC))).
