Solid vs foam dielectric does not change the choke's impedance.", "why_tempting": "Learners assume any construction difference must affect the electrical performance of the choke, so 'more impedance' sounds like the engineering payoff."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The choke is in the common-mode path, not the differential signal path; the dielectric does not add impedance to the desired signal either way. This conflates the two modes.", "why_tempting": "It sounds technical and plausible if you wrongly think dielectric choice trades off signal loss."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Foam dielectric cable is generally MORE flexible than solid, so this is backwards — and flexibility isn't the reason for the preference anyway.", "why_tempting": "Handling ease is a real practical concern when winding tight coils, so it feels like a sensible answer."}], "concept": "A 1:1 current balun / common-mode choke uses ferrite to present high impedance to currents flowing in common mode on the outside of the coax shield, while leaving the differential (inner) transmission-line mode unaffected. The mechanical issue here is purely physical: foam (cellular) dielectric is compressible and the centre conductor can migrate under the stress of tight bends and clamping on a toroid, eventually contacting the shield. Solid polyethylene dielectric maintains consistent conductor spacing and concentricity under bending, preserving the coax's characteristic impedance and preventing internal shorts.", "misconception": "That the dielectric choice affects the choke's RF/electrical performance, when the real difference is mechanical robustness of the cable under tight winding.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Solid dielectric = solid hold on the centre conductor.", "edge_cases": "For high-power or low-loss feedline runs foam has lower loss, but for the short length wound on a choke the deciding factor is mechanical stability, not loss."}

{"core": "Coaxial cable's self-shielding, flexible, single-conductor-to-rig construction lets it be routed almost anywhere — buried, run along metal, bent around corners, fed unbalanced equipment directly — making it the most practical choice across diverse installations.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Coax generally has MORE loss than open-wire/ladder line, especially at VHF/UHF; the dielectric and small conductors dissipate more than air-spaced parallel line.", "why_tempting": "Learners equate 'most popular' with 'best electrically,' assuming low loss drove its adoption."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Open-wire line typically handles higher power for a given size because air dielectric and wider spacing raise the breakdown voltage; coax dielectric limits voltage handling.", "why_tempting": "The shielded, robust look of coax suggests superior power capacity."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Good low-loss coax is often more expensive per metre than simple parallel-wire line you can even build yourself.", "why_tempting": "Coax is so common it feels like the cheap commodity option."}], "concept": "Transmission lines trade off loss, balance, shielding, and convenience. Open-wire (ladder) line has lower loss and higher power handling because of its air dielectric and wide conductor spacing, but it is balanced, radiates if run near metal, can't be bent sharply or buried, and needs a balun/tuner to feed unbalanced gear. Coax is an unbalanced, shielded line: the outer conductor confines the field, so external metal and routing don't disturb the impedance or cause radiation. That field confinement is precisely what makes it 'usable in a wide variety of settings' — the dominant practical reason, even though it sacrifices some loss and power-handling performance.", "misconception": "That the most widely used component must be the best on a single electrical metric (lowest loss or highest power). Popularity here is driven by versatility and ease of installation, not peak performance.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Coax = Convenient, not Champion (of loss).", "edge_cases": "At HF with high SWR, open-wire line can outperform coax dramatically; coax's loss advantage claim is essentially never true versus quality ladder line."}

{"core": "RG-213 is a thick (about 10 mm) 50-ohm coax used at HF, and the standard mating connector for HF gear is the UHF series, of which the PL-259 is the male plug.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "BNC is a small bayonet connector rated for thin coax (RG-58/RG-174) and typically used at lower power and on test/VHF-UHF handheld gear, not bulky RG-213 on an HF rig.", "why_tempting": "BNC is a very common ham connector seen on radios and meters, so it feels like a default answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "F-type is a 75-ohm connector for TV/satellite and broadband coax (RG-6/RG-59); it is not used on 50-ohm amateur HF feedlines.", "why_tempting": "It is the connector most people physically recognize from home cable TV, so it seems familiar."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "SMA is a tiny threaded subminiature connector for microwave/handheld antennas; it cannot accept RG-213 and is unsuited to HF transmit power on large coax.", "why_tempting": "SMA is increasingly common on modern handhelds and SDR dongles, making it look like a current standard."}], "concept": "Connector choice follows cable size, impedance, frequency, and power. RG-213 is a 50-ohm, low-loss, ~10 mm coax intended for HF (and up to lower VHF). The UHF series (SO-239 socket / PL-259 plug) is the long-standing 50-ohm HF interface. Despite the name 'UHF', it is not a true constant-impedance connector and is mediocre above ~300 MHz, but at HF its impedance discontinuity is negligible and it handles high power on large coax — which is exactly why it pairs with RG-213 on HF transceivers. Adapters/reducers (UG-176) let the PL-259 grip the larger RG-213 dielectric.", "misconception": "Assuming the 'UHF' name means PL-259 is a UHF-band connector; in fact it is the classic HF connector and is poor at true UHF.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "PL-259 = 'Plug for the Line' on HF rigs; SO-239 is the Socket it screws into."}

{"core": "Modern handhelds use the small, threaded SMA connector because its compact size suits the limited space on a portable radio while still performing well at VHF/UHF frequencies.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "RCA connectors are unshielded, low-frequency audio/video connectors with poor performance at RF and no impedance control.", "why_tempting": "They are familiar consumer push-on connectors, so a learner may associate them with general signal connections."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The PL-259 (UHF connector) is bulky and is used on base/mobile coax runs, not on the tiny antenna port of a handheld.", "why_tempting": "It is the most iconic amateur RF connector, so it feels like the default answer for any antenna connection."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "F-type connectors are 75-ohm cable-TV/satellite connectors, not used on 50-ohm amateur handhelds.", "why_tempting": "It's a small threaded coax connector, superficially resembling the SMA in size and threading."}], "concept": "Connector choice trades off size, frequency range, and durability. SMA (SubMiniature version A) is a 50-ohm threaded connector rated well into the GHz range, making it ideal for the cramped top of a VHF/UHF HT. Both male-pin (standard SMA) and reverse-polarity variants exist; HTs commonly use SMA-female on the radio with SMA-male on the antenna (or the reverse on some brands).", "misconception": "Assuming the popular PL-259 is universal — in reality it is too large and electrically mediocre at higher frequencies, so handhelds favour the miniature SMA.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "SMA = Small, Modern Antenna connector for handhelds.", "edge_cases": "Watch the polarity: standard SMA and reverse-polarity SMA (RP-SMA) are not interchangeable, and brands differ on which gender lives on the radio."}

{"core": "The Type N connector incorporates a gasket/weatherproofing design and a constant 50-ohm geometry that makes it the moisture-resistant choice among these common RF connectors, suitable for outdoor antenna feedpoints.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The UHF (PL-259/SO-239) connector has no weather seal and a non-constant impedance; it is not rated for moisture resistance and needs external waterproofing outdoors.", "why_tempting": "It is the most common HF connector in amateur stations, so learners assume the 'standard' connector must also be the rugged outdoor one."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The BNC bayonet connector is a quick-disconnect for low-power/test use; its bayonet shell is not designed to keep water out.", "why_tempting": "Its locking bayonet feels secure, which can be mistaken for a weatherproof seal."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The SMA is a small precision microwave connector intended for clean indoor/board-level connections, not weatherproofed outdoor service.", "why_tempting": "Being a precision threaded connector, it seems robust and high-quality, which gets confused with water resistance."}], "concept": "Connector selection for RF depends on impedance match, frequency range, power handling, and environmental sealing. The Type N was specifically engineered (by Paul Neill) with an internal gasket and threaded coupling to exclude moisture while maintaining a controlled 50-ohm characteristic impedance up to several GHz. Even so, in practice any outdoor coax junction is still over-wrapped with self-amalgamating tape; the N's design simply makes it the inherently weather-resistant standard.", "misconception": "That the most familiar connector (UHF/PL-259) is also the best for outdoor/moisture environments — popularity is not the same as environmental rating.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "N = Neill = 'Needs No worry in weather' (the weatherproof one).", "edge_cases": "Type N comes in 50-ohm and 75-ohm variants that are mechanically similar but not interchangeable; mating them can damage the pin."}

{"core": "The BNC connector is a small bayonet-style coaxial connector widely used on low-power VHF/UHF transceivers, handheld radios, and test equipment because it's compact and offers quick connect/disconnect with a 90-degree twist.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "RCA (phono) connectors are unshielded audio/video connectors, not designed for controlled-impedance RF use, and aren't standard on RF test gear.", "why_tempting": "They are coaxial in shape and ubiquitous on consumer audio, so a learner may assume they handle RF too."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The Type N is a precision, weatherproof, threaded connector rated for higher power and microwave frequencies — used on antennas and high-power feedlines, not typically on low-power bench instruments.", "why_tempting": "N connectors do appear on quality RF equipment and labs, so they seem like a 'test instrument' answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The UHF connector (PL-259/SO-239) is a larger threaded connector common on HF and mobile transceivers, but it is not constant-impedance and is bulkier than what's used on low-power/test gear.", "why_tempting": "Its name 'UHF' and prevalence on ham rigs makes it sound like the general-purpose RF choice."}], "concept": "RF connectors are chosen by frequency range, power handling, impedance consistency, and convenience. BNC: bayonet lock, 50- or 75-ohm versions, good to ~4 GHz, low power — ideal for oscilloscopes, signal generators, and HTs. N: threaded, 50-ohm, weatherproof, high power, microwave. UHF/PL-259: threaded, not true constant-impedance, fine for HF/VHF moderate power. RCA: no defined characteristic impedance, audio-grade only. The 'quick bayonet + low power + test bench' combination uniquely points to BNC.", "misconception": "Assuming a connector labelled 'UHF' is the standard for all RF, or that any coaxial-looking connector (RCA) suffices for RF signals.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "BNC = Bench-N-Connect: quick twist for test gear.", "edge_cases": "BNC comes in both 50-ohm (RF/radio) and 75-ohm (video) variants — they look nearly identical but mate poorly; use the 50-ohm type for radio work."}

{"core": "Loose or corroded connectors develop high contact resistance at the metal-to-metal junction, which dissipates RF power as heat and degrades signal both transmitting and receiving. Cleaning and tightening keeps that contact resistance low.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Dielectric loss is a property of the insulating material between conductors, not of the mechanical contact between mating connectors; cleaning a contact surface does not change the dielectric.", "why_tempting": "It uses a real loss mechanism term that sounds technical and plausibly connector-related."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Lightning protection depends on a proper ground system, bonding, and surge arrestors — not on the cleanliness of RF signal connectors.", "why_tempting": "Antenna systems do need lightning protection, so it feels like good practice gets lumped together."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Static buildup is bled off by DC grounding paths (e.g., a static drain choke or grounded mast), not by maintaining clean coax connectors.", "why_tempting": "Static on antennas is a genuine concern, so the option pattern-matches to outdoor maintenance."}], "concept": "A connector junction is effectively a small series resistance R in the feedline. Oxidation, moisture, and looseness raise that R. Any series resistance dissipates power P = I^2*R, so it both wastes transmit power and adds loss/noise on receive. At RF the problem compounds: oxide films and intermittent contact can rectify and cause intermodulation, and a high-resistance, mismatched junction reflects power, worsening SWR. Keeping contact resistance near zero preserves the intended low-loss, matched transmission line.", "misconception": "That a connector's job is anything beyond providing a low-resistance, continuous conductive path — students conflate it with dielectric, grounding, or lightning functions.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Clean contact = low contact resistance = low loss.", "edge_cases": "Corroded outdoor connectors can also act as nonlinear (rectifying) junctions, generating intermod and noise — another reason maintenance matters beyond simple resistive loss."}

{"core": "A solid (continuous) outer conductor, as found in hardline/semi-rigid coax, provides 100% coverage with no gaps, giving the best electromagnetic shielding of any outer-conductor construction.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A single braid has gaps in the weave (typically 70–95% coverage), so RF can leak in or out, especially at higher frequencies.", "why_tempting": "It's the most common construction in everyday coax like RG-58, so it feels like the standard answer."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Double braid improves coverage and reduces leakage versus single braid, but the woven layers still leave microscopic gaps and never reach the continuous coverage of a solid conductor.", "why_tempting": "'More is better' reasoning makes double braid sound like the maximum, and it is indeed better than single braid or foil alone."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Foil gives near-100% coverage but is thin, has higher resistance, and the seam plus poor low-frequency performance make it inferior to a solid tube; foil is usually paired with a braid in practice.", "why_tempting": "Foil offers full coverage so it superficially seems like complete shielding."}], "concept": "Shielding effectiveness depends on coverage (no apertures for fields to couple through) and on the skin-effect path the shield current can flow in. Braids are woven, so they always have small diamond-shaped openings whose size grows the looser the weave; leakage rises with frequency as the aperture becomes a larger fraction of a wavelength. A solid outer conductor (hardline/semi-rigid) is a continuous cylinder — zero apertures, lowest transfer impedance, and the full conductor thickness available for skin-effect current — so it shields best. Foil approaches full coverage but is too thin and high-resistance to match a solid tube.", "misconception": "Assuming the heaviest braid (double) is automatically the best shield, or that foil's full coverage beats a solid conductor — overlooking that a continuous solid wall has no apertures at all.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "Solid = no holes = no leaks.", "edge_cases": "Solid-shield hardline shields best but is rigid and expensive; for flexible runs a foil-plus-double-braid combination is the practical compromise."}

{"core": "Coaxial cable is fully sealed and self-shielded, so accumulated ice, water, and snow on its outer jacket have minimal effect on its characteristic impedance and loss, making it the best choice in icing-prone locations.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Open-wire line relies on the air dielectric and precise conductor spacing between two parallel wires; ice and water bridging the gap change the effective dielectric constant, shifting impedance and dramatically increasing loss.", "why_tempting": "Open-wire line is normally the lowest-loss line in dry conditions, so a learner may pick it forgetting that exposed conductors are exactly what ice ruins."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Window (ladder) line is balanced line with an exposed dielectric web; ice and water collecting on and between the conductors detune it and raise losses, just like open-wire.", "why_tempting": "The 'windows' look like they shed water, but the exposed conductors are still affected by ice accumulation."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Twin-lead has a continuous plastic web between conductors; a film of ice or water on that surface acts as an additional lossy dielectric and changes impedance, degrading performance.", "why_tempting": "It is jacketed in plastic, so it seems weatherproof, but the dielectric is part of the exposed surface and ice sits right where the field is strongest."}], "concept": "In balanced/open lines a large fraction of the electromagnetic field exists in the dielectric medium between the two conductors. Adding water (εr ≈ 80) or ice on or between those conductors alters the effective permittivity, which changes Z0 = √(L/C) (C rises) and introduces dielectric loss. In coax the field is wholly contained inside the outer braid/shield between center conductor and shield, so anything on the outside of the jacket is outside the field region and has essentially no electrical effect. The trade-off is that coax has higher baseline loss when dry, but it is far more weather-stable.", "misconception": "That the 'lowest-loss-when-dry' line is always best. Environmental robustness matters: balanced lines need clean spacing and a known dielectric, both of which ice destroys, whereas coax keeps its fields internal and stays predictable.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Fields inside = weather can't bite. Coax for ice.", "edge_cases": "This is about reliability under ice, not efficiency. In dry installations open-wire/ladder line can have substantially lower loss, especially at HF with high SWR."}

{"core": "Foam (cellular) dielectric is mostly air, which is a near-ideal low-loss dielectric. Replacing solid polyethylene with foam reduces dielectric absorption losses, so foam coax has lower attenuation per unit length than an equivalent solid-dielectric cable.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Foam is softer and more easily crushed or deformed than solid polyethylene, so its mechanical stability is actually worse, not better.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume any 'improved' cable is better in every respect, including ruggedness."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Foam dielectric has a lower dielectric strength and can deform under heat/pressure, so solid-dielectric cable generally handles higher voltage and power, not less.", "why_tempting": "Lower loss feels like it should mean handling more power, but power handling is limited by dielectric breakdown and heat, where solid wins."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Foam raises the velocity factor (toward that of air, ~0.8–0.88) compared with solid PE (~0.66) because the effective dielectric constant is lower; the question's claim of a lower VF is backwards.", "why_tempting": "It's a real, related property of foam coax, and the word 'lower' sounds plausible if you confuse the direction of the change."}], "concept": "Attenuation in coax comes from conductor (I²R) loss and dielectric loss; the dielectric contribution scales with the loss tangent of the insulating material. Air has essentially zero loss tangent, so a foam that is mostly air (lower effective permittivity εr) cuts dielectric loss. The same lower εr also raises the velocity factor, VF = 1/√εr — solid PE εr≈2.3 gives VF≈0.66, foam εr≈1.5 gives VF≈0.8+.", "misconception": "Treating all of foam coax's differences as advantages. Foam trades away mechanical robustness and some power-handling capability in exchange for the single key benefit of lower attenuation.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "Foam = more air = less loss (and faster signal/higher VF).", "edge_cases": "Foam dielectric is moisture-sensitive: water ingress dramatically raises loss, so end seals matter in outdoor runs."}

{"core": "RG-58 is a thin coax with relatively high attenuation per unit length, and that loss climbs sharply with frequency. At 430 MHz (70 cm) the dielectric and conductor losses become severe, so much of the transmitted power is dissipated as heat in the line rather than reaching the antenna.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Reflections at the feed point are caused by an impedance mismatch (SWR), not by the cable type itself. RG-58 is still 50 Ω coax and matches a 50 Ω system fine; its problem is loss, not mismatch.", "why_tempting": "Learners associate any feedline trouble at UHF with SWR/reflection problems, conflating mismatch with cable quality."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Properly constructed coax confines RF inside the shield; a braided cable like RG-58 doesn't significantly radiate from its outer surface unless common-mode currents flow, which isn't the characteristic UHF failing being asked about.", "why_tempting": "It sounds plausible that a 'cheap' cable would leak RF, but radiation is a shielding/balance issue, not what makes RG-58 unsuitable at 70 cm."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Some heating does occur as a byproduct of loss, but the operationally significant consequence is the lost signal power reaching the antenna, not thermal damage to the cable. The question asks for the major adverse consequence — that's reduced effective radiated power.", "why_tempting": "Since loss becomes heat, heating seems like a reasonable answer, and it's literally a true side-effect — but it's the symptom, not the major consequence."}], "concept": "Coaxial attenuation rises with frequency because conductor loss scales roughly with √f (skin effect concentrates current in a thin surface layer, raising effective resistance) and dielectric loss scales roughly linearly with f. Thin cables like RG-58 (~0.195 in) have small centre conductors and high resistance per metre, so loss is already moderate at HF and becomes large at UHF — easily several dB per 10 m at 430 MHz. Each 3 dB of feedline loss halves the power delivered to the antenna, so the practical effect is much weaker transmitted and received signals. For 70 cm you want low-loss line such as RG-213 or better, or hardline.", "misconception": "That feedline problems at UHF are about impedance/SWR, when the dominant issue with thin coax is frequency-dependent attenuation.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Thin coax, high frequency = heat in the wire, not power on the air.", "edge_cases": "Short runs of RG-58 at 70 cm (e.g., a metre patch lead) may be acceptable; loss is proportional to length, so the penalty matters most over long feedlines."}

{"core": "Open-wire (ladder) line has very low dielectric and conductor loss per unit length, so even when standing waves are present the additional I²R and dielectric heating from the high circulating currents/voltages remains small. This lets it run at high SWR without the excessive loss coaxial cable would suffer.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The opposite is true: open-wire line has an exposed external field and must be kept clear of metal, masonry, and wet surfaces, which detune it and cause loss.", "why_tempting": "Learners may confuse it with shielded coax, which can lie against metal because its field is confined."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Open-wire line is a high-impedance line (typically 300–600 Ω), not low impedance, so it does not directly match a 50 Ω transceiver — it generally needs a balun/tuner.", "why_tempting": "The phrase 'facilitates matching' sounds like an advantage, and people assume any feedline matches the rig."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Open-wire line CAN radiate if currents become unbalanced, since its conductors are spaced and unshielded; low radiation is a feature of coax, not ladder line.", "why_tempting": "It's easy to assume an efficient line must also be a quiet, non-radiating one."}], "concept": "Total line loss has two parts: matched (flat-line) loss and additional loss caused by SWR. The SWR-induced loss multiplies the matched loss — roughly, the higher the matched loss, the worse high SWR hurts. Open-wire line uses air dielectric and wide conductor spacing, giving extremely low matched attenuation (a small fraction of a dB per 100 ft at HF). Because the baseline loss is tiny, even a 10:1 SWR adds only a modest absolute loss, whereas the same SWR on lossy coax can dissipate a large fraction of the power as heat. This is why antenna systems fed with ladder line and a tuner (e.g., multiband doublets) tolerate wildly varying feedpoint impedances.", "misconception": "That low SWR is required for low loss on any line. SWR itself doesn't waste power; it only amplifies the line's intrinsic loss — and open-wire line's intrinsic loss is nearly negligible.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Low base loss → SWR can't multiply much. Air-spaced = efficient.", "edge_cases": "Open-wire line's low loss depends on balance and clearance; bringing it near metal or running it wet/dirty raises loss and causes radiation, eroding the advantage."}

{"core": "Loss in a coaxial feed line is proportional to its physical length (dB per metre at a given frequency). With 60 m of cable serving a 15 m run, trimming to roughly 15–20 m removes ~40+ m of unnecessary attenuation.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter attenuates harmonics for spectral cleanliness; it does nothing about ohmic/dielectric loss along the line and actually inserts a small additional loss.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a legitimate feed-line accessory, and learners associate filters with improving signal quality."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A balun corrects balanced-to-unbalanced feed mismatches and common-mode currents; it has no effect on the per-metre dissipative loss of the coax itself.", "why_tempting": "Baluns live at the antenna feed point and 'improve' the feed system, so it seems plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Coiling the cable does not remove any length, so the loss stays the same; tight coiling can add common-mode choking but not reduce attenuation, and may introduce its own issues.", "why_tempting": "It's a real and tidy thing hams do with excess cable, so it feels like the 'manage the slack' answer."}], "concept": "Total line loss = (loss per unit length) × length. RG-58 is a relatively lossy small-diameter coax, with loss rising sharply with frequency. The 45 m of surplus cable contributes pure attenuation in both directions (transmit power lost as heat, received signal reduced). Cutting the line to just longer than the 15 m physical separation minimizes that length term — the only variable here you can change. Note loss is measured in dB and accumulates linearly with length, so halving length roughly halves the dB loss.", "misconception": "Believing that 'doing something' to the excess cable (coiling, filtering, adding components) reduces loss, when only reducing the actual length lowers the dissipative loss.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Less coax, less loss.", "edge_cases": "Leave a little slack for strain relief and connector dressing — don't cut it to exactly 15 m. Also keep the line length appropriate to your installation; shortening helps loss but isn't a tuning device (electrical length matters only for impedance-matching tricks, not for reducing dissipative loss)."}

{"core": "Transmission line loss is distributed along the cable (expressed in dB per unit length), so total loss scales with physical length — the longer the line, the more dB lost.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "More line means more conductor resistance and dielectric loss, so attenuation increases, not decreases, with length.", "why_tempting": "Could be confused with the idea that a longer matched line 'smooths' SWR-related loss, but the underlying attenuation always grows."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Matched-line loss has no minimum at a wavelength-multiple; line loss is a per-metre attenuation figure, independent of the resonant-length tricks used for stubs/matching sections.", "why_tempting": "Learners conflate resonant electrical-length effects (stubs, 1/2λ repeaters) with attenuation, but those affect impedance transformation, not dB/m loss."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Loss is specified as dB per 100 ft or per metre at a given frequency, so it accumulates with length and is definitely not constant.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you imagine loss as a one-time connector/interface penalty rather than a distributed quantity."}], "concept": "Coax/line attenuation is given as α in dB per unit length. Total loss (dB) = α × L. Because dB add linearly, doubling length doubles the dB loss (and quarters the power if α·L was 3 dB, etc.). α itself rises with frequency (skin effect ∝ √f for conductor loss, plus dielectric loss ∝ f), but for a fixed cable and frequency, loss is strictly proportional to length.", "misconception": "Believing line loss depends on electrical resonance (wavelength) rather than being a distributed, length-proportional attenuation.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "Longer line = longer loss: dB just keep adding up.", "edge_cases": "With high SWR, additional loss appears because reflected power makes extra trips along the line — but even then more length still means more total loss."}

{"core": "Transmission-line attenuation rises monotonically with frequency: conductor (skin-effect) losses scale with the square root of frequency and dielectric losses scale roughly linearly with it, so higher frequencies always suffer more loss.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The dominant conductor loss grows as the square root of frequency (skin depth ∝ 1/√f), not as f². The total attenuation is not a clean square law.", "why_tempting": "Learners recall that some line effects depend on frequency strongly and reach for a power law, and 'square of frequency' sounds quantitatively rigorous."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Loss is decidedly frequency-dependent; coax and open-wire feedline specs are always quoted per frequency for exactly this reason.", "why_tempting": "Resistive (DC) loss of the copper is frequency-independent, so someone thinking only of conductor resistance might assume constant loss."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "This reverses the actual trend — loss falls, not rises, as frequency decreases. That is why HF lines are far more efficient than UHF runs of the same cable.", "why_tempting": "It's the exact opposite of the correct answer, so a learner who flips the relationship in their head can land here."}], "concept": "Two mechanisms dominate line attenuation. (1) Skin effect: at higher f the current crowds into a thinner surface layer, skin depth δ = √(ρ/(π f μ)), so effective resistance ∝ √f and conductor loss climbs with frequency. (2) Dielectric loss: power dissipated in the insulator rises roughly linearly with f (∝ f·tan δ). Both push the same direction, so attenuation in dB per 100 m increases continuously with frequency — gently at HF, steeply at VHF/UHF. This is why feedline choice (low-loss coax, hardline) matters far more at 2 m/70 cm than on 80 m.", "misconception": "Assuming feedline loss is either constant or follows a simple integer power law; the trend is the key exam point — loss always increases with frequency.", "link": null, "mnemonic": "Higher frequency, higher loss — 'thinner skin, more heat.'", "edge_cases": "The exact exponent isn't constant: conductor loss ∝ √f while dielectric loss ∝ f, so total attenuation lies between those rates depending on which dominates for a given cable and band."}

{"core": "RG-58 is a thinner, higher-loss coaxial cable than RG-213. For the same input power, more of the transmitter's RF is dissipated as heat in the feedline, so less power reaches and is radiated from the antenna.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "SWR is set by the match between the feedline's characteristic impedance and the antenna impedance, not by cable diameter or loss. Both RG-58 and RG-213 are 50 Ω, so the SWR at the antenna is unchanged.", "why_tempting": "Learners associate 'worse cable' with 'worse everything,' and may assume higher loss must degrade SWR too."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Cable loss can make the SWR measured at the transmitter end look lower (loss masks reflections), but the SWR at the antenna itself is determined by the antenna-to-line impedance match and does not change.", "why_tempting": "It's a true fact that lossy line lowers the SWR reading at the shack end, so this seems plausible if you confuse 'at the antenna' with 'at the transmitter'."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A higher-loss line cannot deliver more power than a lower-loss one; switching to RG-58 reduces, not increases, radiated power.", "why_tempting": "It's simply the opposite of the correct answer and may attract someone who reverses the loss relationship."}], "concept": "Coax attenuation depends on conductor size and dielectric. RG-58 (~5 mm) has higher resistive and dielectric loss per unit length than RG-213 (~10 mm), especially as frequency rises. Power delivered to the antenna is P_load = P_in × 10^(−loss_dB/10). If a run has, say, 1 dB extra loss, the antenna receives 10^(−0.1) ≈ 0.79, i.e. ~21% less power. The characteristic impedance (Z₀ ≈ √(L/C)) is 50 Ω for both cables, so the reflection coefficient and thus the actual SWR at the antenna terminals are identical — only the throughput power differs.", "misconception": "Believing feedline loss changes SWR at the antenna. Loss affects how much power survives the trip, not the impedance match at the antenna; it only alters the SWR *reading* at the transmitter end.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Skinnier coax = skinnier signal: less metal, more heat, less radiated power.", "edge_cases": "At HF the loss difference is modest over short runs; at VHF/UHF or long runs RG-58's extra loss becomes severe. Also, the lower SWR seen at the shack with lossy line is a measurement artifact, not an improved match."}

{"core": "Open-wire (ladder) line has the lowest loss on HF because most of its dielectric is air and its wide conductor spacing keeps resistive and dielectric losses minimal.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "75-ohm twin-lead uses a solid plastic dielectric that introduces dielectric loss, giving more attenuation than open-wire air-spaced line.", "why_tempting": "It's a balanced parallel line like open-wire, so it looks similar, and 75 ohm sounds like a 'matched' efficient value."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Coax confines fields inside a solid dielectric and a relatively small center conductor, so it has the highest loss of these options on HF.", "why_tempting": "Coax is the most commonly used feedline, so learners assume the 'standard' choice must also be the best performing."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "300-ohm twin-lead still relies on a solid dielectric web between conductors, adding loss compared to mostly-air open-wire line.", "why_tempting": "Its higher impedance and wider spacing make it seem close to open-wire performance."}], "concept": "Transmission-line loss comes mainly from conductor (I²R) resistance and dielectric loss in the insulating material. Open-wire line spaces two conductors widely with only spreader insulators, so the dielectric is essentially air (loss tangent ~0) and the wide spacing yields high characteristic impedance, lowering circulating current for a given power and reducing I²R loss. Coax and plastic twin-lead embed the conductors in solid dielectric, which both absorbs energy and concentrates fields, raising attenuation per unit length.", "misconception": "Assuming the most popular or 'matched' feedline (coax) is automatically the lowest loss; popularity reflects convenience and shielding, not minimum attenuation.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "Air beats plastic: more air dielectric = less loss.", "edge_cases": "Open-wire line's low loss assumes it stays balanced, dry, and clear of metal; it tolerates high SWR well but is awkward to route and is unshielded."}

{"core": "Transmission line attenuation is a power ratio that accumulates with physical distance, so it is expressed as decibels per unit length (e.g., dB/100 ft or dB/100 m), usually specified at a stated frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Ohms describe characteristic impedance or resistance, not loss; line loss is a power ratio, not a resistance measurement.", "why_tempting": "Coax is rated by its characteristic impedance in ohms (50/75 Ω), so ohms feel like the natural unit for a cable spec."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Combines two wrong ideas: ohms (impedance) and a per-MHz scaling. Loss does rise with frequency, but it is not expressed as ohms per MHz.", "why_tempting": "It correctly senses that frequency matters, and pairs it with the familiar ohm unit."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Decibels are the right loss unit, but loss is normalized to length, not to frequency. Frequency is a stated condition, not the denominator.", "why_tempting": "Half-right: dB is correct and loss does increase with frequency, so 'dB per MHz' looks plausible."}], "concept": "Attenuation along a line is exponential, so total loss in dB scales linearly with length: L_total = (loss per unit length) × length, at a given frequency. Specifying dB/length lets you multiply by your actual run to get total dB, then convert to a power ratio (10^(-dB/10)). Conductor (skin-effect) and dielectric losses both increase with frequency, which is why a manufacturer's loss figure (e.g., 2.5 dB/100 ft) is always quoted at a reference frequency rather than folded into the unit.", "misconception": "Thinking line loss is a single resistive (ohmic) value, or conflating the frequency dependence of loss with the unit itself.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Loss travels with distance: dB per length.", "edge_cases": "Loss figures are frequency-specific—always compare cables at the same reference frequency; higher frequency means higher dB/length."}

{"core": "Coaxial line loss is expressed in dB per unit length, so it scales linearly with the physical length. Doubling the length from 20 m to 40 m doubles the total loss — a 100% increase.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A 20% increase corresponds to a length increase of only 4 m, not a doubling.", "why_tempting": "Picks a plausible-sounding small percentage that loosely echoes the '20 metres' figure in the question."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A 50% increase would mean going from 20 m to 30 m, not 40 m.", "why_tempting": "Learners may confuse the ratio: 40 is 'half again' more in one wrong mental model, but it's actually twice 20."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "10% would be an extra 2 m of cable, far short of doubling the run.", "why_tempting": "Another small filler percentage that seems modest and 'realistic' for cable loss."}], "concept": "Transmission-line attenuation is specified as a constant loss rate (dB per 100 ft or dB/m) for a given cable type and frequency. Total loss = (loss per metre) × (length). Because dB add linearly with length, L_total ∝ length. Going 20 m → 40 m is a factor of 2 in length, hence a factor of 2 (×100% more) in total dB loss. Note dB is logarithmic in power ratio, but it is linear in length — so the decibel figure simply doubles, even though the actual power surviving is squared in the linear ratio sense.", "misconception": "Confusing the logarithmic nature of dB (power ratio) with how loss accumulates along a line. The dB loss itself grows linearly with distance; only the underlying power ratio behaves exponentially.", "link": "Decibel Converter", "mnemonic": "Twice the cable, twice the dB.", "edge_cases": "This linear-with-length rule assumes a matched line at a fixed frequency. With high SWR, reflected power makes additional passes through the cable, so real-world loss on a mismatched line can rise faster than the simple matched figure."}

{"core": "Transmission line attenuation rises with frequency because conductor (skin effect) and dielectric losses both increase as frequency goes up, so a given length of line dissipates more of the signal at higher frequencies.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "SWR is set by the load-to-line impedance match, not by frequency per se, and lower SWR wouldn't be a consequence of raising frequency.", "why_tempting": "SWR genuinely does affect total line loss, so it sounds like a plausible loss mechanism."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Wave (propagation) velocity on a line is essentially fixed by the dielectric (velocity factor) and does not meaningfully change with frequency, nor does velocity cause dissipation.", "why_tempting": "It correctly says loss increases, and 'wave velocity' sounds physics-y and authoritative."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Skin effect makes loss go UP with frequency, not down — it confines current to a thinner conductor surface, raising effective resistance.", "why_tempting": "Skin effect is the right phenomenon, but the stated direction (decrease) is backwards."}], "concept": "Matched-line attenuation has two main contributors: conductor loss and dielectric loss. Conductor loss scales with the skin effect — current crowds into a surface layer of depth δ = √(ρ/(πμf)), so effective AC resistance rises roughly as √f, increasing I²R loss. Dielectric loss rises faster (roughly proportional to f). Net result: dB-per-100-ft figures on every coax data sheet climb monotonically with frequency. Note SWR amplifies whatever matched loss exists, but the question isolates frequency as the variable.", "misconception": "Confusing skin effect's direction: skin effect increases high-frequency loss, it doesn't reduce it. Option D names the right cause but flips the effect.", "link": "section 6", "mnemonic": "Higher frequency → thinner skin → more resistance → more loss.", "edge_cases": "On a mismatched line, additional loss from high SWR is on top of this frequency-dependent matched loss; the two effects compound."}

{"core": "An SWR of 1:1 means the load impedance exactly matches the transmission line's characteristic impedance, so no power is reflected — the ideal match.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A 1:1 ratio is already a perfect match, so no tuner is required to correct anything.", "why_tempting": "Tuners are associated with SWR, but they're needed when SWR is high, not at 1:1."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "That condition describes total reflection (an open or short), which gives an infinite SWR, not 1:1.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a 'one-to-one' balance of forward and reflected power, but equal reflected power actually means the worst case."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A matched 1:1 condition needs no transformation; an impedance transformer is what you'd add to fix a mismatch.", "why_tempting": "Impedance matching is the right concept, but the device is unnecessary when the match is already perfect."}], "concept": "SWR (Standing Wave Ratio) = (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|), where Γ is the voltage reflection coefficient Γ = (Z_L − Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0). When Z_L = Z_0, Γ = 0, so SWR = (1+0)/(1−0) = 1, i.e. 1:1. Zero reflection means all forward power reaches the load and the standing-wave voltage maxima equal the minima (ratio of 1). As mismatch grows, |Γ|→1 and SWR→∞.", "misconception": "Confusing 'reflected equals forward' (C) — a worst-case total mismatch — with the ideal-match meaning of 1:1, since both phrasings superficially evoke equality/balance.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "1:1 = none reflected, perfectly connected.", "edge_cases": "A 1:1 reading confirms a match to the line's Z_0 (usually 50 Ω) at the measurement point; it does not by itself prove the antenna is efficient — a dummy load also shows 1:1."}

{"core": "An SWR of less than 1.5:1 indicates the antenna system's impedance is close to the transmitter's nominal impedance (usually 50 Ω), so most power is delivered to the antenna with minimal reflection — a fairly good match.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "SWR is a scalar ratio of standing-wave maxima to minima; it does not indicate whether impedance is 'too high' or 'too low' — it cannot distinguish a 25 Ω load from a 100 Ω load, both of which give 2:1.", "why_tempting": "The phrase 'less than 1.5' invites the idea that something is 'low', conflating the ratio's magnitude with the load impedance."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A serious mismatch produces a HIGH SWR (e.g. 3:1 or more). A low ratio near 1:1 is exactly what you want.", "why_tempting": "Learners may reverse the scale, thinking a small number signals a problem."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "SWR has nothing to do with antenna gain; gain is expressed in dBi/dBd, not as a standing-wave ratio.", "why_tempting": "The number 1.5 superficially resembles a gain figure, baiting confusion between two unrelated antenna metrics."}], "concept": "SWR = Vmax/Vmin on the line, equivalently (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|), where Γ is the reflection coefficient = (ZL−Z0)/(ZL+Z0). A perfect match (ZL=Z0) gives Γ=0 and SWR=1:1. At 1.5:1, |Γ|≈0.2, so only |Γ|²≈4% of forward power is reflected — over 96% reaches the antenna. The closer to 1:1, the lower the reflected power and feedline loss.", "misconception": "That a lower SWR number means low impedance or a fault, when in fact lower SWR = better match, and SWR alone cannot reveal whether the mismatch is high or low impedance.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Lower SWR = Less Wasted, Returning power — aim for 1:1.", "edge_cases": "A reading exactly at 1:1 can occasionally mask a lossy feedline that dissipates reflected power before it returns; always interpret SWR together with feedline condition."}

{"core": "Erratic, jumping SWR readings indicate the load impedance the meter sees is changing moment-to-moment — classic symptom of an intermittent connection (loose PL-259, broken solder joint, corroded contact, or a fractured conductor) in the feedline or antenna.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Power-line noise raises the receiver noise floor but contributes negligible RF power at the directional coupler compared to your own transmitted carrier, so it doesn't perturb forward/reflected sampling.", "why_tempting": "Power lines are a real source of trouble (noise, RFI), so a learner may associate them with any 'unstable' meter behaviour."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A missing balun causes feedline radiation and can shift the SWR baseline, but it produces a stable (if elevated) reading, not erratic jumping.", "why_tempting": "Baluns genuinely affect SWR on antennas needing balanced feed, so it sounds like a plausible 'antenna problem' answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Feeding the wrong antenna gives a consistently high or mismatched reading determined by that antenna's fixed impedance — steady, not fluctuating.", "why_tempting": "It clearly causes a 'bad' SWR, so it's easy to confuse a high reading with an unstable one."}], "concept": "An SWR meter (directional coupler) computes ρ=|Γ| from the ratio of sampled reflected to forward voltage, with SWR=(1+ρ)/(1−ρ). A stable mismatch yields a fixed Γ and thus a fixed needle. Erratic readings require Γ itself to vary in time, which happens when the termination impedance changes — exactly what an intermittent joint does as vibration, thermal expansion, arcing or RF current open and close a marginal contact. The arc resistance and contact area fluctuate, modulating the reflection coefficient and making the needle jump.", "misconception": "Confusing a high but steady SWR (a mismatch problem) with an unstable/erratic SWR (a connection-integrity problem). 'Erratic' is the key word pointing to something physically intermittent.", "link": "Tools — SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Erratic = Electrical contact problem (both start with E-r-c sounds: 'erratic→intermittent').", "edge_cases": "A genuinely arcing/corroded connector can also cause TVI and heating; check connectors and solder joints with the rig keyed at low power before suspecting the antenna design itself."}

{"core": "SWR measures the impedance mismatch between the transmission line and its load. An open or short circuit is the extreme mismatch (load impedance of infinity or zero versus the line's characteristic impedance), producing a theoretically infinite reflection coefficient and the highest possible SWR.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Both RG-213 and RG-58 are 50Ω cables, so swapping them doesn't change the impedance presented to a matched antenna. RG-58 only adds more loss, which actually *masks* SWR by attenuating the reflected wave on its return trip.", "why_tempting": "Learners associate cable changes with system problems, and RG-58 is 'worse' cable, so it feels like it should raise SWR."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Grounding the shield at the building entrance is a recommended lightning/safety practice that bonds the outer conductor; it doesn't alter the line's characteristic impedance or the load match.", "why_tempting": "Grounding sounds like it could 'short' the line, suggesting an impedance disturbance."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A long line increases loss but does not by itself create a mismatch. Like option A, extra line loss makes the measured SWR appear *lower* at the transmitter, not higher, because the reflected wave is attenuated.", "why_tempting": "Long runs feel inherently lossy and problematic, so they seem like a cause of bad readings."}], "concept": "SWR derives from the reflection coefficient Γ = (Z_L − Z₀)/(Z_L + Z₀), and SWR = (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|). For a matched load Z_L = Z₀, Γ = 0 and SWR = 1:1. For a short Z_L = 0, Γ = −1; for an open Z_L = ∞, Γ = +1. In both cases |Γ| = 1, giving SWR → ∞. Note that line length and line loss do not appear in the load-side reflection equation — they affect attenuation, which reduces measured SWR at the source end but never cures the underlying mismatch.", "misconception": "Believing that anything 'wrong' with the feedline (wrong cable type, excessive length) automatically raises SWR. SWR is set by the load match, not feedline attributes; cable loss only changes the *measured* value and tends to hide a high SWR.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Open or Short = SWR 'shoots' to infinity.", "edge_cases": "A lossy or very long line makes the SWR meter at the transmitter read better than the actual antenna SWR, which can dangerously mask a real fault at the antenna."}

{"core": "High SWR means standing waves on the line, with large circulating currents and voltages at the maxima. The added I²R and dielectric losses at these points raise the overall feedline loss, especially in already-lossy line.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Receive noise is governed by external noise, antenna pattern, and receiver design — not by SWR. A mismatched line attenuates signal and noise equally, so SNR isn't worsened by SWR alone.", "why_tempting": "Learners associate any 'bad' antenna condition with degraded reception, conflating signal loss with noise."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A properly built coaxial line confines fields within the cable regardless of SWR; standing waves don't make coax radiate. Radiation/feedline current is a balance/common-mode issue, not an SWR issue.", "why_tempting": "It seems intuitive that 'reflected energy' must escape somewhere, suggesting radiation from the line."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Common-mode current arises from feeding a balanced load with unbalanced line (or poor baluns/grounding), independent of the SWR value.", "why_tempting": "Common-mode current is a genuine feedline problem, so it's easy to wrongly attribute it to high SWR."}], "concept": "On a line of characteristic impedance Z0 feeding a mismatched load, the reflection coefficient Γ=(ZL−Z0)/(ZL+Z0) sets SWR=(1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|). Forward and reflected waves superpose into standing waves: current maxima are far above the matched value and voltage maxima stress the dielectric. Loss scales with the square of the local current, so the high-current antinodes dissipate disproportionate heat. The reflected power also makes a round trip through the line, attenuated twice. The net result is 'additional loss due to SWR' — small on short, low-loss line but severe on long runs or at VHF/UHF where matched-line loss is already high.", "misconception": "Believing the chief hazard of high SWR is feedline radiation or noise, rather than the extra ohmic/dielectric loss from the standing-wave current and voltage peaks.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "edge_cases": "On very short, low-loss line the extra SWR loss is negligible — high SWR there mainly threatens the transmitter's output stage, not the line."}

{"core": "An antenna analyzer sweeps a range of frequencies and shows SWR, impedance, and resonant frequency directly, telling you whether the antenna is electrically too long or too short so you can trim it accordingly.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A frequency meter only measures the frequency of a signal; it gives no information about the antenna's resonance, impedance, or SWR, so it can't guide physical trimming.", "why_tempting": "Antenna tuning is all about frequency, so a 'frequency meter' sounds related."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A multimeter reads DC voltage, current, and resistance — it cannot measure RF impedance or resonant frequency, which is what you need to set antenna length.", "why_tempting": "It's the most familiar bench instrument and you might think a DC continuity/resistance check tells you something about the antenna."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A capacitance meter measures lumped capacitance of components; an antenna's behaviour is a distributed RF impedance, not a single capacitance value you can read this way.", "why_tempting": "A short antenna looks capacitive, so a capacitance meter seems plausible for checking it."}], "concept": "A resonant half-wave or quarter-wave antenna has a length tied to wavelength (≈ 468/f in feet for a half-wave dipole, accounting for end effect). At resonance the reactive part of the feedpoint impedance is zero and SWR is minimized. An antenna analyzer injects a low-level RF signal across a frequency span and reports SWR and the complex impedance (R ± jX). If the resonant dip sits below your target frequency the antenna is too long (cut it shorter); if above, it's too long electrically and needs lengthening. Watching the sign of X tells you which way to trim: inductive (+jX) means too long, capacitive (−jX) means too short.", "misconception": "That ordinary DC test gear (multimeter, capacitance meter) or a frequency counter can characterize an antenna. Antenna adjustment requires an RF instrument that measures resonance and impedance versus frequency.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Analyzer = 'Antenna' — both start with A.", "edge_cases": "An SWR meter plus a transmitter can do a similar job but requires transmitting and only shows match at one frequency at a time; an antenna analyzer is self-contained and sweeps frequencies without keying a transmitter."}

{"core": "When the line's characteristic impedance Z0 differs from the antenna impedance, part of the forward wave is reflected at the junction. The forward and reflected waves superimpose to form standing waves on the line.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A mismatch reflects power back toward the source rather than dissipating it as heat at the junction; the reflection point is reactive, not resistive loss.", "why_tempting": "Learners associate 'mismatch' with wasted/lost power and assume it becomes heat right at the connection."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A 1:1 SWR is the signature of a perfect match. A mismatch raises SWR above 1:1, never down to it.", "why_tempting": "1:1 is the memorable 'good' number and a careless reader may flip the logic of what a mismatch does."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The antenna still radiates the power that is delivered to it; only the reflected portion fails to be radiated. A mismatch reduces transferred power, it does not zero it.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you over-interpret 'mismatch' as 'nothing gets through.'"}], "concept": "The reflection coefficient is \\u0393 = (Z_L \\u2212 Z0)/(Z_L + Z0). When Z_L = Z0, \\u0393 = 0 and no wave is reflected, giving a flat line (SWR = 1:1). Any deviation makes |\\u0393| > 0, and the reflected wave interferes with the incident wave: at points where they add you get voltage maxima, where they subtract you get minima \\u2014 these fixed-position peaks and nulls are the standing waves. SWR = (1 + |\\u0393|)/(1 \\u2212 |\\u0393|) quantifies their depth. The reflected energy is not lost at the junction; it returns toward the transmitter.", "misconception": "Believing a mismatch destroys power as heat at the feedpoint, when in reality the energy is reflected and forms standing waves along the feedline.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Mismatch = reflection = standing waves; match = flat line = 1:1.", "edge_cases": "On a lossless line standing waves cause no real power loss, but on a real (lossy) line the higher circulating current/voltage of high SWR does increase I\\u00b2R and dielectric losses \\u2014 that is where heat genuinely appears, distributed along the cable, not at the junction."}

{"core": "Standing waves arise from an impedance mismatch; the reflected power returning toward the transmitter means less RF energy actually reaches and is radiated by the antenna.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Standing waves are precisely the symptom of a mismatch — a perfect match (SWR 1:1) produces no standing waves at all.", "why_tempting": "It reverses cause and effect; a learner may associate 'waves on the line' with proper operation rather than mismatch."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Maximum power transfer occurs only when the line is matched and SWR is low; standing waves indicate reflected power and reduced delivered energy.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like the desirable goal of any antenna system, so it's easy to pick as the 'good outcome.'"}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Mismatch and standing waves can actually increase unwanted radiation from the feedline, not eliminate it; a matched line minimizes feedline radiation.", "why_tempting": "Coax is meant to be non-radiating, so 'lack of radiation' seems plausibly tied to normal line behaviour."}], "concept": "Standing waves form when the load (antenna) impedance differs from the line's characteristic impedance Z0. The reflection coefficient Γ = (ZL − Z0)/(ZL + Z0) sets how much incident power is reflected back: P_reflected = |Γ|²·P_forward. SWR = (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|). Forward and reflected waves interpose to create voltage/current maxima and minima along the line. The net effect is that delivered (radiated) power = P_forward(1 − |Γ|²); any |Γ| > 0 reduces the energy reaching the antenna and can raise feedline losses and voltage stress.", "misconception": "Believing standing waves are a sign of correct operation or efficient transfer, when they actually signal mismatch and reduced power delivery.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Standing waves = standing still: energy bounces back instead of moving to the antenna.", "edge_cases": "On a perfectly lossless line a high SWR alone wastes no power (it just reflects and re-reflects), but real feedlines have loss, so high SWR magnifies cable losses and the practical result is reduced energy to the antenna."}

{"core": "An SWR (or directional) meter samples the forward and reflected voltage (or power) waves travelling along the transmission line; the ratio of these determines the reflection coefficient and hence the standing-wave ratio.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Radiated RF is what a field-strength meter or antenna analyzer's far-field sensor would respond to, not what determines SWR on the feedline.", "why_tempting": "Beginners associate any RF instrument near an antenna with measuring radiation, conflating antenna efficiency with line match."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Conductor temperature relates to I²R heating and current capacity, not to the match between line and load.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible because mismatch causes higher currents and heating, but the meter never senses temperature."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Common-mode current on the feedline shield is measured by a current probe/RF ammeter and indicates feedline radiation, a separate problem from SWR.", "why_tempting": "Both relate to feedline 'problems,' so the terms get confused, but common-mode current is unrelated to the forward/reflected ratio."}], "concept": "A directional coupler separates the forward voltage wave (V_fwd) from the reflected wave (V_ref). The reflection coefficient is Γ = V_ref / V_fwd, and SWR = (1 + |Γ|) / (1 − |Γ|). When the load matches the line's characteristic impedance, V_ref ≈ 0, Γ = 0, and SWR = 1:1. As mismatch grows, the reflected wave increases and SWR rises. Many meters display forward and reflected power (proportional to V²), but the underlying measurement is the two travelling voltage waves and their phase-independent magnitudes.", "misconception": "That an SWR meter somehow measures antenna performance or radiated power, when it actually only compares the forward and reflected waves on the transmission line.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "SWR = forward vs. reflected — it watches the line, not the sky.", "edge_cases": "SWR is a property of the line/load interface; a perfect 1:1 reading only means the line is matched at the meter, not that the antenna is efficient or resonant — a lossy line can mask a high SWR at the antenna end."}

{"core": "An antenna analyzer measures the impedance (and derived SWR/return loss) presented at its terminals, and can sweep across a band, so it directly reveals the SWR of the antenna system versus frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Gain requires comparing radiated field strength against a reference antenna in a calibrated range; an analyzer only sees terminal impedance, not radiated power.", "why_tempting": "Gain is a headline antenna spec, so it feels like the kind of thing a dedicated 'antenna analyzer' would report."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Front-to-back ratio is a directional radiation measurement needing a test range and a rotated antenna under a far-field source, not a port-impedance measurement.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a measurable antenna parameter, and the word 'analyzer' implies thorough analysis."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "The radiation pattern is the spatial distribution of radiated energy, mapped by moving a probe around the antenna in the far field — impossible from a single feedpoint reading.", "why_tempting": "Pattern is a core antenna characteristic, so it's easy to assume an analyzer captures it too."}], "concept": "An antenna analyzer is essentially a low-power signal source plus a vector or scalar impedance bridge. It injects a known signal at the feedpoint and measures the complex impedance Z = R + jX (or just the reflection coefficient |Γ|). From Γ it computes SWR = (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|). Because the source frequency is steppable, it can plot SWR, R, X, or return loss across a frequency span — exactly what you need to find resonance and bandwidth. What it cannot do is measure anything about radiated fields (gain, pattern, F/B), because those are far-field properties requiring a separate transmitter/receiver geometry.", "misconception": "Conflating feedpoint/impedance measurements (what an analyzer does) with far-field radiation performance (what an antenna range does). The two are physically distinct measurement domains.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Analyzer = at the feedpoint; pattern/gain/F-B = out in the field.", "edge_cases": "A vector analyzer additionally gives R and X separately and can be used for transmission-line and tuning-network measurements, but still never radiated performance."}

{"core": "Feedline loss attenuates the reflected wave twice as it travels antenna→source, so an SWR meter at the station reads lower than the actual SWR at the antenna. The lossier the line, the closer the indicated SWR creeps toward 1:1.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The mechanism is wrong: the SWR meter responds to the ratio of forward to reflected power, not to how much power survives to the antenna. The reduced reading comes from extra round-trip attenuation of the reflected wave, not from delivered power.", "why_tempting": "Both the right answer (A) and this one say SWR decreases, and 'less power reaches the antenna' is a true statement about lossy line — so it looks plausible if you don't scrutinize the cause."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "SWR is the ratio of standing-wave voltage maxima to minima, but line loss still affects that ratio because the reflected component is attenuated relative to the forward component along the line.", "why_tempting": "It's true that SWR is fundamentally a voltage (or power) ratio, which tempts you to conclude the ratio is loss-immune."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Forward and reflected power are NOT affected equally. The forward wave is attenuated once on its way out; the reflected wave is attenuated again on its way back, so the reflected-to-forward ratio measured at the station is reduced.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a tidy symmetry argument — 'both get attenuated so it cancels' — but the reflected wave makes an extra trip through the loss."}], "concept": "SWR relates to the magnitude of the reflection coefficient at the meter: SWR = (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|). For a line with one-way loss factor (matched line loss) of L (a fraction <1 in linear terms), the forward wave reaching the load is scaled by L and the reflected wave returning to the source is scaled by L again. So |Γ_station| = L² · |Γ_load|. Because |Γ| at the station is smaller, the computed SWR is lower. In the limit of very high line loss, |Γ_station|→0 and SWR→1:1 regardless of how badly mismatched the antenna actually is — which is why a long, lossy run can mask a bad match.", "misconception": "Believing SWR is a property of the load alone and therefore identical everywhere on the line. It is actually measured locally, and intervening line loss reduces the apparent SWR seen at the transmitter.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Loss flatters SWR: a lossy line always makes the match look better than it is.", "edge_cases": "This is exactly why you can't fully trust a 1:1 reading through a long, lossy coax run — the antenna itself may be significantly mismatched. Measuring SWR at the antenna feedpoint gives the true value."}

{"core": "Modern solid-state finals are protected by a foldback circuit that monitors reflected power; a high SWR (impedance mismatch) triggers automatic power reduction to protect the output transistors.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A lightning ground fault is a DC/safety bonding issue invisible to the RF SWR-sensing circuitry; it doesn't change the impedance the transmitter sees at RF.", "why_tempting": "Sounds like a serious antenna 'fault,' so it seems plausible the rig would react to it."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Element motion in wind doesn't significantly alter the feedpoint impedance, and the transceiver has no mechanical sensor — it only watches forward/reflected RF.", "why_tempting": "Physical movement of the antenna feels like it should disturb performance and trip a protection circuit."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A balun mismatch causes common-mode current and feedline radiation, but if the impedance presented to the rig is near 50 Ω, SWR stays low and no foldback occurs.", "why_tempting": "It's a genuine antenna fault, so a learner may assume the rig detects the 'wrong' configuration."}], "concept": "Solid-state PA stages lack the rugged overload tolerance of tube finals. They include an ALC/foldback loop driven by a directional coupler (SWR bridge) at the output. As reflected power rises, the standing-wave ratio increases: SWR = (1+|Γ|)/(1−|Γ|), where Γ = (Z_L−Z0)/(Z_L+Z0). A high mismatch raises the voltage/current peaks on the transistors beyond their safe operating area. To prevent thermal/voltage breakdown, the protection circuit reduces drive — typically beginning around SWR ≈ 1.5–2:1 and folding back hard near 3:1. The key point: the rig reacts only to the RF impedance environment it can measure, not to mechanical or DC-grounding conditions.", "misconception": "Believing the transceiver senses any 'antenna problem.' It only senses reflected RF power (SWR); faults that don't change the feedpoint impedance at RF go undetected.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "High SWR = foldback: the rig protects itself from the power bouncing back.", "edge_cases": "Tube transmitters tolerate mismatch far better and generally don't foldback; only solid-state designs do this. An external antenna tuner can mask a high SWR from the rig by presenting 50 Ω at the transmitter, restoring full power even though the line beyond the tuner still has standing waves."}

{"core": "An antenna tuner is an impedance-matching network that transforms the complex load impedance presented by a non-resonant antenna/feedline into the resistive value (typically 50 Ω) the transmitter wants to see, presenting a low SWR at the transmitter output.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A tuner is a transmit-side impedance matcher, not a receiver frequency-selection device; it does nothing to 'find' distant stations.", "why_tempting": "The word 'tune' wrongly suggests dial-tuning a receiver."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "That describes a transmit/receive (T/R) switch or changeover relay, a separate device.", "why_tempting": "Both involve the antenna and switching during operation, so they get conflated."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "That describes an antenna/coax switch (or sharing device), which selects among lines/rigs — it performs no impedance transformation.", "why_tempting": "Also an 'antenna' accessory that 'matches' equipment to a line, so the wording sounds plausible."}], "concept": "The tuner is an L, T, or pi reactive network. The transmitter's final stage is designed to deliver rated power into a specific resistive load (50 Ω). If the antenna system presents Z = R ± jX with R≠50 and X≠0, reflected power rises and SWR climbs, which can fold back the PA. The tuner inserts adjustable series/shunt L and C to cancel the reactance (resonate out the jX) and transform the remaining resistance to 50 Ω. Note it tunes out the mismatch at the transmitter end — it does not reduce SWR on the line between tuner and antenna; that section still carries the original standing wave.", "misconception": "Believing a tuner physically 'tunes the antenna' or eliminates SWR everywhere. It only matches the transmitter to the line/antenna system, masking the mismatch as seen by the rig.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Tuner = transformer of impedance, not a tuner of stations.", "edge_cases": "With high feedline loss, a tuner at the shack can still show a perfect 1:1 to the rig while large reflected power is dissipated as heat in the coax — low SWR at the rig doesn't guarantee an efficient antenna."}

{"core": "An EFHW antenna presents an impedance of roughly 2000-3000 ohms at its end feed point. A transformer (typically a ~49:1 impedance-ratio unun) steps that high impedance down to the 50 ohms the coax expects, providing the match.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A counterpoise provides a small return/reference for the tiny residual current but does nothing to transform the high feed-point impedance down to 50 ohms.", "why_tempting": "EFHW installations often do add a short counterpoise, so it seems associated with making the antenna work."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Grounding the coax shield does not change the impedance ratio; it only affects common-mode currents and shielding, leaving the gross mismatch intact.", "why_tempting": "Grounding is a common 'fix-all' instinct for antenna feed problems."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A quarter-wave stub can act as an impedance transformer, but a single stub of standard line cannot bridge the ~50:1 (≈2500Ω to 50Ω) ratio practically, and it isn't the standard EFHW solution.", "why_tempting": "Stubs are legitimate matching devices, so it looks like a plausible engineering answer."}], "concept": "Impedance matching here is about transforming a very high source impedance to the line's characteristic impedance. For a transformer, Z_in/Z_out = (N_p/N_s)^2. To go from ~2450Ω down to 50Ω the ratio is 49:1, requiring a turns ratio of 7:1 — hence the classic EFHW 49:1 unun wound on a ferrite toroid. This delivers maximum power transfer and keeps SWR low across the band.", "misconception": "That any antenna accessory (counterpoise, ground, stub) will fix a mismatch. Matching specifically means transforming impedance by the correct ratio, which only the transformer does cleanly for the large step required.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "EFHW = 49:1 — square root of 49 is 7, so 7:1 turns.", "edge_cases": "A small counterpoise is still useful on an EFHW to stabilize the match, but it complements the transformer rather than replacing it."}

{"core": "Maximum power transfer occurs when the load resistance equals the source resistance. For purely resistive impedances, R_load = R_source maximizes power dissipated in the load.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Halving the load resistance creates a mismatch; more current flows but the load drops less voltage, so delivered power falls below the matched optimum.", "why_tempting": "Lower resistance sounds like it draws more current, and more current feels like more power — but the source resistance then hogs the dissipation."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Doubling the load raises load voltage but cuts current, again giving less than maximum power because the division of power between source and load is no longer balanced.", "why_tempting": "A bigger load resistor drops more voltage, which superficially seems to deliver more power."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The square root of the source impedance has no physical basis here; it isn't even dimensionally a resistance in any meaningful matching sense.", "why_tempting": "Square-root relationships appear in geometric-mean matching (e.g., quarter-wave transformers), so it can feel familiar."}], "concept": "For a source of EMF V with internal resistance Rs feeding load RL, load power is P = V²·RL/(Rs+RL)². Differentiate with respect to RL and set dP/dRL = 0: this yields (Rs+RL)² − RL·2(Rs+RL) = 0, simplifying to Rs = RL. At the match, exactly half the total power is dissipated in the source and half in the load (50% efficiency). Note this is the maximum-power condition, distinct from maximum-efficiency, which would want RL → ∞.", "misconception": "Confusing 'maximum power to the load' with 'maximum efficiency' or with simply maximizing current/voltage. Power is a product of both, optimized only at the match.", "link": "Ohm's Law & Power", "mnemonic": "Match for max: equal resistances split power 50/50.", "edge_cases": "With reactive impedances the rule becomes conjugate matching (RL = Rs, XL = −Xs); the simple equality only holds when both are purely resistive."}

{"core": "An antenna tuner at the feed point matches the antenna to the line's characteristic impedance, so the feedline operates with low SWR and minimal loss. Placing it at the transceiver leaves the line running with high SWR (and its associated reflected-power loss) all the way out to the antenna.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A tuner's location does nothing to change atmospheric noise pickup, which depends on the antenna and band, not where matching occurs.", "why_tempting": "Learners may loosely associate 'cleaner' signal paths with reduced noise, conflating SNR with feedline efficiency."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Usable bandwidth is a property of the antenna's Q and design; a tuner only re-presents a match at the transceiver and doesn't widen the antenna's inherent bandwidth.", "why_tempting": "A tuner does let you operate across more frequencies, which can be mistaken for added bandwidth."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Harmonic suppression comes from low-pass filtering, not impedance matching; tuner placement is irrelevant to harmonic content.", "why_tempting": "A matching network is a tuned circuit and superficially resembles a filter, so it seems it might reject harmonics."}], "concept": "Feedline loss has two parts: matched (resistive) loss and additional loss caused by SWR. When SWR > 1, energy reflected from a mismatched load travels back and forth, and each pass through the lossy line adds attenuation, so total loss rises sharply with both matched loss and SWR. A tuner located at the antenna feed point forces the line to see (close to) its Z0, so SWR on the line is ~1:1 and only the small matched loss applies. A tuner at the transceiver presents a 1:1 match to the radio but the entire run between tuner and antenna still carries the high SWR, incurring the extra reflected-power loss along its full length.", "misconception": "Believing that any tuner location that gives a 1:1 reading at the rig means a low-loss system — the radio sees a match either way, but only feed-point matching keeps the line itself at low SWR.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Match at the antenna, not at the radio — keep the high SWR off the wire.", "edge_cases": "On very short, low-loss runs the SWR-induced loss is small, so the benefit shrinks; the advantage is greatest on long lines, at higher frequencies, or with high SWR."}
