The blocking is broadband and depends on the receiver front-end's dynamic range and selectivity, not on producing new frequencies (IM) or on tone signalling (CTCSS).", "misconception": "Assuming any close-proximity strong-signal problem must be intermodulation; in fact pure overload/blocking of the receiver front end (desensitization) is the more direct and likely cause here.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Close + strong + can't hear = front-end overload (desense).", "edge_cases": "Desense and intermod can coexist near high-power or multi-transmitter sites; the distinguishing test is whether interference disappears on a different frequency (intermod) or affects sensitivity broadly (desense)."}

{"core": "The interference appears on the TV only when you transmit on 14 MHz, so your fundamental HF signal is overloading the TV's front end. A high-pass filter at the TV input rejects the low-frequency (14 MHz) energy while passing the desired VHF/UHF television signals, so it's the correct first remedy.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter at the HF transmitter attenuates harmonics and energy above the HF band; it does nothing about your 14 MHz fundamental, which is what is overloading the TV.", "why_tempting": "Low-pass filters at the transmitter are a standard cure for harmonic-related TVI, and the 14 MHz transmit correlation makes the transmitter feel like the natural place to act."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter at the transmitter would block your own 14 MHz signal from reaching the antenna — it would cripple your station rather than fix interference.", "why_tempting": "The word 'high-pass' matches the correct answer, so a learner who picks the right filter type but the wrong location can fall for it."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter at the TV would pass low frequencies (including the offending 14 MHz) and reject the high VHF channel-5 signals you want — exactly backwards.", "why_tempting": "'Filter at the TV receiver' is the right location, and low-pass sounds like it should suppress the lower-frequency interference."}], "concept": "This is fundamental overload (front-end overload / blocking), not harmonic generation: the TV is being swamped by your strong on-frequency 14 MHz fundamental. The remedy is placed at the victim's input and must pass the wanted band while rejecting the interferer. Channel 5 sits at 76–82 MHz and the wanted TV/FM spectrum is well above 14 MHz, so a high-pass filter (passband above ~50 MHz) attenuates the 14 MHz energy at the TV antenna terminals yet leaves the desired signals intact. The mention of the 92.5 MHz FM tower is a distractor — that station's frequency has nothing to do with interference that only occurs while YOU transmit on 14 MHz.", "misconception": "Confusing where the filter goes and which type to use: learners reflexively reach for a low-pass filter at the transmitter (the harmonic cure) when the symptom — interference only while transmitting on a much lower frequency — points to overload of the receiver, fixed with a high-pass filter at the victim.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Filter the victim, pass what it wants: TV wants the highs, so high-pass at the TV.", "edge_cases": "A high-pass filter cures front-end overload but won't help if your transmitter is actually radiating harmonics that land in the TV band — then a low-pass filter at the transmitter is needed. Here the single-band correlation indicates overload, so try the receiver filter first."}

{"core": "When two off-frequency signals reach the front end and mix to produce intermodulation products, the cure is to keep the offending signals out of the receiver — a suitable filter (band-pass, band-reject, or notch) ahead of the receiver attenuates the unwanted carriers before they can mix.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A common-mode choke addresses currents flowing on the outside of a feedline/cable (RFI conducted along shields), not two RF carriers arriving at the antenna and mixing in the front end.", "why_tempting": "Common-mode chokes are a standard interference fix, so it feels like a generic 'reduce interference' answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "An RF attenuator reduces both wanted and unwanted signals equally and lowers gain across the whole band; it may help only if the front end is overloaded, but it does not selectively remove the mixing carriers and degrades desired sensitivity.", "why_tempting": "Attenuation can reduce strong-signal overload, so it seems like a plausible intermod remedy."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A narrow IF filter improves selectivity after mixing — but intermodulation occurs before/at the front end, so the spurious product is already inside the passband and the IF filter cannot remove it.", "why_tempting": "Narrow filters obviously improve selectivity, so it sounds like the right tool for unwanted signals."}], "concept": "Receiver intermodulation arises when two strong signals f1 and f2 drive a non-linear stage (typically the first RF amp or mixer) into producing products such as 2f1−f2 or 2f2−f1 (third-order) that may fall on the wanted channel. Because the mechanism depends on both carriers reaching the non-linear device, the effective fix is to prevent at least one of them from getting there. A frequency-selective filter at the antenna input — band-pass for the desired band, or a notch/band-reject tuned to an offending transmitter — knocks down the interfering carriers ahead of the first active stage, so the mixing never occurs. Note third-order product amplitude rises roughly 3 dB for every 1 dB of input increase, which is why even modest front-end filtering yields a large reduction in the spurious response.", "misconception": "Believing IF selectivity or general attenuation can remove an intermod product. Once two signals mix in a non-linear stage, the resulting product lands in-band; only stopping the input signals before mixing, or improving front-end linearity, actually helps.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Filter the cause, not the symptom: kill the carriers before they mix.", "edge_cases": "If the front end is being overloaded by a single very strong nearby signal (desensitization rather than two-signal mixing), an attenuator can help; but for two-signal intermod a selective filter is the proper remedy."}

{"core": "Common-mode RF currents riding on speaker leads and power cords are the usual interference path. Winding those leads into coils on ferrite cores forms a common-mode choke whose high reactance at HF blocks the unwanted RF without affecting audio/AC signals.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A resistor in shunt would dissipate signal power and degrade the audio/AC line; it provides no frequency-selective blocking of common-mode RF and is not how RFI is cured.", "why_tempting": "'Bypass' sounds like it routes unwanted signal away, and the word appears in real bypass-capacitor techniques."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "MOVs are transient/surge suppressors that clamp high-voltage spikes (lightning, switching). They do nothing to attenuate steady HF common-mode interference.", "why_tempting": "They are genuine line-protection devices, so a learner may assume any line-protection part helps with RFI."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A series inductor in one conductor presents reactance to differential currents but doesn't suppress the common-mode RF current that actually causes the interference, and would distort the desired signal.", "why_tempting": "Inductors do block high frequencies, so 'bypass inductors' seems plausible as an RF blocker."}], "concept": "HF RFI into entertainment gear is almost always conducted in common mode: equal-phase RF currents flow on all conductors of a cable (speaker pair, mains lead) referenced to ground. Passing all conductors of the cable through a ferrite core (or several turns on a toroid) creates a high series impedance Z = jωL only to the common-mode current — differential signal currents cancel in the core and pass unimpeded. The ferrite's high permeability at HF, often with deliberately lossy material, gives both inductive reactance and resistive loss, dumping RF energy. This is why ferrite chokes/beads are the standard fix.", "misconception": "That RFI is cured by clamping voltage spikes or shunting to ground with a single component, rather than by choking the common-mode current path on the cables.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Ferrite on the feed = choke the RF, not the signal.", "edge_cases": "For very strong fields you may also need bypass capacitors at the equipment terminals; the ferrite choke addresses the conducted common-mode path, capacitors address residual RF reaching internal circuitry."}

{"core": "A telephone that picks up amateur RF is suffering from inadequate RF immunity in its own circuitry; the cure is an RFI filter (ferrite/capacitor module) installed at the telephone's line jack to block the RF before it reaches the susceptible audio stages.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "The problem is the individual telephone's susceptibility, not the distribution amplifier; grounding/shielding the amplifier won't keep RF out of the affected handset.", "why_tempting": "Grounding and shielding sound like legitimate RFI engineering practice, so it reads as a competent technical fix."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Ceasing transmission penalizes a properly operating station for someone else's poorly filtered equipment and is not a remedy at all.", "why_tempting": "It seems like the courteous, problem-avoiding choice when a neighbour complains."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Opening and modifying the telephone's internal circuitry is intrusive, often voids equipment approval, and exceeds what should be done — an external filter solves it without internal changes.", "why_tempting": "Since the fault lies in the phone, tinkering inside it feels like the direct cure."}], "concept": "RFI to consumer telephones is almost always a fundamental-overload / rectification problem: strong RF on the phone line is detected by nonlinear junctions in the phone's audio path, demodulating your signal into audible interference. The fix is to present a high impedance to RF while passing audio/DC — a plug-in modular filter combines bypass capacitors and ferrite chokes (a common-mode choke / low-pass network) right at the phone where the RF enters. Placing it 'close to the telephone device' keeps the unfiltered line stub short so it can't re-radiate into the susceptible equipment.", "misconception": "That interference to a neighbour's device automatically implies a problem with your transmitter, when a properly operating station is not at fault — the deficiency is in the victim equipment's RF immunity.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Filter the victim, not the transmitter.", "edge_cases": "This assumes your station is genuinely 'properly operating' (clean signal, no spurious emissions); if your transmitter were producing spurious output, the obligation would shift to correcting your own equipment."}

{"core": "Audio rectification of an SSB signal demodulates it imperfectly in a non-linear junction, recovering the voice but distorted — so the PA system reproduces garbled, unintelligible speech.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Rectification by a stray semiconductor junction is a crude, non-linear detector lacking proper carrier reinsertion and filtering, so the recovered audio is never clean.", "why_tempting": "You correctly recognize SSB carries voice, but you forget the recovery mechanism is poor."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "On-off clicking/humming characterizes keyed or pulsed emissions (e.g. CW or some digital modes), not continuous SSB voice.", "why_tempting": "Interference is often described as clicking, so it sounds plausible as a generic symptom."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A steady hum implies a continuous carrier or AC ripple being detected; SSB has no carrier and the audio varies with speech.", "why_tempting": "Hum is a classic rectification symptom for AC mains, so it gets misapplied here."}], "concept": "Audio rectification (RFI) happens when strong RF reaches a non-linear junction — a transistor input, diode, or even a corroded metal joint — in audio equipment. That junction acts as an envelope detector. For AM it would recover speech fairly well because the envelope follows the modulation around a carrier. SSB, however, has the carrier suppressed and only one sideband, so its envelope does NOT track the original audio. An envelope detector without proper carrier reinsertion (BFO) produces a heavily distorted, often unintelligible version of the speech. The emission type therefore dictates the perceived symptom: continuous voice modulation → distorted speech, not a tone or hum.", "misconception": "Assuming any detected RF interference yields a hum or click, or that SSB would demodulate cleanly like AM through a stray junction.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "SSB + bad detector = garbled gabble (distorted speech).", "edge_cases": "AM under the same fault would sound clearer; CW would produce keyed clicks/tones — the symptom is a clue to the interfering mode."}

{"core": "CW (Morse) is an interrupted carrier keyed on and off. When that RF is rectified by a nonlinear junction in the PA system, the detected output follows the keying, producing on-and-off humming or clicking in step with the dots and dashes.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Audible speech only results from rectifying an AM/SSB voice transmission, not an unmodulated keyed CW carrier which contains no audio modulation.", "why_tempting": "Audio rectification is most famously associated with hearing a station's speech through unintended detection, so a learner may default to a speech answer."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Muffled, distorted speech again implies a voice-modulated signal being detected; CW carries no voice, so no speech is recovered.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like the 'realistic' degraded version of detected audio, fitting the expectation that rectification mangles whatever it picks up."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A steady whistle would require a continuous carrier producing a constant tone, but CW is intermittently keyed, so the output is intermittent, not steady.", "why_tempting": "People associate Morse with tones/beeps, so 'whistling' feels close — but the keyed on/off nature is the key distinction."}], "concept": "Audio rectification (RFI) happens when strong RF reaches a nonlinear element (transistor junction, diode, corroded connection) in audio gear; that junction acts as an envelope detector. The recovered audio is the modulation envelope of the offending signal. For AM/SSB you get speech; for FM you typically get little or nothing because amplitude is constant. For CW the 'modulation' is simply the carrier being switched on and off by the key, so the detected envelope is a series of on/off bursts — heard as clicks, buzzes, or hum matching the Morse keying rhythm.", "misconception": "That rectification always produces intelligible (or distorted) speech. The output is dictated entirely by the offending signal's modulation type — keyed CW yields keyed clicks/hum, not speech.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "CW = Carrier Wobbling on/off → clicks, not chatter.", "edge_cases": "Cures (ferrite chokes, bypass capacitors at the audio device, proper bonding) are the same regardless of which modulation type causes the rectification; identifying the symptom just helps pinpoint the offending mode."}

{"core": "Audio rectification occurs when strong RF energy is unintentionally detected (rectified) by a non-linear junction in a device that has no RF stages — such as an audio amplifier, telephone, or hearing aid — producing audible interference.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Splatter is excessive transmitter bandwidth from overmodulation, an emission fault at the source, not a reception effect in a non-RF device.", "why_tempting": "It's a familiar interference term and sounds like spurious noise appearing somewhere it shouldn't."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Intermodulation requires two or more signals mixing in a non-linear RF stage to produce new frequencies — by definition it involves RF components, which this device lacks.", "why_tempting": "Both IMD and audio rectification rely on non-linearity, so they're easily confused."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Front-end overload is a receiver problem where a strong signal saturates the RF input stage — but the device here has no RF front end to overload.", "why_tempting": "It's the classic answer for strong-signal interference in radios and TVs."}], "concept": "Any semiconductor junction (a diode, a transistor's base-emitter junction, even a corroded metal-to-metal contact) is non-linear and acts as a detector. When sufficient RF voltage is coupled onto such a junction in an audio device, the junction rectifies the RF, recovering the modulation envelope. The recovered audio (for AM/SSB) then appears in the speaker. The defining clue is 'contains no RF components': only audio rectification explains interference in equipment that was never designed to handle RF. Cures target the entry point — bypass capacitors, ferrite chokes on leads, and improved shielding — rather than the transmitter.", "misconception": "Assuming RF interference can only enter devices that have radio circuitry; in fact any non-linear junction can demodulate RF, so even purely audio gear is susceptible.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "No RF inside? It RECTIFIES — audio rectification.", "edge_cases": "Because the cure is at the affected device, the transmitting amateur is often not at fault even though their signal is the trigger."}

{"core": "When a transmitter is heard across the entire tuning dial of a broadcast receiver regardless of where it's tuned, the receiver's audio stages are directly demodulating the RF signal — audio rectification — rather than the signal entering through the normal antenna/tuned front end.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Poor image rejection produces interference only at a specific image frequency (offset by twice the IF), not across the entire dial.", "why_tempting": "Image problems are a genuine receiver front-end defect, so they sound plausible for receiver-side interference."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Splatter is excessive transmitter bandwidth from overmodulation/distortion; it spreads only into adjacent channels near the operating frequency, not across the whole broadcast band.", "why_tempting": "Splatter is a real transmitter fault and the phrase 'across the dial' hints at broad spectral spread."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Harmonics appear at discrete multiples of the fundamental — specific spot frequencies — so they would interfere at a few points on the dial, not everywhere.", "why_tempting": "Harmonics are a classic transmitter problem and could land within the broadcast band, seeming to explain unwanted signals."}], "concept": "A correctly tuned superheterodyne receiver only responds to signals passing its tuned RF/IF chain. When a strong nearby RF field is picked up on speaker leads, power cords, or PC-board traces and reaches a non-linear junction (a transistor or diode in the audio amplifier), that junction rectifies the RF envelope and recovers the modulation as audio. Because this demodulation happens AFTER the tuning stages, the dial setting is irrelevant — hence interference at every dial position. The cure is at the receiver: bypass capacitors, ferrite chokes/beads on leads, and RF filtering at the audio input, indicating the receiver lacks adequate immunity rather than the transmitter being at fault.", "misconception": "Assuming any broadcast-receiver interference must originate as a transmitter defect. The key diagnostic clue 'across the entire dial' points to a receiver immunity problem (audio rectification), since true transmitter spectral faults are frequency-specific.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "'Whole dial = no dial' — if tuning doesn't matter, the trouble is rectification past the tuner, in the receiver's audio.", "edge_cases": "Audio rectification is a receiver/immunity issue; the operator's transmitter may be perfectly clean and legal, so the responsibility for the fix often lies with filtering the affected device."}

{"core": "A strong nearby HF signal is rectified by a non-linear junction inside the audio equipment (a diode, transistor junction, or corroded connection), demodulating the SSB envelope and reproducing your voice as muffled audio independent of the volume control.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Harmonics fall on higher frequencies (TV, VHF) and would interfere with specific channels, not produce demodulated voice on a non-radio audio system.", "why_tempting": "Harmonics are a classic transmitter-side cause of interference, so they sound plausible for any RFI complaint."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Poor transmitter filtering produces spurious emissions or splatter on adjacent ham frequencies; it does not explain how a sound system with no tuner picks up your voice.", "why_tempting": "Blaming the transmitter feels safe, and 'improper filtering' is genuine cause of some interference."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Receiver selectivity applies to actual radio receivers; a living-room amplifier/speaker system has no RF receiver to be selective, so selectivity is irrelevant.", "why_tempting": "Selectivity is a real cause of interference between radio receivers, so it seems technically reasonable."}], "concept": "Audio rectification (also called the 'BFO effect' or RFI by demodulation) occurs when a strong RF field couples into audio wiring and reaches a non-linear element — a semiconductor junction or oxidized metal-to-metal contact. That junction acts as an envelope detector, just like a crystal-set diode. For an SSB signal the recovered audio is your speech but with distorted, garbled character because there is no carrier to coherently demodulate it — hence 'muffled.' The key diagnostic clue is that the interference is independent of the volume setting: the rectification happens *after* the volume control (in the audio output/amplifier stage), so the volume pot has no authority over it. The cure is at the affected device: bypass capacitors across the rectifying junction, ferrite chokes on leads, and good RF grounding/shielding.", "misconception": "Assuming all RFI originates at the transmitter. Here the offending device is the victim equipment itself, which lacks RF immunity; the transmitter may be perfectly clean and legal.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Volume control does nothing → rectification happens downstream of the volume pot.", "edge_cases": "If reducing the volume DID change the interference, the RF would be entering ahead of the gain control — pointing to a different coupling path, not classic audio rectification."}

{"core": "A coil wound on a ferrite core forms an RF choke (common-mode choke) that presents high impedance to RF while passing audio, suppressing the RF currents induced on speaker/telephone/intercom leads.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter passes high frequencies (the RF) and blocks lows — the opposite of what audio circuits need; it would let the interfering RF through.", "why_tempting": "Filtering sounds like the right idea, and learners may not register that 'high-pass' lets the unwanted RF through rather than blocking it."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An attenuator reduces signal level uniformly across frequency, knocking down the wanted audio along with the RF, and does nothing to specifically reject the common-mode RF.", "why_tempting": "'Reducing a signal' superficially matches the goal of reducing interference."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A surge suppressor clamps transient overvoltages (lightning, switching spikes); it has no effect on continuous RF pickup on audio leads.", "why_tempting": "It's a protective device on wiring, so it sounds like general interference protection."}], "concept": "Audio leads act as antennas that pick up RF, which is then rectified at semiconductor junctions in the amplifier input, demodulating it into audible interference. Winding the lead several turns through/around a ferrite core adds series inductive reactance X_L = 2*pi*f*L that rises with frequency: negligible at audio frequencies but large at HF/VHF. Wound as a common-mode choke (both conductors together), it impedes the unwanted RF current flowing in the same direction on both wires while leaving the differential audio signal untouched. The ferrite also adds frequency-dependent loss, dissipating RF as heat.", "misconception": "That any 'filter' or protective device cures RFI. The fix must present high impedance to RF specifically while leaving the audio passband and the differential signal intact — a ferrite choke does exactly this.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Ferrite = 'RF roadblock, audio open road.'", "edge_cases": "Effectiveness depends on the ferrite mix (choose one optimized for the offending frequency band) and number of turns; a snap-on bead near the equipment is the common-mode version of the same idea."}

{"core": "Speaker leads act as receiving antennas whose efficiency depends on length relative to wavelength; shortening the leads reduces the captured RF and the resulting interference.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Audio attenuators reduce signal level in the audio band but do nothing to the RF picked up on the wires before it reaches the amplifier's nonlinear stages where detection occurs.", "why_tempting": "Attenuators sound like they 'reduce interference,' but they only cut the desired audio, not the conducted/radiated RF."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A series capacitor passes RF (low reactance at high frequency) while blocking low-frequency audio — the opposite of what you want. To bypass RF you'd use a shunt capacitor or a ferrite choke, not a series cap.", "why_tempting": "Capacitors are associated with filtering RFI, so 'series capacitor' seems plausible without checking which way the reactance works."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Longer leads make a more effective antenna and capture MORE RF, worsening the pickup.", "why_tempting": "Easy to confuse the direction of the effect if you don't tie antenna efficiency to electrical length."}], "concept": "A wire's ability to intercept RF scales with its length relative to wavelength — pickup approaches a maximum as the conductor nears a half- or quarter-wave resonance. Shortening leads moves them well below resonance, lowering the induced RF voltage. Complementary fixes (not offered here) include winding the lead through a ferrite bead to add common-mode RF choking impedance, or a shunt bypass capacitor at the amplifier terminals to short RF to ground while leaving audio untouched.", "misconception": "That capacitors always 'filter' interference regardless of topology — a SERIES capacitor is a high-pass element that helps RF through, while a SHUNT capacitor is what bypasses it.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Long wire = better antenna; short wire = worse antenna — so for less RFI, keep it short.", "edge_cases": "Shortening helps only because it detunes the lead from the offending frequency; a lead can still be problematic if it happens to land near resonance, so ferrite choking is often more reliable in practice."}

{"core": "Wrapping speaker leads around a ferrite core forms a common-mode choke whose high RF impedance blocks unwanted RF currents while passing audio essentially unaffected.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Copper is non-magnetic (µr ≈ 1), so wrapping leads on a copper bar adds negligible inductance and no RF suppression; the bar would actually act as a shorted turn dissipating energy, not choking RF.", "why_tempting": "Copper is the familiar 'good conductor' of radio, so learners assume it must help with RF."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Solid iron has high conductivity at RF, so eddy-current losses and very low resistivity make it lossy and ineffective as an RF core; iron's permeability collapses at radio frequencies. Ferrite is used precisely because it is a high-resistivity ceramic.", "why_tempting": "Iron is magnetic and used in audio/power transformers, so it seems like a logical magnetic core."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Wood is non-magnetic and non-conductive, contributing essentially no inductance increase, so it provides no RF choking — it's just a coil form in air.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a harmless insulating coil form, and winding turns alone seems like it should help."}], "concept": "RF entering through speaker leads is largely common-mode current. Winding the leads through/around a ferrite core raises series inductance and, more importantly, adds frequency-dependent loss: Z = R(f) + jωL. Ferrite (a sintered iron-oxide ceramic) has high permeability AND high resistivity, so it presents a large impedance at HF/VHF while its low inductance is negligible at audio frequencies (tens of Hz to ~20 kHz). The reactance ωL scales with frequency, so RF (MHz) sees thousands of ohms while audio passes through. A copper or iron bar fails because their low resistivity invites eddy losses and shorted-turn behaviour rather than clean choking.", "misconception": "Assuming any magnetic or conductive material works as an RF core — the key property is high permeability combined with high resistivity, which only ferrite provides.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Ferrite = 'Ferrous-ceramic' bead: high impedance to RF, transparent to audio.", "edge_cases": "For best common-mode suppression, route both leads through the same core so audio (differential) currents cancel and only RF (common-mode) sees the choke impedance."}

{"core": "An OCF antenna is inherently unbalanced and tends to carry RF current on the outside of the coax shield (common-mode current). A common-mode choke (current balun/choke balun) at the feedpoint or along the line blocks this stray RF, stopping it from radiating near the shack and being rectified by audio gear.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A surge suppressor protects against lightning/voltage transients; it does nothing to attenuate RF common-mode current on the feedline.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a protective 'line' device, and learners may associate any in-line accessory with cleaning up problems."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "An antenna tuner only transforms impedance to present a good match to the transmitter; it does not suppress shield/common-mode current and can even sit between the radio and the choke without solving RFI.", "why_tempting": "OCF antennas often need a tuner, so it feels like the natural fix for any 'antenna' issue."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter attenuates harmonics above the operating band (e.g. TVI from harmonics). The problem here is fundamental-frequency RF on the shield being demodulated by the speaker, not harmonic content.", "why_tempting": "Audio interference plus the word 'filter' suggests harmonics, the classic RFI culprit, but the mechanism here is common-mode pickup."}], "concept": "RFI into audio equipment is usually caused by fundamental RF being rectified at a non-linear junction (the speaker amplifier's input stage). The energy path is common-mode current flowing on the outside of the coax shield — distinct from the differential signal between centre conductor and shield. A common-mode choke presents high impedance to that outside-the-shield current (typically ferrite-loaded coax or a string of ferrite beads/clamp-on cores) while leaving the differential transmission-line mode unaffected. Unbalanced antennas like the OCF, fed against an unbalanced coax, lack inherent balance, so the shield becomes part of the radiating system unless choked.", "misconception": "That audio interference must come from transmitter harmonics (cured by a low-pass filter). In reality, fundamental common-mode RF on the feedline is the dominant cause of audio rectification, and only a choke addresses it.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Choke the shield, not the signal.", "edge_cases": "A choke at the feedpoint is most effective, but adding a second choke near the shack or ferrites on the offending audio cable can further reduce RF rectification."}

{"core": "When two or more signals pass through a non-linear device, the curvature of its transfer function generates sum, difference, and harmonic products at new frequencies — this is intermodulation.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Phase distortion alters the relative timing/phase of frequency components but does not create entirely new frequencies.", "why_tempting": "It's a real distortion mechanism, so it sounds plausible as a generic 'distortion' answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Linear distortion (gain or phase varying with frequency) by definition produces no new frequency components — that's what makes it linear.", "why_tempting": "The word 'distortion' is in it, and learners may not recall that 'linear' specifically excludes new-frequency generation."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Aliasing is a sampling artifact in digital systems where frequencies above the Nyquist limit fold into the baseband; it's not a non-linear analog mixing effect.", "why_tempting": "Aliasing also produces 'new' apparent frequencies, blurring the distinction for those familiar with DSP."}], "concept": "A non-linear transfer function can be modeled as a power series: Vout = a1·Vin + a2·Vin² + a3·Vin³ + … . With two input tones f1 and f2, the squared term yields 2nd-order products (f1±f2, 2f1, 2f2) and the cubed term yields 3rd-order products (2f1±f2, 2f2±f1). The troublesome 3rd-order intermodulation products (2f1−f2 and 2f2−f1) fall close to the original signals and inside the passband, so they can't be filtered out — which is why receiver/amplifier linearity (e.g. IP3) matters. A perfectly linear device (only the a1 term) produces no new frequencies.", "misconception": "Assuming any 'distortion' creates new frequencies. Only non-linear distortion does; linear distortion merely reshapes existing components.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "IMD = signals Inter-Modulating; Mixing needs Distortion (non-linearity).", "edge_cases": "Intentional mixing in a mixer/multiplier uses the same non-linear mechanism — it's only called 'distortion' when the new products are undesired."}

{"core": "Unwanted signals appearing on frequencies adjacent to your operating frequency are the textbook definition of spurious emissions — energy radiated outside the intended channel that lands on and disrupts nearby users.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A chirp (frequency drift during keying from sagging supply voltage) affects your own signal's tone and stability, not adjacent-channel interference to others.", "why_tempting": "Low battery voltage is a real cause of transmitter problems, so it sounds plausible as a generic 'something's wrong' answer."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A wrong repeater offset means you transmit on the wrong frequency entirely (the repeater won't hear you), not that you splatter onto neighbouring frequencies.", "why_tempting": "Offset errors are a common hand-held mistake on repeaters, so it feels like a relevant operating fault."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Reducing power lowers the absolute level but does not eliminate emissions falling outside your channel; the spurious products are still present, just weaker. The defect is spectral purity, not raw power.", "why_tempting": "Cutting power is the standard fix for general interference, so it reads like sensible advice."}], "concept": "Spurious emissions are any RF output at frequencies other than the carrier and its intended modulation sidebands — including harmonics, parasitic oscillations, and intermodulation products. A clean transmitter confines its energy to the assigned channel and rolls off sharply at the edges. When a defective or overdriven stage generates products that fall on adjacent frequencies, those products radiate and interfere with other users near your frequency. The cure is improving the transmitter (filtering, reducing distortion), not merely reducing power, because the unwanted products scale with the carrier but persist.", "misconception": "Assuming that interference to neighbours is always about being 'too loud' (power) when the real issue here is spectral cleanliness — emitting energy where it shouldn't be.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Spurious = Spilling Outside your channel.", "edge_cases": "If interference is from strong harmonics on completely different bands rather than immediately adjacent frequencies, the same spurious-emission principle applies, but the remedy emphasizes low-pass/harmonic filtering."}

{"core": "Spurious emissions are any RF outputs at frequencies outside the necessary bandwidth of the intended transmission — harmonics, parasitic oscillations, and mixer products that fall outside your operating band.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Side tones are an audio monitoring signal (e.g. CW keying feedback heard in your own receiver), not radiated out-of-band RF.", "why_tempting": "The word 'side' sounds like it could mean energy beside the carrier, mimicking sidebands or out-of-band content."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Chirp is a slight frequency shift of a CW signal during keying caused by an unstable VFO/power supply sag — it stays essentially within the band, it doesn't define out-of-band emission.", "why_tempting": "It's a real transmitter defect term, so it feels like a plausible 'signal where it shouldn't be' answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "'Off-frequency emissions' is not the standard regulatory/engineering term; the defined category for unwanted energy outside the band is spurious emissions.", "why_tempting": "It literally describes 'signals off the intended frequency,' so it reads like the obvious plain-language match."}], "concept": "Transmitter output is specified by a necessary bandwidth around the carrier. Anything beyond that is unwanted: harmonics (integer multiples nf0), parasitic oscillations, and intermodulation/mixer products. Collectively these unwanted outputs outside the necessary bandwidth are 'spurious emissions.' Good design suppresses them with low-pass/harmonic filters, proper neutralization, and adequate shielding so radiated spurs stay well below the carrier.", "misconception": "Assuming the term is descriptive ('off-frequency') rather than the defined technical category 'spurious emissions.'", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Spurs = spurious: unwanted spikes spurting out of band.", "edge_cases": "Harmonics are a subset of spurious emissions; in-band phase noise or chirp is a separate stability issue, not classed as spurious out-of-band emission."}

{"core": "Without its shielding, a transmitter's internal RF can leak out directly rather than being confined to the antenna path, allowing unwanted spurious emissions to radiate and cause interference.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Removing the cover doesn't reduce the desired output power; the PA still drives the antenna normally.", "why_tempting": "Learners may assume any 'leakage' steals power from the main signal, weakening it."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The cover is an electromagnetic shield, not part of the DC bias or current path, so removing it doesn't change current draw.", "why_tempting": "Sounds plausible if you confuse the metal enclosure with a heatsink or current-carrying chassis ground."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A chirp is a frequency shift during keying caused by oscillator/power-supply instability, unrelated to shielding.", "why_tempting": "Chirp is a known transmitter fault, so it can feel like a generic 'bad transmitter' answer."}], "concept": "A transmitter generates RF at harmonics, mixer products, and oscillator frequencies in addition to the desired carrier. The metal enclosure acts as a Faraday shield, containing stray RF and forcing the intended signal out only through the filtered antenna port. With the cover off, these unwanted products couple to surrounding wiring and free space and become spurious emissions — radiation on frequencies other than the assigned channel. Amateur operators are required to suppress spurious emissions, so operating unshielded both degrades spectral cleanliness and risks causing interference.", "misconception": "Thinking the cover affects the wanted signal's strength or the supply current, rather than recognizing it as an EMI/shielding measure against unintended radiation.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "No cover = no cage = spurs escape.", "edge_cases": "The same shielding principle applies to receivers (preventing ingress) and to keeping the enclosure grounded so the shield actually works."}

{"core": "Key clicks are wideband transients caused by abruptly switching the RF on and off at the keying point; the sharp rise/fall of the keyed waveform contains broad sidebands that spread well beyond the intended signal.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Frequency shift (chirp) from poor voltage regulation moves the transmit frequency during keying — that's chirp, not the broadband click splatter.", "why_tempting": "It's a real CW keying defect tied to power supply behaviour, so it sounds plausible as a source of interference."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Parasitic oscillations are spurious emissions at unintended frequencies due to feedback in amplifier stages, not the on/off transient of the key.", "why_tempting": "Parasitics do cause spurious RF, so a learner may conflate any spurious output with key clicks."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A defective filter capacitor produces hum/ripple on the signal, not the click transients of keying.", "why_tempting": "It mentions 'poor wave-shaping,' and since clicks ARE a wave-shaping problem, the wording is misleading."}], "concept": "A keyed RF envelope can be modelled as the carrier multiplied by the keying waveform. The bandwidth of the emitted signal is set by the rise and fall times of that envelope: a near-step transition (very fast make/break) has a Fourier spectrum with energy at many harmonics of the keying transient, producing sidebands far from the carrier. Slowing the edges with proper shaping (an RC or LC keying filter giving rise/fall times around a few milliseconds) confines the spectrum. The root cause is the abrupt making and breaking of the circuit — option A — and the cure is wave-shaping, not the absence of it.", "misconception": "Confusing the cause (abrupt switching at the key) with related but distinct CW faults like chirp, parasitics, or supply ripple. The defect being asked about is the keying transient itself.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Hard key = hard edges = wide spectrum. Soft keying (shaped rise/fall) tames clicks.", "edge_cases": "Clicks worsen at higher keying speeds and with hard, square keying envelopes; proper rise/fall shaping keeps occupied bandwidth narrow without making the keying 'mushy.'"}

{"core": "Key clicks are wideband spectral splatter caused by the keyed CW envelope switching on and off too abruptly. A rectangular keying waveform has fast rise and decay times, which by Fourier analysis contain high-frequency sidebands extending well beyond the intended signal.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Power-supply hum modulating the carrier produces a steady 60/120 Hz buzz on the signal, audible as a hum tone, not the sharp ticking clicks heard on adjacent frequencies during keying transitions.", "why_tempting": "It's a real transmitter defect that affects signal quality, so a learner who hasn't pinned 'clicks' to keying transients may grab it."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Arcing key contacts can radiate broadband noise, but in a properly designed transmitter the key controls low-level keying circuits, and the clicks heard at a distant receiver originate from the transmitted carrier's envelope, not contact sparking.", "why_tempting": "The word 'key contacts' plus 'sparks' superficially matches the 'click' sound and seems mechanically plausible."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Frequency shift on keying is called 'chirp,' a different defect heard as a pitch change at the start of each dot/dash, not the click splatter on neighbouring frequencies.", "why_tempting": "It is a genuine keying problem, so a learner who confuses chirp with clicks may pick it."}], "concept": "The keyed CW signal is the carrier multiplied by a keying envelope. The occupied bandwidth is set by how fast that envelope changes. A near-instantaneous (rectangular) transition has theoretically infinite bandwidth — its Fourier transform is a sinc function with energy far from the carrier. Slowing the rise and decay (shaping the envelope, e.g. raised-cosine over a few milliseconds) limits the spectral spreading. The cure is keying waveform shaping to give gradual rise/fall times, narrowing the emitted bandwidth so adjacent stations don't hear clicks.", "misconception": "Believing clicks are caused by something audio-domain (hum) or mechanical (sparks) rather than recognizing they are a bandwidth/transient problem of the RF envelope itself.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Sharp edges = wide spectrum; soft edges = clean signal.", "edge_cases": "Distinguish key clicks (abrupt on/off, broadband splatter) from chirp (frequency shift on keying) and hum (power-supply modulation) — all three are separate CW defects."}

{"core": "Key clicks are wide-bandwidth sidebands generated when the CW envelope rises and falls too abruptly. Proper waveform shaping (controlled rise/fall times) softens the keying envelope and confines the emission to a narrow bandwidth.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Frequency shift during keying (chirp) is a frequency-domain defect from oscillator pulling under load, heard as a tone that slides — not the broadband clicks of hard keying.", "why_tempting": "It's a genuine CW keying fault, so a learner who conflates all keying defects might pick it."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Sparking at relay/key contacts does create broadband noise, but the question concerns interference heard at a distance from the radiated signal's spectrum — the textbook cause is the keyed envelope shape, not local arcing.", "why_tempting": "Sparks obviously make broadband RF, so it sounds physically plausible."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Mechanical instability produces frequency/phase jitter and FM-like noise, not the sharp transient sidebands characteristic of key clicks.", "why_tempting": "It's another transmitter defect and 'instability' loosely sounds like it could splatter."}], "concept": "A CW signal is a carrier multiplied by a keying envelope. The transmitted spectrum is the convolution of the carrier with the envelope's spectrum. A near-rectangular on/off envelope has very fast transitions; by Fourier analysis, fast edges contain high-frequency components, so the modulating waveform's spectrum is broad. Convolving that with the carrier spreads sidebands far from the operating frequency — these are the clicks heard up and down the band. Slowing the rise and fall times (typically a few milliseconds, e.g. raised-cosine shaping) low-pass-filters the envelope, dramatically narrowing the occupied bandwidth while keeping the keying crisp enough to read.", "misconception": "Assuming clicks come from a physical spark or oscillator fault, when they are actually a spectral consequence of abrupt amplitude switching (the time–bandwidth relationship).", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Sharp edges = wide spectrum; soften the keying to silence the clicks.", "edge_cases": "Over-shaping (too slow rise/fall) rounds the keying so much that high-speed CW becomes unreadable, so there's a practical trade-off between bandwidth and keying sharpness."}

{"core": "Key clicks are sharp transients caused by abrupt make/break of the keyed waveform; the fix is to inspect the keying (shaping) filter and verify the later amplifier stages are functioning properly so the envelope rises and falls smoothly.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "An RF choke in the output affects RF energy flow, not the slope of the keying envelope, so it does nothing about the broadband clicks.", "why_tempting": "Adding a choke 'to clean up RF' sounds like a plausible filtering cure for spurious emissions."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The mechanical key only closes and opens the circuit; its adjustment controls feel and contact, not the rise/fall shaping that produces clicks.", "why_tempting": "'Key clicks' literally contains 'key,' so adjusting the key feels like the obvious target."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Oscillator supply regulation cures frequency instability or chirp, not the transient sidebands from hard keying.", "why_tempting": "Power-supply regulation is a common remedy for transmitter problems, so it seems generally helpful."}], "concept": "Key clicks are wideband sidebands generated when a CW signal is switched on/off too quickly. In the frequency domain, a near-rectangular keying envelope has a wide sinc-like spectrum; clicks are the high-frequency components of those abrupt edges. The cure is to shape the envelope: a keying filter (RC/LC shaping network) lengthens the rise and fall times so the transitions are gradual, narrowing the occupied bandwidth. If the shaping filter is misadjusted or a downstream stage isn't passing the shaped waveform correctly (e.g. an amplifier clipping or restoring the hard edge), clicks reappear—hence you check both the keying filter and the functioning of later stages.", "misconception": "That 'key clicks' come from the physical key or general RF leakage, rather than from the unshaped (too-fast) keying waveform and the stages that process it.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Clicks = sharp EDGES → soften the edges with the keying (shaping) filter.", "edge_cases": "Excessive shaping (too-slow edges) degrades CW keying quality and can sound mushy—the goal is a balance between bandwidth and readability."}

{"core": "A parasitic oscillation is an unintended, self-sustaining oscillation—usually at a frequency unrelated to the intended signal—that arises from stray inductance, capacitance, and feedback paths within an amplifier or oscillator.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Secondary emission is the ejection of electrons from a surface (e.g., a tube grid or anode) bombarded by primary electrons; it is a physical electron-emission effect, not an oscillation.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a circuit malfunction term and appears in vacuum-tube discussions, so it can feel related to amplifier problems."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Frequency instability describes drift or wandering of a desired output frequency, not the spontaneous appearance of an entirely separate unwanted oscillation.", "why_tempting": "Both involve 'unwanted' behaviour in oscillators, and instability is a real fault, so it reads plausibly."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Harmonic emission is energy radiated at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency—a byproduct of nonlinearity, not a self-generated parasitic oscillation.", "why_tempting": "Harmonics are also 'unwanted output' from amplifiers and are a common spurious-emission topic, so they blur with parasitics."}], "concept": "Any active stage with gain plus a feedback path can satisfy the Barkhausen criterion (loop gain ≥1, total phase shift =360°) at some frequency. Designers want this only at the intended frequency; but stray lead inductance, interelectrode/junction capacitance, and ground-loop coupling create unintended resonant feedback loops—often at VHF/UHF, far from the operating frequency. The result is a parasitic oscillation that wastes power, distorts the signal, generates spurious emissions, and can damage devices. Cures include parasitic suppressors (a small resistor paralleled by a few turns of wire/ferrite bead), neutralization, and careful lead-length/decoupling layout.", "misconception": "Confusing 'unwanted oscillation' with 'unwanted emission' (harmonics) or with frequency drift; the question targets the specific named phenomenon of self-excited, off-frequency oscillation due to stray feedback.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Parasitic = an uninvited oscillation feeding off your circuit's gain.", "edge_cases": "Parasitic oscillations are frequently at frequencies much higher than the intended signal and may not be visible without VHF/UHF-capable test gear, which is why they're sometimes detected only by their effects (excess current, distortion, spurious signals)."}

{"core": "Parasitic oscillations arise when unintended positive (regenerative) feedback at some frequency satisfies the Barkhausen criterion — loop gain ≥1 with in-phase feedback — turning an amplifier into an unintended oscillator.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Supply instability can cause hum, ripple modulation, or motorboating, but parasitics are a feedback-path phenomenon set by stray L and C, not by the DC source quality.", "why_tempting": "A noisy or poorly decoupled supply does cause spurious behaviour, so it sounds like a plausible 'instability' source."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Negative feedback is degenerative — it reduces gain and improves stability, the opposite of what sustains oscillation.", "why_tempting": "Any 'feedback' word sounds related to oscillation, and a learner may not recall that the polarity (sign/phase) is what matters."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Oscillator drift is a slow change in an intended oscillator's frequency (thermal/component aging); it isn't the mechanism that creates a new, unwanted oscillation in an amplifier stage.", "why_tempting": "It contains 'oscillator' and 'drift,' loosely associated with frequency instability."}], "concept": "An amplifier becomes an oscillator when the loop gain magnitude ≥1 and the total phase shift around the loop is 360° (0°, i.e. positive feedback) — the Barkhausen condition. Stray reactances (lead inductance, interelectrode/device capacitance, layout coupling) form unintended resonant feedback networks, usually at VHF/UHF well above the operating frequency. Cures attack the loop: ferrite beads or small resistors in base/grid/plate leads, neutralization, good bypassing/decoupling, and tight layout to break the regenerative path.", "misconception": "Confusing the polarity of feedback — assuming any feedback causes oscillation, when only positive (in-phase) feedback with sufficient gain does; negative feedback stabilizes.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Positive feedback = Parasitic problems; Negative feedback = Nice and stable.", "edge_cases": "Parasitics typically occur far from the design frequency (often VHF in an HF amp), which is why they can be hard to find without a spectrum analyzer."}

{"core": "Parasitic oscillations are unwanted self-oscillations whose frequency is set by stray circuit elements (lead inductance, interelectrode/stray capacitance), not by the tuned operating circuit—so they can appear at frequencies either above or below the intended transmitter frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Parasitics are not restricted to VHF; the resonant loops formed by stray L and C can land at HF, VHF, or UHF depending on physical layout.", "why_tempting": "VHF parasitics are common in HF amplifiers because short leads have small inductance giving high resonant frequencies, so learners overgeneralize to 'VHF only'."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Oscillation at the fundamental is the intended, deliberate output of the amplifier driven by the exciter, not a parasitic. Parasitics are by definition at unrelated frequencies.", "why_tempting": "It mentions the transmitter's own frequency, which sounds like where an amplifier 'oscillates,' confusing intended amplification with spurious oscillation."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Harmonics are integer multiples of the fundamental produced by nonlinear distortion, a separate spurious mechanism; parasitics are unrelated to the fundamental and not harmonically locked to it.", "why_tempting": "Both are 'spurious emissions,' so harmonics and parasitics are easily conflated, but they arise from different causes."}], "concept": "A parasitic oscillation occurs when an amplifier stage becomes a self-sustaining oscillator: stray inductance in leads/wiring combines with interelectrode and stray capacitance to form a high-Q resonant loop, and enough feedback (often through the tube/transistor's internal capacitance or shared ground paths) exists at that loop's resonant frequency to satisfy the Barkhausen conditions (loop gain ≥1, 0° net phase). Because the resonant frequency f = 1/(2π√(LC)) is governed by these stray reactances—completely independent of the tuned tank set for the operating frequency—the oscillation can fall above OR below the transmitter frequency. Cures include VHF parasitic suppressors (a small resistor paralleled with a few turns of wire) in the plate/anode lead, neutralization, and improved lead dressing to reduce stray L.", "misconception": "Believing parasitics are tied to (or a multiple/submultiple of) the operating frequency, when in fact their frequency is set entirely by stray circuit reactances and is unrelated to the transmit frequency.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Parasitic = 'unwanted guest' — it shows up wherever it wants (above or below), not where you invited it.", "edge_cases": "Don't confuse parasitic oscillation with harmonic generation or with intermodulation—all are spurious but have distinct causes and cures (suppressors/neutralization vs. filtering vs. linearity improvements)."}

{"core": "Interference confined to only one or two specific TV channels points to harmonics from your transmitter falling within those channels' frequency bands. The 15 m band's harmonics can land squarely in VHF TV channels.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Overmodulation splatter spreads energy across adjacent frequencies near your operating band, not up into discrete TV channels, and would affect your own band's neighbours rather than specific TV channels.", "why_tempting": "Splatter is a real interference mechanism, so it feels plausible if you don't notice the clue that only a few specific channels are affected."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Front-end overload (fundamental overload) is broadband — it degrades many or all channels at once because the strong fundamental desensitizes the receiver's RF amplifier. It does not selectively hit one or two channels.", "why_tempting": "Overload is the other classic TVI cause, and learners often confuse it with the harmonic mechanism; the 'only when you transmit' clue applies to both."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Parasitic oscillations occur at unpredictable, often VHF/UHF frequencies and tend to cause erratic, broad interference rather than a clean appearance on just one or two channels related harmonically to 15 m.", "why_tempting": "Parasitics do produce spurious emissions, so they sound like a credible source of out-of-band energy."}, {"option": "A", "why_wrong": "", "why_tempting": ""}], "concept": "A 21 MHz fundamental on 15 m has integer-multiple harmonics: 2nd ≈ 42 MHz, 3rd ≈ 63 MHz, etc. These spurious products are generated in the transmitter (nonlinear stages) and radiated through the antenna. When a harmonic frequency falls within a particular TV channel's passband, that channel — and only that channel — shows interference, because each harmonic is a single discrete frequency. The diagnostic signature is therefore *selectivity*: a couple of channels affected, not the whole band. The cure is at the source: low-pass filtering and good shielding on the transmitter to attenuate harmonics below the radiated fundamental.", "misconception": "That any TVI caused by transmitting must be the receiver's fault (overload). The key discriminator is breadth: harmonics → a few specific channels; fundamental overload → many/all channels.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Few channels = your Frequency multiples (harmonics); All channels = receiver Awash (overload).", "edge_cases": "Harmonic TVI is your responsibility to fix at the transmitter; broadband front-end overload is generally remedied at the affected receiver (e.g., high-pass filter), even though your transmission triggers it."}

{"core": "Harmonic emissions are spurious outputs at integer multiples (2×, 3×, 4×…) of the fundamental operating frequency, generated by nonlinearities in the transmitter's amplifying stages.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Mixing of two signals produces intermodulation products (sum and difference frequencies), not harmonics — that's a distinct interference mechanism.", "why_tempting": "Both are unwanted spurious emissions, and mixing genuinely creates new frequencies, so it sounds plausible to lump them together."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Skip propagation is an ionospheric refraction phenomenon and has nothing to do with the spectral content of a transmitter's output.", "why_tempting": "The word 'signals' plus a propagation concept can fool someone pattern-matching on familiar exam vocabulary."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A 60 Hz component is power-line/AC hum (a modulation/filtering artifact), not a frequency multiple of the carrier.", "why_tempting": "Hum is a classic 'unwanted' emission, so it feels like a reasonable definition of an undesired signal."}], "concept": "Any active stage operating in a nonlinear region (Class C amplifiers especially) distorts the sine wave; Fourier analysis shows distortion of a pure tone at f produces energy at 2f, 3f, 4f, … The amplitudes typically fall off with order but the 2nd and 3rd harmonics can be strong enough to cause interference far outside your assigned band. Low-pass filtering, output tank/π-network tuning, and operating amplifiers more linearly all suppress harmonic content. This is why a transmitter on 7 MHz can radiate spurs at 14, 21, 28 MHz and beyond.", "misconception": "Confusing harmonics (integer multiples of one signal's own frequency, from nonlinear distortion) with intermodulation/mixing products (sums and differences of two different signals).", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Harmonic = whole-number multiple: 'Harmonics are 2×, 3×, 4× the home frequency.'", "edge_cases": "Subharmonics (f/2, f/3) can occur in some circuits like frequency multipliers/dividers, but the exam definition of a harmonic is strictly integer multiples of the fundamental."}

{"core": "Harmonics are spurious multiples of your fundamental frequency that land in other allocated bands; radiating them causes interference to other services and stations operating there, which is why they must be suppressed.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Harmonics are about emitting unwanted spectral energy into other bands, not about feeding power into another transmitter; overloading a nearby receiver front end is a different phenomenon (desensitization/blocking).", "why_tempting": "Strong nearby fields can affect other equipment, so 'overload' sounds plausible — but a harmonic's problem is where it lands in the spectrum."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Harmonics fall on integer multiples of the fundamental (2f, 3f, …), typically far away in other bands — not on frequencies adjacent to your own. Adjacent-channel problems come from splatter/over-modulation, not harmonics.", "why_tempting": "'Adjacent frequencies' sounds like the same idea as interference, but it describes the wrong spectral location."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Power dissipation is an efficiency/thermal concern inside your equipment, not the reason harmonic *emissions* are objectionable. The harm is radiated, off-band energy.", "why_tempting": "Harmonics do represent wasted output power, so dissipation seems related — but the regulatory concern is interference, not heat."}], "concept": "A non-linear PA generates output at f, 2f, 3f, 4f… The 2nd harmonic of a 7 MHz signal is 14 MHz, the 3rd is 21 MHz — all within or near other amateur or commercial allocations. Even at -40 to -60 dBc these spurs can be received far away. Low-pass filtering and proper PA biasing/drive levels keep harmonic output below regulated limits so you don't radiate into bands you aren't operating in. Option C is the broadest and most accurate statement of the harm: interference to other stations/services.", "misconception": "Conflating harmonics (integer-multiple, far-off-band spurious emissions) with adjacent-channel splatter or with internal power-efficiency issues.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Harmonics = whole-number multiples landing in someone else's band.", "edge_cases": "Distinguish harmonics (2f, 3f) from spurious/intermodulation products and from key-click or splatter sidebands close to the carrier — all are 'unwanted emissions' but arise differently."}

{"core": "Energy that falls immediately adjacent to the necessary bandwidth — the result of the modulation process spilling beyond the channel edges — is by definition the out-of-band (OOB) domain of emissions.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Harmonics appear at integer multiples (2f, 3f...) of the carrier, far from the operating channel, not just outside the necessary bandwidth.", "why_tempting": "Harmonics are the most familiar form of unwanted emission, so they're an easy default answer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Parasitic emissions are self-generated oscillations at unrelated frequencies due to unintended feedback/resonances in the circuit — not necessarily near the carrier.", "why_tempting": "'Parasitic' sounds like stray, unwanted energy, which loosely fits the description."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Spurious emissions are defined as those that can be reduced without affecting the information being transmitted — they lie in the spurious domain *outside* the OOB domain, and include harmonics, intermods, and parasitics.", "why_tempting": "'Spurious' is the broad term for unwanted output, and the phrase 'just outside the necessary bandwidth' makes it seem to apply directly."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "(duplicate-safe note) The key word is 'just outside' — that adjacency defines the out-of-band domain, distinguishing it from the more distant spurious domain.", "why_tempting": "Learners conflate spurious with out-of-band because both are unwanted, but the regulatory framework separates them by frequency proximity."}], "concept": "ITU/regulatory framework splits unwanted emissions into two adjacent regions of the spectrum relative to the carrier: the OUT-OF-BAND domain — emissions immediately adjacent to the necessary bandwidth, arising directly from the modulation/keying process (e.g., excessive bandwidth, overmodulation splatter) — and the SPURIOUS domain, which lies beyond the OOB domain and contains harmonics, parasitics, intermodulation products, and frequency-conversion products that can be removed without degrading the intended signal. So OOB ⊂ unwanted emissions, but is distinct from spurious. The distinguishing criterion: OOB is a byproduct of the modulation itself and sits right at the band edges; spurious can be suppressed without affecting the transmitted information.", "misconception": "Treating 'spurious' as a catch-all for everything unwanted, when the standards specifically reserve 'out-of-band' for energy immediately adjacent to the necessary bandwidth.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Out-of-band = Out the edges, just Outside the channel; Spurious = Spread far away.", "edge_cases": "Harmonics and parasitics are subsets of spurious emissions, so if a question lists both 'spurious' and 'harmonic', spurious is the broader correct choice — but only for energy in the spurious domain, not adjacent to the channel."}

{"core": "21.375 MHz is exactly 3 × 7.125 MHz, the third harmonic of your operating frequency. A transmitter with inadequate output filtering radiates these harmonics, so your signal appears on a band you weren't actually transmitting on.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A defective filter choke causes power-supply ripple (hum on the signal) or poor DC filtering, not radiation on an unrelated higher frequency.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like a real transmitter fault, and learners may not check whether the fault mechanism produces out-of-band radiation."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Keying speed has no effect on the carrier frequency; fast CW only affects readability and bandwidth slightly, never moving you to a harmonic frequency.", "why_tempting": "It's an operating-related distractor that might seem to cause 'splatter,' but splatter is near the operating frequency, not at 3× it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A bad filter capacitor produces ripple/hum and poor regulation, again an audio-rate defect, not emission at a harmonic three times higher.", "why_tempting": "Like the choke option, it's a plausible-sounding hardware fault that confuses power-supply problems with RF spectral purity."}], "concept": "Any non-linear stage (a Class C amplifier, an overdriven final, or a mistuned/poorly-filtered output) generates integer multiples of the fundamental: 2f, 3f, 4f, etc. Here f = 7.125 MHz, so 2f = 14.250, 3f = 21.375 MHz — an exact match to the third harmonic. Output low-pass filtering and proper tuning are required to suppress these. The diagnostic shortcut: divide the reported frequency by your actual frequency; if you get a small integer, it's a harmonic.", "misconception": "That an unexpected far-off-frequency report indicates a power-supply or operating-technique problem, rather than recognizing the simple integer-multiple relationship that signals harmonic radiation.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Reported ÷ Actual = whole number ⇒ harmonic. 21.375 / 7.125 = 3.", "edge_cases": "If the reported frequency is NOT a clean integer multiple, suspect spurious mixing products, parasitic oscillation, or an image — not simple harmonics."}

{"core": "Splatter is the spurious emission of energy outside a transmitter's intended bandwidth, produced when an SSB or AM signal is overmodulated, driving the modulator/PA into nonlinear flat-topping.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Unwanted feedback causes self-oscillation (parasitic oscillations or 'singing'), not the wideband sideband splatter heard on adjacent channels.", "why_tempting": "Feedback is a real amplifier fault that produces spurious output, so it sounds plausible as an interference source."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A non-resonant antenna causes high SWR and inefficient radiation/possible RF feedback, but it doesn't generate the out-of-band modulation products that define splatter.", "why_tempting": "Antenna mismatch is a common cause of many transmitter problems, so learners reach for it."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Keying a CW transmitter too fast (or with sharp keying waveforms) produces key clicks, not splatter; clicks come from abrupt rise/fall times in the keying envelope.", "why_tempting": "Key clicks are also a bandwidth-related artifact, and 'too fast' suggests excessive bandwidth, so it's easy to confuse with splatter."}], "concept": "Splatter arises from nonlinearity. When modulation drives the stage beyond its linear region, the envelope clips (flat-tops), and clipping is mathematically equivalent to adding harmonics of the audio. These mix with the carrier to create high-order sidebands that extend well beyond the normal ~2.7 kHz SSB or AM bandwidth, spilling into adjacent channels. The fix is to keep audio gain/mic gain and ALC within limits so the PA stays linear. Note the distinction by emission mode: overmodulation/flat-topping → splatter (phone); abrupt keying envelope → key clicks (CW); positive feedback → parasitic oscillation.", "misconception": "That any source of adjacent-channel interference is 'splatter.' Splatter specifically means overmodulation-induced out-of-band products on phone; key clicks and parasitics are distinct mechanisms.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Splatter = Speak/Shout too loud — overmodulation splashes onto the neighbours.", "edge_cases": "Speech processors and compression raise average modulation but, if set too high, push toward flat-topping and worsen splatter; proper ALC action is the safeguard."}

{"core": "A harmonic of your 15-m signal (~21 MHz) lands inside channel 3 (60–66 MHz): 3 × 21 = 63 MHz falls squarely in the band. Because only the channel coincident with a harmonic is affected, the interference is harmonic emission from your transmitter.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter on the TV blocks low-frequency HF energy at the antenna input, helping with fundamental overload — but a 21 MHz signal would be rejected by it anyway, and it wouldn't explain why only one channel is hit.", "why_tempting": "High-pass filters are the classic TVI cure, so it sounds plausible as 'the missing filter.'"}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A transmitter low-pass filter does suppress harmonics, but the question asks for the CAUSE of the interference, not the remedy. The cause is the harmonic being radiated; missing filtering is just why it isn't attenuated.", "why_tempting": "It's the correct fix for this very problem, so it feels like the right answer to a 'why' question."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Front-end (fundamental) overload from a strong nearby signal degrades ALL channels and other devices, not a single channel — the selective, single-channel symptom rules it out.", "why_tempting": "Overload is a common TVI mechanism and learners reach for it whenever a TV misbehaves near a transmitter."}], "concept": "Harmonics are integer multiples of the fundamental: f_n = n·f0. The 15-m band is ~21 MHz; its 3rd harmonic is 3×21 = 63 MHz, inside channel 3 (60–66 MHz). The diagnostic key is selectivity: harmonic interference appears only on the specific channel(s) whose passband contains a harmonic, whereas fundamental overload is broadband and channel-independent. So a single-channel symptom that coincides with an exact multiple of your operating frequency points unambiguously to harmonic radiation.", "misconception": "Conflating the two distinct TVI mechanisms — harmonics (transmitter-side, frequency-selective) versus front-end overload (receiver-side, broadband) — and confusing the cause with its cure.", "link": "Tools — Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "One channel = harmonic; all channels = overload.", "edge_cases": "The fix is a low-pass filter at the transmitter to suppress the harmonic; a high-pass filter at the TV addresses overload instead. Match the filter to the mechanism."}

{"core": "Flat topping is overdriving the amplifier into compression/clipping; in SSB this is most often caused by feeding too much audio in, i.e. excessive microphone gain driving the amplifier beyond its linear region.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Inadequate supply current may cause voltage sag or dropout but does not produce the symmetrical clipping of envelope peaks called flat topping.", "why_tempting": "A weak supply can cause distortion under load, so it sounds plausible for a power-related fault."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter sits after the amplifier to suppress harmonics; a defective one affects spurious output, not the linearity of the envelope itself.", "why_tempting": "LPFs are associated with clean transmitter output, so a defect seems like it could degrade signal quality."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A defective sideband filter produces unwanted sideband/carrier leakage or poor audio shaping, not the peak compression characteristic of flat topping.", "why_tempting": "Sideband filters are central to SSB generation, making them an easy scapegoat for any SSB distortion."}], "concept": "In SSB the RF envelope follows the audio amplitude. The PA must stay in its linear region so output peaks track input peaks proportionally. Driving the chain too hard (high mic gain → high audio → high RF drive) pushes the PA past saturation: the peaks of the envelope are clipped flat ('flat topping'). This clipping generates intermodulation products and splatter, widening the occupied bandwidth and causing adjacent-channel interference. The cure is to reduce gain (mic gain / ALC drive) so peaks stay below the saturation point — verified on a two-tone test or by monitoring the ALC/scope envelope.", "misconception": "Assuming non-linearity in an amplifier must come from a hardware fault, when the dominant operational cause is simply overdriving it with too much audio level.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Flat top = too much drive on top — back off the mic gain.", "edge_cases": "Proper ALC action is designed to limit drive and prevent flat topping; running mic gain so high that ALC is constantly pinned still produces distortion, so set audio for only light ALC deflection on peaks."}

{"core": "Overdriving an amplifier stage pushes it into nonlinear (often clipping) operation, where the output waveform departs from a clean sinusoid; Fourier analysis of a distorted/clipped waveform shows strong energy at integer multiples of the fundamental — i.e. harmonics.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "An antenna tuner matches impedance at the operating frequency; a malfunction causes high SWR or power transfer problems, not generation of new harmonic frequencies inside the transmitter.", "why_tempting": "Tuner faults are a common real-world problem and learners associate any RF misbehaviour with spurious emissions."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A non-resonant antenna produces a mismatch (high SWR) and inefficient radiation, but the transmitter still generates only the frequencies its stages produce — it doesn't create harmonics.", "why_tempting": "It sounds like 'wrong frequency' behaviour, which is easily conflated with emitting harmonics."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A defective driver circuit typically reduces output, distorts in unpredictable ways, or kills the signal; while severe defects could distort, it isn't the textbook cause of excessive harmonics the way deliberate overdriving is.", "why_tempting": "'Defective' implies something is broken and producing bad output, so it feels plausible as a harmonic source."}], "concept": "Any linear stage has a transfer characteristic. While operating within its linear region, output is a scaled copy of the input and remains spectrally pure. Drive it beyond saturation/cutoff and the transfer function becomes nonlinear: the output expands as a power series y = a1x + a2x² + a3x³ + ... The higher-order terms (x², x³) generate components at 2f, 3f, etc. Symmetric clipping (a flat-topped sine) is rich in odd harmonics; asymmetric distortion adds even harmonics too. Class C amplifiers deliberately conduct in pulses and therefore require output tank/low-pass filtering to suppress these harmonics. Overdriving compounds the problem by deepening the clipping.", "misconception": "That harmonics are caused by antenna/matching problems.
