Antenna and tuner issues are impedance/SWR effects at the operating frequency; harmonic generation is a nonlinearity (distortion) phenomenon inside the transmitter's amplifying stages.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Clipping = harmonics: a squared-off sine is a stack of harmonics.", "edge_cases": "Even a properly driven Class C or push-pull stage produces some harmonics; the issue is 'excessive' levels, which overdriving and inadequate output filtering make worse. Low-pass filters and pi-network tanks are the standard cure."}

{"core": "The 10-metre band is around 28 MHz; its second harmonic is 2 × 28 = 56 MHz, which lands squarely on the observed interfering frequency.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A crystal oscillator on its fundamental produces a single design frequency; 'fundamental' by definition is not a harmonic and there's no reason it would fall at 56 MHz, nor would it be described as an HF-transmitter spurious product.", "why_tempting": "Sounds technical and plausible if you forget that the question is asking about a harmonic relationship from an HF transmission."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "The 80-metre band is roughly 3.5 MHz. The seventh harmonic is 7 × 3.5 ≈ 24.5 MHz, nowhere near 56 MHz.", "why_tempting": "A high harmonic number invites you to assume it reaches a high frequency without doing the arithmetic."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "The 15-metre band is about 21 MHz; the third harmonic is 3 × 21 = 63 MHz, which misses 56 MHz.", "why_tempting": "63 MHz is in the same VHF ballpark as 56 MHz, so it feels close enough if you don't multiply precisely."}], "concept": "Harmonics occur at integer multiples of the fundamental: f_n = n × f0. To diagnose a spurious signal, take each candidate band's fundamental frequency and test which n×f0 equals the measured frequency. Band centres: 80 m ≈ 3.5 MHz, 15 m ≈ 21 MHz, 10 m ≈ 28 MHz. Only 28 MHz × 2 = 56 MHz matches. Second harmonics are especially common offenders because even-order distortion in unbalanced or poorly filtered output stages produces strong 2f0 content right at the edge of the VHF region.", "misconception": "Assuming a larger harmonic number or a higher band automatically yields a higher frequency, rather than computing n × f0 for each option and comparing to the target.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Wavelength → frequency: 10 m ≈ 28 MHz, double it for the 2nd harmonic = 56 MHz.", "edge_cases": "Amateur band edges are ranges, not single points (10 m spans 28.0–29.7 MHz), so the second harmonic falls across 56.0–59.4 MHz; 56 MHz corresponds to the low end of the band."}

{"core": "Driving an RF power amplifier too hard pushes it into nonlinear (clipping/saturation) operation, and any nonlinear transfer function generates harmonics — integer multiples of the fundamental — in the output.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Tuning the tank to the fundamental is exactly what you want: the resonant tank presents high impedance at the fundamental and low impedance to harmonics, suppressing them.", "why_tempting": "It mentions tuning and harmonics in one sentence, so it sounds like it could be a cause — but it's actually the cure."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Oscillator instability causes frequency drift or chirp, not harmonic generation. Harmonics are a function of amplifier nonlinearity, independent of how stable the carrier frequency is.", "why_tempting": "'Unstable oscillator' sounds like a generic transmitter fault, so a learner may lump it in with spurious emissions."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "High-level (e.g. plate) modulation is a legitimate, properly designed technique for AM and does not inherently produce harmonics; it produces sidebands about the carrier.", "why_tempting": "Modulation does add spectral content, so it's easy to confuse intended sidebands with unwanted harmonics."}], "concept": "A harmonic is an integer multiple (2f, 3f, ...) of the fundamental. They arise whenever a stage's transfer function is nonlinear: expand the output as a power series Vout = a1·Vin + a2·Vin² + a3·Vin³ + ..., and the higher-order terms generate the harmonic frequencies. A linear amplifier (operated within its drive limits) ideally has only the a1 term. Overdriving it forces clipping/saturation, dramatically increasing a2, a3, etc., so harmonic energy rises. The output tank's job is then to filter these out, presenting maximum impedance at f and shunting harmonics.", "misconception": "Believing harmonics come from frequency-domain problems (oscillator drift) or from modulation, rather than from amplitude nonlinearity caused by overdrive.", "link": "section 8", "mnemonic": "Overdrive = distort = harmonics.", "edge_cases": "Class C amplifiers are intentionally nonlinear and rich in harmonics — they rely entirely on the tuned tank for filtering — so even at normal drive a poorly filtered Class C stage radiates harmonics."}

{"core": "A notch filter is a band-reject filter with an extremely narrow stopband — ideally it attenuates a single frequency (the notch) while passing everything else.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "This describes a general band-stop (band-reject) filter that rejects a wide band, not the narrow single-frequency null that defines a notch.", "why_tempting": "A notch IS a band-reject filter, so this is technically the parent category — the difference is bandwidth, which is easy to overlook."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "Passing only one frequency while rejecting all others is a narrowband band-pass filter — the inverse of a notch.", "why_tempting": "It uses the same 'single frequency' wording as the correct answer but inverts pass and attenuate."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Passing a range and rejecting everything else is a band-pass filter, the opposite function of a notch.", "why_tempting": "'Except a range of frequencies' sounds selective, and band-pass and notch are often discussed together."}], "concept": "Filters are classified by which frequencies they pass versus reject. Low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, and band-stop differ in the shape of their response. A band-stop filter creates a stopband; as that stopband narrows toward a single frequency with a high Q (Q = f0/BW), it becomes a notch. An ideal notch has infinite attenuation at exactly f0 and unity gain everywhere else — useful for nulling a single interfering carrier (e.g., heterodyne tone, power-line harmonic) without disturbing the rest of the passband.", "misconception": "Confusing 'notch/band-reject' with 'band-pass,' or failing to distinguish a narrow single-frequency notch from a wide band-reject filter.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Notch = knock out ONE note; everything else plays through.", "edge_cases": "Real notch filters have finite depth and nonzero bandwidth; the deeper the notch, the higher the Q and the sharper (narrower) the response."}

{"core": "A filter that attenuates frequencies below its cutoff and passes frequencies above it is a high-pass filter — the 60 MHz cutoff marks the lower edge of the pass band.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A notch (band-reject) filter attenuates only a narrow band of frequencies while passing everything above and below it — not a broad range below a single cutoff.", "why_tempting": "Both involve attenuation, so a learner skimming for 'rejects something' might pick it."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter does the opposite: it passes frequencies below cutoff and attenuates those above it.", "why_tempting": "The question mentions 'below' and 'low-pass' contains 'low', inviting a careless word match instead of reading what is attenuated."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A band-pass filter passes a defined band between two cutoff frequencies and attenuates both above and below — it needs two cutoffs, not one.", "why_tempting": "It's a common filter type and the single stated cutoff might be misread as one edge of a band."}], "concept": "Classify filters by which side of the cutoff is the pass band. High-pass: |H(jω)| → pass for ω > ωc, stop for ω < ωc. Here attenuation occurs below 60 MHz, so everything above passes — that is the textbook high-pass response. A simple first-order high-pass is a series capacitor (or shunt inductor) whose reactance falls with rising frequency, so high frequencies couple through and low frequencies are blocked. Don't be misled by the keyword 'below'; the defining question is always 'what gets through?'", "misconception": "Matching the word 'below' (or 'low') to the answer 'low-pass' instead of identifying which band is attenuated versus passed.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Name the filter for what it PASSES, not what it blocks: attenuates the low end → high-PASS.", "edge_cases": "Real filters don't cut off sharply at the cutoff; the cutoff is conventionally the −3 dB (half-power) point, with a gradual roll-off whose steepness depends on filter order."}

{"core": "A band-pass filter passes a defined range of frequencies while attenuating energy both above and below that passband.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter attenuates frequencies below a cutoff and passes everything above it — it only rolls off on the low side.", "why_tempting": "It does attenuate some frequencies, so a hasty reader may forget that it lets all higher frequencies through."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A notch (band-stop) filter does the opposite: it attenuates a narrow range and passes everything above and below it.", "why_tempting": "The phrasing 'above and below a certain range' sounds like notch behaviour reversed — easy to invert the logic."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter attenuates frequencies above a cutoff and passes everything below it — only one-sided rejection.", "why_tempting": "Low-pass filters are the classic harmonic-suppression filter on transmitters, so it's the familiar default answer."}], "concept": "Filters are classified by which portion of the spectrum they pass. A band-pass response has two cutoff frequencies, fL and fH; gain is near unity between them and falls off (typically −3 dB at each corner, then by the filter order's slope) outside. It can be modelled as a series combination of a high-pass (sets fL) and a low-pass (sets fH) section, or as a resonant LC network whose center frequency is f0 = 1/(2π√(LC)) and whose selectivity is set by Q = f0/BW. Because it rejects on both sides of the passband, it's the only choice that 'attenuates energy above AND below a range.'", "misconception": "Confusing two-sided rejection outside a passband (band-pass) with the single-sided cutoff of high-/low-pass filters, or with the inverse selectivity of a notch.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Band-PASS lets the band PASS; everything outside (both sides) is blocked.", "edge_cases": "A wide band-pass with low Q has gentle skirts; a high-Q band-pass approaches a narrow peaked response — both still attenuate above and below the passband."}

{"core": "A filter inserted in a transmission line must present the line's characteristic impedance at both ports so that energy passes through without creating an impedance discontinuity, which would otherwise reflect power back toward the source.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "Spurious emissions are suppressed by a filter's frequency response (its stopband attenuation), not by its impedance match — a perfectly matched filter with poor selectivity still passes spurs.", "why_tempting": "Filters are commonly fitted to reduce spurious/harmonic output, so it's easy to conflate the filter's job with the reason for matching its impedance."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "Line balance concerns common-mode symmetry between two conductors; impedance matching is about characteristic impedance (Z0), a separate property. A matched filter can still be balanced or unbalanced.", "why_tempting": "Both 'match' and 'balance' sound like keeping the line orderly, so they blur together if you don't distinguish balance from impedance."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "Receiver noise is dominated by front-end noise figure and external noise, not by whether an inline filter matches the feedline; mismatch causes reflections, not added noise.", "why_tempting": "Filters are often used to clean up receive paths, so 'minimize noise' feels plausible as a benefit of correct installation."}], "concept": "A transmission line of characteristic impedance Z0 only delivers all incident power to a termination when the load equals Z0. Inserting a filter places it in series with the line; if the filter's input/output impedance ≠ Z0, the discontinuity produces a reflection coefficient Γ = (Z − Z0)/(Z + Z0) ≠ 0. The reflected wave sets up standing waves (raising SWR), wastes power, and can stress the transmitter. Properly designed filters (e.g. constant-k, image-impedance, or modern doubly-terminated designs) are synthesized to present Z0 (commonly 50 Ω) across the passband so insertion loss stays low and the response shape is preserved. A mismatch also distorts the intended filter response because the design assumes correct source/load termination.", "misconception": "Believing the impedance match exists to improve filtering or reduce emissions/noise, when in fact its purpose is purely to avoid reflections and the resulting SWR/power loss.", "link": "SWR & Impedance Match", "mnemonic": "Match the impedance, or the power bounces back — reflection, not rejection.", "edge_cases": "Reactive filters rely on being terminated in their design impedance at BOTH ends; a mismatch at the load end can ripple back and degrade the passband even if the input looks fine."}

{"core": "A low-pass filter passes the desired low (shortwave) frequencies while attenuating the much higher-frequency signals that are aliasing or breaking through into the receiver's intended band.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter rejects low frequencies and passes high ones — the opposite of what's needed; it would attenuate your wanted shortwave signals and let the interfering high-frequency stations through.", "why_tempting": "The interferers are 'high frequency,' so a learner may grab 'high-pass' by keyword association, forgetting that 'high-pass' describes what is passed, not what is blocked."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A band-pass filter could work but is unnecessary and over-specified here — you only need to remove energy above the shortwave band, not also reject everything below it. A simple low-pass is the targeted, minimal solution.", "why_tempting": "Band-pass selecting only the shortwave band would indeed exclude the high-frequency stations, so it seems valid; the exam wants the simplest filter matching the symptom."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A band-reject (notch) filter removes a narrow slice of spectrum, but the problem is multiple stations across a broad high-frequency range, not one narrow interferer.", "why_tempting": "'Reject' sounds like exactly what you want for unwanted signals, but it implies a narrow notch rather than rejecting an entire high-frequency region."}], "concept": "Cheap SDR front ends often lack adequate input filtering and use direct-sampling architectures. Without an anti-alias/preselector filter, strong out-of-band signals above the ADC's effective bandwidth fold (alias) back into the displayed passband, appearing as 'ghost' stations at the wrong frequency. The fix is to attenuate everything above the frequencies of interest before the mixer/ADC — that is the definition of a low-pass filter (passband from DC up to a cutoff fc, increasing attenuation beyond fc). Naming convention: a filter is named for the band it PASSES, not the band it stops.", "misconception": "Assuming a filter named for 'high' frequencies is what you use to remove high-frequency interference — confusing what a filter passes with what it blocks.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "A filter is named for what it lets THROUGH: low-pass keeps the lows.", "edge_cases": "If the unwanted signals were a single narrow carrier, a band-reject/notch would be appropriate; the broadband 'much higher frequencies' description is what makes low-pass the right call."}

{"core": "AC mains power is 60 Hz (DC-like compared to RF), so a filter that passes the low-frequency line voltage while blocking the high-frequency RF noise is a low-pass filter.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter passes RF and blocks the low-frequency mains — exactly backwards, it would let the noise through.", "why_tempting": "If you confuse which signal you want to keep (the RF) versus block, high-pass sounds like it 'targets' the high-frequency noise."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A band-pass passes a narrow window of frequencies and rejects everything outside it; RF noise spans a wide range, so this wouldn't reliably suppress it.", "why_tempting": "Sounds selective and sophisticated, as if it could isolate just the wanted 60 Hz."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A band-reject (notch) filter only kills a narrow band, while conducted RF noise is broadband — a single notch won't clean it up.", "why_tempting": "'Reject' the noise sounds appealing, but RFI isn't confined to one narrow band."}], "concept": "The mains carries 60 Hz power; RFI on the line is at radio frequencies (kHz–MHz). An AC line filter is a low-pass network — typically series inductors (high X_L at RF) plus shunt capacitors (low X_C at RF) forming an L/Pi/T section. Its cutoff sits far above 60 Hz but below the RF you want to stop, so power passes with negligible loss while RF is attenuated. X_L = 2πfL rises and X_C = 1/(2πfC) falls with frequency, giving the characteristic low-pass slope.", "misconception": "Thinking the filter should 'target' the high-frequency noise (high-pass/band-reject), when in fact you design around what you want to PASS — the low-frequency power.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Pass the power, kill the RF — power is low frequency, so Low-pass.", "edge_cases": "Real line filters are bidirectional and often include common-mode chokes and Y/X capacitors; safety-rated caps must be used across AC mains."}

{"core": "A notch filter has a very narrow rejection band, so it can null out a single interfering carrier sitting close to your desired signal without removing the wanted signal itself.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter only blocks energy below a cutoff frequency; it cannot reject a single signal that is near (and likely above or just beside) your receive frequency.", "why_tempting": "Filters are associated with blocking unwanted signals, so any named filter type sounds plausible."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter passes everything below cutoff and attenuates higher frequencies — far too broad to remove one interferer adjacent to your tuned frequency.", "why_tempting": "Low-pass filters are the classic TVI/harmonic remedy, so learners reach for them reflexively."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A band-reject filter rejects a band of frequencies. Its stopband is too wide and would also attenuate your desired signal that sits right next to the interferer.", "why_tempting": "'Band-reject' literally means reject signals, and it is the broad cousin of a notch, so it is easily confused with the correct answer."}], "concept": "All these are frequency-selective networks, but the key parameter is bandwidth of rejection. A notch (band-stop with very high Q) produces a sharp null only a few hundred Hz to a couple kHz wide, centered on the interferer. Because the desired and interfering signals are very close in frequency, only a high-Q, narrow notch can attenuate the unwanted carrier while preserving the wanted one. A general band-reject filter is the low-Q version with a wide stopband; a notch is effectively a band-reject filter taken to a narrow, deep extreme. High-pass and low-pass are single-cutoff topologies and inherently cannot isolate a frequency that lies adjacent to the passband you want to keep.", "misconception": "Treating 'band-reject' and 'notch' as interchangeable. Both attenuate within a stopband, but selectivity (Q / bandwidth) is what matters when the interferer is close to the wanted signal — only the narrow notch spares the desired signal.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Notch = narrow; pick the sharpest tool to remove one nearby tone.", "edge_cases": "An automatic DSP notch can track and null multiple carriers; analog notch filters are manually tuned and best for a single steady heterodyne."}

{"core": "A band-pass filter on each receiver passes only the desired band's frequencies and attenuates the strong signals from co-located transmitters operating on other bands, minimizing front-end overload and interference.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter only rejects frequencies above a cutoff; it would still admit strong signals from any transmitter operating below the receiver's band.", "why_tempting": "Low-pass filters are commonly cited for harmonic suppression on transmitters, so they're easy to associate with Field Day interference."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter only rejects frequencies below a cutoff and would pass everything above, including nearby transmitters on higher bands.", "why_tempting": "It seems like the complementary 'protective' filter, and HPFs are familiar from TVI mitigation."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A band-reject (notch) filter removes one narrow band while passing everything else — the opposite of what's needed when you want to accept only your own band and reject all the others.", "why_tempting": "'Reject' sounds right when the goal is rejecting interference, but here you must reject many bands and keep only one."}], "concept": "In a multi-transmitter, multi-band site the receiver's front end is bombarded by very strong off-band signals that cause desensitization and intermodulation. The fix is selectivity ahead of the first mixer: a band-pass filter forms a pass window around the operating band and provides steep attenuation outside it, so out-of-band transmitter energy is rejected before it can overload the receiver. Choosing pass-band vs reject logic comes down to the ratio of wanted-to-unwanted bands — when you keep one band and dump all others, pass-band is the efficient choice; a single notch (band-reject) only helps when one interferer dominates.", "misconception": "Confusing 'reject the interference' with a band-reject filter. The desired behaviour is defined by what you keep (one band), so a band-pass filter is correct.", "link": "Tools — Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Keep one band → band-PASS; kill one band → band-REJECT.", "edge_cases": "If interference came from a single dominant signal rather than many bands, a band-reject (notch) filter would be the better targeted solution."}

{"core": "A band-reject (notch) filter is needed because the offending signal lies in a narrow band (31 m ≈ 9.5 MHz) that sits between or near your wanted bands (40 m ≈ 7 MHz and 30 m ≈ 10.1 MHz), so you must attenuate that specific band while passing frequencies on either side.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter passes everything above its cutoff and would let the 9.5 MHz broadcast signal through while also affecting your lower 40 m band — it cannot selectively kill one band sandwiched in the middle.", "why_tempting": "The interferer is higher than 40 m, so blocking 'low' frequencies feels logical, but it ignores the 30 m band sitting above the interferer."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A band-pass filter passes one band and rejects the rest; you need to receive TWO separate bands (40 m and 30 m), so a single pass band can't accommodate both while notching out the 31 m signal between them.", "why_tempting": "You do want to 'pass' your desired bands, but a band-pass can't selectively remove a signal lying between two wanted bands."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter passes everything below its cutoff; the 9.5 MHz interferer is above 7 MHz but below 10.1 MHz, so any cutoff would either kill your 30 m band or admit the interferer.", "why_tempting": "Setting a cutoff just below 9.5 MHz seems to block the broadcast, but it also blocks your wanted 30 m band at 10.1 MHz."}], "concept": "The defining feature is that the unwanted signal (31 m ≈ 9.5 MHz) is positioned BETWEEN your two desired bands: 40 m at ~7 MHz and 30 m at ~10.1 MHz. Only a band-reject (band-stop/notch) response — which attenuates a narrow band while passing frequencies both below and above it — can null one band while preserving signals on either side. High-pass and low-pass each have a single cutoff and can only protect one side; band-pass passes only one contiguous band. Mapping the frequencies to wavelength (λ = 300/f in MHz) makes the ordering obvious: 7 < 9.5 < 10.1 MHz.", "misconception": "Assuming that because the interferer is at a 'higher' or 'lower' wavelength designation you can simply cut off everything above/below it. The trap is failing to notice the interferer sits in the gap between two wanted bands, which mandates a notch rather than a single-cutoff filter.", "link": "Wavelength ↔ Frequency", "mnemonic": "Interference IN-BETWEEN two keepers → reject just the band in the middle (band-REJECT).", "edge_cases": "A band-reject filter is sometimes called a notch or band-stop filter; a very narrow version is a notch filter aimed at a single carrier frequency."}

{"core": "A band-pass filter passes the 2-metre amateur band (144–148 MHz) while attenuating signals above and below it, rejecting the adjacent land mobile transmitters causing the overload.", "distractors": [{"option": "A", "why_wrong": "A low-pass filter attenuates everything above its cutoff, so it would block the 2-metre signal you want if the cutoff is near the band, and it cannot reject interferers that sit below 144 MHz.", "why_tempting": "Low-pass filters are the classic fix for HF transmitter harmonics into TV, so learners reach for it reflexively."}, {"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter only attenuates signals below its cutoff; land mobile interference exists on both adjacent sides of 2 m, so it can't reject the higher-frequency offenders.", "why_tempting": "It seems right because it blocks lower-frequency interference, but the problem spans both sides of the band."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A notch (band-stop) filter rejects one narrow frequency or band; with several interferers spread across adjacent bands you'd need many notches, and it's the wrong tool for broad adjacent-band rejection.", "why_tempting": "'Several transmitters' makes people think of nulling individual carriers, but the issue is broadband adjacent-band energy overloading the front end."}], "concept": "Receiver front-end overload (blocking/desensitization) occurs when strong off-frequency signals exceed the linear range of the RF amplifier or mixer, generating intermodulation or gain compression. The cure is to attenuate the out-of-band energy before it reaches the active stages while preserving the wanted band. A band-pass filter centred on 144–148 MHz does exactly this: it provides a passband over the amateur allocation and increasing attenuation on both skirts where the land mobile services (e.g. just below 144 and just above 148 MHz) operate. Low-pass and high-pass each guard only one side; only a band-pass (or a cavity/helical resonator acting as one) rejects strong signals on both adjacent sides simultaneously.", "misconception": "That filters are interchangeable, or that 'several transmitters' implies notching each one. The key is matching filter response to the geometry of the interference: it surrounds the wanted band on both sides, so you need a band-pass.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Band-pass = 'keep only my band, kill both sides.'", "edge_cases": "If all interferers were strictly on one side of the band, a low- or high-pass could suffice; a band-pass is the general solution when interference brackets the band."}

{"core": "A filter that passes frequencies below a cutoff and attenuates those above it is a low-pass filter; here the 40 MHz cutoff marks the boundary above which signals are rejected.", "distractors": [{"option": "B", "why_wrong": "A band-pass filter passes only a band between two cutoffs and attenuates both below and above; this filter has a single cutoff and rejects only the high side.", "why_tempting": "'Band-pass' sounds generic and a learner may forget it requires two cutoffs."}, {"option": "C", "why_wrong": "A high-pass filter passes frequencies above its cutoff and attenuates those below — exactly opposite to the described behaviour.", "why_tempting": "The phrase 'above its cutoff frequency of 40 MHz' draws the eye, and 'high' pairs mentally with the high number, inviting a reversed answer."}, {"option": "D", "why_wrong": "A notch (band-stop) filter rejects only a narrow band and passes everything else; this filter rejects an entire continuous range above 40 MHz, not a slot.", "why_tempting": "Both notch and the described filter 'attenuate' something, so the word 'attenuates' can mislead."}], "concept": "Filters are classified by which frequencies survive (pass) versus which are reduced (attenuate), relative to a cutoff defined at the −3 dB point. Low-pass: passband below f_c, stopband above. High-pass: the mirror image. Band-pass and band-stop/notch use two cutoffs to define a passed or rejected band. The key is to read what is *passed*, not what is named in the question: 'attenuates above f_c' means everything below survives — low-pass.", "misconception": "Conflating the stated cutoff value with the filter name — seeing 'above 40 MHz' and choosing 'high-pass' because of word association rather than analyzing pass vs. stop behaviour.", "link": "Reactance & Resonance", "mnemonic": "Name the filter after what it PASSES, not what it stops: passes the lows = low-pass.", "edge_cases": "Cutoff is conventionally the −3 dB (half-power) point, not a perfectly sharp wall; real filters roll off gradually beyond it."}
